Section 1:
FIRST CONFERENCE REPORT
between the two Houses concerning
Senate Bill No. 878, entitled
A bill to make, supplement, adjust, and consolidate
appropriations for various state departments and agencies, the
judicial branch, and the legislative branch for the fiscal year
ending September 30, 2027; to provide for certain conditions on
appropriations; and to provide for the expenditure of the
appropriations.
Recommends:
First: That the House recede from the Substitute of the
House as passed by the House.
Second: That the Senate and House agree to the Substitute of
the Senate as passed by the Senate, amended to read as follows:
(attached)
Third: That the Senate and House agree to the title of the
bill to read as follows:
A bill to make, supplement, adjust, and consolidate
appropriations for various state departments and agencies, the
judicial branch, the legislative branch, and capital outlay
purposes for the fiscal years ending September 30, 2026 and
September 30, 2027; to provide for certain conditions on
appropriations; to provide for the expenditure of the
appropriations; and to repeal acts and parts of acts.
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 cs mbfz3k
_______________________ ________________________
Sarah E. Anthony Ann Bollin
_______________________ ________________________

SUBSTITUTE FOR
SENATE BILL NO. 878
A bill to make, supplement, adjust, and consolidate
appropriations for various state departments and agencies, the
judicial branch, the legislative branch, and capital outlay
purposes for the fiscal years ending September 30, 2026 and
September 30, 2027; to provide for certain conditions on
appropriations; to provide for the expenditure of the
appropriations; and to repeal acts and parts of acts.

17 PART 2
18 PROVISIONS CONCERNING APPROPRIATIONS

20 GENERAL SECTIONS
21 Sec. 201. In accordance with section 30 of article IX of the
22 state constitution of 1963, for the fiscal year ending September
23 30, 2027, total state spending under part 1 from state sources is
24 $120,784,100.00 and total state spending under part 1 from state
25 sources to be paid to local units of government is $10,400,000.00.
26 The following itemized statement identifies appropriations from
27 which spending to local units of government will occur:
28 DEPARTMENT OF AGRICULTURE AND RURAL DEVELOPMENT
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
9
1 Agriculture preservation easement grants $ 1,900,000
2 Environmental stewardship/MAEAP 4,100,000
3 Local conservation districts 3,000,000
4 Qualified forest program 1,400,000
5 TOTAL $ 10,400,000
6 Sec. 202. The appropriations under this part and part 1 are
7 subject to the management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1101
8 to 18.1594.
9 Sec. 203. As used in part 1 and this part:
10 (a) "Department" means the department of agriculture and rural
11 development.
12 (b) "Director" means the director of the department.
13 (c) "Fiscal agencies" means the Michigan house fiscal agency
14 and the Michigan senate fiscal agency.
15 (d) "FTE" means full-time equated.
16 (e) "IDG" means interdepartmental grant.
17 (f) "MAEAP" means the Michigan agriculture environmental
18 assurance program.
19 (g) "MDEGLE" means the Michigan department of environment,
20 Great Lakes, and energy.
21 (h) "Standard report recipients" means the house and senate
22 appropriations committees, the house and senate appropriations
23 subcommittees on agriculture and rural development, the house and
24 senate fiscal agencies, the house and senate policy offices, and
25 the state budget office.
26 (i) "Subcommittees" means all members of the subcommittees of
27 the house and senate appropriations committees with jurisdiction
28 over the budget for the department.
29 (j) "TB" means tuberculosis.
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
10
1 (k) "USDA" means the United States Department of Agriculture.
2 Sec. 204. If the state administrative board, acting under
3 section 3 of 1921 PA 2, MCL 17.3, transfers funds from an amount
4 appropriated under part 1, the legislature may, by a concurrent
5 resolution adopted by a majority of the members elected to and
6 serving in each house, intertransfer funds within part 1 for the
7 particular department, board, commission, officer, or institution.
8 Sec. 205. (1) The department shall use the internet to fulfill
9 the reporting requirements of this part and shall make each report
10 readily accessible to the public and conspicuously post each
11 required report in a single archivable location on the department's
12 website not later than the due date required for each report.
13 (2) In addition to placing all reports required in the current
14 fiscal year on the department's website, the department shall
15 maintain on its website all reports placed on the website from
16 previous fiscal years posted by fiscal year in the same single
17 archivable location.
18 (3) The department shall transmit all required reports for the
19 current fiscal year to the standard report recipients and any other
20 required recipients by email. The email shall include a copy of the
21 report and a link to access the report online.
22 Sec. 206. The department shall receive and retain copies of
23 all reports funded from appropriations in part 1. The department
24 shall follow federal and state law and guidelines for short-term
25 and long-term retention of records. The department may
26 electronically retain copies of reports unless otherwise required
27 by federal and state guidelines.
28 Sec. 207. The department shall cooperate with the department
29 of technology, management, and budget to maintain a searchable
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
11
1 website accessible by the public at no cost that includes, but is
2 not limited to, all of the following for the department:
3 (a) Fiscal year-to-date expenditures by category.
4 (b) Fiscal year-to-date expenditures by appropriation unit.
5 (c) Fiscal year-to-date payments to a selected vendor,
6 including the vendor name, payment date, payment amount, and
7 payment description.
8 Sec. 208. (1) In addition to any other requirements under this
9 part, if the department is authorized under this part to expend
10 funds in addition to those appropriated in part 1, the department
11 must do all of the following:
12 (a) Not later than December 1, provide a report to the
13 chairpersons of the house and senate appropriations committees, the
14 house and senate fiscal agencies, and the state budget office that
15 details all of the following:
16 (i) The type of funding received during the previous fiscal
17 year that was authorized in part 2 of the article that made
18 appropriations for the department in the previous fiscal year.
19 (ii) When the funding was received.
20 (iii) The amount of funding received.
21 (iv) How much of the funding was spent and for what purpose or
22 purposes.
23 (b) Not later than 60 days after receipt of funds authorized
24 under this part, provide a report to the chairpersons of the house
25 and senate appropriations committees, the house and senate fiscal
26 agencies, and the state budget office that details all of the
27 following:
28 (i) The type of funding received.
29 (ii) When the funding was received.
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
12
1 (iii) The amount of funding received.
2 (iv) The anticipated or actual amount to be spent and the
3 specified purpose or purposes.
4 (c) Not later than February 15, provide a report to the
5 chairpersons of the house and senate appropriations committees, the
6 house and senate fiscal agencies, and the state budget office with
7 an estimate of funding authorized by this part that the department
8 anticipates it will receive in the subsequent fiscal year,
9 identifying all of the following:
10 (i) The type or types of funding anticipated.
11 (ii) The amount or amounts of funding anticipated.
12 (iii) The purpose or purposes of the funding.
13 (2) If another reporting requirement under this part would
14 provide substantially similar information on a substantially
15 similar timeframe as would be reported under subsection (1),
16 subsection (1) does not apply.
17 Sec. 209. Not later than December 15, the state budget office
18 shall prepare and submit a report that provides estimates of the
19 total general fund/general purpose appropriation lapses at the
20 close of the previous fiscal year. The report must summarize the
21 projected year-end general fund/general purpose appropriation
22 lapses by major departmental program or program areas. The state
23 budget office shall submit the report to the standard report
24 recipients and to the chairpersons of the house and senate
25 appropriations committees.
26 Sec. 210. Not later than 14 days after the release of the
27 executive budget recommendation, the department shall cooperate
28 with the state budget office to provide an annual report on
29 estimated state restricted fund balances, state restricted fund
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
13
1 projected revenues, and state restricted fund expenditures for the
2 previous 2 fiscal years. The report must be submitted to the
3 standard report recipients and to the chairpersons of the house and
4 senate appropriations committees.
5 Sec. 211. Not later than November 15, the department shall
6 disclose on a publicly accessible website private and other third-
7 party funds received by the department in the previous fiscal year.
8 The report must include the amount of funding received, the
9 specific source of funding received, the purpose for which funding
10 was expended, and the amount of any remaining funds. The report
11 must be submitted to the standard report recipients and to the
12 chairpersons of the house and senate appropriations committees.
13 Sec. 212. Consistent with section 217 of the management and
14 budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1217, each department and agency
15 receiving appropriations in part 1 shall prepare a report on out-
16 of-state travel expenses not later than January 1. The report must
17 list all travel by classified and unclassified employees outside
18 this state in the previous fiscal year that was funded in whole or
19 in part with funds appropriated in the department's or agency's
20 budget. The department shall submit the report to the standard
21 report recipients and to the house and senate appropriations
22 committees. The report must include all of the following
23 information:
24 (a) The dates of each travel occurrence.
25 (b) The total transportation and related costs of each travel
26 occurrence and the proportions funded with state general
27 fund/general purpose revenues, state restricted revenues, federal
28 revenues, local revenues, and other revenues.
29 Sec. 213. On a quarterly basis, the department or agency
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
14
1 receiving appropriations in part 1, shall report on the number of
2 full-time equated positions in pay status by civil service
3 classification, including a comparison by line item of the number
4 of full-time equated positions authorized from funds appropriated
5 in part 1 to the actual number of full-time equated positions
6 employed by the department at the end of the reporting period. The
7 report must be submitted to the house and senate appropriations
8 committees and to the standard report recipients.
9 Sec. 214. Not later than April 1, the department shall report
10 on each specific policy change made to implement a public act
11 affecting the department that took effect during the previous
12 calendar year. The report must include reference to the public act
13 number. The department shall submit the report to the standard
14 report recipients, the house and senate appropriations committees,
15 and the joint committee on administrative rules.
16 Sec. 215. Not later than April 1, the department shall provide
17 to the standard report recipients a copy of its annual strategic
18 plan prepared in compliance with section 363 of the management and
19 budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1363. The plan must include the
20 mission, vision, goals, strategies, and performance measures of the
21 department.
22 Sec. 216. The department shall report on any court settlement
23 that may require further legislative review of state statutory
24 programs or regulations.
25 Sec. 217. (1) In addition to the funds appropriated in part 1,
26 there is appropriated an amount not to exceed $3,000,000.00 for
27 federal contingency authorization. Amounts appropriated are not
28 available for expenditure until they have been transferred to
29 another line item in part 1 under section 393(2) of the management
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
15
1 and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1393.
2 (2) In addition to the funds appropriated in part 1, there is
3 appropriated an amount not to exceed $2,000,000.00 for state
4 restricted contingency authorization. Amounts appropriated are not
5 available for expenditure until they have been transferred to
6 another line item in part 1 under section 393(2) of the management
7 and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1393.
8 (3) In addition to the funds appropriated in part 1, there is
9 appropriated an amount not to exceed $100,000.00 for local
10 contingency authorization. Amounts appropriated are not available
11 for expenditure until they have been transferred to another line
12 item in part 1 under section 393(2) of the management and budget
13 act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1393.
14 (4) In addition to the funds appropriated in part 1, there is
15 appropriated an amount not to exceed $100,000.00 for private
16 contingency authorization. Amounts appropriated are not available
17 for expenditure until they have been transferred to another line
18 item in part 1 under section 393(2) of the management and budget
19 act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1393.
20 Sec. 218. Total authorized appropriations from all sources
21 under part 1 for legacy costs for the fiscal year ending September
22 30, 2027 are estimated at $6,633,500.00. From this amount, total
23 appropriations for pension-related legacy costs for the department
24 are estimated at $6,633,500.00. Total appropriations for retiree
25 health care legacy costs for the department are estimated at $0.00.
26 Sec. 219. To the extent possible, the department shall not
27 expend appropriations under part 1 until all existing authorized
28 work project funds available for the same purposes are exhausted.
29 Sec. 220. Not later than 6 months after the state budget
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
16
1 office issues work project letters, and again on or by April 15,
2 the department shall submit an annual report that summarizes all
3 work project accounts. The report must include all of the
4 following:
5 (a) A list of all work project accounts.
6 (b) The status of all work project accounts, including amounts
7 expended, amounts encumbered, and available balances for each
8 account.
9 (c) The amount of funds that lapsed from any previously
10 designated work project accounts, the name and description of the
11 work project account, and the funds that received the lapsed
12 amounts.
13 Sec. 221. To the extent permissible under section 261 of the
14 management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1261, all of the
15 following apply to the expenditure of funds appropriated in part 1:
16 (a) The funds must not be used for the purchase of foreign
17 goods or services, or both, if competitively priced and of
18 comparable quality American goods or services, or both, are
19 available.
20 (b) Preference must be given to goods or services, or both,
21 manufactured or provided by Michigan businesses, if they are
22 competitively priced and of comparable quality.
23 (c) Preference must be given to goods or services, or both,
24 that are manufactured or provided by Michigan businesses owned and
25 operated by veterans, if they are competitively priced and of
26 comparable quality.
27 Sec. 222. The department shall not take disciplinary action
28 against an employee of the department because the employee
29 communicates with a member of the legislature or legislative staff,
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
17
1 unless the communication is prohibited by law and the department is
2 exercising its authority as provided by law.
3 Sec. 223. (1) The department shall maximize utilization of its
4 in-person state workforce. The department shall prioritize
5 occupancy utilization of office space for each division within the
6 department. Employees with job responsibilities that require the
7 employees to serve in their capacities outside of an office shall
8 be monitored each pay period to ensure all work hours reported on
9 the timesheet were actually worked.
10 (2) The department shall comply with requirements set forth by
11 the office of the state employer on in-person work and utilization
12 and occupancy rates of state buildings to ensure in-person work is
13 optimized and occupancy rates are 80% or higher, subject to market
14 conditions.
15 (3) The department shall adhere to civil service rules and
16 regulations that state the standard biweekly work period for a
17 full-time employee in the classified service of this state is the
18 equivalent of 80 hours of work. The department shall establish
19 policies and processes to ensure all employees are working their
20 jobs during agreed-upon business hours.
21 (4) The office of the state employer must create and implement
22 an occupancy utilization uniform policy on occupancy, utilization,
23 and in-person and remote work. The office of the state employer
24 must make each uniform policy publicly available on the
25 department's website.
26 Sec. 224. Each agency shall complete a space utilization
27 assessment by July 1, 2027, using a form developed by the
28 department of technology, management, and budget, for all space
29 assigned under its building occupancy agreement and leased office
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
18
1 locations and post on their website. Agencies shall develop, in
2 coordination with the department of technology, management, and
3 budget, a plan to reduce, consolidate, or otherwise optimize
4 assigned space. The plans shall prioritize the use of state-owned
5 facilities, wherever possible, and comply with space standards
6 established by the department of technology, management, and budget
7 unless an exception is approved by the department of technology,
8 management, and budget.
9 Sec. 225. To the extent permissible under the management and
10 budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1101 to 18.1594, the director of
11 each department or agency receiving appropriations in part 1, shall
12 take all reasonable steps to ensure geographically disadvantaged
13 business enterprises compete for and perform contracts to provide
14 services or supplies, or both. The director shall strongly
15 encourage firms with which the department contracts to subcontract
16 with certified geographically disadvantaged business enterprises
17 for services, supplies, or both. As used in this section,
18 "geographically disadvantaged business enterprises" means that term
19 as defined in Executive Directive No. 2019-8.
20 Sec. 226. No later than December 31, 2026, and again by June
21 1, 2027, the department must provide a report to the standard
22 report recipients that includes detail regarding any federal
23 guidelines, rules, regulations, or other significant federal policy
24 changes, including H.R. 1 and the Rural Health Transformation
25 Program, that do, or are expected to, significantly impact the
26 operations of the department, including increases or reductions in
27 federal revenue and changes that are likely to improve or impede
28 the department's ability to safeguard the health or welfare of the
29 public.
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
19
1 Sec. 227. (1) Within 30 days of enactment of this act, the
2 house and senate shall provide to the state budget office a
3 jointly-agreed-upon list of legislatively directed spending items
4 funded in part 1 as defined in 2025 PA 32 and 2025 PA 33. The list
5 must include all information and documents pertaining to the funded
6 items as publicly disclosed in accordance with 2025 PA 32 and 2025
7 PA 33.
8 (2) In accordance with section 364(4) of the management and
9 budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1364, the department or agency
10 administering the grant shall post a report in a publicly
11 accessible location on its website beginning March 15 of the
12 current fiscal year. The department or agency shall update the
13 report and shall post an updated report not later than June 15 of
14 the current fiscal year and again not later than September 15 of
15 the current fiscal year. The department shall include in the report
16 the most comprehensive information the department has available at
17 the time of posting for grants awarded.
18 Sec. 228. The state budget director shall take steps to ensure
19 that all state fiscal recovery funds allocated to this state under
20 the American rescue plan act of 2021, Public Law 117-2, are
21 expended by December 31, 2026, as required by law. Any state fiscal
22 recovery funds that would otherwise lapse after September 30, 2026
23 are automatically reappropriated for the same purpose as originally
24 authorized and available for expenditure through December 31, 2026
25 and any subsequent financial closeout period.
26 Sec. 229. (1) The state budget director shall take steps to
27 ensure that all state fiscal recovery funds allocated to this state
28 under the American rescue plan act of 2021, Public Law 117-2, are
29 expended by December 31, 2026, as required by law. The state budget
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
20
1 director may reallocate appropriated funds for the purpose of fully
2 utilizing state fiscal recovery funds that are in jeopardy of not
3 meeting the expenditure deadline for reasons that may include, but
4 are not limited to, completed projects coming in under budget or
5 funds unable to be fully used by subrecipients. The state budget
6 director shall reallocate any of the funds reallocated under this
7 subsection to the programs or purposes specified in this section.
8 Any funds reallocated are unappropriated and immediately
9 reappropriated for the following purposes:
10 (a) To reclassify general fund/general purpose appropriations
11 for payroll and covered benefits for eligible public health and
12 safety employees at the department of corrections.
13 (b) To reclassify general fund/general purpose appropriations
14 for payroll and covered benefits for eligible public health and
15 safety employees at the department of state police.
16 (2) All applicable guidance, implementation, and reporting
17 provisions of American rescue plan act of 2021, Public Law 117-2,
18 must be followed for state fiscal recovery funds reallocated and
19 reappropriated under subsection (1).
20 (3) The state budget director shall notify the senate and the
21 house appropriations committees not later than 10 business days
22 after making any reallocations under subsection (1). The
23 notification must include the authorized program under which funds
24 were originally appropriated, the amount of the reallocation, the
25 program, or programs, or purpose, and the department to which the
26 funds are being reallocated under subsection (1), and the amount
27 reallocated to each program or purpose.
28 Sec. 231. The department must establish a policy for
29 conducting precontract risk assessments to evaluate contractor
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
21
1 financial risk, security risks, and insurance requirements prior to
2 contract execution. The department must report back to the standard
3 report recipients by March 31, 2027 on the assessments implemented
4 and used to evaluate contractors, as well as contracts executed
5 under the assessments.

7 DEPARTMENTAL ADMINISTRATION AND SUPPORT
8 Sec. 301. (1) The department may establish a fee schedule and
9 collect fees for the following work activities and services:
10 (a) Pesticide and plant pest management propagation and
11 certification of virus-free foundation stock.
12 (b) Fruit and vegetable inspection and grading services at
13 shipping and termination points and processing plants.
14 (c) Laboratory support analyses of food, livestock, and
15 agricultural products for disease, foreign products for disease,
16 toxic materials, foreign substances, and quality standards.
17 (d) Laboratory support test samples for other state and local
18 agencies and public or private organizations.
19 (2) The department may receive and expend revenue from the
20 fees authorized under subsection (1), subject to appropriation, to
21 recover expenses associated with the work activities and services
22 described in subsection (1). Fee revenue collected by the
23 department under subsection (1) does not lapse to the state general
24 fund at the end of the fiscal year but carries forward for
25 appropriation by the legislature in the subsequent fiscal year.
26 (3) The department shall notify the subcommittees, the fiscal
27 agencies, and the state budget office 30 days before proposing
28 changes in fees authorized under this section or under section 5 of
29 1915 PA 91, MCL 285.35.

23 BUREAU OF ENVIRONMENT AND SUSTAINABILITY
24 Sec. 501. The department shall report on the previous calendar
25 year's activities of the bureau of environment and sustainability
26 on or before April 1 of each year.
27 Sec. 502. (1) The purpose of the part 1 appropriation for soil
28 health/regenerative agriculture is to advance the adoption and
29 implementation of best regenerative agricultural practices and new
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
26
1 environmentally sustainable technologies, promoting soil health and
2 regenerative agriculture principles throughout the state.
3 (2) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for soil
4 health/regenerative agriculture, the department shall do both of
5 the following:
6 (a) Promote the principles of soil health and regenerative
7 agriculture through at least the following:
8 (i) The maintenance of soil cover.
9 (ii) The minimization of soil disturbance.
10 (iii) The maximization of plant and crop diversity.
11 (iv) The maximization of the presence of living roots.
12 (v) The integration of livestock into the cropping systems.
13 (b) Ensure that program outcomes include at least the
14 following:
15 (i) The increase of soil organic matter content.
16 (ii) The improvement of soil water infiltration capacity.
17 (iii) The increase in soil water holding capacity.
18 (iv) The improvement of soil biological capacity to break down
19 plant residue and other substances and to maintain soil
20 aggregation.
21 (v) The improvement of soil nutrient sequestration and cycling
22 capacity.
23 (vi) The reduction of nutrient losses.
24 (3) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for soil
25 health/regenerative agriculture, the department shall promote
26 practices of soil health and regenerative agriculture, including
27 the use of no-till farming, intercropping, cover crops,
28 multispecies cover crops, roller crimping, managed rotational
29 grazing, and other practices identified that utilize natural
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
27
1 biological processes to advance the goals of soil health and
2 regenerative agriculture.
3 (4) Funds appropriated in part 1 for soil health/regenerative
4 agriculture shall not be used for applied research into the
5 precision application of fertilizer, pesticides, or herbicides.
6 (5) It is the intention of the legislature that the department
7 engage with program partners to achieve the purposes of the soil
8 health/regenerative agriculture programs through research,
9 education, and outreach. Agreements with program partners receiving
10 funds through soil health/regenerative agriculture appropriations
11 must describe intended outcomes and how intended outcomes will be
12 measured and require the provision of a report to the department on
13 uses of funding received and a progress report on outcomes.
14 (6) In the report required under section 501 of this part, the
15 department shall provide information on the program described in
16 this section, including department activities, uses of program
17 funds by activity or project, contractors, grantees, and a summary
18 of projects and project results.
19 (7) Of the funds appropriated in part 1 for soil
20 health/regenerative agriculture, not less than $1,000,000.00 must
21 be used by the department to partner with the state land grant
22 university through MSU Extension and AgBioResearch to develop,
23 implement, and evaluate a soil health/regenerative agriculture
24 program. The partnership described in this subsection must be
25 focused on researching and assisting the agricultural industry in
26 implementing soil health/regenerative agricultural principles and
27 techniques. Partnership goals must include, but are not limited to,
28 establishing program priorities, developing metrics, implementing
29 goals, evaluating outcomes, and engaging with stakeholders.
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
28

13 AGRICULTURE DEVELOPMENT BUREAU
14 Sec. 601. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for the
15 food and agriculture investment program, the department shall
16 operate a food and agriculture investment program.
17 (2) The food and agriculture investment program shall do all
18 of the following:
19 (a) Expand the Michigan food and agriculture sector.
20 (b) Promote food security.
21 (c) Develop local and regional food systems.
22 (d) Grow Michigan exports.
23 (e) Promote the development of value-added agricultural
24 production.
25 (f) Support urban farms, food hubs, food incubators, and
26 community-based processing facilities with a focus on new and
27 expanding protein processors.
28 (g) Promote the expansion of farm markets, flower markets, and
29 urban agriculture, including hoop houses.
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
30
1 (h) Increase food processing activities within this state by
2 accelerating investment projects and infrastructure development
3 that support growth in production agriculture and food and
4 agriculture processing, expand opportunity to new agricultural
5 producers and processors, promote agriculture tourism and
6 agricultural heritage, and develop agricultural education and
7 interpretation activities.
8 (3) In addition to the funds appropriated in part 1, the
9 department may receive and expend funds received from outside
10 sources for the food and agriculture investment program.
11 (4) Before the allocation of funding, all projects must
12 receive approval from the Michigan commission of agriculture and
13 rural development, except for projects selected through a
14 competitive process by a joint evaluation committee selected by the
15 director and consisting of representatives that have agriculture,
16 food security, local and regional food systems, business, and
17 economic development expertise. Projects funded through the food
18 and agriculture investment program will be required to have a grant
19 agreement that outlines milestones and activities that must be met
20 in order to receive a disbursement of funds. Projects must also
21 identify measurable project outcomes.
22 (5) The department shall include, in the agriculture
23 development annual report, a report on the food and agriculture
24 investment program for the previous fiscal year that includes a
25 listing of the grantees, award amounts, match funding, project
26 locations, and project outcomes.

10 Sec. 902. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for Fresh
11 Food in Schools, funds must be allocated to the scratch cooking
12 pilot program.
13 (2) A local education agency providing locally produced
14 agriculture products for schools may submit an application to the
15 department for a grant under the pilot program. The application
16 must include both of the following:
17 (a) A needs assessment to evaluate equipment needs, equipment
18 utilization, procurement process, and workforce capabilities for
19 the pilot program.
20 (b) A strategic plan identifying all of the following:
21 (i) How the local education agency will use the grant funding.
22 (ii) How project activities will be monitored and evaluated.
23 (iii) All proposed costs with a clear explanation about how the
24 costs were determined.
25 (3) Grants shall be used by a local education agency to
26 promote scratch cooking, which may include any of the following:
27 (a) Implementing professional development and training related
28 to preparing, procuring, advertising, serving, and creating menus
29 of scratch-cooked meals that prioritize fresh, locally procured
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
37
1 items.
2 (b) Purchasing kitchen equipment or making infrastructure
3 modifications necessary for scratch cooking.
4 (c) Hiring of staff or third-party entities whose primary
5 focus is creating sustainable, actionable change within a school
6 food program to increase their scratch cooking that sustains beyond
7 the grant period.
8 (d) Providing technical assistance, pupil engagement, and
9 education related to scratch cooking, such as taste tests, recipe
10 development, and culinary education.
11 (e) Investing in K to 12 software and technology systems for
12 procurement to support scratch cooking.
13 (f) Carrying out any additional activities to promote scratch
14 cooking that will help local education agencies meet or exceed
15 nutrition standards.
16 (4) In evaluating grant applications, the department shall
17 give priority to local education agencies that serve the greatest
18 proportion of students eligible for free or reduced-price lunch.
19 (5) Not later than 180 days after the expiration of the pilot
20 program, each local education agency that received a grant shall
21 submit a report to the department that compares the change at the
22 end of the grant period to the school year immediately preceding
23 the beginning of the grant period, including all of the following:
24 (a) Recipes prepared.
25 (b) Nutrient analysis information.
26 (c) Invoices or bid lists identifying the cost of equipment
27 needed for scratch cooking.
28 (d) The number of staff, type of staff, and time required to
29 prepare each meal.
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
38
1 (e) Salary and benefit rates for each food service employee
2 involved in food preparation of selected meals.
3 (f) Production record and meal counts.
4 (g) Free and reduced-price meal eligibility and average daily
5 lunch participation.
6 (h) Pre- and post-program scratch cooked menu analysis.
7 (i) Pre- and post-program local procured item analysis.
8 (j) Program cost savings.
9 (k) Any other information required by the department.

19 (c) Recommendations on the continuation or expansion of the
20 pilot program.
21 (d) Any other information required by the department.

18 Sec. 202. The appropriations under this part and part 1 are
19 subject to the management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1101
20 to 18.1594.Sec. 203. As used in this part and part 1:
21 (a) "Administrative segregation" means confinement for
22 maintenance of order or discipline to a cell or room apart from
23 accommodations provided for inmates who are participating in
24 programs of the facility.
25 (b) "Department" means the department of corrections.
26 (c) "Director" means the director of the department.
27 (d) "DOJ" means the United States Department of Justice.
28 (e) "DOJ-BOP" means the DOJ Bureau of Prisons.
29 (f) "Evidence-based" means a decision-making process that
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
47
1 integrates the best available research, clinician expertise, and
2 client characteristics.
3 (g) "FTE" means full-time equated position in the classified
4 service of this state.
5 (h) "Jail" means a facility operated by a local unit of
6 government for the physical detention and correction of individuals
7 charged with or convicted of criminal offenses.
8 (i) "OCC" means the office of community corrections.
9 (j) "Offender success" means that an offender has, with the
10 support of the community, intervention of the field agent, and
11 benefit of any participation in programs and treatment, made an
12 adjustment while at liberty in the community such that the offender
13 has not been sentenced to or returned to prison for the conviction
14 of a new crime or the revocation of probation or parole.
15 (k) "Recidivism" means that term as defined in section 1 of
16 2017 PA 5, MCL 798.31.
17 (l) "Serious emotional disturbance" means that term as defined
18 in section 100d(3) of the mental health code, 1974 PA 258, MCL
19 330.1100d.
20 (m) "Serious mental illness" means that term as defined in
21 section 100d(4) of the mental health code, 1974 PA 258, MCL
22 330.1100d.
23 (n) "SSA" means the United States Social Security
24 Administration.
25 (o) "SSA-SSI" means SSA supplemental security income.
26 (p) "Standard report recipients" means the senate and house
27 appropriations committees, the senate and house appropriations
28 subcommittees on corrections and judiciary, the senate and house
29 fiscal agencies, the senate and house policy offices, the
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
48
1 legislative corrections ombudsman, and the state budget office.

3 section 3 of 1921 PA 2, MCL 17.3, transfers funds from an amount
4 appropriated under part 1, the legislature may, by a concurrent

6 serving in each house, intertransfer funds within part 1 for the
7 particular department, board, commission, officer, or institution.
8 Sec. 205. (1) The department shall use the internet to fulfill
9 the reporting requirements of this part and shall make each report
10 readily accessible to the public and conspicuously post each
11 required report in a single archivable location on the department's
12 website not later than the due date required for each report.
13 (2) In addition to placing all reports required in the current
14 fiscal year on the department's website, the department shall
15 maintain on its website all reports placed on the website from
16 previous fiscal years posted by fiscal year in the same single
17 archivable location.
18 (3) The department shall transmit all required reports for the
19 current fiscal year to the standard report recipients and any other
20 required recipients by email. The email shall include a copy of the
21 report and a link to access the report online.

23 all reports funded from appropriations in part 1. The department
24 shall follow federal and state law and guidelines for short-term
25 and long-term retention of records. The department may
26 electronically retain copies of reports unless otherwise required
27 by federal and state guidelines.

29 following apply to the expenditure of funds appropriated in part 1:
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
54
1 (a) The funds must not be used for the purchase of foreign
2 goods or services, or both, if competitively priced and of
3 comparable quality American goods or services, or both, are
4 available.
5 (b) Preference must be given to goods or services, or both,
6 manufactured or provided by Michigan businesses, if they are
7 competitively priced and of comparable quality.

12 Sec. 222. The department shall not take disciplinary action
13 against an employee of the department or a prisoner because the
14 employee or prisoner communicates with a member of the legislature
15 or legislative staff unless the communication is prohibited by law

17 Sec. 223. (1) The department shall maximize utilization of its
18 in-person state workforce. The department shall prioritize
19 occupancy utilization of office space for each division within the
20 department. Employees with job responsibilities that require the
21 employees to serve in their capacities outside of an office shall
22 be monitored each pay period to ensure all work hours reported on
23 timesheets were actually worked.
24 (2) The department shall comply with requirements set forth by
25 the office of the state employer on in-person work and utilization
26 and occupancy rates of state buildings to ensure in-person work is
27 optimized and occupancy rates are 80% or higher, subject to market
28 conditions.
29 (3) The department shall adhere to civil service rules and
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
55
1 regulations that state the standard biweekly work period for a
2 full-time employee in the classified service of this state is the
3 equivalent of 80 hours of work. The department shall establish
4 policies and processes to ensure all employees are working their
5 jobs during agreed-upon business hours.
6 (4) The office of the state employer must create and implement
7 an occupancy utilization uniform policy on occupancy, utilization,
8 and in-person and remote work. The office of the state employer
9 must make each uniform policy publicly available on the
10 department's website.

11 DEPARTMENTAL ADMINISTRATION AND SUPPORT
12 Sec. 301. For 3 years after a felony offender is released from
13 the department's jurisdiction, the department shall maintain the
14 offender's file on the offender tracking information system and
15 make it publicly accessible in the same manner as the file of the
16 current offender. The department shall immediately remove the
17 offender's file from the offender tracking information system upon
18 determination that the offender was wrongfully convicted and the
19 offender's file is not otherwise required to be maintained on the
20 offender tracking information system.
21 Sec. 302. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the

23 department's staff retention strategies. The report must include,
24 but is not limited to, all of the following:

28 including metrics the department is using to measure success of

1 (b) Mechanisms by which the department receives employee
2 feedback in areas under subdivision (a) and how the department

4 (c) Steps the department has taken, and future plans and goals
5 the department has, for retention and improving employee wellness.
6 Sec. 303. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the

17 15 years, 15 to 20 years, and 20 and more years.
18 (c) A section that shows the distinction between all of the

21 employment.
22 (ii) Recruits who are in training at a facility that depart
23 employment.
24 (iii) Employees who have been on the job that depart employment.

25 Sec. 318. (1) The department shall administer a county jail
26 reimbursement program from the funds appropriated in part 1 for the
27 purpose of reimbursing counties for housing in jails certain felons
28 who otherwise would have been sentenced to prison.
29 (2) The county jail reimbursement program must be used to
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
66
1 reimburse counties for convicted felons in the custody of the
2 sheriff if the conviction was for a crime committed on or after
3 January 1, 1999 and 1 of the following applies:
4 (a) The felon's sentencing guidelines recommended range upper
5 limit is more than 18 months, the felon's sentencing guidelines
6 recommended range lower limit is 12 months or less, the felon's
7 prior record variable score is 35 or more points, and the felon's
8 sentence is not for commission of a crime in crime class G or crime
9 class H or a nonperson crime in crime class F under chapter XVII of
10 the code of criminal procedure, 1927 PA 175, MCL 777.1 to 777.69.
11 (b) The felon's minimum sentencing guidelines range minimum is
12 more than 12 months under the sentencing guidelines described in
13 subdivision (a).
14 (c) The felon was sentenced to jail for a felony committed
15 while the felon was on parole and under the jurisdiction of the
16 parole board and for which the sentencing guidelines recommended
17 range for the minimum sentence has an upper limit of more than 18
18 months.
19 (3) State reimbursement under this section must be $70.00 per
20 diem per diverted offender for offenders with a presumptive prison
21 guideline score, $60.00 per diem per diverted offender for
22 offenders with a straddle cell guideline for a group 1 crime, and
23 $45.00 per diem per diverted offender for offenders with a straddle
24 cell guideline for a group 2 crime. Reimbursements must be paid for
25 sentences up to a 1-year total.
26 (4) County jail reimbursement program expenditures must not
27 exceed the amount appropriated in part 1 for the county jail
28 reimbursement program. Payments to counties under the county jail
29 reimbursement program must be made in the order in which properly
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
67
1 documented requests for reimbursements are received. A request is
2 properly documented if it meets departmental requirements for
3 documentation. Not later than October 15, the department shall
4 distribute the documentation requirements to all counties.
5 (5) Any county that receives funding under this section for
6 the purpose of housing in jails certain felons who otherwise would
7 have been sentenced to prison shall, as a condition of receiving
8 the funding, report not later than September 30 an annual average
9 jail capacity and annual average jail occupancy for the previous
10 fiscal year.

24 subsection (3).

1 reimbursement program as inmates of a state prison.
2 (7) As used in this section:
3 (a) "Group 1 crime" means a crime in 1 or more of the
4 following offense categories: arson, assault, assaultive other,
5 burglary, criminal sexual conduct, homicide or resulting in death,
6 other sex offenses, robbery, and weapon possession as determined by
7 the department based on specific crimes for which counties received
8 reimbursement under the county jail reimbursement program in fiscal
9 year 2007 and fiscal year 2008, and listed in the county jail
10 reimbursement program document titled "FY 2007 and FY 2008 Group
11 One Crimes Reimbursed", dated March 31, 2009.
12 (b) "Group 2 crime" means a crime that is not a group 1 crime,
13 including larceny, fraud, forgery, embezzlement, motor vehicle
14 offenses, malicious destruction of property, controlled substance
15 offense, felony drunk driving, and other nonassaultive offenses.
16 (c) "In the custody of the sheriff" means that the convicted
17 felon has been sentenced to the county jail and either is housed in
18 a county jail, is in custody but is being housed at a hospital or
19 medical facility for a medical or mental health purpose, or has
20 been released from jail and is being monitored through the use of
21 the sheriff's electronic monitoring system.

11 versus new offenses by major offense type: assaultive,
12 nonassaultive, drug, and sex.

16 substance use disorder violations. For parole technical rule

18 time since release from prison, by the most recent violation, and

10 Sec. 601. Not later than April 1, the department shall provide

1 information form. The department shall ensure that all prisoners,
2 upon any health care treatment funded from appropriations in part
3 1, are given the opportunity to sign a medical release of
4 information form designating a family member or other individual to
5 whom the department shall release records and information regarding
6 the prisoner upon the request of the prisoner. The prisoner may
7 elect to withdraw or amend the medical release of information form
8 at any time.
9 (2) The department shall ensure that a signed medical release
10 of information form follows a prisoner upon transfer to another
11 department facility or to the supervision of a parole officer.
12 (3) The medical release of information form must be placed
13 online on a public website managed by the department.
14 Sec. 603. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
15 department shall provide a report not later than April 1 on
16 prisoner health care utilization in the previous fiscal year, by
17 facility, that includes all of the following:
18 (a) The number of inpatient hospital days.
19 (b) The number of outpatient visits.
20 (c) The number of emergency room visits.
21 (d) The number of prisoners receiving off-site inpatient
22 medical care.

24 treatment must be used only to purchase specialty medication for

ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

1 following:

13 Sec. 605. Not later than March 1, the department shall provide
14 an annual report on the utilization of Medicaid benefits for
15 prisoners.
16 Sec. 606. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
17 department shall support medication-assisted treatment clinics at
18 designated correctional facilities that allow the department to
19 treat prisoners with opioid and alcohol use disorder while
20 incarcerated. The department shall collaborate with substance use
21 disorder treatment providers and community-based clinics to provide
22 postrelease assessment and treatment. Funding must be used by the
23 department to support costs of staff, including nurses, qualified
24 mental health professionals, recovery coaches, and corrections
25 officers, and costs of medication and supplies. Participating
26 prisoners must be encouraged to receive 1 injection of nonaddictive
27 medication, if clinically appropriate, before being released from
28 prison into the community.
29 (2) The department shall submit quarterly reports on the

1 operation of medication-assisted treatment clinics. A report under

11 and injectable medications.

19 Sec. 608. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
20 department shall request that the appropriate medical examiner
21 conduct an autopsy for all unexpected or unexplained deaths, for
22 deaths occurring under suspicious circumstances, for unattended
23 deaths, and for deaths that are non-natural or injury related that
24 occur within a prison. The department shall submit a biannual
25 report, by not later than March 1 and September 1 of each year,
26 that contains a de-identified list of prisoner deaths that occurred
27 in the previous 6 months within correctional facilities. This
28 report must include, but not be limited to, all of the following:
29 (a) The date of death.

8 CORRECTIONAL FACILITIES AND ADMINISTRATION
9 Sec. 701. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for prison
10 food service, the department shall report not later than January 15
11 on the following:
12 (a) Average per-meal cost for prisoner food service. Per-meal
13 cost includes all costs directly related to the provision of food
14 for the prisoner population, including, but not limited to, actual
15 food costs, total compensation for all food service workers,
16 including benefits and legacy costs, and inspection and compliance
17 costs for food service.
18 (b) Food service-related contracts, including goods or
19 services to be provided and the vendor.
20 (c) Major sanitation violations.
21 (2) To the extent possible, the department shall work with its
22 food supplier to source products grown and produced in this state
23 if they are of similar quality and price to products from non-
24 Michigan sources.
25 Sec. 702. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
26 department shall provide a report on the cost per prisoner per day
27 for each security custody level not later than January 15. This
28 cost must include all actual direct and indirect costs for the
29 previous fiscal year. To calculate the cost per prisoner per day,
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
83
1 the department shall divide the prisoner-related costs by the total
2 number of prisoner days for each custody level and correctional
3 facility. For multilevel facilities, costs that cannot be
4 accurately allocated to each custody level may be included in the
5 calculation on a per-prisoner basis for each facility. A report
6 summarizing these calculations must be submitted not later than
7 January 15. Prisoner-related costs included in the cost per
8 prisoner per day calculation must include all expenditures for the
9 following, from all fund sources:
10 (a) New custody staff training.
11 (b) Prison industries operations.
12 (c) Education/skilled trades/career readiness programs.
13 (d) Enhanced food technology program.
14 (e) Higher education in prison.
15 (f) Offender success programming.
16 (g) Central records.
17 (h) Correctional facilities administration.
18 (i) Housing inmates in federal institutions.
19 (j) Inmate legal services.
20 (k) Leased beds and alternatives to leased beds.
21 (l) Prison food service.
22 (m) Prison store operations.
23 (n) Transportation.
24 (o) Health care.
25 (p) Correctional facilities.
26 (q) Northern and southern region administration and support.
27 Sec. 704. The department shall allow the Michigan Braille
28 transcribing fund program to operate at the G. Robert Cotton
29 Correctional Facility and the Women's Huron Valley Correctional
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
84
1 Complex. The department shall continue to encourage the Michigan
2 Braille transcribing fund program to produce high-quality materials
3 for use by the visually impaired.
4 Sec. 705. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
5 department shall report all of the following regarding critical
6 incidents by facility:
7 (a) Within 72 hours of occurrence, any critical incident
8 occurring at a correctional facility. The report must identify the
9 facility at which the incident occurred.
10 (b) Not later than March 1, the number of critical incidents
11 occurring each month at each facility during the previous calendar
12 year, categorized by type and severity of each incident.
13 (2) As used in this section, "critical incident" includes a
14 prisoner assault on staff that results in a serious physical injury
15 to staff, an escape or attempted escape, a prisoner disturbance
16 that causes facility operation concerns, the implementation of a
17 phase plan or similar significant restriction on activity within a
18 facility, a drug overdose or suspected overdose that results in
19 inpatient hospitalization, and an unexpected death of a prisoner.
20 Sec. 706. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
21 department shall report not later than March 1 on all of the
22 following ratios for each correctional facility:
23 (a) Corrections officers to prisoners.
24 (b) Shift command staff to line custody staff.
25 (c) Noncustody institutional staff to prisoners.
26 Sec. 707. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
27 department shall focus on providing core programming interventions
28 to prisoners as early as possible during the prisoner's sentence to
29 impact the prisoner's behavior while incarcerated, and prioritize
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
85
1 individuals who are past their earliest release date and have not
2 been paroled because of not having received the required
3 programming. Programming includes, but is not limited to, violence
4 prevention programming, sexual abuse prevention programming,
5 substance use treatment programming, and core cognitive
6 programming. Nothing in this section makes parole denial appealable
7 in court.
8 (2) The department shall submit a quarterly report detailing
9 enrollment in sex abuse prevention programming, violence prevention
10 programming, substance use treatment programming, and core
11 cognitive programming. At a minimum, the report must include all of
12 the following:
13 (a) The current number of individuals who are required to
14 complete 1 or more of these types of programming, but have not yet
15 done so.
16 (b) The number of individuals who have reached their earliest
17 release date, but who have not completed required programming.
18 (c) The number of individuals who have completed 1 or more of
19 these types of programming during their current term of
20 incarceration.
21 (d) A plan of action for addressing any waiting lists or
22 backlogs for programming that may exist.
23 Sec. 708. If a pregnant prisoner in a facility funded from
24 appropriations in part 1 consents to a visitor being present, the
25 department shall allow 1 person to be present during the prisoner's
26 labor and delivery, in addition to a doula being present if the
27 pregnant prisoner wants to work with a doula. The person allowed to
28 accompany the prisoner must be an immediate family member, legal
29 guardian, spouse, or domestic partner. The department is authorized
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
86
1 to deny access to a visitor if the department has a safety concern
2 with that visitor's access. The department is authorized to conduct
3 a criminal background check on the visitor.
4 Sec. 709. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
5 department shall evaluate all prisoners at intake for substance use
6 disorders, serious developmental disorders, serious mental illness,
7 and other mental health disorders. Prisoners with serious mental
8 illness or serious developmental disorders must not be removed from
9 the general population as a punitive response to behavior caused by
10 their serious mental illness or serious developmental disorder. A
11 prisoner with serious mental illness or serious developmental
12 disorder that is unresponsive to treatment who presents a
13 persistent high violence risk or engages in severe disruptive
14 behavior may be placed in secure residential housing programs that
15 facilitate access to institutional programming and ongoing mental
16 health services funded from appropriations in part 1. A prisoner
17 with serious mental illness or serious developmental disorder who
18 is confined in these specialized housing programs must be evaluated
19 or monitored by a medical professional at a frequency of not less
20 than every 12 hours.
21 Sec. 710. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
22 department shall submit a report by April 1, for the previous
23 fiscal year, with the following information:
24 (a) The number of times prisoners were placed in punitive
25 segregation/detention and the reason for the placements.
26 (b) The number of prisoners who were classified to
27 administrative segregation, including a chart listing the number of
28 prisoners housed in administrative segregation for each of the
29 following time periods:
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
87
1 (i) A continuous period of 3 months or longer but less than 6
2 months.
3 (ii) A continuous period of 6 months or longer but less than 12
4 months.
5 (iii) A continuous period of 12 months or longer.
6 (c) For any prisoner housed in administrative segregation for
7 12 months or longer, an explanation of the circumstances
8 surrounding the prisoner's placement in administrative segregation.
9 (d) The department shall report the number of prisoners
10 classified to an administrative segregation unit or cell who, at
11 any time during the current or a prior prison term, were diagnosed
12 with serious mental illness or have a developmental disorder.
13 Sec. 711. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
14 department shall do all of the following:
15 (a) Ensure that any inmate care and control staff in contact
16 with prisoners less than 18 years of age are adequately trained
17 with regard to the developmental and mental health needs of
18 prisoners less than 18 years of age. Not later than April 1, the
19 department shall report on the training curriculum used and the
20 number and types of staff receiving annual training under that
21 curriculum.
22 (b) Provide appropriate placement for prisoners less than 18
23 years of age who have serious mental illness, serious emotional
24 disturbance, or a serious developmental disorder and need to be
25 housed separately from the general population. Prisoners less than
26 18 years of age who have serious mental illness, serious emotional
27 disturbance, or a serious developmental disorder must not be
28 removed from an existing placement as a punitive response to
29 behavior caused by their serious mental illness, serious emotional
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
88
1 disturbance, or a serious developmental disorder. A prisoner who is
2 less than 18 years of age with serious mental illness or a serious
3 developmental disorder that is unresponsive to treatment who
4 presents a persistent high violence risk or engages in severe
5 disruptive behavior may be placed in secure residential housing
6 programs that facilitate access to institutional programming and
7 ongoing mental health services. A prisoner less than 18 years of
8 age with serious mental illness, serious emotional disturbance, or
9 a serious developmental disorder who is confined in these
10 specialized housing programs must be evaluated or monitored by a
11 medical professional at a frequency of not less than every 12
12 hours.
13 (c) Implement a specialized offender success program that
14 recognizes the needs of prisoners less than 18 years of age for
15 supervised offender success.

11 Sec. 714. Funds appropriated in part 1 for intelligence unit

21 relocation. The preliminary report must include the projected

24 on staff positions.

3 Sec. 716. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
4 department shall consult with the legislature and other appropriate
5 state agencies to develop a framework to provide investment in
6 communities that have formerly operational state correctional
7 facilities that have been closed. This framework must include plans
8 to ensure that vacant state correctional facilities do not become a
9 nuisance or danger to the community.
10 Sec. 717. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
11 department shall make an information packet for the families of
12 incoming prisoners available on the department's website. The
13 information packet must be reviewed not later than February 1 and
14 updated as necessary. The department may partner with external
15 advocacy groups and actual families of prisoners in the packet-
16 writing process to ensure that the information is useful and
17 complete. The packet must provide information on topics including,
18 but not limited to, all of the following:
19 (a) How to put money into prisoner accounts.
20 (b) How to make telephone calls or create Jpay email accounts.
21 (c) How to visit in person.
22 (d) Proper procedures for filing complaints or grievances.
23 (e) The rights of prisoners to physical and mental health
24 care.
25 (f) The purpose and importance of prisoners signing a medical
26 release of information form.
27 (g) How to utilize the offender tracking information system
28 (OTIS).
29 (h) Truth in sentencing and how it applies to minimum
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
91
1 sentences.
2 (i) The parole process.
3 (j) Guidance on the importance of the role of families in the
4 reentry process.
5 Sec. 718. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
6 department shall pursue all opportunities to reduce costs for
7 prisoners and prisoners' families for financial deposit fees and
8 commissary fees when the department negotiates or renews any
9 contract to provide these services.
10 Sec. 719. (1) Funds appropriated in part 1 for contraband
11 prevention must be used by the department to enhance a multifaceted
12 approach to contraband prevention that combines technology,
13 rigorous policies, vigilant staff, intelligence gathering, and a
14 commitment to addressing the root causes of contraband, all of
15 which are necessary for preventing contraband introductions and
16 maintaining safe and secure correctional facilities.
17 (2) Funds appropriated in part 1 for contraband prevention
18 must be used by the department to support the prevention of
19 contraband in correctional facilities, including increasing the
20 frequency and enhancing the methods of screening all items and all
21 individuals entering into correctional facilities. Under this
22 section, individuals who must be screened include, but are not
23 limited to, all department employees, contractors, third-party
24 vendors, visitors, and volunteers. Emergency response personnel may
25 be exempt from being screened when arriving at correctional
26 facilities in response to emergent emergencies.
27 (3) From the funds appropriated in part 1, the department
28 shall develop and implement contraband prevention policies that are
29 uniform across all correctional facilities.
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
92
1 (4) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for contraband
2 prevention, the department shall submit a report not later than
3 March 1 on contraband and prevention efforts in correctional
4 facilities. The report must include, but not be limited to, all of
5 the following:
6 (a) Prevention efforts and strategies utilized by the
7 department.
8 (b) Challenges faced by correctional staff and other staff in
9 addressing contraband.
10 (c) Ideas and recommendations on how the legislature can
11 better assist the department with contraband prevention efforts and
12 strategies.
13 Sec. 720. The department shall ensure that Policy Directive
14 04.01.110 "Access to Correctional Facilities" does not require
15 active legislative members in good standing to provide prior notice
16 before being granted access to a department facility when
17 conducting official legislative business.
18 Sec. 721. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
19 department shall conduct a 1-year pilot program covering at least 5
20 facilities selected by the department that does the following:
21 (a) At the selected facilities, all incarcerated individuals
22 participating in contact visits at the facility shall pass through
23 a body scanner at the conclusion of their visits to search for
24 contraband. If a body scanner is temporarily unavailable or
25 inoperable, the pilot site shall comply with existing policy
26 requiring a routine strip search after each contact visit. The
27 department shall document these body scanner searches.
28 (b) At the selected facilities, incarcerated individuals
29 participating in contact visits at the facility may also be
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
93
1 randomly selected to be strip searched to search for contraband at
2 the conclusion of a visit in addition to passing through the body
3 scanner. An incarcerated individual who is randomly selected to
4 participate in a strip search shall comply with all requirements.
5 This random sampling shall be selected via an impartial process,
6 such as a random number selection generator. The department shall
7 document these strip searches.
8 (2) The department shall retain the authority to conduct
9 suspicion-based strip searches of any individual who has
10 participated in a visit based on the belief that they may possess
11 contraband or be involved in smuggling. The department shall
12 document these suspicion-based searches.
13 (3) At the conclusion of the 1-year pilot program, the
14 department shall produce data including, but not limited to, the
15 following:
16 (a) The number of individuals who are searched using a body
17 scanner after a visit and the incidents of contraband being
18 discovered during this search process at the pilot facilities.
19 (b) The number of individuals who were randomly selected to be
20 strip searched after a contact visit and the incidents of
21 contraband being discovered during this search process at the pilot
22 facilities.
23 (c) The number of individuals who were subject to suspicion-
24 based searches after a contact visit at the pilot facilities and
25 the incidents of contraband being discovered during this search
26 process at the pilot facilities.
27 (d) The total number of substance use and smuggling
28 misconducts issued during the year within each facility.
29 (e) Any other information the department believes should be
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
94
1 publicly released related to the results of the pilot.
2 (4) The department shall review the process of conducting
3 strip searches to ensure that the search process is conducted in a
4 consistent manner that allows for complete and thorough searches
5 while minimizing the time that a person is in a state of complete
6 undress.
7 Sec. 722. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
8 department shall provide a report, by facility, not later than
9 April 1 on the prisoner benefit fund. The report must include, for
10 each facility, all of the following information:
11 (a) The balance of the fund as of September 30 of the previous
12 fiscal year.
13 (b) The total amount deposited into the fund in the previous
14 fiscal year, categorized by the source of deposit.
15 (c) The total amount spent from the fund in the previous
16 fiscal year.
17 (d) The general categories of the expenditure of the funds and
18 the amounts spent by category.
19 Sec. 723. From the funds appropriated in part 1, it is the
20 intent of the legislature that except as otherwise provided under
21 this section, the department shall allow a prisoner at least 1 in-
22 person visit per 6-month period, regardless of the prisoner's
23 violation status, and including a prisoner who is on restricted
24 visitation due to substance use. However, a prisoner who is on
25 restricted visitation for violence against other prisoners or
26 violence against any department staff is excluded from the 1 in-
27 person visit requirement under this section. In addition, the
28 department shall do all of the following regarding the visits
29 required under this section:
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
95
1 (a) Explore the use of staggered time frames for visiting sign
2 up.
3 (b) Ensure visitation areas are child-friendly by providing
4 toys and other child-friendly games and activities.
5 (c) Work with the vending contractor to provide healthy food
6 choices in vending machines located in visitation areas of
7 correctional facilities subject to there being sufficient demand to
8 consistently sell these products.
9 (d) Provide a report, not later than April 1, detailing the
10 efforts taken to meet the requirements of subdivisions (a), (b),
11 and (c) and the outcomes of these changes and effects on visitation
12 privileges for prisoners.
13 Sec. 724. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
14 department shall provide a report not later than April 1 that
15 includes the number of medical accommodations issued for
16 wheelchairs, by type and facility. This report must also include a
17 count of the number of wheelchairs available for use at each
18 facility, as well as the number of incarcerated persons assigned

20 individuals with an accommodation for an attendant.
21 Sec. 725. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
22 department shall provide a copy of the medical examiner's report
23 and autopsy report, subject to appropriate redactions consistent

3 ONE-TIME APPROPRIATIONS
4 Sec. 801. (1) The one-time funds appropriated in part 1 for
5 Goodwill Flip the Script must be expended for administration of the
6 program. The program must serve a population of individuals aged 16
7 to 39 and must target individuals who are entering the criminal
8 justice system for the first or second time. The program must
9 assist those individuals through the following program types:
10 (a) Alternative sentencing programs in partnership with a
11 local district or circuit court.
12 (b) Educational recovery for special adult populations with
13 high rates of illiteracy.
14 (c) Career development and continuing education.
15 (d) Financial counseling and coaching services.
16 (2) Not later than March 30, Goodwill Flip the Script shall
17 report on all of the following:
18 (a) Program performance measurements.
19 (b) The number of individuals diverted from incarceration.
20 (c) The number of individuals served.
21 (d) The outcomes of participants who completed the program.
22 Sec. 802. From the one-time funds appropriated in part 1 for
23 peer-supported reentry, the department shall conduct a competitive
24 process for selecting an organization that primarily employs
25 individuals who have been incarcerated to provide reentry or other
26 supportive services, as determined by the department, to those
27 leaving prison and returning to 1 or more regions of this state.

6 Sec. 202. The appropriations under this part and part 1 are
7 subject to the management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1101
8 to 18.1594.
9 Sec. 203. As used in this part and part 1:
10 (a) "Department" means the department of education.
11 (b) "DHHS" means the department of health and human services.
12 (c) "District" means a local school district as that term is
13 defined in section 6 of the revised school code, 1976 PA 451, MCL
14 380.6, or a public school academy as that term is defined in
15 section 5 of the revised school code, 1976 PA 451, MCL 380.5.
16 (d) "FTE" means full-time equated position in the classified
17 service of this state.
18 (e) "HHS" means the United States Department of Health and
19 Human Services.
20 (f) "Standard report recipients" means the senate and house
21 appropriations committees, the senate and house appropriations
22 subcommittees on the department, the senate and house fiscal
23 agencies, the senate and house policy offices, and the state budget
24 office.

26 section 3 of 1921 PA 2, MCL 17.3, transfers funds from an amount
27 appropriated under part 1, the legislature may, by a concurrent

29 serving in each house, intertransfer funds within part 1 for the
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

1 particular department, board, commission, officer, or institution.
2 Sec. 205. (1) The department shall use the internet to fulfill
3 the reporting requirements of this part and shall make each report
4 readily accessible to the public and conspicuously post each
5 required report in a single archivable location on the department's
6 website not later than the due date required for each report.
7 (2) In addition to placing all reports required in the current
8 fiscal year on the department's website, the department shall
9 maintain on its website all reports placed on the website from
10 previous fiscal years posted by fiscal year in the same single
11 archivable location.
12 (3) The department shall transmit all required reports for the
13 current fiscal year to the standard report recipients and any other
14 required recipients by email. The email shall include a copy of the
15 report and a link to access the report online.
16 Sec. 206. The department shall receive and retain copies of
17 all reports funded from appropriations in part 1. The department
18 shall follow federal and state law and guidelines for short-term
19 and long-term retention of records. The department may
20 electronically retain copies of reports unless otherwise required
21 by federal and state guidelines.

20 purposes.

8 Sec. 221. To the extent permissible under section 261 of the
9 management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1261, all of the
10 following apply to the expenditure of funds appropriated in part 1:
11 (a) The funds must not be used for the purchase of foreign
12 goods or services, or both, if competitively priced and of
13 comparable quality American goods or services, or both, are
14 available.
15 (b) Preference must be given to goods or services, or both,
16 manufactured or provided by Michigan businesses, if they are
17 competitively priced and of comparable quality.
18 (c) Preference must be given to goods or services, or both,
19 that are manufactured or provided by Michigan businesses owned and
20 operated by veterans, if they are competitively priced and of
21 comparable quality.
22 Sec. 222. The department shall not take disciplinary action
23 against an employee of the department because the employee
24 communicates with a member of the legislature or legislative staff,
25 unless the communication is prohibited by law and the department is
26 exercising its authority as provided by law.
27 Sec. 223. (1) The department shall maximize utilization of its
28 in-person state workforce. The department shall prioritize
29 occupancy utilization of office space for each division within the
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
113
1 department. Employees with job responsibilities that require the
2 employees to serve in their capacities outside of an office shall
3 be monitored each pay period to ensure all work hours reported on
4 the timesheet were actually worked.
5 (2) The department shall comply with requirements set forth by
6 the office of the state employer on in-person work and utilization
7 and occupancy rates of state buildings to ensure in-person work is
8 optimized and occupancy rates are 80% or higher, subject to market
9 conditions.
10 (3) The department shall adhere to civil service rules and
11 regulations that state the standard biweekly work period for a
12 full-time employee in the classified service of this state is the
13 equivalent of 80 hours of work. The department shall establish
14 policies and processes to ensure all employees are working their
15 jobs during agreed-upon business hours.
16 (4) The office of the state employer must create and implement
17 an occupancy utilization uniform policy on occupancy, utilization,
18 and in-person and remote work. The office of the state employer
19 must make each uniform policy publicly available on the
20 department's website.
21 Sec. 224. Each agency shall complete a space utilization
22 assessment by July 1, 2027, using a form developed by the
23 department of technology, management, and budget, for all space
24 assigned under its building occupancy agreement and leased office
25 locations and post on their website. Agencies shall develop, in
26 coordination with the department of technology, management, and

29 facilities, wherever possible, and comply with space standards

1 established by the department of technology, management, and budget
2 unless an exception is approved by the department of technology,

13 Sec. 228. The state budget director shall take steps to ensure

17 recovery funds that would otherwise lapse after September 30, 2026

19 authorized and available for expenditure through December 31, 2026
20 and any subsequent financial closeout period.
21 Sec. 229. (1) The state budget director shall take steps to
22 ensure that all state fiscal recovery funds allocated to this state
23 under the American rescue plan act of 2021, Public Law 117-2,
24 are expended by December 31, 2026, as required by law. The state
25 budget director may reallocate appropriated funds for the purpose

27 of not meeting the expenditure deadline for reasons that may
28 include, but are not limited to, completed projects coming in under

1 budget director shall reallocate any of the funds reallocated under

22 DEPARTMENT-SPECIFIC GENERAL SECTIONS
23 Sec. 301. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
24 department shall provide through the internet the state board of
25 education agenda and all supporting documents, and shall notify the
26 state budget director and the senate and house fiscal agencies that
27 the agenda and supporting documents are available on the internet,
