ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
118
1 Sec. 302. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
2 department may assist the department of health and human services,
3 other departments, intermediate school districts, and local school
4 districts to secure reimbursement for eligible services provided in
5 Michigan schools from the federal Medicaid program. The department
6 may submit reports of direct expenses related to this effort to the
7 department of health and human services for reimbursement.
8 Sec. 303. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
9 department shall do both of the following:
10 (a) Post on its website a link to the federal Institute of
11 Education Sciences' What Works Clearinghouse.
12 (b) Disseminate knowledge about the What Works Clearinghouse
13 to districts and intermediate school districts so that it may be
14 used to improve reading proficiency for pupils in grades K to 3.
15 Sec. 304. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
16 department shall coordinate with the other departments to
17 streamline state services and resources, reduce duplication, and
18 increase efficiency, including, but not limited to, all of the
19 following:
20 (a) Working with the department of treasury to coordinate with
21 the financial independence team and overseeing deficit districts.
22 (b) Working with the department of health and human services
23 and the department of lifelong education, advancement, and
24 potential to coordinate with early childhood programs and
25 overseeing child care providers.
26 Sec. 305. (1) As a condition of receiving appropriations in
27 part 1, the department shall, in collaboration with the department
28 of health and human services, promote and support initiatives in
29 schools and other educational organizations that include, but are
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
119
1 not limited to, training for educators, teachers, and other
2 personnel in school settings for all of the following:
3 (a) Using trauma-informed practices.
4 (b) Age-appropriate education and information on human
5 trafficking.
6 (c) Age-appropriate education and information on sexual abuse
7 prevention.
8 (2) If requested by the department, the department of state
9 police and the department of attorney general shall consult with
10 the department in the promotion and support of initiatives in
11 schools and other educational organizations under subsection (1).
12 Sec. 306. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
13 department shall ensure that the most recently issued report of
14 regional in-demand occupations issued by the department of
15 technology, management, and budget is distributed in electronic or
16 paper form to all high schools in each school district,
17 intermediate school district, and public school academy, to the
18 standard report recipients, and to the members of the senate and
19 house standing committees on education.
20 Sec. 307. Funds appropriated in part 1 are prohibited from
21 being provided to the Michigan Organization on Adolescent Sexual
22 Health.

1 rates:

6 member a per diem for more than 30 days per year.

8 SPECIAL EDUCATION SERVICES
9 Sec. 401. From the funds appropriated in part 1 for special
10 education operations, the department shall use $100,000.00 to
11 design and distribute to all parents and legal guardians of a
12 student with a disability the following information:
13 (a) Federal and state mandates regarding the rights and
14 protections of students with disabilities, including, but not
15 limited to, individualized education programs to ensure that
16 parents and legal guardians are fully informed about laws, rules,
17 procedural safeguards, and problem-solving options.
18 (b) Any other education-related information the department
19 determines is necessary to allow parents and legal guardians to
20 provide meaningful input in collaboration with districts to develop
21 and implement an individualized education program.

23 MICHIGAN SCHOOLS FOR THE DEAF AND BLIND
24 Sec. 451. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the employees
25 at the Michigan Schools for the Deaf and Blind who work on a
26 school-year basis are considered annual employees for purposes of
27 service credits, retirement, and insurance benefits.
28 Sec. 452. For each student enrolled at the Michigan Schools
29 for the Deaf and Blind, the department shall assess the
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
121
1 intermediate school district of residence 100% of the cost of
2 operating the student's instructional program, excluding room and
3 board related costs and the cost of weekend transportation between
4 the school and the student's home.
5 Sec. 456. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
6 Michigan Schools for the Deaf and Blind may promote its residential
7 program as a possible appropriate option for children who are deaf
8 or hard of hearing or who are blind or visually impaired. From the
9 funds appropriated in part 1, the Michigan Schools for the Deaf and
10 Blind shall distribute information detailing its services to all
11 intermediate school districts in this state.
12 (2) If an intermediate school district knows that a child in
13 the district is deaf or hard of hearing or blind or visually
14 impaired, the intermediate school district shall provide to the
15 parents of the child the literature distributed by the Michigan
16 Schools for the Deaf and Blind to intermediate school districts
17 under subsection (1).
18 (3) Parents will continue to have a choice regarding the
19 educational placement of their deaf or hard-of-hearing children.
20 Sec. 457. Revenue received by the Michigan Schools for the
21 Deaf and Blind from gifts, bequests, and donations that is
22 unexpended at the end of the state fiscal year may be carried over
23 to the succeeding fiscal year and does not revert to the general
24 fund.
25 Sec. 458. (1) The funds appropriated in part 1 for the low
26 incidence outreach fund are appropriated from money collected by
27 the Michigan Schools for the Deaf and Blind and the low incidence
28 outreach program for providing qualified services and may be used
29 for any expenses necessary to provide the qualified services. Any
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
122
1 money that is unexpended at the end of the current fiscal year does
2 not revert to the general fund and may be carried forward into the
3 succeeding fiscal year.
4 (2) As used in this section, "qualified services" means any of
5 the following:
6 (a) Document reproduction and services.
7 (b) Conducting conferences, workshops, and training classes.
8 (c) Providing specialized equipment, facilities, and software.
9 Sec. 459. When conducting a due process hearing resulting from
10 a parent's appeal of that parent's child's individualized education
11 program team's decision on the child's educational placement, a
12 state administrative law judge shall consider designating the
13 Michigan School for the Deaf as 1 of the options for the least
14 restrictive environment under federal law for the parent's child
15 who is deaf, deafblind, or hard of hearing.
16 Sec. 460. From the funds appropriated in part 1 for ASL
17 literacy resources, the department shall expend the funds to comply
18 with all requirements in section 1705 of the revised school code,
19 1976 PA 451, MCL 380.1705.

1 Sec. 507. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the

10 SCHOOL SUPPORT SERVICES
11 Sec. 601. From the funds appropriated in part 1 for adolescent
12 and school health, the department shall use the funds to replace
13 federal funding reductions from the HHS - Centers for Disease
14 Control and Prevention to the department and section 39a(2)(a) of
15 the state school aid act of 1979, 1979 PA 94, MCL 388.1639a.
16 Sec. 602. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for school
17 board member training, there is appropriated $150,000.00 for school
18 board member training. The department shall approve 1 or more
19 training programs for school board members that include courses of
20 instruction for school board members in 1 or more of the following
21 topic areas:
22 (a) Conflicts of interest, including, but not limited to, the
23 application of section 1203 of the revised school code, 1976 PA
24 451, MCL 380.1203.
25 (b) Labor relations, including, but not limited to, a school
26 board's role in collective bargaining agreements in 1947 PA 336,
27 MCL 423.201 to 423.217, and in other laws related to employment.
28 (c) Education law, including, but not limited to, the revised
29 school code, 1976 PA 451, MCL 380.1 to 380.1852, the state school
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
125
1 aid act of 1979, 1979 PA 94, MCL 388.1601 to 388.1896, the open
2 meetings act, 1976 PA 267, MCL 15.261 to 15.275, and 1937 (Ex Sess)
3 PA 4, MCL 38.71 to 38.191, dealing with teacher tenure.
4 (d) School finance, including, but not limited to, the
5 creation and management of school district budgets.
6 (e) Board governance, including, but not limited to, roles and
7 responsibilities, parliamentary procedure, and best practices.
8 (f) Rater reliability training.
9 (g) District requirements established under section 1280f of
10 the revised school code, 1976 PA 451, MCL 380.1280f.
11 (2) On completion of an eligible training program, a school
12 board member may apply for reimbursement for the cost of the
13 eligible training program through the board member's local
14 district, up to $100.00 per course. The department may determine
15 the form and manner of the application to reimburse the district
16 for the cost.
17 (3) The department shall create a process for the provider of
18 a course in a topic listed in subsection (1) to apply to the
19 department to have the course approved and be eligible for a school
20 board member to be reimbursed for completing that course as
21 provided under subsection (2).
22 (4) As used in this section:
23 (a) "Eligible training program" means a training program that
24 is approved under subsection (1).
25 (b) "School board member" means a member of the board of a
26 school district or intermediate school district or a member of the
27 board of directors of a public school academy in this state.
28 Sec. 604. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1, not less
29 than $159,500.00 and not fewer than 1.0 FTE position shall provide

5 fees are appropriated from money collected by the library of
6 Michigan for providing qualified services and may be used for any
7 expenses necessary to provide the qualified services. Any money
8 that is unexpended at the end of the current fiscal year does not

10 succeeding fiscal year.

12 the following:

17 in part 1 for renaissance zone reimbursements to reimburse public
18 libraries under section 12 of the Michigan renaissance zone act,
19 1996 PA 376, MCL 125.2692, for taxes levied in 2026. The department
20 shall allocate the funds not later than 60 days after the
21 department of treasury certifies to the department and to the state
22 budget director that the department of treasury has received all
23 necessary information to properly determine the amounts due to each
24 eligible recipient.
25 (2) If the amount appropriated under this section is not
26 sufficient to fully pay obligations under this section, the
27 department shall prorate payments on an equal basis among all
28 eligible recipients.

11 Sec. 203. As used in this part and part 1:
12 (a) "Department" means the department of environment, Great
13 Lakes, and energy.

18 system.

23 the state budget office.

29 particular department, board, commission, officer, or institution.

9 management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1261, all of the
10 following apply to the expenditure of funds appropriated in part 1:

14 available.

23 against an employee of the department because the employee
24 communicates with a member of the legislature or legislative staff
25 unless the communication is prohibited by law and the department is
26 exercising its authority as provided by law.
27 Sec. 223. (1) The department shall maximize utilization of its
28 in-person state workforce. The department shall prioritize
29 occupancy utilization of office space for each division within the
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
150
1 department. Employees with job responsibilities that require the
2 employees to serve in their capacities outside of an office shall
3 be monitored each pay period to ensure all work hours reported on
4 the timesheet were actually worked.
5 (2) The department shall comply with requirements set by the
6 office of the state employer to meet the in-person and vacancy rate
7 objectives outlined by the office of the state employer.
8 (3) The department shall adhere to the rules and regulations
9 of civil service, which state that the standard biweekly work
10 period for a full-time employee in the classified service is the
11 equivalent of 80 hours of work. The department shall establish
12 policies and processes to ensure all employees are working their
13 jobs during agreed-upon business hours.
14 (4) The office of the state employer must create and implement
15 an occupancy utilization uniform policy on occupancy, utilization,
16 and in-person and remote work. The office of the state employer
17 must make each uniform policy publicly available on the
18 department's website.

11 Sec. 258. (1) For any permit application, as defined in part
12 13 of the natural resources and environmental protection act, 1994
13 PA 451, MCL 324.1301 to 324.1317, the department shall approve or
14 deny the permit application by the processing deadline. For any
15 permit application where the department fails to approve or deny
16 the permit by the processing deadline, the department shall refund
17 the applicant an amount equal to 15% of the greater of the
18 following, as applicable:
19 (a) The amount of the application fee for that permit.
20 (b) If an assessment or other fee is charged on an annual or
21 other periodic basis by the department to a person holding the
22 permit for which the application was submitted, the amount of the
23 first periodic charge of that assessment or other fee for that
24 permit.

20 and the state budget office that provides a summary of the
21 expenditures incurred under this section during the preceding
22 fiscal year.
23 Sec. 305. It is the intent of the legislature to repay the
24 refined petroleum fund for the $70,000,000.00 that was transferred
25 to the environmental protection fund created in section 503a of the
26 natural resources and environmental protection act, 1994 PA 451,
27 MCL 324.503a, as part of the resolution for the fiscal year 2006-
28 2007 budget.
29
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
160
1 WATER RESOURCES DIVISION
2 Sec. 405. If a certified health department does not exist in a
3 city, county, or district or does not fulfill its responsibilities
4 under part 117 of the natural resources and environmental
5 protection act, 1994 PA 451, MCL 324.11701 to 324.11721, then the
6 department may spend funds appropriated in part 1 for drinking
7 water and environmental health in accordance with section 11716 of
8 the natural resources and environmental protection act, 1994 PA
9 451, MCL 324.11716.
10 Sec. 410. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
11 department shall compile a report by November 1 of every fiscal
12 year ending in an odd number on the status of the implementation
13 plan for the western Lake Erie basin collaborative agreement. In an
14 effort to learn more about the presence and timing of harmful algal
15 blooms, the report must contain all of the following:
16 (a) An estimated cost of removal of total phosphorus per pound
17 at the 4 major wastewater treatment plants.
18 (b) A description of the grants that have been awarded.
19 (c) A description of the work that has commenced on the issue
20 of dissolved reactive phosphorus, the expected objectives and
21 outcomes of that work, and a list of the parties involved in that
22 effort.
23 (d) A description of the efforts and outcomes aimed at the
24 total phosphorus reduction for the River Raisin watershed.
25
26 MATERIALS MANAGEMENT DIVISION
27 Sec. 901. In addition to the money appropriated in part 1, the
28 department may receive and expend money from the Volkswagen
29 Environmental Mitigation Trust Agreement to provide funding for
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
161
1 activities as outlined within the State's Mitigation Plan. The
2 department shall prepare a report to the appropriations
3 subcommittees, the fiscal agencies, and the state budget office by
4 February 1, 2027 of the expenditures incurred under this section
5 during the fiscal year ending September 30, 2026.
6
7 WATER INFRASTRUCTURE
8 Sec. 951. The funds appropriated in part 1 for lead service
9 line replacement must be used to support water infrastructure
10 projects, including, but not limited to, lead service line
11 replacement and associated activities, drinking water projects,
12 wastewater management, or stormwater management to promote
13 coordinated water infrastructure work.
14 Sec. 952. From the funds appropriated in part 1 for water
15 state revolving funds, the department shall submit a report to the
16 standard report recipients detailing the percentage of funds
17 awarded to systems based on population served.

16 Sec. 204. If the state administrative board, acting under
17 section 3 of 1921 PA 2, MCL 17.3, transfers funds from an amount
18 appropriated under part 1, the legislature may, by a concurrent
19 resolution adopted by a majority of the members elected to and
20 serving in each house, intertransfer funds within part 1 for the
21 particular department, board, commission, officer, or institution.
22 Sec. 205. (1) A department or agency shall use the internet to
23 fulfill the reporting requirements of this part and shall make each
24 report readily accessible to the public and conspicuously post each
25 required report in a single archivable location on the department's
26 or agency's website not later than the due date required for each
27 report.
28 (2) In addition to placing all reports required in the current
29 fiscal year on the department's or agency's website, a department
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
198
1 or agency shall maintain on its website all reports placed on the
2 website from previous fiscal years posted by fiscal year in the
3 same single archivable location.
4 (3) A department or agency shall transmit all required reports
5 for the current fiscal year to the standard report recipients and
6 any other required recipients by email. The email shall include a
7 copy of the report and a link to access the report online.
8 Sec. 206. A department or agency shall receive and retain
9 copies of all reports funded from appropriations in part 1. A
10 department or agency shall follow federal and state law and
11 guidelines for short-term and long-term retention of records. A
12 department or agency may electronically retain copies of reports
13 unless otherwise required by federal and state guidelines.
14 Sec. 207. (1) A department or agency shall cooperate with the
15 MDTMB to maintain a searchable website accessible by the public at
16 no cost that includes, but is not limited to, all of the following
17 for the department or agency:
18 (a) Fiscal year-to-date expenditures by category.
19 (b) Fiscal year-to-date expenditures by appropriation unit.
20 (c) Fiscal year-to-date payments to a selected vendor,
21 including the vendor name, payment date, payment amount, and
22 payment description.
23 (2) A department or agency shall cooperate with the MDTMB to
24 update the searchable website on a quarterly basis.
25 Sec. 208. (1) In addition to any other requirements under this
26 part, if a department or agency is authorized under this part to
27 expend funds in addition to those appropriated in part 1, the
28 department or agency shall do all of the following:
29 (a) Not later than December 1, provide a report to the
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
199
1 chairpersons of the house and senate appropriations committees, the
2 house and senate fiscal agencies, and the state budget office that
3 details all of the following:
4 (i) The type of funding received during the previous fiscal
5 year that was authorized in part 2 of the article that made
6 appropriations for the department or agency in the previous fiscal
7 year.
8 (ii) When the funding was received.
9 (iii) The amount of funding received.
10 (iv) How much of the funding was spent and for what purpose.
11 (b) Not later than 60 days after receiving the funds
12 authorized under this part, provide a report to the chairpersons of
13 the house and senate appropriations committees, the house and
14 senate fiscal agencies, and the state budget office that details
15 all of the following:
16 (i) The type of funding received.
17 (ii) When the funding was received.
18 (iii) The amount of funding received.
19 (iv) The anticipated or actual amount to be spent and the
20 specified purpose or purposes.
21 (c) Not later than February 15, provide a report to the
22 chairpersons of the house and senate appropriations committees, the
23 house and senate fiscal agencies, and the state budget office with
24 an estimate of funding authorized by this part that the department
25 or agency anticipates it will receive in the subsequent fiscal year
26 that details all of the following:
27 (i) The type or types of funding anticipated.
28 (ii) The amount or amounts of funding anticipated.
29 (iii) The purpose or purposes of the funding.
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
200
1 (2) If another reporting requirement under this part would
2 provide substantially similar information on a substantially
3 similar time frame as would be reported under subsection (1),
4 subsection (1) does not apply.
5 Sec. 209. Not later than December 15, the state budget office
6 shall prepare and submit a report that provides estimates of the
7 total GF/GP appropriation lapses at the close of the previous
8 fiscal year. The report must summarize the projected year-end GF/GP
9 appropriation lapses by major departmental program or program
10 areas. The state budget office shall submit the report to the
11 standard report recipients and to the chairpersons of the senate
12 and house appropriations committees.
13 Sec. 210. Not later than 14 days after the release of the
14 executive budget recommendation, a department or agency shall
15 cooperate with the state budget office to provide an annual report
16 on estimated state restricted fund balances, state restricted fund
17 projected revenues, and state restricted fund expenditures for the
18 previous 2 fiscal years. The report must be submitted to the
19 standard report recipients and to the chairpersons of the senate
20 and house appropriations committees.
21 Sec. 211. Not later than November 15, a department or agency
22 shall report on private and other third-party funds received by the
23 department or agency in the previous fiscal year. The report must
24 include the amount of funding received, the specific source of
25 funding received, the purpose for which funding was expended, and
26 the amount of any remaining funds. The report must be submitted to
27 the standard report recipients and to the chairpersons of the
28 senate and house appropriations committees.
29 Sec. 212. Consistent with section 217 of the management and
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
201
1 budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1217, a department or agency shall
2 prepare a report on out-of-state travel expenses by not later than
3 January 1. The report must list all travel by classified and
4 unclassified employees outside this state in the previous fiscal
5 year that was funded in whole or in part with funds appropriated in
6 the department's or agency's budget. The department or agency shall
7 submit the report to the standard report recipients and to the
8 house and senate appropriations committees. The report must include
9 all of the following information:
10 (a) The dates of each travel occurrence.
11 (b) The total transportation and related costs of each travel
12 occurrence and the proportions funded with state GF/GP revenues,
13 state restricted revenues, federal revenues, and other revenues.
14 Sec. 213. On a quarterly basis, a department or agency
15 receiving appropriations in part 1 and the office of the auditor
16 general shall report on the number of FTEs in pay status by type of
17 staff and civil service classification, including comparison by
18 line item of the number of FTEs authorized from funds appropriated
19 in part 1 to the actual number of FTE positions employed by the
20 department or agency or the office of the auditor general at the
21 end of the reporting period. The report must be submitted to the
22 senate and house appropriations committees and to the standard
23 report recipients.
24 Sec. 214. Not later than April 1, a department or agency shall
25 report on each specific policy change made to implement a public
26 act affecting the department or agency that took effect during the
27 previous calendar year. The report must include reference to the
28 public act that necessitates the policy change. The department or
29 agency shall submit the report to the standard report recipients,
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
202
1 to the senate and house appropriations committees, and to the joint
2 committee on administrative rules.
3 Sec. 215. Not later than April 1, a department or agency shall
4 provide to the standard report recipients a copy of its annual
5 strategic plan prepared in compliance with section 363 of the
6 management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1363. The plan must
7 include the mission, vision, goals, strategies, and performance
8 measures of the department.
9 Sec. 216. A department or agency shall report on any court
10 settlement that may require further legislative review of state
11 statutory programs or regulations.
12 Sec. 219. To the extent possible, a department or agency shall
13 not expend appropriations under part 1 until all existing
14 authorized work project funds available for the same purposes are
15 exhausted.
16 Sec. 220. Not later than 6 months after the state budget
17 office issues work project letters, and again on or by April 15,
18 each department or agency shall submit an annual report that
19 summarizes all work project accounts. The reports must include all
20 of the following:
21 (a) A list of all work project accounts.
22 (b) The status of all work project accounts, including amounts
23 expended, amounts encumbered, and available balances for each
24 account.
25 (c) The amount of funds that lapsed from any previously
26 designated work project accounts, the name and description of the
27 work project account, and the funds that received the lapsed
28 amounts.
29 Sec. 221. To the extent permissible under section 261 of the
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
203
1 management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1261, all of the
2 following apply to the expenditure of funds appropriated in part 1:
3 (a) The funds must not be used for the purchase of foreign
4 goods or services, or both, if competitively priced and of
5 comparable quality American goods or services, or both, are
6 available.
7 (b) Preference must be given to goods or services, or both,
8 manufactured or provided by Michigan businesses, if they are
9 competitively priced and of comparable quality.
10 (c) Preference must be given to goods or services, or both,
11 that are manufactured or provided by Michigan businesses owned and
12 operated by veterans, if they are competitively priced and of
13 comparable quality.
14 Sec. 222. A department or agency shall not take disciplinary
15 action against an employee of the department or agency because the
16 employee communicates with a member of the legislature or
17 legislative staff unless the communication is prohibited by law and
18 the department or agency is exercising its authority as provided by
19 law.
20 Sec. 223. (1) A department or agency shall maximize the
21 efficiency of the state workforce and utilization and occupancy of
22 office space, leased or owned, for each division within the
23 department. Employees with job responsibilities that require
24 employees to serve in their capacities outside of an office shall
25 be monitored each pay period to ensure all work hours reported on
26 timesheets are actually worked.
27 (2) A department or agency shall comply with requirements set
28 forth by the office of the state employer on in-person work and
29 utilization and occupancy rates of state buildings to ensure in-
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
204
1 person work is optimized and occupancy rates are at least 80% or
2 higher, subject to market conditions.
3 (3) A department or agency shall adhere to civil service rules
4 and regulations that state the standard biweekly work period for a
5 full-time employee in the classified service of this state is the
6 equivalent of 80 hours of work. Each department or agency shall
7 establish policies and processes to ensure all employees are
8 working their jobs during agreed upon business hours.
9 (4) The office of the state employer must create and implement
10 uniform policies on occupancy, utilization, in-person work, and
11 remote work. The office of the state employer must make each
12 uniform policy publicly available on the department's or agency's
13 website.
14 Sec. 224. A department or agency shall complete a space
15 utilization assessment by July 1 using a form developed by the
16 MDTMB, for all space assigned under the department's or agency's
17 building occupancy agreement and leased office locations and shall
18 post the assessment on the department's or agency's website. A
19 department or agency shall develop, in coordination with the MDTMB,
20 a plan to reduce, consolidate, or otherwise optimize assigned
21 space. The plans must prioritize the use of state-owned facilities,
22 wherever possible, and comply with the MDTMB-established space
23 standards unless an exception is approved by the MDTMB.

3 Sec. 600. The senate, the house of representatives, or an

6 Sec. 601. (1) Funds appropriated in part 1 to an entity within
7 the legislative branch must not be expended or transferred to
8 another account without written approval of the authorized agent of
9 the legislative entity. If the authorized agent of the legislative
10 entity notifies the state budget director of its approval of an
11 expenditure or transfer before the year-end book-closing date for
12 that legislative entity, the state budget director shall
13 immediately make the expenditure or transfer. The authorized
14 legislative entity must be designated by the speaker of the house
15 of representatives for house entities, the senate majority leader
16 for senate entities, and the legislative council for legislative
17 council entities.

20 or other subgroup included in that component without the approval
21 of the legislative council.

22 technology services" means services that involve all aspects of

24 all of the following:

27 (c) Cybersecurity.
28 (d) Social media.
29 (e) Mainframe computer support and management.

252
1 (f) Cloud services support and management, including, but not
2 limited to, infrastructure as a service, platform as a service, and
3 software as a service.

5 not limited to, wired and wireless network build-out, support, and
6 management.
7 (h) Information technology project management.
8 (i) Information technology procurement and contract
9 management.
10 (j) Telecommunication services, security, infrastructure, and
11 support.
12 (k) Server support and management.
13 (l) Information technology planning and budget management.

20 or agency, all independent verification and validation reporting
21 must be submitted to each technology vendor on the project and to
22 the standard report recipients.

4 will be earned based on the initiatives undertaken with the funds
5 in part 1. The state budget director shall determine and authorize
6 the appropriate manner for implementing this section.
7 Sec. 840. From the funds appropriated in part 1, a state
8 department or agency shall not issue an RFP for a contract for
9 information technology software development unless the RFP includes
10 a clear statement of objective that is not longer than 5 pages and
11 that communicates all essential operational requirements of the
12 contracted service.
13
14 STATE BUILDING AUTHORITY RENT
15 Sec. 842. (1) Funds appropriated in part 1 for state building
16 authority rent may, in addition to this purpose, be expended for
17 the payment of required premiums for insurance on facilities owned
18 by the state building authority or payment of costs that may be
19 incurred as the result of any deductible provisions in the
20 applicable insurance policies.
21 (2) If the amount appropriated in part 1 for state building
22 authority rent is not sufficient to pay the rent obligations and
23 insurance premiums and deductibles identified in subsection (1) for
24 state building authority projects, there is appropriated from the
25 general fund of this state the amount necessary to pay the
26 obligations.

28 OFFICE OF THE STATE EMPLOYER
29 Sec. 843. (1) The funds appropriated in part 1 for statewide
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
259
1 appropriations must be funded by assessments against longevity and
2 insurance appropriations throughout state government in a manner
3 prescribed by the MDTMB. The funds must be used as specified in
4 joint labor/management agreements, or through the coordinated
5 compensation hearings process. Any deposits of assessments made
6 under this subsection and any unencumbered funds are restricted
7 revenues, may be carried over into the succeeding fiscal years, and
8 are appropriated.
9 (2) In addition to the funds appropriated in part 1 for
10 statewide appropriations, the MDTMB may receive and expend funds in
11 the additional amounts specified in joint labor/management
12 agreements, or through the coordinated compensation hearings
13 process, in the same manner and subject to the same conditions as
14 prescribed in subsection (1).
15 Sec. 844. In addition to the funds appropriated in part 1, the

17 departments and state agencies to implement administrative leave
18 bank transfer provisions specified in joint labor/management
19 agreements. The funds may also be transferred to other principal
20 executive departments and state agencies under the joint
21 labor/management agreement and any amounts transferred under the
22 joint labor/management agreement are authorized for receipt and
23 expenditure by the receiving principal executive department or
24 state agency. Any funds received by the MDTMB under this section
25 and intended, under the joint labor/management agreements, to be
26 available for use beyond the close of the fiscal year, and any
27 unencumbered funds, may be carried over into the next fiscal year.

29 CIVIL SERVICE COMMISSION
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
260
1 Sec. 850. (1) In accordance with section 5 of article XI of
2 the state constitution of 1963, all restricted funds must be
3 assessed a sum not less than 1% of the total aggregate payroll paid
4 from those funds for financing the civil service commission on the
5 basis of actual 1% restricted sources total aggregate payroll of
6 the classified service for the preceding fiscal year. This
7 includes, but is not limited to, restricted funds appropriated in
8 part 1 of any appropriations act. The civil service commission
9 shall return any unexpended funds appropriated under this
10 subsection to each 1% fund source not later than 6 months after the
11 end of the fiscal year.
12 (2) The appropriations in part 1 are estimates of actual
13 charges based on payroll appropriations. With the approval of the
14 state budget director, the civil service commission may adjust
15 financing sources for civil service charges based on actual payroll
16 expenditures, if the adjustments do not increase the total
17 appropriation for the civil service commission.
18 (3) The financing from restricted sources must be credited to
19 the civil service commission by the end of the second fiscal
20 quarter.
21 Sec. 851. Except where specifically appropriated for this
22 purpose, financing from restricted sources must be credited to the
23 civil service commission. For restricted sources of funding within
24 the general fund that have the legislative authority for carryover,
25 if current spending authorization or revenues are insufficient to
26 accept the charge, the shortage must be taken from carryforward
27 balances of that funding source. Restricted revenue sources that do
28 not have carryforward authority must be utilized to satisfy civil
29 service commission operating deductions first and civil service
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
261
1 commission obligations second. General fund dollars are
2 appropriated for any shortfall, if approved by the state budget
3 director.
4 Sec. 852. The appropriation in part 1 to the civil service
5 commission, for state-sponsored group insurance, flexible spending
6 accounts, and COBRA, represents amounts, in part, included within
7 the various appropriations throughout state government for the
8 current fiscal year to fund the flexible spending account program
9 included within the civil service commission. Deposits against
10 state-sponsored group insurance, flexible spending accounts, and
11 COBRA for the flexible spending account program must be made from
12 assessments levied during the fiscal year in a manner prescribed by
13 the civil service commission. Unspent employee contributions to the
14 flexible spending accounts may be used to offset administrative
15 costs for the flexible spending account program, and any remaining
16 balance of unspent employee contributions lapses to the general
17 fund.
18 Sec. 853. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the Michigan
19 civil service commission shall continue to work toward completing
20 its review of current employee classifications and educational
21 requirements necessary for employment. On completion of the review,
22 the commission, where possible, shall substitute relevant
23 experience for the default educational requirement of a bachelor's
24 degree.

5 university.

15 and part 1 or any previous capital outlay act shall comply with the

16 Sec. 865. (1) A site preparation economic development fund is

18 shall determine whether a specific state-owned site qualifies for
19 inclusion in the site preparation economic development fund.
20 (2) Any proceeds from the sale of an economic development site
21 must be deposited in the site preparation economic development fund
22 and are available for site preparation expenditures, unless
23 otherwise provided by law. The economic development sites are
24 authorized for sale consistent with state law. Expenditures from
25 the site preparation economic development fund are authorized for
26 site preparation activities that enhance the marketable sale value
27 of the economic development sites.

29 $25,000,000.00 is authorized from the general fund to the site
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
265
1 preparation economic development fund.
2 (4) Not later than December 31, the MDTMB shall submit a
3 report to the standard report recipients and the senate and house
4 of representatives standing committees on appropriations that
5 includes both of the following:
6 (a) The revenue and expenditure activity in the site
7 preparation economic development fund for the immediately preceding
8 fiscal year.
9 (b) The sites identified as economic development sites.
10 (5) As used in this section:
11 (a) "Economic development site" means a state-owned site that
12 is declared as surplus property under section 251 of the management
13 and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1251, and would provide
14 economic benefit to the area of the site or to this state.
15 (b) "Site preparation activities" includes, but is not limited
16 to, demolition, environmental studies and abatement, utility
17 enhancement, and site excavation.
18 Sec. 866. (1) The energy efficiency revolving fund is created
19 within the state treasury. The state treasurer may receive money or
20 other assets from any source for deposit into the energy efficiency
21 revolving fund. The state treasurer shall direct the investment of
22 the energy efficiency revolving fund. The state treasurer shall
23 credit to the energy efficiency revolving fund interest and
24 earnings from energy efficiency revolving fund investments.
25 (2) Money in the energy efficiency revolving fund at the close
26 of the fiscal year remains in the energy efficiency revolving fund
27 and does not lapse to the general fund.
28 (3) All money received and deposited from any source into the
29 energy efficiency revolving fund is appropriated to support
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
266
1 qualifying projects in accordance with the terms and conditions
2 established in subsections (4) and (5).
3 (4) The MDTMB shall provide oversight and direction for the
4 energy efficiency revolving fund, coordinate a call for projects,
5 and prioritize the award of projects that will contribute to a
6 reduction in this state's carbon footprint. State administrative
7 costs must be not more than 10% of the total project cost.
8 (5) The MDTMB shall set terms with agencies participating in
9 the energy efficiency revolving fund program that include the scope
10 of each project, funding commitments, data collection and reporting
11 requirements, and any other financial terms related to realization
12 of energy savings related to implementation of the project. The
13 MDTMB may enter into a memorandum of understanding to memorialize
14 these terms.
15 (6) Not later than February 1, the MDTMB shall submit a report
16 to the standard report recipients on projects funded under this
17 section in the immediately preceding fiscal year. The report must
18 list each approved project, the amount provided from the energy
19 efficiency revolving fund for each project, the department or
20 agency under which the project belongs, anticipated annual savings
21 from each project, and revenue from savings deposited into the
22 energy efficiency revolving fund by project.

25 Sec. 873. (1) This section applies only to projects for
26 community colleges.
27 (2) State support is directed towards the remodeling and
28 additions, special maintenance, or construction of certain
29 community college buildings. The community college shall obtain or
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
267
1 provide for site acquisition and initial main utility installation
2 to operate the facility. The funding must be composed of local and
3 state shares and not more than 50% of a capital outlay project, not
4 including a lump-sum special maintenance project or remodeling and
5 addition project, for a community college may be appropriated from
6 state and federal funds, unless otherwise appropriated by the
7 legislature.
8 (3) An expenditure under this part and part 1 is authorized
9 when the release of the appropriation is approved by the board on
10 the recommendation of the director. The director may recommend to
11 the board the release of any appropriation in part 1 only after the
12 director is assured that the legal entity operating the community
13 college to which the appropriation is made has complied with this
14 part and part 1 and has matched the amounts appropriated as
15 required by this part and part 1. A release of funds in part 1 must
16 not exceed 50% of the total cost of planning and construction of
17 any project, not including lump-sum remodeling and additions and
18 special maintenance, unless otherwise appropriated by the
19 legislature. Further planning and construction of a project
20 authorized by this part and part 1 or applicable sections of the
21 management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1101 to 18.1594,
22 must be in accordance with the purpose and scope as defined and
23 delineated in the approved program statements and planning
24 documents. This part and part 1 are applicable to all projects for
25 which planning appropriations were made in previous PAs.
26 (4) The community college shall take the steps necessary to
27 secure available federal construction and equipment money for
28 projects funded for construction in this part and part 1 if an
29 application was not previously made. If there is a reasonable
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
268
1 expectation that a previous year unfunded application may receive
2 federal money in a subsequent year, the community college shall
3 take whatever action necessary to keep the application active.
4 Sec. 874. If university and community college matching
5 revenues are received in an amount less than the appropriations for
6 capital projects contained in this part and part 1, the state funds
7 must be reduced in proportion to the amount of matching revenue
8 received.
9 Sec. 875. (1) The director may require that community colleges
10 and universities that have an authorized project described in part
11 1 submit documentation regarding the project match and governing
12 board approval of the authorized project not more than 60 days
13 after the beginning of the fiscal year.
14 (2) If the documentation required by the director under
15 subsection (1) is not submitted, or does not adequately
16 authenticate the availability of the project match or governing
17 board approval of the authorized project, the director may
18 terminate the authorization. The authorization terminates 30 days
19 after the director notifies the JCOS of the intent to terminate the
20 project unless the JCOS approves an extension of the authorization.

7 REVENUE SHARING
8 Sec. 950. The department of treasury shall distribute the
9 funds appropriated in part 1 for constitutional revenue sharing to
10 cities, villages, and townships, as required under section 10 of
11 article IX of the state constitution of 1963. Revenue collected in
12 accordance with section 10 of article IX of the state constitution
13 of 1963 in excess of the amount appropriated in part 1 for
14 constitutional revenue sharing is appropriated for distribution to
15 cities, villages, and townships, on a population basis as required
16 under section 10 of article IX of the state constitution of 1963.
17 Sec. 952. (1) The funds appropriated in part 1 for city,
18 village, and township revenue sharing are for grants to cities,
19 villages, and townships and must be distributed as provided in this
20 section.
21 (2) From the first $299,126,400.00 appropriated in part 1 for
22 city, village, and township revenue sharing, each city, village, or
23 township shall receive an amount equal to 100.0% of the revenue
24 sharing payment for which the city, village, or township received
25 under section 952(2) of article 5 of 2025 PA 22 rounded to the
26 nearest dollar.
27 (3) The remaining amount appropriated in part 1 for city,
28 village, and township revenue sharing after the distributions under
29 subsection (2) must be distributed as follows:
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
295
1 (a) 1/3 shall be distributed as taxable value payments as
2 provided under subsection (4).
3 (b) 1/3 must be distributed as unit type population payments
4 as provided under subsection (5).
5 (c) 1/3 must be distributed as yield equalization payments as
6 provided under subsection (6).
7 (4) A taxable value payment must be made to each city,
8 village, and township, determined as follows:
9 (a) Determine the per capita taxable value for each city,
10 village, and township by dividing the taxable value of that city,
11 village, or township by the population of that city, village, or
12 township.
13 (b) Determine the statewide per capita taxable value by
14 dividing the total taxable value of all cities, villages, and
15 townships by the total population of all cities, villages, and
16 townships.
17 (c) Determine the per capita taxable value ratio for each
18 city, village, and township by dividing the statewide per capita
19 taxable value by the per capita taxable value for that city,
20 village, or township.
21 (d) Determine the adjusted taxable value population for each
22 city, village, and township by multiplying the per capita taxable
23 value ratio as determined under subdivision (c) for that city,
24 village, or township by the population of that city, village, or
25 township.
26 (e) Determine the total statewide adjusted taxable value
27 population, which is the sum of all adjusted taxable value
28 population for all cities, villages, and townships.
29 (f) Determine the taxable value payment rate by dividing the
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
296
1 amount to be distributed under this subsection by the total
2 statewide adjusted taxable value population as determined under
3 subdivision (e).
4 (g) Determine the taxable value payment for each city,
5 village, and township by multiplying the result under subdivision
6 (f) by the adjusted taxable value population for that city,
7 village, or township.
8 (5) A unit type population payment must be made to each city,
9 village, and township, determined as follows:
10 (a) Determine the unit type population weight factor for each
11 city, village, and township as follows:
12 (i) For a township with a population of 5,000 or less, 1.0.
13 (ii) For a township with a population of more than 5,000 but
14 less than 10,001, 1.2.
15 (iii) Except as otherwise provided in subparagraph (xix), for a
16 township with a population of more than 10,000 but less than
17 20,001, 1.44.
18 (iv) For a township with a population of more than 20,000 but
19 less than 40,001, 4.32.
20 (v) For a township with a population of more than 40,000 but
21 less than 80,001, 5.18.
22 (vi) For a township with a population of more than 80,000,
23 6.22.
24 (vii) For a village with a population of 5,000 or less, 1.5.
25 (viii) For a village with a population of more than 5,000 but
26 less than 10,001, 1.8.
27 (ix) For a village with a population of more than 10,000, 2.16.
28 (x) For a city with a population of 5,000 or less, 2.5.
29 (xi) For a city with a population of more than 5,000 but less
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

1 than 10,001, 3.0.
2 (xii) For a city with a population of more than 10,000 but less
3 than 20,001, 3.6.
4 (xiii) For a city with a population of more than 20,000 but less
5 than 40,001, 4.32.
6 (xiv) For a city with a population of more than 40,000 but less
7 than 80,001, 5.18.
8 (xv) For a city with a population of more than 80,000 but less
9 than 160,001, 6.22.
10 (xvi) For a city with a population of more than 160,000 but
11 less than 320,001, 7.46.
12 (xvii) For a city with a population of more than 320,000 but
13 less than 640,001, 8.96.
14 (xviii) For a city with a population of more than 640,000,
15 10.75.
16 (xix) For a township that has a population of not less than
17 10,000 and certifies to the department of treasury that the
18 township provides for or makes available all of the following, the
19 township must receive the unit type population weight factor for a
20 city with the same population:
21 (A) Fire services.
22 (B) Police services on a 24-hour basis either through
23 contracting for or directly employing personnel.
24 (C) Water services to 50% or more of its residents.
25 (D) Sewer services to 50% or more of its residents.
26 (b) Determine the adjusted unit type population for each city,

28 weight factor for that city, village, or township as determined
29 under subdivision (a) by the population of the city, village, or

1 township.
2 (c) Determine the total statewide adjusted unit type

13 (6) A yield equalization payment must be made to each city,

15 the guaranteed tax base for a local tax effort, but not to exceed
16 0.02. The payment must be determined as follows:
17 (a) The guaranteed tax base is the maximum combined state and
18 local per capita taxable value that can be guaranteed in a state
19 fiscal year to each city, village, and township for a local tax
20 effort, not to exceed 0.02, if an amount equal to the amount
21 described in subsection (3)(c) is distributed to cities, villages,
22 and townships whose per capita taxable value is below the
23 guaranteed tax base.
24 (b) The full yield equalization payment to each city, village,
25 and township is the product of the amounts determined under
26 subparagraphs (i) and (ii):
27 (i) An amount greater than zero that is equal to the difference
28 between the guaranteed tax base determined in subdivision (a) and
29 the per capita taxable value of the city, village, or township.

1 (ii) The local tax effort of the city, village, or township,
2 not to exceed 0.02, multiplied by the population of that city,
3 village, or township.
4 (7) For purposes of this section, any city, village, or

2 Sec. 956. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for
3 financially distressed cities, villages, or townships, the
4 department of treasury shall create and operate a grant program to
5 award grants to cities, villages, and townships that have 1 or more
6 conditions that indicate probable financial distress, as determined
7 by the department of treasury. A city, village, or township with 1
8 or more conditions that indicate probable financial distress may
9 apply in a manner determined by the department of treasury for a
10 grant to pay for specific projects or services that move the city,
11 village, or township toward financial stability. Grants must be
12 used for specific projects or services that move the city, village,
13 or township toward financial stability. The city, village, or
14 township must use the grants under this section to do 1 or more of
15 the following:
16 (a) Make payments to reduce unfunded accrued liability.
17 (b) Repair or replace critical infrastructure and equipment
18 owned or maintained by the city, village, or township.
19 (c) Reduce debt obligations.
20 (d) Pay for costs associated with a transition to shared
21 services with another jurisdiction.
22 (e) Administer other projects that move the city, village, or
23 township toward financial stability.
24 (2) The department of treasury shall award not more than
25 $2,000,000.00 to any city, village, or township under this section.
26 (3) Not later than 30 days after grants are awarded, the
27 department of treasury shall submit a report to the standard report
28 recipients that includes all of the following for each grant
29 recipient.
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
303
1 (a) The name of the grant recipient.
2 (b) The date the grant was approved.
3 (c) The amount of the grant.
4 (d) A description of the project or projects that will be paid
5 by the grant.
6 Sec. 957. A term that is defined in the Glenn Steil state

9 Sec. 959. (1) The department of treasury shall distribute
10 funds appropriated in part 1 for public safety revenue sharing
11 grants as provided for in subsection (9) and as follows:
12 (a) $3,250,000.00 to the MDHHS to establish and administer a
13 grant program to award funds to community violence intervention
14 programs.
15 (b) $35,062,500.00 for a public safety assistance payment to
16 each city, village, or township. The public safety assistance
17 payment must be calculated as follows:
18 (i) Determine the average violent crime count for each city,
19 village, and township by adding the 2 highest annual violent crime
20 counts for each city, village, and township from the 3 most
21 recently available annual crime reports published by the MDSP as of
22 the first day of the current fiscal year and dividing by 2.
23 (ii) Determine the statewide total violent crime count by
24 summing the average violent crime count for each city, village, and
25 township as determined under subparagraph (i).
26 (iii) Determine the proportional factor for each city, village,
27 and township by dividing the average violent crime count for each
28 city, village, and township as determined under subparagraph (i) by
29 the statewide total violent crime count determined under
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
304
1 subparagraph (ii).
2 (iv) Multiply the proportional factor determined in
3 subparagraph (iii), for each city, village, and township by the total
4 amount available for distribution under this subdivision, and round
5 to the nearest dollar.
6 (2) A public safety assistance payment to a city, village, or
7 township as determined under subsection (1)(b) is limited to not
8 more than 25% of the total amount available for distribution under
9 subsection (1)(b).
10 (3) All of the following apply to a distribution under
11 subsection (1)(b):
12 (a) A city, village, or township must use the distribution
13 only for operational and capital expenditures that serve the
14 purposes of public safety.
15 (b) Not less than 75% of a public safety assistance payment
16 distributed under subsection (1)(b) to a city, village, or township
17 must be used to fund, either directly or indirectly through a
18 subgrant to another governmental entity, a law enforcement agency
19 or law enforcement officers as defined in section 2 of the Michigan
20 commission on law enforcement standards act, 1965 PA 203, MCL
21 28.602.
22 (c) Not more than 25% of a public safety assistance payment
23 distributed under subsection (1)(b) to a city, village, or township
24 must be used to fund other non-law-enforcement-related public
25 safety purposes, which include, but are not limited to: public
26 safety initiatives to improve recruitment or retention efforts;
27 training programs; equipment purchases; programs designed to reduce
28 identified risks to public safety; crime diversion programs;
29 operational emergency medical or firefighter services; or capital
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
305
1 improvements to public safety buildings or structures. All local
2 public safety initiative expenses must be related to public safety
3 and designed to reduce identified risks to public safety and cannot
4 include unproven intervention solutions to community violence.
5 (d) A distribution made under subsection (1)(b) must not be
6 used for the following nonoperating expenses:
7 (i) Pension and other post employee benefit (OPEB) payments.
8 (ii) Lawsuits and claims payments.
9 (iii) Debt service payments.
10 (iv) The acquisition or use of a vehicle weighing more than
11 15,000 pounds that is designed or used for a tactical police
12 purpose.
13 (v) The acquisition or use of facial recognition technology.
14 (vi) The acquisition or use of a chemical weapon.
15 (4) A city, village, or township may subgrant all or part of
16 the distribution under subsection (1)(b) if the subgrant is used
17 for the purpose of public safety as described under subsection (3).
18 (5) Subject to subsections (6), (7), and (8), not later than
19 November 30, the director of the MDSP shall provide the department
20 of treasury with a certified list that contains all of the

22 (a) Base crime level.
23 (b) Current violent crime counts.

25 of the MDSP.

ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
307
1 other non-law-enforcement-related public safety purposes, which

24 (11) As used in subsections (1) to (8):
25 (a) "Base crime level" means the average of a city, village,
26 or township's 2 highest annual rates of violent crime, as certified
27 by the director of the MDSP and determined by the annual crime
28 reports published by the MDSP in the 3 calendar years immediately
29 preceding the current calendar year.
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
308
1 (b) "Population" means the counts, as defined by the Federal
2 Bureau of Investigation and used by the director of the MDSP, to
3 determine the population for each city, village, and township.
4 (c) "Violent crime" means that term as defined by the director
5 of the MDSP in accordance with the department's incident crime
6 reporting program and the corresponding annual crime reports.
7 (d) "Violent crime count" means the number of violent crimes
8 based on victim counts, as certified by the director of the MDSP.
9 When a victim is connected to multiple offenses, the victim is
10 counted under the highest-ranked offense, as defined by the
11 director of the MDSP.
12 (e) "Violent crime rate" means the number of crimes per
13 100,000 people, determined by dividing a particular city, village,
14 or township violent crime count by the population, then multiplying
15 by 100,000 and rounding to the nearest whole number.
16 (12) As used in this section:
17 (a) "Chemical weapon" means a munition or device that is
18 specifically designed to cause death or other harm through a toxic
19 chemical that would be released as a result of the employment of
20 the munition or device.
21 (b) "Facial recognition technology" means an automated or a
22 semiautomated technological process that assists in identifying or
23 verifying an individual based on the individual's face.

14 year to a level lower than the amount appropriated in part 1.
15 Sec. 978. The Michigan gaming control board shall use actual
16 expenditure data in determining the actual regulatory costs of
17 conducting racing dates and shall submit a report of that data to
18 the standard report recipients and the senate and house of
19 representatives appropriations subcommittees on agriculture. The
20 Michigan gaming control board may not be reimbursed for more than
21 the actual regulatory cost of conducting race dates. In determining
22 actual costs, the Michigan gaming control board shall take into
23 account that each specific breed of horse may require different
24 regulatory mechanisms.
25 Sec. 979. From the funds appropriated in part 1 for
26 millionaire party regulation, the Michigan gaming control board may
27 receive and expend internet gaming fund revenue in an amount that
28 is not more than the amount appropriated in part 1 for necessary
29 expenses incurred in the licensing and regulation of millionaire
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
314
1 parties under article 2 of the Traxler-McCauley-Law-Bowman bingo
2 act, 1972 PA 382, MCL 432.132 to 432.152. Any unused internet
3 gaming fund revenues are subject to the distribution requirements
4 in section 16 of the lawful internet gaming act, 2019 PA 152, MCL
5 432.316. Not later than March 1, the Michigan gaming control board
6 shall submit a report to the standard report recipients that
7 includes all of the following:
8 (a) The total expenditures related to the licensing and
9 regulating of millionaire parties.
10 (b) The steps taken to ensure charities are receiving revenue
11 due to them.
12 (c) A description of the progress on promulgating rules to
13 ensure compliance with the Traxler-McCauley-Law-Bowman bingo act,
14 1972 PA 382, MCL 432.101 to 432.152.
15 (d) Any enforcement actions taken.

17 ONE-TIME APPROPRIATIONS
18 Sec. 991. The funds appropriated in part 1 for local
19 government financial reporting shall be used to improve web portal
20 data collection and financial reporting, audit and compliance
21 documentation, and other information sharing capabilities between
22 local units of government and the department of treasury.
23 Sec. 992. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for local
24 prosecutor support grants, the department shall issue grants to
25 county prosecutors that received a grant under section 991 of
26 article 5 of 2023 PA 119, section 805 of article 16 of 2025 PA 22,
27 or section 991(1) of article 5 of 2025 PA 22. The grant amount
28 shall be the greatest amount received under section 991 of article
29 5 of 2023 PA 119, section 805 of article 16 of 2025 PA 22, or
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
315
1 section 991(1) of article 5 of 2025 PA 22.
2 (2) County prosecutors must show that the total amount
3 appropriated to the county prosecutor from the county in the
4 current fiscal year is equal to or greater than the amount received
5 in the previous fiscal year.
6 (3) The department of treasury shall issue grants to county
7 prosecutors within 10 business days after receiving documentation
8 that the total amount appropriated to the county prosecutors is
9 equal to or greater than the amount received in the previous fiscal
10 year.
11 (4) By February 1, 2027, if counties are found ineligible
12 under subsection (2), remaining funds shall be used for local
13 prosecutor adjustment grants under section 993, to be disbursed in
14 a manner as determined in section 993.
15 Sec. 993. (1) For the funds appropriated in part 1 for local
16 prosecutor support adjustment grants, the department of treasury
17 shall issue adjustment grants to county prosecutors that qualify
18 under subsection (2) in the amount calculated under subsection (3)
19 and distributed in order of subsection (4).
20 (2) County prosecutors are eligible to receive an adjustment
21 grant under this section if both of the following conditions are
22 met:
23 (a) The county prosecutors received a grant under section 991
24 of article 5 of 2023 PA 119 and section 805 of article 16 of 2025
25 PA 22.
26 (b) The amount that the county prosecutors received under
27 section 991 of article 5 of 2023 PA 119 was greater than the amount
28 that the county prosecutors received under section 805 of article
29 16 of 2025 PA 22.
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
316
1 (3) The amount of adjustment grants to each county prosecutor
2 that is qualified under subsection (2) must be calculated as the
3 sum of the amount received in section 991 of article 5 of 2023 PA
4 119 minus the amount received under section 805 of article 16 of
5 2025 PA 22, plus the amount received in section 991 of article 5 of
6 2023 PA 119 minus the amount received under section 991(1) of
7 article 5 of 2025 PA 22.
8 (4) The funds calculated in subsection (3) shall be disbursed
9 in the following order:
10 (a) County prosecutors that have received all funds eligible
11 under section 991 of article 5 of 2023 PA 119 as of April 1, 2025
12 shall receive the amount calculated under subsection (3) within 10
13 business days after the effective date of this section.
14 (b) Remaining funds shall be disbursed to county prosecutors
15 that receive all eligible funds under section 991 of article 5 of
16 2023 PA 11, 10 business days after the county prosecutor has
17 received all eligible funds under section 991 of article 5 of 2023
18 PA 119, on a first-come, first-served basis.
19 Sec. 994. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for public
20 safety academy assistance grant program, the funds must be used by
21 the Michigan commission on law enforcement standards to do all of
22 the following:
23 (a) Subject to subsection (3), administer a competitive public
24 safety academy assistance scholarship program that provides police
25 academy scholarships of not more than $20,000.00 per recruit on a
26 first-come, first-served basis to an individual who meets the
27 requirements of subsection (2) and any necessary requirements to
28 enroll in a police academy program.
29 (b) Pay the salaries of training academy recruits from local
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
317
1 public safety agencies or pay the salaries of police cadets who are
2 receiving tuition assistance under subsection (2), and academy
3 tuition and eligible related costs as determined by the Michigan
4 commission on law enforcement standards.
5 (2) In order to receive a scholarship under subdivision (a),
6 an individual must have applied to at least 1 law enforcement basic
7 training academy approved by the Michigan commission on law
8 enforcement standards, have completed an interview, and have
9 received approval for the scholarship from the public safety agency
10 that the individual intends to serve.
11 (3) For the purposes of subsection (1)(a), not more than 25
12 scholarships may be approved for a particular public safety agency.
13 (4) The Michigan commission on law enforcement standards may
14 use not more than $140,000.00 for administration of the scholarship
15 program established under subsection (1)(a).
16 (5) The Michigan commission on law enforcement standards may
17 set any necessary additional requirements for the distribution of
18 the funds disbursed under subsection (1).

20 STATE BUILDING AUTHORITY
21 Sec. 1100. (1) Subject to section 242 of the management and
22 budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1242, and on the approval of the
23 state building authority, the department of treasury may expend
24 from the general fund of this state during the fiscal year an
25 amount necessary to meet the cash flow requirements of those state
26 building authority projects solely for lease to a state agency
27 identified in both part 1 and this section, and for which state
28 building authority bonds or notes have not been issued, and for the
29 sole acquisition by the state building authority of equipment and
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
318
1 furnishings for lease to a state agency as permitted by 1964 PA
2 183, MCL 830.411 to 830.425, for which the issuance of bonds or
3 notes is authorized by an appropriations PA that is effective for
4 the immediately preceding fiscal year. Any general fund advances
5 for which state building authority bonds have not been issued must
6 bear an interest cost to the state building authority at a rate
7 that is not greater than the rate earned by the state treasurer's
8 common cash fund during the period in which the advances are
9 outstanding and are repaid to the general fund of this state.
10 (2) On sale of bonds or notes for the projects identified in
11 part 1 or for equipment as authorized by an appropriations PA and
12 in this section, the state building authority shall credit the
13 general fund of this state an amount equal to the amount expended
14 from the general fund plus interest, if any, as described in this
15 section.
16 (3) For state building authority projects for which bonds or
17 notes have been issued and on the request of the state building
18 authority, the state treasurer shall make advances without interest
19 from the general fund as necessary to meet cash flow requirements
20 for the projects. The state building authority shall reimburse the
21 state treasurer for the advances when the investments earmarked for
22 the financing of the projects mature.
23 (4) If a project identified in part 1 is terminated after
24 final design is complete, advances made on behalf of the state
25 building authority for the costs of final design must be repaid to
26 the general fund in a manner recommended by the director of the
27 state building authority.
28 Sec. 1102. (1) The state building authority shall not release
29 state building authority funding to a university or community
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
319
1 college to finance the construction or renovation of a facility
2 that collects revenue in excess of money required for the operation
3 of that facility unless the university or community college agrees
4 to use that excess revenue to reimburse the state building
5 authority. The excess revenue received by the state building
6 authority as reimbursement must be credited to the general fund to
7 offset rent obligations associated with the retirement of bonds
8 issued for the applicable facility. The auditor general shall
9 annually identify and audit the facilities that are subject to this
10 section. Costs associated with the administration of the audit must
11 be charged against money received by the state building authority
12 as reimbursement under this section.
13 (2) As used in this section, "revenue" includes state
14 appropriations, facility opening money, other state aid, indirect
15 cost reimbursement, and other revenue generated by the activities
16 of the facility.
17 Sec. 1103. Not later than October 15, the state building
18 authority shall submit a report to the standard report recipients
19 and the JCOS regarding the status of construction projects
20 associated with state building authority bonds as of the end of the
21 immediately preceding fiscal year. Not later than 30 days after a
22 refinancing or restructuring bond issue is sold, the state building
23 authority shall submit a report to the standard report recipients
24 and the JCOS regarding the status of construction projects
25 associated with that bond issue. Each report must include all of
26 the following:
27 (a) A list of all completed construction projects for which
28 state building authority bonds have been sold, and which bonds are
29 currently active.
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
320
1 (b) A list of all projects under construction for which sale
2 of state building authority bonds is pending.
3 (c) A list of all projects authorized for construction or
4 identified in an appropriations act for which approval of
5 schematic/preliminary plans or total authorized cost is pending
6 that have state building authority bonds identified as a source of
7 financing.

11 Sec. 202. The appropriations under this part and part 1 are
12 subject to the management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1101
13 to 18.1594.
14 Sec. 203. As used in this part and part 1:
15 (a) "AIDS" means acquired immunodeficiency syndrome.
16 (b) "CCBHC" means certified community behavioral health
17 clinic.
18 (c) "CMHSP" means a community mental health services program
19 as that term is defined in section 100a of the mental health code,
20 1974 PA 258, MCL 330.1100a.
21 (d) "CMS" means the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid
22 Services.
23 (e) "CPT" means current procedural terminology.
24 (f) "Current fiscal year" means the fiscal year ending
25 September 30, 2027.
26 (g) "Department" means the department of health and human
27 services.
28 (h) "Director" means the director of the department.
29 (i) "EPSDT" means early and periodic screening, diagnosis, and
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
349
1 treatment.
2 (j) "Federal poverty level" means the poverty guidelines
3 revised periodically and published in the Federal Register by the
4 Secretary of the United States Department of Health and Human
5 Services under the Secretary's authority to revise the poverty line
6 under 42 USC 9902.
7 (k) "FQHC" means federally qualified health center.
8 (l) "FTE" means full-time equated.
9 (m) "GME" means graduate medical education.
10 (n) "Health plan" means, at a minimum, an organization that
11 meets the criteria for delivering the comprehensive package of
12 services under the department's comprehensive health plan.
13 (o) "HEDIS" means health care effectiveness data and
14 information set.
15 (p) "HMO" means health maintenance organization.
16 (q) "IDEA" means the individuals with disabilities education
17 act, 20 USC 1400 to 1482.
18 (r) "IDG" means interdepartmental grant.
19 (s) "MCH" means maternal and child health.
20 (t) "Medicaid" means benefits under the medical assistance
21 program established under title XIX of the social security act, 42
22 USC 1396 to 1396w-8, and administered by the department under the
23 social welfare act, 1939 PA 280, MCL 400.1 to 400.119b.
24 (u) "Medicare" means benefits under the federal Medicare
25 program established under title XVIII of the social security act,
26 42 USC 1395 to 1395mmm.
27 (v) "MiCAFE" means Michigan's coordinated access to food for
28 the elderly.
29 (w) "MIChild" means the program described in section 1670 of
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
350
1 this part.
2 (x) "MiSACWIS" means Michigan statewide automated child
3 welfare information system.
4 (y) "PACE" means program of all-inclusive care for the
5 elderly.
6 (z) "PAS/ARR-OBRA" means the preadmission screening and annual
7 resident review required under the omnibus budget reconciliation
8 act of 1987, section 1919(e)(7) of the social security act, 42 USC
9 1396r.
10 (aa) "PATH" means Partnership. Accountability. Training. Hope.
11 (bb) "PFAS" means perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl
12 substances.
