19 Sec. 204. If the state administrative board, acting under
20 section 3 of 1921 PA 2, MCL 17.3, transfers funds from an amount
21 appropriated under part 1, the legislature may, by a concurrent
22 resolution adopted by a majority of the members elected to and
23 serving in each house, intertransfer funds within part 1 for the
24 particular department, board, commission, officer, or institution.
25 Sec. 205. (1) The department shall use the internet to fulfill
26 the reporting requirements of this part and shall make each report
27 readily accessible to the public and conspicuously post each
28 required report in a single archivable location on the department's
29 website not later than the due date required for each report.
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
352
1 (2) In addition to placing all reports required in the current
2 fiscal year on the department's website, the department shall
3 maintain on its website all reports placed on the website from
4 previous fiscal years posted by fiscal year in the same single
5 archivable location.
6 (3) The department shall transmit all required reports for the
7 current fiscal year to the standard report recipients and any other
8 required recipients by email. The email shall include a copy of the
9 report and a link to access the report online.
10 Sec. 206. The department shall receive and retain copies of
11 all reports funded from appropriations in part 1. The department
12 shall follow federal and state guidelines for short-term and long-
13 term retention of records. The department may electronically retain
14 copies of reports unless otherwise required by federal and state
15 guidelines.
16 Sec. 207. (1) The department shall cooperate with the
17 department of technology, management, and budget to maintain a
18 searchable website accessible by the public at no cost that
19 includes, but is not limited to, all of the following for the
20 department:
21 (a) Fiscal year-to-date expenditures by category.
22 (b) Fiscal year-to-date expenditures by appropriation unit.
23 (c) Fiscal year-to-date payments to a selected vendor,
24 including the vendor name, payment date, payment amount, and
25 payment description.
26 (2) The department shall cooperate with the department of
27 technology, management, and budget to update the searchable website
28 on a quarterly basis.
29 Sec. 208. (1) In addition to any other requirements under this
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
353
1 part, if the department is authorized under this part to expend
2 funds in addition to those appropriated in part 1, the department
3 must do all of the following:
4 (a) Not later than December 1, provide a report to the
5 chairpersons of the house and senate appropriations committees, the
6 house and senate fiscal agencies, and the state budget office that
7 details all of the following:
8 (i) The type of funding received during the previous fiscal
9 year that was authorized in part 2 of the article that made
10 appropriations for the department in the previous fiscal year.
11 (ii) When the funding was received.
12 (iii) The amount of funding received.
13 (iv) How much of the funding was spent and for what purpose or
14 purposes.
15 (b) Not later than 60 days after receipt of funds authorized
16 under this part, provide a report to the chairpersons of the house
17 and senate appropriations committees, the house and senate fiscal
18 agencies, and the state budget office that details all of the
19 following:
20 (i) The type of funding received.
21 (ii) When the funding was received.
22 (iii) The amount of funding received.
23 (iv) The anticipated or actual amount to be spent and the
24 specified purpose or purposes.
25 (c) Not later than February 15, provide a report to the
26 chairpersons of the house and senate appropriations committees, the
27 house and senate fiscal agencies, and the state budget office that
28 contains an estimate of funding authorized by this part that the
29 department anticipates it will receive in the subsequent fiscal
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
354
1 year, and identifies all of the following:
2 (i) The type or types of funding anticipated.
3 (ii) The amount or amounts of funding anticipated.
4 (iii) The purpose or purposes of the funding.
5 (2) If another reporting requirement under this part would
6 provide substantially similar information on a substantially
7 similar time frame as would be reported under subsection (1),
8 subsection (1) does not apply.
9 Sec. 209. Not later than December 15, the state budget office
10 shall prepare and submit a report that provides estimates of the
11 total general fund/general purpose appropriation lapses at the
12 close of the previous fiscal year. The report must summarize the
13 projected year-end general fund/general purpose appropriation
14 lapses by major departmental program or program areas. The state
15 budget office shall submit the report to the standard report
16 recipients and the chairpersons of the senate and house
17 appropriations committees.
18 Sec. 210. Not later than 14 days after the release of the
19 executive budget recommendation, the department shall cooperate
20 with the state budget office to provide an annual report on
21 estimated state restricted fund balances, state restricted fund
22 projected revenues, and state restricted fund expenditures for the
23 previous 2 fiscal years. The report must be submitted to the
24 standard report recipients and to the chairpersons of the senate
25 and house appropriations committees.
26 Sec. 211. Not later than November 15, the department shall
27 disclose on a publicly accessible website private and other third-
28 party funds received by the department in the previous fiscal year.
29 The report must include the amount of funding received, the
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
355
1 specific source of funding received, the purpose for which funding
2 was expended, and the amount of any remaining funds. The report
3 must be submitted to the standard report recipients and to the
4 chairpersons of the senate and house appropriations committees.
5 Sec. 212. Consistent with section 217 of the management and
6 budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1217, each department and agency
7 receiving appropriations in part 1 shall prepare a report on out-
8 of-state travel expenses by not later than January 1. The report
9 must list all travel by classified and unclassified employees
10 outside this state in the previous fiscal year that was funded in
11 whole or in part with funds appropriated in the department's or
12 agency's budget. The department or agency shall submit the report
13 to the standard report recipients and to the house and senate
14 appropriations committees. The report must include all of the
15 following information:
16 (a) The dates of each travel occurrence.
17 (b) The total transportation and related costs of each travel
18 occurrence and the proportions funded with state general
19 fund/general purpose revenues, state restricted revenues, federal
20 revenues, and other revenues.
21 Sec. 213. On a quarterly basis, the department or agency
22 receiving appropriations in part 1 shall report on the number of
23 full-time equated positions in pay status by civil service
24 classification, including a comparison by line item of the number
25 of full-time equated positions authorized from funds appropriated
26 in part 1 to the actual number of full-time equated positions
27 employed by the department at the end of the reporting period. The
28 report must be submitted to the senate and house appropriations

8 Sec. 221. To the extent permissible under section 261 of the
9 management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1261, all of the
10 following apply to the expenditure of funds appropriated in part 1:
11 (a) The funds must not be used for the purchase of foreign

22 Sec. 222. The department shall not take disciplinary action
23 against an employee of the department because the employee
24 communicates with a member of the legislature or legislative staff
25 unless the communication is prohibited by law and the department is
26 exercising its authority as provided by law.
27 Sec. 223. (1) The department shall maximize the utilization of
28 its in-person state workforce. The department shall prioritize
29 occupancy utilization of office space for each division within the
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
359
1 department. Employees with job responsibilities that require
2 employees to serve in their capacities outside of an office shall
3 be monitored each pay period to ensure all work hours reported on
4 timesheets are actually worked.
5 (2) The department shall comply with requirements set forth by
6 the office of the state employer on in-person work and utilization
7 and occupancy rates of state buildings to ensure in-person work is
8 optimized and occupancy rates are 80 percent or higher, subject to
9 market conditions.
10 (3) The department shall adhere to the rules and regulations
11 of civil service, which state that the standard biweekly work
12 period for a full-time employee in the classified services is the
13 equivalent of 80 hours of work. The department shall establish
14 policies and processes to ensure all employees are working their
15 jobs during agreed-upon business hours.
16 (4) The office of the state employer must create and implement
17 an occupancy utilization uniform policy on occupancy, utilization,
18 in-person, and remote work. The office of the state employer must
19 make each uniform policy publicly available on the department's
20 website.

6 the law of this state to collect an overpayment owed to the
7 department, beginning 60 days after the initial notification date
8 of the overpayment amount, the department may assess a penalty of

10 penalty may be assessed 6 months after the initial notification
11 date of the overpayment amount. The department shall not collect
12 penalty interest in an amount that exceeds the amount of the
13 original overpayment. This state's share of any funds collected
14 under this section must be deposited in the general fund of this
15 state.

2 capped federal funds include, but are not limited to, all of the

4 (a) TANF.

6 (c) Title IV-B subpart I child welfare services block grant.

8 funds.

10 Sec. 274. (1) On a quarterly basis, the department, with the
11 approval of the state budget director, is authorized to realign
12 sources between other federal, TANF, and capped federal financing
13 authorizations to maximize federal revenues. The realignment of
14 financing must not produce any of the following:
15 (a) A gross increase or decrease in the department's total
16 individual line item authorizations.
17 (b) A net increase or decrease in total federal revenues.
18 (c) A net increase in TANF authorization.

22 subsection (1), including the dates, line items, and amounts of the
23 transactions. If, at the time a quarterly report is due, a
24 transaction was not made under subsection (1), a report is not
25 required.

19 Sec. 401. (1) The appropriations in part 1 assume a total
20 federal child support incentive payment of $26,500,000.00.
21 (2) From the federal money received for child support
22 incentive payments, $12,000,000.00 must be retained by this state
23 and expended for child support program expenses.
24 (3) From the federal money received for child support
25 incentive payments, $14,500,000.00 must be paid to counties based
26 on each county's performance level for each of the performance
27 measures under 45 CFR 305.2.
28 (4) If the child support incentive payment to this state from
29 the federal government is greater than $26,500,000.00, then 100% of
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
377
1 the amount in excess must be retained by this state and is
2 appropriated until the total retained by this state reaches
3 $15,397,400.00.
4 (5) If the child support incentive payment to this state from
5 the federal government is greater than the amount needed to satisfy
6 subsections (1), (2), (3), and (4), the additional funds are
7 subject to appropriation by the legislature.
8 (6) If the child support incentive payment to this state from
9 the federal government is less than $26,500,000.00, then the state
10 share and the county share must each be reduced by 50% of the
11 shortfall.
12 Sec. 409. (1) If statewide retained child support collections

15 excess appropriation may be distributed to eligible counties to
16 supplement, but not supplant, county title IV-D funding.
17 (2) Each county whose retained child support collections in
18 the current fiscal year exceed its fiscal year 2004-2005 retained
19 child support collections, excluding tax offset and financial
20 institution data match collections in both the current fiscal year
21 and fiscal year 2004-2005, shall receive its proportional share of
22 the 75% excess appropriation.

7 COMMUNITY SERVICES AND OUTREACH
8 Sec. 453. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for
9 homeless programs, the department shall allocate funds to the
10 emergency shelter program to support efforts of shelter providers
11 to move homeless individuals and households into permanent housing
12 as quickly as possible. The funds must be equal to or exceed the
13 amount that a provider would receive if the provider is paid a
14 $19.00 per diem rate. Expected outcomes are increased shelter
15 discharges to stable housing destinations, decreased recidivism
16 rates for shelter clients, and a reduction in the average length of
17 stay in emergency shelters.
18 (2) Not later than March 1 of the current fiscal year, the
19 department shall submit, to the standard report recipients, a
20 report on the total amount expended for the emergency shelter
21 program in the prior 2 fiscal years, the total number of shelter
22 nights provided, and the average length of stay in an emergency
23 shelter.
24 Sec. 454. The department shall allocate the full amount of
25 funds appropriated in part 1 for homeless programs to provide
26 services for homeless individuals and families, including, but not
27 limited to, third-party contracts for emergency shelter services.
28 Sec. 455. As a condition of receipt of federal TANF revenue,

ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
379
1 shelter and human services agencies shall collaborate with the
2 department to obtain necessary TANF eligibility information on the
3 family as soon as possible. From the funds appropriated in part 1
4 for homeless programs, the department is authorized to make
5 allocations of TANF revenue only to the homeless shelters and human
6 services agencies that report necessary data to the department to
7 meet TANF eligibility reporting requirements. Homeless shelters or
8 human services agencies that do not report necessary data to the
9 department to meet TANF eligibility reporting requirements shall

8 Sec. 512. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for foster
9 care payments, the department shall allocate $500,000.00 of TANF
10 revenue to provide luggage to a child who is being removed from the
11 child's home or changing placement and is a TANF eligible
12 individual. The luggage provided under this section is considered
13 to belong to the child and may not be confiscated by the department
14 or the child's foster parent. The department is not required to
15 provide new luggage under this section to a child who is changing
16 placement and has had luggage previously provided by the
17 department.
18 (2) The department may partner with local charities to
19 establish and maintain the supply of luggage to be used to
20 transport a child's personal belongings. Additionally, the
21 department may accept donations of luggage to fulfill the
22 requirements of this section.
23 (3) As used in this section, "luggage" means any of the
24 following:
25 (a) A suitcase of any size.
26 (b) A duffel bag that holds at least 30 liters.
27 Sec. 513. (1) The department shall not expend funds
28 appropriated in part 1 to pay for the department's direct placement
29 of a child in an out-of-state facility unless all of the following
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
388
1 conditions are met:
2 (a) An appropriate placement is not available in this state,
3 as determined by the department's interstate compact office.
4 (b) An out-of-state placement exists that is nearer to the
5 child's home than the closest appropriate in-state placement, as
6 determined by the department's interstate compact office.
7 (c) The out-of-state facility meets all of the licensing
8 standards for a comparable facility in this state.
9 (d) The out-of-state facility meets all of the applicable
10 licensing standards of the state in which it is located.
11 (e) The department has visited the site of the out-of-state
12 facility; has reviewed the facility records, licensing records, and
13 reports; and believes that the facility is an appropriate placement
14 for the child.
15 (2) The department shall not expend money for a child placed
16 in an out-of-state facility without approval of the executive
17 director of the children's services agency.

5 Sec. 550. (1) The department shall not offset against
6 reimbursements to counties or seek reimbursement from counties for
7 charges that were received by the department more than 12 months
8 before the department seeks to offset against reimbursement. A

10 be paid, for a charge that is more than 12 months after the date of
11 service or original status determination when initially submitted
12 by the county.
13 (2) Not later than 12 months after a date of service, a
14 service provider shall submit a request for payment. A request for
15 payment submitted later than 12 months after the date of service
16 requires the provider to submit an exception request to the county
17 or the department for approval or denial.

10 Sec. 601. After a client agrees to the release of the client's
11 name and address to the local housing authority, the department
12 shall request from the local housing authority information
13 regarding whether the housing unit for which vendoring has been
14 requested meets applicable local housing codes. Vendoring must be
15 terminated if the local housing authority indicates in writing that
16 the unit does not meet local housing codes and until the local
17 housing authority indicates in writing that the local housing codes
18 have been met.
19 Sec. 602. The department shall conduct a full evaluation of an
20 individual's assistance needs if the individual has applied for
21 disability more than 1 time in a 1-year period.
22 Sec. 603. For any change in the income of a recipient of the
23 food assistance program, the family independence program, or state
24 disability assistance that results in a benefit decrease, the
25 department shall notify the recipient of the amount of the decrease
26 not later than 15 work days before the first day of the month in
27 which the decrease takes effect.
28 Sec. 604. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for state
29 disability assistance payments, the department shall operate a
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
406
1 state disability assistance program. Except as provided in
2 subsection (3), to be eligible for the program, an individual must
3 be a needy citizen of the United States or alien exempted from the
4 SSI citizenship requirement who is not less than 18 years of age,
5 or an emancipated minor, and meets 1 or more of the following
6 requirements:
7 (a) Is a recipient of SSI, Social Security, or medical
8 assistance due to disability or being 65 years of age or older.
9 (b) Is an individual with a physical or mental impairment that
10 meets federal SSI disability standards, except that the minimum
11 duration of the disability must be 90 days. Substance use disorder
12 alone is not a basis for eligibility.
13 (c) Is a resident of an adult foster care facility, a home for
14 the aged, a county infirmary, or a substance use disorder treatment
15 center.
16 (d) Is an individual receiving 30-day postresidential
17 substance use disorder treatment.
18 (e) Is an individual diagnosed as having AIDS.
19 (f) Is an individual receiving special education services
20 through a local intermediate school district.
21 (g) Is a caretaker of a disabled individual who meets the
22 requirements specified in subdivision (a), (b), (e), or (f).
23 (2) An applicant for or recipient of state disability
24 assistance is considered needy if the applicant or recipient does
25 both of the following:
26 (a) Meets the same asset test as is applied for the family
27 independence program.
28 (b) Has a monthly budgetable income that is less than the
29 payment standards.
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
407
1 (3) Except for an individual described in subsection (1)(c) or
2 (d), an individual is not disabled under this section if the
3 individual's drug addiction or alcoholism is a contributing factor
4 material to the determination of disability.
5 (4) As used in this section:
6 (a) "Material to the determination of disability" means that,
7 if the individual stopped using drugs or alcohol, the individual's
8 remaining physical or mental limitations would not be disabling. If
9 the individual's remaining physical or mental limitations would be
10 disabling, then the drug addiction or alcoholism is not material to
11 the determination of disability and the individual may receive
12 state disability assistance, but the individual must actively
13 participate in a substance abuse treatment program, and the
14 assistance must be paid to a third party or through vendor
15 payments.
16 (b) "Substance abuse treatment" includes receipt of inpatient
17 or outpatient services or participation in Alcoholics Anonymous or
18 a similar program.
19 Sec. 605. The level of reimbursement provided to state

21 facilities must be the same as the prevailing SSI rate under the
22 personal care category.

12 Sec. 608. An adult foster care facility that provides
13 domiciliary care or personal care to a resident receiving SSI or a
14 home for the aged serving a resident receiving SSI shall not
15 require a resident described in this section to reimburse the home
16 for the aged or adult foster care facility for care at a rate in
17 excess of a rate that is authorized by the legislature. To the
18 extent permitted by federal law, an adult foster care facility and
19 home for the aged that serves a resident receiving SSI is not
20 prohibited from accepting a third-party payment in addition to SSI
21 if the payment is not for food, clothing, or shelter, or would
22 result in a reduction in the resident's SSI payment.
23 Sec. 609. The department shall not reduce the state
24 supplementation level under the SSI program for the personal
25 care/adult foster care and home for the aged categories during the
26 current fiscal year. Not later than 30 days before a proposed
27 reduction in the state supplementation level, the department shall
28 notify the legislature of the proposed reduction.
29 Sec. 610. (1) The department shall grant an exemption from the
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
409
1 good-cause criteria for the state emergency relief program if an
2 emergency results from an unexpected expense related to maintaining
3 or securing employment.
4 (2) In determining housing affordability eligibility for state

7 obligation does not exceed 75% of the group's total net income.
8 (3) The department shall not make a state emergency relief

9 Sec. 614. By January 15 of the current fiscal year, the

14 Sec. 615. Except as required by federal law, the department
15 shall not use funds appropriated in part 1 to provide public
16 assistance to an individual who is not a United States citizen,
17 permanent resident alien, or refugee. This section does not
18 prohibit the department from entering into a contract with a food
19 bank, emergency shelter provider, or another human service agency
20 that may, as a normal part of doing business, provide food or
21 emergency shelter.
22 Sec. 616. The department shall require a retailer that
23 participates in the electronic benefits transfer program to charge
24 no more than a $2.50 fee for cash back as a condition of
25 participation.

6 Sec. 620. (1) The department shall determine a Medicaid
7 applicant's Medicaid eligibility not later than 90 days after the
8 Medicaid applicant completes a Medicaid application if the Medicaid
9 applicant's disability is an eligibility factor. For other Medicaid
10 applicants, including an applicant who is a patient of a nursing
11 home, the department shall determine the applicant's Medicaid
12 eligibility within 45 days after receiving the Medicaid applicant's
13 application.
14 (2) On a quarterly basis, the department shall submit a report
15 to the standard report recipients on the number of recipients who
16 were ineligible for Medicaid after Medicaid eligibility
17 redeterminations. The report must include, in a monthly data
18 format, the number of recipients who had their eligibility examined
19 directly, through an ex parte eligibility process or through a
20 passive eligibility process. The report must also include a copy of
21 each baseline and monthly report that the department provides to
22 CMS for unwinding data reporting and the number of recipients who
23 did not respond to the department through eligibility outreach or
24 data requests.

4 Sec. 627. (1) The department must conduct food assistance
5 certification renewals or redeterminations, semi-annual contacts,
6 and mid-certification contacts in accordance with state and federal
7 law. Upon receiving information that may affect food assistance
8 eligibility or benefit amounts, the department must review the
9 information, verify the accuracy of the information as required,
10 and, where appropriate, take action, including denying, reducing,
11 terminating, or adjusting benefits in accordance with state and
12 federal law.
13 (2) The department must review information received through
14 federal and state data matches and other information received
15 through established eligibility and program integrity processes to
16 determine whether eligibility or benefit amounts may require
17 further review. These processes include, but are not limited to, as
18 applicable, information related to deceased recipients, residency,
19 incarceration, income, lottery winnings, or other eligibility
20 factors.
21 (3) The department must review Michigan bridge card
22 transaction data on at least a monthly basis to identify patterns
23 of exclusive out-of-state use over a 60-day period. Cases with
24 potential residency concerns may be reviewed through existing
25 eligibility and program integrity processes. If appropriate, the
26 department must refer cases to the office of inspector general for
27 investigation consistent with established fraud referral
28 procedures. In determining whether to refer cases to the office of
29 inspector general, the department may consider the recipient's
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
414
1 residence's proximity to Indiana, Ohio, and Wisconsin if the
2 department identifies a pattern of exclusive out-of-state use over
3 a 60-day period in 1 of the states listed in this subsection and
4 whether individual situations allow for online purchases in
5 accordance with federal and state standards.
6 (4) On a quarterly basis, the department must submit a report
7 to the standard report recipients identifying all of the following:
8 (a) The data sources used to verify eligibility factors
9 including income, residency, identity, household composition,
10 citizenship or immigration status, or death for the purpose of
11 determining food assistance eligibility.

13 changes to an eligibility factor identified in subdivision (a).

15 assistance after Public Law 119-21 policy changes were implemented.
16 Sec. 634. The funds appropriated in part 1 for food assistance
17 program benefits must not be used to expand eligibility for the
18 restaurant meal program beyond the federal eligibility standards.
19 The department shall limit eligibility for the restaurant meal
20 program to households receiving food assistance program benefits in
21 which all members are either:
22 (a) Individuals who are 60 years of age or older.
23 (b) Individuals who are disabled.
24 (c) Individuals who are homeless.
25 (d) A spouse of a food assistance program recipient who is
26 eligible for the restaurant meal program.

1 with another in order to escape domestic violence. The department

10 Sec. 672. (1) By February 15 of the current fiscal year, the
11 department's office of inspector general shall submit a report to

16 card inappropriately and the current status of each case, the

18 temporarily revoked as a result of inappropriately using their

20 or removed from the electronic benefit transfer program for

22 report must also include the number of Michigan bridge card
23 trafficking instances and overall welfare fraud referrals, that
24 includes, but is not limited to, information on the number of
25 investigations completed, fraud and intentional program violation
26 dollar amounts identified, the number of referrals to prosecutors,

28 number of program disqualifications imposed. The report must
29 distinguish between savings and cost avoidance. As used in this
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
417
1 subsection:
2 (a) "Cost avoidance" includes expenditures avoided due to
3 front-end eligibility investigations and other preemptive actions
4 undertaken in the prevention of fraud.
5 (b) "Savings" includes receivables established from instances
6 of fraud committed.
7 (2) If a fourth Michigan bridge card has been issued to a
8 household in a 12-month period, the department shall notify the
9 household that the household has reached the number of issued cards
10 threshold. At a household's fifth and each subsequent card
11 replacement request, a card will not be issued until a recipient
12 from the household has spoken directly to the local office district
13 manager or county director. The district manager or county director
14 may issue a new Michigan bridge card based on the district
15 manager's or county director's assessment of the recipient's
16 situation and the recipient's explanation.
17 (3) As used in this section:
18 (a) "Food assistance trafficking" means the buying and selling
19 of food assistance benefits for cash or items not authorized under
20 7 USC 2036b.
21 (b) "Inappropriate use" means not used to meet a family's
22 ongoing basic needs, including, but not limited to, food, clothing,
23 shelter, utilities, household goods, personal care items, and
24 general incidentals.

20 revisions to a county's service spending plan outside of the
21 requested revision notification submitted to the county by the
22 department. The department shall notify a county that its service
23 spending plan is approved not later than 30 days after the

13 justice residential foster care services must prohibit a contractor
14 from denying a referral for placing a youth, or terminating a
15 youth's placement, if the youth's assessed treatment needs are in

17 treatment capacity, as approved by the department in the facility's
18 licensing program statement. A contractor is not required to accept
19 or retain a youth whose assessed treatment, behavioral, medical,
20 psychiatric, safety, or supervision needs exceed or are
21 inconsistent with the facility's approved residential program type,
22 treatment capacity, staffing model, or ability to safely and
23 appropriately serve the youth. The master contract must also
24 require that a youth placed in a juvenile justice residential
25 foster care facility receives clinically appropriate treatment
26 services, including regularly scheduled treatment sessions with a
27 licensed clinician and regularly scheduled reviews with a licensed
28 psychiatrist for medication management when clinically indicated,

6 Sec. 960. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for autism
7 services, the department shall continue to cover all Medicaid
8 autism services to Medicaid enrollees eligible for the services
9 that were covered on January 1, 2019.
10 (2) To restrain cost increases in the autism services line
11 item, the department shall do all of the following:
12 (a) Maintain specific written guidance for standardization of
13 Medicaid PIHPs and CMHSPs autism spectrum disorder administrative
14 services, including, but not limited to, reporting requirements,
15 coding, and reciprocity of credentialing and training between PIHPs
16 and CMHSPs to reduce administrative duplication at the PIHP, CMHSP,
17 and service provider levels.
18 (b) Require consultation with the client's evaluation
19 diagnostician and PIHP to approve the client's ongoing therapy for
20 3 years, unless the client's evaluation diagnostician recommended
21 an evaluation before the 3 years or if a clinician on the treatment
22 team recommended an evaluation for the client before the third
23 year.
24 (c) Limit the authority to perform a diagnostic evaluation for
25 Medicaid autism services to qualified licensed practitioners as
26 determined by the department.
27 (d) Allow and expand the utilization of telemedicine and
28 telepsychiatry to increase access to diagnostic evaluation
29 services.
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
441
1 (e) Coordinate with the department of insurance and financial
2 services on oversight for compliance with the Paul Wellstone and
3 Pete Domenici mental health parity and addiction equity act of
4 2008, Public Law 110-343, as it relates to autism spectrum disorder
5 services, to ensure appropriate cost sharing between public and
6 private payers.
7 (f) Require that Medicaid eligibility be confirmed through
8 prior evaluations conducted by qualified licensed practitioners as
9 determined by the department.
10 (g) Maintain regular statewide provider trainings on autism
11 spectrum disorder standard clinical best practice guidelines for
12 treatment and diagnostic services.

25 Sec. 1008. (1) A PIHP and CMHSP shall do all of the following:
26 (a) Work to reduce administration costs by ensuring that PIHP
27 and CMHSP responsible functions are efficient in allowing optimal
28 transition of dollars to the direct services considered most
29 effective in assisting individuals served. Any consolidation of
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
448
1 administrative functions must demonstrate, by independent analysis,
2 a reduction in dollars spent on administration resulting in greater
3 dollars spent on direct services. Savings resulting from increased
4 efficiencies must not be applied to PIHP and CMHSP net assets,
5 internal service fund increases, building costs, increases in the
6 number of PIHP and CMHSP personnel, or other areas not directly
7 related to the delivery of improved services.
8 (b) Take an active role in managing mental health care by
9 ensuring consistent and high-quality service delivery throughout
10 its network and promote a conflict-free care management
11 environment.
12 (c) Ensure that direct service rate variances are related to
13 the level of need or other quantifiable measures to ensure that the
14 most money possible reaches direct services.
15 (d) Whenever possible, promote fair and adequate direct care
16 reimbursement, including, but not limited to, fair wages for direct
17 service workers.
18 (2) Not later than May 1 of the current fiscal year, the
19 department shall submit a report to the standard report recipients
20 on any actual reduction of administrative costs over the prior 2
21 fiscal years.

4 subcontractor described in subsection (1) that receives greater
5 than $1,000,000.00 in state grant funding to comply with
6 performance-related metrics to maintain their eligibility for

9 (a) Each contractor or subcontractor has accreditations that
10 attest to their competency and effectiveness as behavioral health
11 and social service agencies.
12 (b) Each contractor or subcontractor has a mission that is
13 consistent with the purpose of an agency providing physical and
14 behavioral health services to multicultural populations.
15 (c) Each contractor shall validate that any subcontractors
16 utilized within these appropriations share the same mission as the
17 lead agency receiving funding.
18 (d) Each contractor or subcontractor shall demonstrate cost-
19 effectiveness.
20 (e) Each contractor or subcontractor shall ensure the
21 contractor or subcontractor's ability to leverage private dollars
22 to strengthen and maximize service provision.
23 (f) Each contractor or subcontractor shall provide timely and
24 accurate reports regarding the number of clients served, units of
25 service provision, and ability to meet their stated goals.
26 (3) The department shall require each contractor or
27 subcontractor described in subsection (1) to ensure that the funds
28 appropriated under this section are used only on proven or

11 Sec. 1034. (1) PIHPs must verify, on a quarterly basis, to the
12 department and to the standard report recipients that every
13 provider within the PIHP's provider network receives not less than
14 the applicable reimbursement rates or fees required in sections 924
15 and 264 of this part. The verification under this subsection must
16 provide actual claims and utilization data.
17 (2) The department shall seek CMS approval to exclude PIHPs
18 that are not compliant with subsection (1) from all performance
19 incentives available to PIHPs.
20 (3) The department shall audit the claims and utilization data
21 provided in this section. If the department audit determines that a
22 PIHP reimburses any provider within that PIHP's provider network at
23 a rate less than the applicable reimbursement rates or fees
24 required in sections 924 and 264 of this part, the department shall
25 notify that PIHP that it is not eligible for performance incentives
26 funded in part 1. Not later than 10 days after a notification to a
27 PIHP under this subsection, the department shall notify the
28 standard report recipients that the PIHP is not eligible for
29 performance incentives funded in part 1.

2 STATE PSYCHIATRIC HOSPITALS AND FORENSIC MENTAL HEALTH SERVICES
3 Sec. 1051. The department shall continue a revenue recapture
4 project to generate additional revenues from third parties related
5 to cases that have been closed or are inactive. A portion of
6 revenues collected through the project's efforts may be used for
7 departmental costs and contractual fees associated with retroactive
8 collections under the project and to improve ongoing departmental
9 reimbursement management functions.
10 Sec. 1052. The department shall use gift and bequest donations
11 received for patient living and treatment environments for
12 additional private funds to provide specific enhancements for
13 individuals residing at state-operated facilities. The department
14 shall use the gifts and bequest donations consistent with the
15 stipulation of the donor. The department shall use gift and bequest
16 donations described under this section within 3 years unless
17 otherwise stipulated by the donor.
18 Sec. 1055. (1) The department shall not implement a closure or
19 consolidation of a state hospital, center, or agency, until each
20 CMHSP or PIHP affected by the closure or consolidation has programs
21 and services in place for the individuals currently in the
22 hospital, center, or agency that is to be closed or consolidated,
23 and has a plan for providing services to the individuals who would
24 have been admitted to the hospital, center, or agency.
25 (2) A closure or consolidation is dependent on adequate
26 department-approved CMHSP and PIHP plans that include a discharge
27 and aftercare plan for each individual currently in a facility
28 described in subsection (1). A discharge and aftercare plan must
29 address an individual's housing needs. A homeless shelter or
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
453
1 similar temporary shelter arrangement is inadequate to meet an
2 individual's housing needs.
3 (3) Four months after a closure is certified under section
4 19(6) of the state employees' retirement act, 1943 PA 240, MCL
5 38.19, the department shall provide a closure plan to the standard
6 report recipients.
7 (4) On the closure of a hospital, center, or agency and after
8 transitional costs have been paid, the remaining balances of funds
9 appropriated for the hospital, center, or agency must be
10 transferred to CMHSPs or PIHPs responsible for providing services
11 for individuals previously served by the hospital, center, or
12 agency.
13 Sec. 1056. The department may collect revenue for patient
14 reimbursement from first- and third-party payers, including
15 Medicaid and local county CMHSP payers, to cover the cost of
16 patient placement in state hospitals and centers. The department
17 may adjust financing sources for patient reimbursement based on
18 actual revenues earned. If the revenue collected exceeds current
19 year expenditures, the revenue may be carried forward with approval
20 of the state budget director. The department shall use the revenue
21 carried forward as a first source of funds in the subsequent year.
22 Sec. 1058. Effective October 1 of the current fiscal year, the
23 department, in consultation with the department of technology,
24 management, and budget, may maintain a bid process to identify 1 or
25 more private contractors to provide food and custodial services for
26 the administrative areas at a state hospital identified by the
27 department as capable of generating savings through the outsourcing
28 of food and custodial services.

21 Needs and Strengths tool for youth and families served through the
22 unit or support team.

6 designated state office of rural health. Funding levels must not be

9 with the Indian Health Service, tribal health program facilities,
10 or Urban Indian Health Program facilities, that provide services
11 under a contract with a Medicaid managed care entity to ensure that
12 the facilities described in this section receive the maximum amount
13 allowable under federal law for Medicaid services.

22 tribal victim service organization.

24 not less than 1 domestic violence or sexual assault victim service
25 organization.

5 uniform statewide sexual assault evidence kit tracking system, in
6 accordance with the final report of the Michigan sexual assault
7 evidence kit tracking and reporting commission, the department
8 shall allocate $369,500.00 for administering a uniform statewide
9 sexual assault evidence kit tracking system. The system must
10 include all of the following:
11 (a) A uniform statewide system to track the submission and
12 status of sexual assault evidence kits.
13 (b) A uniform statewide system to audit untested kits that
14 were collected on or before March 1, 2015 and were released by
15 victims to law enforcement.
16 (c) Secure electronic access for victims.
17 (d) The ability to accommodate concurrent data entry with kit
18 collection through mechanisms that include, but are not limited to,
19 web entry through computers or smartphones, and through scanning
20 devices.

9 EPIDEMIOLOGY, EMERGENCY MEDICAL SERVICES, AND LABORATORY
10 Sec. 1180. From the funds appropriated in part 1 for
11 epidemiology administration and for childhood lead program, the
12 department shall maintain a public health drinking water program
13 and maintain enhanced efforts to monitor child blood lead levels.
14 The public health drinking water program must ensure that
15 appropriate investigations of potential health hazards occur for
16 all community and noncommunity drinking water supplies where
17 chemical exceedances of action levels, health advisory levels, or
18 maximum contaminant limits are identified. The goals of the
19 childhood lead program must include improving the identification of
20 children affected by lead exposure, improving the timeliness of
21 case follow-up, and attaining nurse care management for children
22 with lead exposure, and to achieve a long-term reduction in the
23 percentage of children in this state with elevated blood lead
24 levels.
25 Sec. 1181. From the funds appropriated in part 1 for
26 epidemiology administration, the department shall maintain a vapor
27 intrusion response program. The vapor intrusion response program
28 shall assess risks to public health at vapor intrusion sites and
29 respond to vapor intrusion risks if appropriate. The goals of the
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
462
1 vapor intrusion response program must include reducing the number
2 of individuals who are exposed to toxic substances through vapor
3 intrusion and improving health outcomes for individuals who are
4 identified as having been exposed to vapor intrusion.
5 Sec. 1182. Not later than April 1 of the current fiscal year,
6 the department shall submit a report to the standard report
7 recipients on the expenditures and activities undertaken by the
8 lead abatement program during the previous fiscal year using the
9 funds previously appropriated for the healthy homes program. The
10 report must include, but is not limited to, a funding allocation
11 schedule, the expenditures by category of expenditure and by
12 subcontractor, a description of program elements, the number of
13 housing units abated of lead-based paint hazards by zip code, and a
14 description of program accomplishments and progress.

5 Sec. 1227. The department shall establish criteria for all
6 funds allocated for health and wellness initiatives. The criteria
7 must include a requirement that a program receiving funding is
8 evidence-based and supported by research, includes interventions
9 that have been shown to demonstrate outcomes that lower cost and
10 improve quality, and is designed for statewide impact. The
11 department shall give preference to a program that uses the funding
12 as match for additional resources, including, but not limited to,
13 federal sources.

23 vapor intrusion, drinking water contamination, and lead exposure.

25 including, but not limited to, staffing, planning and response, and
26 creating and disseminating materials related to PFAS contamination
27 issues and other emerging public health issues and threats.

21 through the programs described in subsection (1) takes into account
22 the needs of rural communities.
23 (3) As used in this section, "rural community" means any of
24 the following:
25 (a) A county, city, village, or township with a population of
26 30,000 or less.
27 (b) A county, city, village, or township described in
28 subdivision (a), if it is located within a metropolitan statistical
29 area.

10 Sec. 1314. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
11 department shall enhance the department's education and outreach
12 efforts that encourage women of childbearing age to seek the
13 confirmation of a pregnancy at the earliest indication of a
14 possible pregnancy and to initiate continuous and routine prenatal
15 care on the confirmation of a pregnancy. The department shall
16 ensure that the department's programs, policies, and practices
17 promote prenatal and obstetrical care by doing all of the
18 following:
19 (a) Supporting access to care.
20 (b) Reducing and eliminating barriers to care.
21 (c) Supporting recommendations for best practices.
22 (d) Encouraging optimal prenatal habits, including, but not
23 limited to, prenatal medical visits, use of prenatal vitamins, and
24 the cessation of tobacco use, alcohol use, or drug use.
25 (e) Tracking birth outcomes to study improvements in
26 prevalence of neonatal substance exposure, fetal alcohol syndrome,
27 and other preventable neonatal disease.
28 (f) Tracking maternal increase in healthy behaviors following
29 childbirth.

8 (c) Make recommendations that will expand the provision of
9 palliative care.

11 measures for reimbursement of the services.
12 (e) Develop key program metrics for palliative care services

15 expand high-quality palliative care services.

17 access to palliative care and to improve an individual's ability to
18 make informed decisions on preferred care.

20 provide palliative care services.

24 option.

9 Sec. 1605. The protected income level for Medicaid coverage

17 Sec. 1607. (1) The department shall immediately presume that
18 an applicant for Medicaid whose qualifying condition is pregnancy
19 is eligible for Medicaid coverage, unless the preponderance of
20 evidence in the applicant's application indicates otherwise. The
21 applicant who is qualified as described in this subsection is
22 allowed to select or remain with the Medicaid participating
23 obstetrician of the applicant's choice.
24 (2) Each qualifying applicant is entitled to receive all
25 medically necessary obstetrical and prenatal care without
26 preauthorization from a health plan. All claims submitted for
27 payment for obstetrical and prenatal care must be paid at the
28 Medicaid fee-for-service rate if a contract does not exist between
29 the Medicaid participating obstetrical or prenatal care provider
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
479
1 and the managed care plan. The applicant must receive a listing of
2 Medicaid physicians and managed care plans in the immediate
3 vicinity of the applicant's residence.
4 (3) If an applicant, presumed to be eligible for Medicaid
5 under subsection (1), is subsequently found to be ineligible, a
6 Medicaid physician or managed care plan that has been providing
7 pregnancy services to the applicant is entitled to reimbursement
8 for the services until the Medicaid physician or managed care plan
9 is notified by the department that the applicant was found to be
10 ineligible for Medicaid.
11 (4) If the preponderance of evidence in an application under
12 subsection (1) indicates that the applicant is not eligible for
13 Medicaid, the department shall refer the applicant to the nearest
14 public health clinic or similar entity as a potential source for
15 receiving pregnancy-related services.
16 (5) The department shall develop an enrollment process for
17 applicants covered under this section that facilitates the
18 selection of a managed care plan at the time of application.
19 (6) The department shall require that Medicaid managed care
20 plans enroll women whose qualifying condition for Medicaid is
21 pregnancy.
22 (7) The department shall encourage physicians to provide an
23 applicant whose qualifying condition for Medicaid is pregnancy with
24 a referral to a Medicaid participating dentist at the applicant's
25 first pregnancy-related appointment.

24 Sec. 1657. (1) The department shall not make reimbursement for
25 Medicaid to screen and stabilize a Medicaid recipient, including
26 stabilization of a psychiatric crisis, in a hospital emergency
27 room, contingent on obtaining prior authorization from the
28 recipient's HMO. If the recipient is discharged from the emergency
29 room, the hospital shall notify the recipient's HMO within 24 hours
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
489
1 of the diagnosis and treatment received.
2 (2) If the treating hospital determines that the recipient
3 will require further medical service or hospitalization beyond the
4 point of stabilization, that hospital shall receive authorization
5 from the recipient's HMO prior to admitting the recipient.
6 (3) Subsections (1) and (2) do not require an alteration to an
7 existing agreement between an HMO and its contracting hospitals and
8 do not require an HMO to reimburse for services that are not
9 considered to be medically necessary.
10 Sec. 1662. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
11 department shall require an annual external quality review of each
12 contracting HMO. The external quality review must analyze and
13 evaluate aggregated information on quality, timeliness, and access
14 to health care services that the HMO or its contractors furnish to
15 Medicaid beneficiaries. The department shall create a report
16 containing each quality review required under this subsection.
17 (2) The department shall require Medicaid HMOs to provide
18 EPSDT utilization data through the encounter data system, and HEDIS
19 well child health measures in accordance with the National
20 Committee for Quality Assurance prescribed methodology.
21 (3) The department shall submit a copy of the analysis of the
22 Medicaid HMO annual audited reports on HEDIS and the report under
23 subsection (1) to the standard report recipients within 30 days
24 after the department's receipt of the final information required
25 from the contractors.

29 below 212% of the federal poverty level, who are uninsured and have
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

1 not had coverage by other comprehensive health insurance within 6
2 months of applying for MIChild benefits, and who are residents of
3 this state. The department shall develop detailed eligibility

5 services administration public concurrence process. The eligibility
6 criteria must be consistent with the provisions of this part and
7 part 1.
8 (2) The department shall provide up to 1 year of continuous
9 eligibility to a child eligible for the MIChild program unless the
10 child reaches age 19.

28 Sec. 1763. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
29 department shall ensure that the pharmacy and therapeutics
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
494
1 committee and the drug utilization review board account for net
2 costs of individual prescription drugs for this state's Medicaid
3 program before recommending or approving the addition of a drug to
4 this state's single preferred drug list.
5 (2) For the purpose of this section, "net cost" means a cost
6 associated with a pharmaceutical that includes, but is not limited
7 to, all of the following:
8 (a) Wholesale acquisition cost.
9 (b) A cost of a brand name drug with a generic alternative,
10 compared to the lowest-cost generic version of the drug if a
11 maximum allowable cost price has been determined, and the lowest-
12 cost alternative is preferred.
13 (c) A cost associated with federal and supplemental rebates
14 that are available, or anticipated, under 42 USC 1396r-8.
15 (d) Dispensing and administrative costs associated with the
16 provision of a drug.
17 (3) The department shall require that the pharmacy and
18 therapeutics committee and the drug utilization review board
19 document their consideration of cost information, including
20 comparative cost effectiveness among clinically appropriate
21 alternatives, in the meeting materials or minutes supporting each
22 recommendation.
23 (4) A drug must not be included in this state's single
24 preferred drug list unless the pharmacy and therapeutics committee
25 or drug utilization review board have affirmatively determined that
26 the drug's inclusion is both clinically appropriate and cost
27 effective.
28 (5) Not later than February 1 of the current fiscal year, the
29 department shall submit a report to the standard report recipients

495
1 that includes both of the following:

3 under this section.

3 Sec. 1870. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for
4 hospital services and therapy, the department shall allocate
5 $11,400,000.00 in restricted revenue received from a public entity,
6 and any associated federal match, to create new primary care
7 residency slots in underserved communities. The new primary care
8 residency slots must be in 1 of the following specialties: family
9 medicine, general internal medicine, general pediatrics, general
10 OB-GYN, psychiatry, or general surgery.
11 (2) The department shall seek any necessary approvals from CMS
12 to allow the department to implement the program described in this
13 section.
14 (3) Assistance with repayment of medical education loans, loan
15 interest payments, or scholarships provided by the grantee shall be
16 contingent upon a minimum 2-year commitment to practice in an
17 underserved community in this state post-residency and an agreement
18 to forego any sub-specialty training for at least 2 years post-
19 residency with the exception of a child and adolescent psychiatry
20 fellowship that must be integrated with a psychiatry residency
21 training program in a MiDocs consortium affiliated institution.
22 (4) The grantee shall work with the department to integrate
23 the Michigan inpatient psychiatric admissions discussion (MIPAD)
24 recommendations and, when possible, prioritize training
25 opportunities in state psychiatric hospitals and community mental
26 health organizations.
27 (5) The department shall maintain the MiDocs consortium
28 initiative advisory council to help support implementation of the
29 program described in this section, and to provide oversight. The
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
510
1 advisory council must be composed of the MiDocs consortium, the
2 Michigan Area Health Education Centers, the Michigan Primary Care
3 Association, the Michigan Center for Rural Health, the Michigan
4 Academy of Family Physicians, and any other appointees designated
5 by the department.
6 (6) Not later than September 1 of the current fiscal year, the
7 grantee shall submit a report to the standard report recipients
8 that includes all of the following information:
9 (a) Audited financial statement of per-resident costs.
10 (b) Education and clinical quality data.
11 (c) Roster of trainees, including areas of specialty and
12 locations of training.
13 (d) Medicaid revenue by training site.
14 (7) The department shall monitor outcome and performance
15 measures for this program, including, but not limited to, the
16 following:
17 (a) Increasing this state's ability to recruit, train, and
18 retain primary care physicians and other select specialty
19 physicians in underserved communities.
20 (b) Maximizing training opportunities with community health
21 centers, rural critical access hospitals, solo or group private
22 practice physician practices, schools, and other community-based
23 clinics, in addition to the required training through rotations at
24 inpatient hospitals.
25 (c) Increasing the number of residency slots for family
26 medicine, general internal medicine, general pediatrics, general
27 OB-GYN, psychiatry, and general surgery.
28 (8) Unexpended and unencumbered funds up to a maximum of
29 $11,400,000.00 in restricted revenue received from a public entity,

16 Sec. 1880. (1) The department shall align all pharmacy-related
17 policies with the United States Food and Drug Administration
18 quality and clinical standards. Any single preferred drug list
19 utilization management criteria will be established in consultation
20 with the Medicaid health plans and the Michigan pharmacy and
21 therapeutics committee described in section 9705 of the public
22 health code, 1978 PA 368, MCL 333.9705, with consideration given to
23 applicable United States Food and Drug Administration dosing
24 guidelines, subsequent evidence-based literature or studies, and
25 current treatment guidelines.
26 (2) If the department does not participate in a federal
27 pharmaceutical savings program during the current fiscal year, the
28 department shall revise existing pharmacy coverage policies to
29 limit the authorization of anti-obesity GLP-1 receptor agonists
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
514
1 exclusively to individuals classified as class III obesity.
2 Coverage is contingent on documented failure of all other
3 clinically appropriate weight-loss interventions and must be
4 considered only as a measure to avert the need for higher-cost
5 bariatric surgery.
6 (3) The department shall utilize generic and biosimilar
7 pharmaceuticals on this state's preferred drug list for both
8 Medicaid managed care and fee-for-service.

5 Sec. 1965. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for water
6 affordability, the department shall allocate $5,000,000.00 as
7 grants to qualified providers to assist eligible residents who have
8 a financial burden, have accumulated a balance on their water
9 utility bill, have had their water service shut off, and/or are at
10 risk of having their water service shut off. Eligible expenditures
11 from these funds must be income-based and must include all of the
12 following:
13 (a) Restoring residential water service.
14 (b) Paying down water bills currently in arrears.
15 (c) Supporting reasonable water affordability plans that are
16 based on an individual's ability to pay, including capped payments
17 based on household income to prevent accumulating a balance on
18 future water bills and funding to qualified providers to cover the
19 remaining cost of service.
20 (d) Protecting participating residents from water shutoffs.
21 (2) To be considered a qualified provider under this section,
22 the provider must be 1 of the following:
23 (a) A community water system.
24 (b) A community action agency.
25 (c) A nonprofit, community-based organization organized under
26 the laws of this state that is exempt from federal income tax under
27 section 501(c)(3) of the internal revenue code of 1986, 26 USC 501,
28 with a history of doing utility assistance work.
29 (3) Qualified providers receiving grants under this section
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
527
1 may spend not more than 3% of the total grant award for
2 administrative services related to the implementation of this
3 section.
4 (4) Qualified providers receiving grants under this section
5 shall report to the department by September 30 of the current
6 fiscal year on outcomes and performance measures for the program,
7 including, but not limited to, all of the following:
8 (a) The total grant award received by the qualified provider.
9 (b) The percentage of the grant award that was used for
10 administrative costs.
11 (c) The total dollars spent broken down by type of assistance
12 provided.
13 (d) The number of individuals helped broken down by type of
14 assistance provided.
15 (e) The number of individual applicants denied assistance.
16 (5) Upon receipt of the information required under subsection
17 (4), the department shall compile and forward the report to the
18 standard report recipients.
19 (6) The unexpended funds appropriated in part 1 for water
20 affordability are designated as a work project appropriation, and
21 any unencumbered or unallotted funds shall not lapse at the end of
22 the fiscal year and shall be available for expenditures for
23 projects under this section until the projects have been completed.
24 The following is in compliance with section 451a of the management
25 and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1451a:
26 (a) The purpose of the project is to provide grants to
27 qualified providers that assist eligible residents who have a
28 financial burden, have accumulated a balance on their water utility
29 bill, have had their water service shut off, and/or are at risk of
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
528
1 having their water service shut off.
2 (b) The project will be accomplished through competitive
3 grants to qualified providers.
4 (c) The total estimated cost of the project is $5,000,000.00.
5 (d) The tentative completion date is September 30, 2031.

13 $750,000.00 for autism services coordination. The department shall

17 must include, but not be limited to, all of the following:
18 (a) Each contractor shall have an accreditation that attests
19 to the contractor's competency and effectiveness in providing
20 services.
21 (b) Each contractor shall demonstrate cost-effectiveness.
22 (c) Each contractor shall ensure the contractor's ability to
23 leverage private dollars to strengthen and maximize the provision
24 of services.

26 reports to the department on the number of clients served by PIHP
27 region, units of service provision by PIHP region, and ability to
28 meet the contractor's stated goals.

ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

9 Sec. 223. (1) The department shall maximize the utilization of
10 its in-person state workforce. The department shall prioritize
11 occupancy utilization of office space for each division within the
12 department. Employees with job responsibilities that require the
13 employees to serve in their capacities outside of an office shall
14 be monitored each pay period to ensure all work hours reported on
15 the timesheet were actually worked.
16 (2) The department shall comply with requirements set forth by
17 the office of the state employer on in-person work and utilization
18 and occupancy rates of state buildings to ensure in-person work is
19 optimized and occupancy rates are 80% or higher, subject to market
20 conditions.
21 (3) The department shall adhere to civil service rules and
22 regulations that state the standard biweekly work period for a
23 full-time employee in the classified service of this state is the
24 equivalent of 80 hours of work. The department shall establish
25 policies and processes to ensure all employees are working their
26 jobs during agreed-upon business hours.
27 (4) The office of the state employer must create and implement
28 an occupancy utilization uniform policy on occupancy, utilization,
29 and in-person and remote work. The office of the state employer
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
543
1 must make each uniform policy publicly available on the
2 department's website.

6 assigned under its building occupancy agreement and leased office
7 locations and post on their website. Agencies shall develop, in

11 facilities, wherever possible, and comply with space standards

13 unless an exception is approved by the department of technology,

27 Sec. 231. The department shall establish a policy for
28 conducting precontract risk assessments to evaluate contractor
29 financial risk, security risks, and insurance requirements prior to
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
547
1 contract execution. The department shall report back to the
2 standard report recipients by March 31, 2027 on the assessments
3 implemented and used to evaluate contractors, as well as contracts
4 executed under the assessments.
5 Sec. 250. Unless prohibited by law, the department may accept
6 credit card or other electronic means of payment for licenses,
7 fees, or permits. Not later than February 1, the department shall
8 report on fees collected from credit card payments for licenses,
9 fees, and permits in the previous year.
10
11 INSURANCE AND FINANCIAL SERVICES REGULATION
12 Sec. 301. The annual health insurance rate change report
13 prepared pursuant to 45 CFR 154.301(b) shall be transmitted
14 electronically to the standard report recipients and must include
15 the following:
16 (a) The number that are approved by the department.
17 (b) The number of denials issued by the department.
18 (c) The number of objections issued by the department.
19 (d) The percentage of rate filings processed within the
20 applicable statutory time frames.
21 (e) The average number of calendar days to process rate
22 filings.
23 Sec. 302. In addition to the funds appropriated in part 1, the
24 funds collected by the department in connection with a
25 conservatorship under section 32 of the mortgage brokers, lenders,
26 and servicers licensing act, 1987 PA 173, MCL 445.1682, and funds
27 collected by the department from corporations being liquidated
28 under the insurance code of 1956, 1956 PA 218, MCL 500.100 to
29 500.8302, must be appropriated for all expenses necessary to
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
548
1 provide for the required services. Funds are available for
2 expenditure when they are received by the department of treasury
3 and must not lapse to the general fund at the end of the fiscal
4 year. The total amount appropriated under this section and section
5 303 must not exceed $600,000.00.
6 Sec. 303. The department may make available to interested
7 entities customized listings of nonconfidential information in its
8 possession. The department may establish and collect a reasonable
9 charge to provide this service. The revenue from this service is
10 appropriated when received and must be used to offset expenses to
11 provide the service. Any balance of this revenue collected and
12 unexpended at the end of the fiscal year must lapse to the
13 appropriate restricted fund. The total amount appropriated under
14 this section and section 302 must not exceed $600,000.00.
15 Sec. 305. The department shall update examination manuals and
16 letters of guidance to state chartered financial institutions as
17 necessary to reflect how the department will evaluate institutions
18 that provide banking or other financial services to marihuana-
19 related businesses or businesses that transport, test, grow,
20 process, or sell marihuana, based on state statute and guidance.
21 The department may also include guidance or information on how
22 federal law and regulations may impact state-chartered
23 institutions.
24 Sec. 307. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for
25 consumer services and protection, at least 1.0 FTE must provide
26 direct assistance in understanding automobile insurance coverage
27 and identifying available coverage options. Assistance must be
28 provided to households 300% of the federal poverty level or below.
29 The FTE must develop referral partnerships with not fewer than 10
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
549
1 community-based organizations serving low-income households.
2 (2) Not later than September 30, the department shall submit a
3 report to the standard report recipients and the chairpersons of
4 the senate and house standing committees that provides all of the
5 following:
6 (a) The number of consumers assisted.
7 (b) The barriers encountered to obtaining coverage,
8 maintaining coverage, or both.
9 (c) The geographic distribution of need.
10 (d) The partner community organization.
11 (e) The legislative or departmental, or both, recommendations
12 based on barrier patterns identified.
13 (f) Average income level of those assisted.
14 Sec. 308. From the funds appropriated in part 1 for consumer
15 services and protection, the department of insurance and financial
16 services may hire up to 2.0 limited-term FTEs to support PRIRA
17 appeal reviews and consumer complaint reviews and evaluations. As
18 used in this section, "PRIRA" means the patient's right to
19 independent review act, 2000 PA 251, MCL 550.1901 to 550.1929.
20 Sec. 309. From the funds appropriated in part 1 for insurance
21 evaluation, the department of insurance and financial services may
22 hire up to 1.0 limited-term FTE to support data review and
23 analysis.

6 budget office.

12 Sec. 205. (1) The judicial branch shall use the internet to
13 fulfill the reporting requirements of this part and shall make each
14 report readily accessible to the public and conspicuously post each
15 required report in a single archivable location on the judicial
16 branch's website not later than the due date required for each
17 report.

19 fiscal year on the judicial branch's website, the judicial branch
20 shall maintain on its website all reports placed on the website
21 from previous fiscal years posted by fiscal year in the same single
22 archivable location.

24 for the current fiscal year to the standard report recipients and

7 branch within a fiscal year. A post must include the purpose of the
8 expenditure. The judicial branch shall not provide financial
9 information on the public website that would violate a federal or
10 state law, rule, regulation, or guideline that establishes privacy
11 or security standards applicable to that financial information.

1 disorder cases.

23 Sec. 313. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1, the state
24 appellate defender office shall operate the program to ensure this
25 state's compliance with Montgomery v Louisiana, 577 US 190 (2016),
26 People v Parks, 510 Mich 225 (2022), People v Stovall, 510 Mich 301
27 (2022), People v Poole, 349 Mich App 594 (2024), People v
28 Czarnecki, Mich (2025), and People v Taylor, Mich (2025). The
29 purpose of the program is to ensure competent, resourced, and
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
565
1 supervised counsel in cases that involve resentencing individuals
2 who are serving a life sentence for an offense committed when the
3 individuals were 20 years of age or younger.
4 (2) The state appellate defender office shall submit a report
5 not later than March 1 on the number of cases investigated and
6 prepared by the state appellate defender office under subsection
7 (1). The report must include a calculation of the hours spent and
8 the incremental costs associated with the investigation and robust
9 examination of each case.
10 Sec. 314. (1) The funds appropriated in part 1 for Michigan
11 appellate assigned counsel system roster attorney compensation
12 grants must be deposited into the restricted Michigan appellate
13 assigned counsel system attorney compensation fund created in
14 subsection (2).
15 (2) The Michigan appellate assigned counsel system attorney
16 compensation fund is created in the state treasury. The state
17 treasurer may receive money or other assets from any source for
18 deposit into the fund. The state treasurer shall direct the
19 investment of the fund and credit to the fund interest and earnings
20 from fund investments. Unexpended funds at the close of the fiscal
21 year must remain in the fund and shall not lapse to the general
22 fund. The judicial branch shall be the administrator of the fund
23 for auditing purposes. The judicial branch shall expend money from
24 the fund to provide payments to indigent defense systems as
25 provided under section 8a of the appellate defender act, 1978 PA
26 620, MCL 780.718a.
27 (3) All funds available in the Michigan appellate assigned
28 counsel system attorney compensation fund are appropriated and
29 available for expenditure as provided by law.

1
2 ONE-TIME APPROPRIATIONS
3 Sec. 401. From the one-time funds appropriated in part 1 for
4 branchwide appropriations, the judicial branch may do the
5 following:
6 (a) The supreme court may hire 1.0 limited term employee to
7 serve as an editor for the office of reporter of decisions. The
8 editor shall work to reduce the court of appeals backlog of
9 opinions awaiting review and editing.
10 (b) The judicial tenure commission may hire up to 3.0 limited
11 term employees to assist the commission with addressing the
12 judicial complaint backlog.
13 (c) The state appellate defender office may hire up to 14.0
14 limited term employees to support the financial impact of recent
15 supreme court decisions that require resentencing of individuals
16 who were sentenced to life without parole for crimes they committed
17 at 19 or 20 years of age.

18 GENERAL SECTIONS
19 Sec. 201. In accordance with section 30 of article IX of the
20 state constitution of 1963, for the fiscal year ending September
21 30, 2027, total state spending under part 1 from state sources is
22 $578,110,000.00 and total state spending under part 1 from state
23 sources to be paid to local units of government is $34,399,500. The
24 following itemized statement identifies appropriations from which
25 spending to local units of government will occur:
26 DEPARTMENT OF LABOR AND ECONOMIC OPPORTUNITY
27 Arts and cultural program $ 2,940,000
28 At-risk youth grants 5,184,500
