1 Michigan rehabilitation services 275,000
2 Workforce development programs 26,000,000
3 TOTAL $ 34,399,500
4 Sec. 202. The appropriations under this part and part 1 are
5 subject to the management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1101
6 to 18.1594.
7 Sec. 203. As used in this part and part 1:
8 (a) "Department" means the department of labor and economic
9 opportunity and entities contained within its organization,
10 including, but not limited to, the fund.
11 (b) "Director" means the director of the department.
12 (c) "DTMB" means the department of technology, management, and
13 budget.
14 (d) "FTE" means full-time equated.
15 (e) "Fund", unless the context clearly implies a different
16 meaning, means the Michigan strategic fund.
17 (f) "MEDC" means the Michigan economic development
18 corporation, which is the public body corporate created under
19 section 28 of article VII of the state constitution of 1963 and the
20 urban cooperation act of 1967, 1967 (Ex Sess) PA 7, MCL 124.501 to
21 124.512, by contractual interlocal agreement effective April 5,
22 1999, between local participating economic development corporations
23 formed under the economic development corporations act, 1974 PA
24 338, MCL 125.1601 to 125.1636, and the fund.
25 (g) "MEGA" means the Michigan economic growth authority.
26 (h) "MiSTEM" means Michigan science, technology, engineering,
27 and mathematics.
28 (i) "MSHDA" means the Michigan state housing development
29 authority.
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
578
1 (j) "PATH" means Partnership. Accountability. Training. Hope.
2 (k) "Standard report recipients" means the senate and house
3 appropriations committees, the senate and house appropriations
4 subcommittees on labor and economic opportunity, the senate and
5 house fiscal agencies, the senate and house policy offices, and the
6 state budget office.
7 (l) "STEM" means science, technology, engineering, and
8 mathematics.
9 (m) "USDOL" means the United States Department of Labor.
10 Sec. 204. If the state administrative board, acting under
11 section 3 of 1921 PA 2, MCL 17.3, transfers funds from an amount
12 appropriated under part 1, the legislature may, by a concurrent
13 resolution adopted by a majority of the members elected to and
14 serving in each house, intertransfer funds within part 1 for the
15 particular department, board, commission, officer, or institution.
16 Sec. 205. (1) The department shall use the internet to fulfill
17 the reporting requirements of this part and shall make each report
18 readily accessible to the public and conspicuously post each
19 required report in a single archivable location on the department's
20 website not later than the due date required for each report.
21 (2) In addition to placing all reports required in the current
22 fiscal year on the department's website, the department shall
23 maintain on its website all reports placed on the website from
24 previous fiscal years posted by fiscal year in the same single
25 archivable location.
26 (3) The department shall transmit all required reports for the
27 current fiscal year to the standard report recipients and any other
28 required recipients by email. The email shall include a copy of the
29 report and a link to access the report online.
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
579
1 Sec. 206. The department shall receive and retain copies of
2 all reports funded from appropriations in part 1. The department
3 shall follow federal and state law and guidelines for short-term
4 and long-term retention of records. The department may
5 electronically retain copies of reports unless otherwise required
6 by federal and state guidelines.
7 Sec. 207. (1) The department shall cooperate with DTMB to
8 maintain a searchable website accessible by the public at no cost
9 that includes, but is not limited to, all of the following for the
10 department:
11 (a) Fiscal year-to-date expenditures by category.
12 (b) Fiscal year-to-date expenditures by appropriation unit.
13 (c) Fiscal year-to-date payments to a selected vendor,
14 including the vendor name, payment date, payment amount, and
15 payment description.
16 (2) The department shall cooperate with DTMB to update the
17 searchable website on a quarterly basis.
18 Sec. 208. (1) In addition to any other requirements under this
19 part, if the department is authorized under this part to expend
20 funds in addition to those appropriated in part 1, the department
21 must do all of the following:
22 (a) Not later than December 1, provide a report to the
23 chairpersons of the senate and house appropriations committees, the
24 senate and house fiscal agencies, and the state budget office that
25 details all of the following:
26 (i) The type of funding received during the previous fiscal
27 year that was authorized in part 2 of the article that made
28 appropriations for the department in the previous fiscal year.
29 (ii) When the funding was received.
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
580
1 (iii) The amount of funding received.
2 (iv) How much of the funding was spent and for what purpose or
3 purposes.
4 (b) Not later than 60 days after receipt of funds authorized
5 under this part, provide a report to the chairpersons of the senate
6 and house appropriations committees, the senate and house fiscal
7 agencies, and the state budget office that details all of the
8 following:
9 (i) The type of funding received.
10 (ii) When the funding was received.
11 (iii) The amount of funding received.
12 (iv) The anticipated or actual amount to be spent and the
13 specified purpose or purposes.
14 (c) Not later than February 15, provide a report to the
15 chairpersons of the senate and house appropriations committees, the
16 senate and house fiscal agencies, and the state budget office with
17 an estimate of funding authorized by this part that the department
18 anticipates it will receive in the subsequent fiscal year, and
19 identifying all of the following:
20 (i) The type or types of funding anticipated.
21 (ii) The amount or amounts of funding anticipated.
22 (iii) The purpose or purposes of the funding.
23 (2) If another reporting requirement under this part would
24 provide substantially similar information on a substantially
25 similar time frame as would be reported under subsection (1),
26 subsection (1) does not apply.
27 Sec. 209. Not later than December 15, the state budget office
28 shall prepare and submit a report that provides estimates of the
29 total general fund/general purpose appropriation lapses at the
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
581
1 close of the previous fiscal year. The report must summarize the
2 projected year-end general fund/general purpose appropriation
3 lapses by major departmental program or program areas. The state
4 budget office shall submit the report to the standard report
5 recipients and to the chairpersons of the senate and house
6 appropriations committees.
7 Sec. 210. Not later than 14 days after the release of the
8 executive budget recommendation, the department shall cooperate
9 with the state budget office to provide an annual report on
10 estimated state restricted fund balances, state restricted fund
11 projected revenues, and state restricted fund expenditures for the
12 previous 2 fiscal years. The report must be submitted to the
13 standard report recipients and to the chairpersons of the senate
14 and house appropriations committees.
15 Sec. 211. Not later than November 15, the department shall
16 disclose on a publicly accessible website private and other third-
17 party funds received by the department in the previous fiscal year.
18 The report must include the amount of funding received, the
19 specific source of funding received, the purpose for which funding
20 was expended, and the amount of any remaining funds. The report
21 must be submitted to the standard report recipients and to the
22 chairpersons of the senate and house appropriations committees.
23 Sec. 212. Consistent with section 217 of the management and
24 budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1217, the department shall prepare
25 a report on out-of-state travel expenses by not later than January
26 1. The report must list all travel by classified and unclassified
27 employees outside this state in the previous fiscal year that was
28 funded in whole or in part with funds appropriated in the
29 department's budget. The department shall submit the report to the
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
582
1 standard report recipients and to the senate and house
2 appropriations committees. The report must include the following
3 information:
4 (a) The dates of each travel occurrence.
5 (b) The total transportation and related costs of each travel
6 occurrence and the proportions funded with state general
7 fund/general purpose revenues, state restricted revenues, federal
8 revenues, and other revenues.
9 Sec. 213. On a quarterly basis, the department shall report on
10 the number of full-time equated positions in pay status by civil
11 service classification, including a comparison by line item of the
12 number of full-time equated positions authorized from funds
13 appropriated in part 1 to the actual number of full-time equated
14 positions employed by the department at the end of the reporting
15 period. The report must be submitted to the standard report
16 recipients and to the senate and house appropriations committees.
17 Sec. 214. Not later than April 1, the department shall report
18 on each specific policy change made to implement a public act
19 affecting the department that took effect during the previous
20 calendar year. The report must include reference to the public act
21 that necessitates the policy change. The department shall submit
22 the report to the standard report recipients and to the joint
23 committee on administrative rules.
24 Sec. 215. Not later than April 1, the department shall provide
25 to the standard report recipients a copy of its annual strategic
26 plan prepared in compliance with section 363 of the management and
27 budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1363. The plan must include the
28 mission, vision, goals, strategies, and performance measures of the
29 department.
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
583
1 Sec. 216. The department shall report on any court settlement
2 that may require further legislative review of state statutory
3 programs or regulations.
4 Sec. 217. (1) In addition to the funds appropriated in part 1,
5 there is appropriated an amount not to exceed $30,000,000.00 for
6 federal contingency authorization. Amounts appropriated are not
7 available for expenditure until they have been transferred to
8 another line item in part 1 under section 393(2) of the management
9 and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1393.
10 (2) In addition to the funds appropriated in part 1, there is
11 appropriated an amount not to exceed $560,000,000.00 for state
12 restricted contingency authorization. Amounts appropriated are not
13 available for expenditure until they have been transferred to
14 another line item in part 1 under section 393(2) of the management
15 and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1393.
16 (3) In addition to the funds appropriated in part 1, there is
17 appropriated an amount not to exceed $2,000,000.00 for local
18 contingency authorization. Amounts appropriated are not available
19 for expenditure until they have been transferred to another line
20 item in part 1 under section 393(2) of the management and budget
21 act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1393.
22 (4) In addition to the funds appropriated in part 1, there is
23 appropriated an amount not to exceed $11,000,000.00 for private
24 contingency authorization. Amounts appropriated are not available
25 for expenditure until they have been transferred to another line
26 item in part 1 under section 393(2) of the management and budget
27 act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1393.
28 Sec. 218. Total authorized appropriations from all sources
29 under part 1 for legacy costs for the fiscal year ending September
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
584
1 30, 2027, are estimated at $29,919,000.00. From this amount, total
2 appropriations for pension-related legacy costs for the department
3 are estimated at $29,919,000.00. Total appropriations for retiree

5 Sec. 219. To the extent possible, the department shall not
6 expend appropriations under part 1 until all existing authorized
7 work project funds available for the same purposes are exhausted.
8 Sec. 220. Not later than 6 months after the state budget
9 office issues work project letters, and again on or by April 15,
10 the department shall submit a report that summarizes all work
11 project accounts. The report must include all of the following:
12 (a) A list of all work project accounts.
13 (b) The status of all work project accounts, including amounts
14 expended, amounts encumbered, and available balances for each
15 account.
16 (c) The amount of funds that lapsed from any previously
17 designated work project accounts, the name and description of the
18 work project account, and the funds that received the lapsed
19 amounts.
20 Sec. 221. To the extent permissible under section 261 of the
21 management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1261, all of the
22 following apply to the expenditure of funds appropriated in part 1:
23 (a) The funds must not be used for the purchase of foreign
24 goods or services, or both, if competitively priced and of
25 comparable quality American goods or services, or both, are
26 available.
27 (b) Preference must be given to goods or services, or both,
28 manufactured or provided by Michigan businesses, if they are
29 competitively priced and of comparable quality.
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
585
1 (c) Preference must be given to goods or services, or both,
2 that are manufactured or provided by Michigan businesses owned and
3 operated by veterans, if they are competitively priced and of
4 comparable quality.
5 Sec. 222. The department shall not take disciplinary action
6 against an employee of the department because the employee
7 communicates with a member of the legislature or legislative staff
8 unless the communication is prohibited by law and the department is
9 exercising its authority as provided by law.
10 Sec. 223. (1) The department shall maximize utilization of its
11 in-person state workforce. The department shall prioritize
12 occupancy utilization of office space for each division within the
13 department. Employees with job responsibilities that require the
14 employees to serve in their capacities outside of an office must be
15 monitored each pay period to ensure all work hours reported on
16 timesheets were actually worked.
17 (2) The department shall comply with requirements set forth by
18 the office of the state employer on in-person work and utilization
19 and occupancy rates of state buildings to ensure in-person work is
20 optimized and occupancy rates are 80% or higher, subject to market
21 conditions.
22 (3) The department shall adhere to civil service rules and
23 regulations that state the standard biweekly work period for a
24 full-time employee in the classified service of this state is the
25 equivalent of 80 hours of work. The department shall establish
26 policies and processes to ensure all employees are working their
27 jobs during agreed upon business hours.
28 (4) The office of the state employer must create and implement
29 an occupancy utilization uniform policy on occupancy, utilization,
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
586
1 in-person work, and remote work. The office of the state employer
2 must make each uniform policy publicly available on the
3 department's website.
4 Sec. 224. Each agency shall complete a space utilization
5 assessment by July 1, 2027, using a form developed by DTMB, for all
6 space assigned under its building occupancy agreement and leased
7 office locations and post on their website. Agencies shall develop,
8 in coordination with DTMB, a plan to reduce, consolidate, or
9 otherwise optimize assigned space. The plans shall prioritize the
10 use of state-owned facilities, wherever possible, and comply with
11 DTMB-established space standards unless an exception is approved by
12 DTMB.
13 Sec. 225. To the extent permissible under the management and
14 budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1101 to 18.1594, the director shall
15 take all reasonable steps to ensure geographically disadvantaged
16 business enterprises compete for and perform contracts to provide
17 services or supplies, or both. The director shall strongly
18 encourage firms with which the department contracts to subcontract
19 with certified geographically disadvantaged business enterprises
20 for services, supplies, or both. As used in this section,
21 "geographically disadvantaged business enterprises" means that term
22 as defined in Executive Directive No. 2019-8.
23 Sec. 226. Not later than December 31, 2026, and again by June
24 1, 2027, the department must provide a report to the standard
25 report recipients that includes detail regarding any federal
26 guidelines, rules, regulations, or other significant federal policy
27 changes, including H.R. 1 and the Rural Health Transformation
28 Program, that do, or are expected to, significantly impact the
29 operations of the department, including increases or reductions in
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
587
1 federal revenue and changes that are likely to improve or impede
2 the department's ability to safeguard the health or welfare of the
3 public.
4 Sec. 227. (1) Within 30 days after enactment of this act, the
5 senate and house shall provide to the state budget office a jointly
6 agreed upon list of legislatively directed spending items as that
7 term is defined in section 364 of the management and budget act,
8 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1364, funded in part 1. The list must include
9 all information and documents pertaining to the funded items as

11 management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1364 and 18.1364a.

13 budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1364, the department or agency
14 administering the grant shall post a report in a publicly
15 accessible location on its website beginning March 15 of the
16 current fiscal year. The department or agency shall update the
17 report and post an updated report not later than June 15 of the
18 current fiscal year and again not later than September 15 of the
19 current fiscal year. The department shall include in the report the
20 most comprehensive information the department has available at the
21 time of posting for grants awarded.
22 Sec. 228. The state budget director shall take steps to ensure
23 that all state fiscal recovery funds allocated to this state under
24 the American rescue plan act of 2021, Public Law 117-2, are expended
25 by December 31, 2026, as required by law. Any state fiscal recovery
26 funds that would otherwise lapse after September 30, 2026, are
27 automatically reappropriated for the same purpose as originally
28 authorized and available for expenditure through December 31, 2026,
29 and any subsequent financial close out period.

2 Sec. 308. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
3 department shall develop recommendations for employers on best
4 practices to support, attract, and retain women in the workforce.
5 The department may partner with any additional stakeholders the
6 department deems necessary for completing the recommendations,
7 including the department of civil rights, the department of health
8 and human services, and the Michigan women's commission. The
9 recommendations must be published on the department's website not
10 later than January 1, 2027. The recommendations must include, but
11 are not limited to, guidance on all of the following:
12 (a) Postpartum care, including, but not limited to, flexible
13 scheduling, reasonable accommodations, lactation policies, and
14 mental health resources.
15 (b) Menstruation and menopause support, including, but not
16 limited to, counseling or medical resources, symptoms training, and
17 insurance coverage.
18 (c) Fair and transparent pay practices, including, but not
19 limited to, salary disclosures, clear criteria for raises, and
20 employer tools to assess compensation and address wage disparities.

3 Sec. 526. (1) The funds appropriated in part 1 for Michigan

3 Sec. 527. As a condition of receiving funds appropriated under

7 businesses that have received a grant, loan, or other economic

9 125.2001 to 125.2094, that have ceased operations in this state
10 after January 1, 2021. The notice must include all of the

14 economic assistance.

16 assistance received by the business.

19 any portion of the grant, loan, or other economic assistance due
20 under the agreement.
21 (b) The fund must post a list of qualified businesses that are
22 seeking to amend an agreement with the fund. The list must be
23 available on the fund's website and must be updated monthly.
24 (c) The fund must post a list of qualified businesses that
25 have defaulted on the terms of an agreement with the fund. The list
26 must be available on the fund's website and must be updated
27 monthly. The posting must include all of the following information:
28 (i) The terms of the written agreement that have not been met.
29 (ii) The number of created or retained qualified jobs at the
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
607
1 qualified business.

3 the qualified business has received.

5 agreement with the fund, a statement by the fund on the likelihood
6 of recovering any portion of the grant, loan, or other economic
7 assistance due under the agreement.

11 Sec. 905. (1) Funds appropriated in part 1 for centers for

21 state plan for independent living. Services provided should assist
22 people with disabilities to move toward self-sufficiency,
23 including, but not limited to, support for accessing transportation
24 and health care, obtaining employment, community living, nursing
25 home transition, information and referral services, education,
26 youth transition services, veterans, and stigma reduction

1 (2) Not later than March 15 and in partnership with service

4 (3) From the funds appropriated in part 1, Michigan
5 rehabilitation services must enter into and operate under a
6 memorandum of understanding that unambiguously identifies all
7 sources and the exact amounts of general fund/general purpose
8 appropriations and federal funds that Michigan rehabilitation
9 services will provide to the centers for independent living. The
10 memorandum of understanding must clearly state that these funds
11 must be used solely for services that are expressly permitted under
12 federal law and regulation. All activities supported by funds

15 alignment with the state plan for independent living. No funds may
16 be obligated, distributed, or expended for purposes outside the
17 parameters established under the memorandum of understanding.
18 Sec. 906. Federal workforce innovation and opportunity
19 vocational rehabilitation funds from prior years that are received
20 in amounts in addition to those included in part 1 and that have
21 already met state matching requirements are appropriated for the
22 purposes intended. The department may carry forward into the
23 succeeding fiscal year unexpended federal workforce innovation and
24 opportunity vocational rehabilitation funds that do not require
25 additional state matching funds.
26 Sec. 907. (1) The appropriation in part 1 for bureau of
27 services for blind persons includes funds for case services. These
28 funds may be used for tuition payments for blind clients.
29 (2) Revenue collected by the bureau of services for blind
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
626
1 persons and from private and local sources that is unexpended at
2 the end of the fiscal year must carry forward to the subsequent
3 fiscal year.
4 Sec. 908. The bureau of services for blind persons may provide
5 and enter into agreements to provide general services, training,
6 meetings, information, special equipment, software, facility use,
7 and technical consulting services to other principal executive
8 departments, state agencies, local units of government, the
9 judicial branch of government, other organizations, and patrons of
10 department facilities. The department may charge fees for these
11 services that are reasonably related to the cost of providing the
12 services. In addition to the funds appropriated in part 1, funds
13 collected by the department for these services are appropriated for
14 all expenses necessary. The funds appropriated under this section
15 are allotted for expenditure when they are received by the
16 department of treasury.
17 Sec. 909. (1) The funds appropriated in part 1 for a regional
18 or subregional library must not be released until a budget for that
19 regional or subregional library has been approved by the department
20 for expenditures for library services directly serving the blind
21 and persons with disabilities.
22 (2) To receive subregional state aid appropriated in part 1, a
23 regional or subregional library's fiscal agency must agree to
24 maintain local funding support at the same level in the current
25 fiscal year as in the fiscal agency's previous fiscal year. If a
26 reduction in expenditures equally affects all agencies in a local
27 unit of government that includes the regional or subregional
28 library's fiscal agency, the reduction must not be interpreted as a
29 reduction in local support and must not disqualify a regional or
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
627
1 subregional library from receiving state aid under part 1. If a
2 reduction in income affects a library cooperative or district
3 library that includes a regional or subregional library's fiscal
4 agency or a reduction in expenditures for the regional or
5 subregional library's fiscal agency, a reduction in expenditures
6 for the regional or subregional library must not be interpreted as
7 a reduction in local support and must not disqualify a regional or
8 subregional library from receiving state aid under part 1.

18 initiated to promote cooperation with other commissions.

1 has conducted to attract and retain international, advanced degree,

6 ONE-TIME APPROPRIATIONS
7 Sec. 1001. (1) The funds appropriated in part 1 for 23+ high
8 school diploma program must be awarded for a program to assist
9 Michigan residents who are 23 years of age or older in obtaining
10 high school diplomas and placement in career training programs.
11 (2) For purposes of this section, an eligible program provider
12 may be a public, nonprofit, or private accredited diploma-granting
13 institution, but must have not less than 2 years of experience
14 providing dropout recovery services in this state.
15 (3) The department shall issue a request for qualifications
16 for eligible program providers to participate in the program. To be
17 considered a qualified program provider, the institution must offer
18 all of the following:
19 (a) Dropout reengagement services.
20 (b) Academic intake assessments.
21 (c) An integrated learning plan.
22 (d) A course catalog that includes all graduation
23 requirements.
24 (e) Remediation coursework.
25 (f) Academic resilience assessment and intervention.
26 (g) Employability skills development, such as WorkKeys
27 preparation.
28 (h) Industry recognized credentials.
29 (i) Credit for on-the-job training.
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
629
1 (j) A robust support framework, including technology, social
2 support, and academic support.
3 (4) The department shall announce qualified program providers
4 not later than January 1 of the current fiscal year. Qualified
5 program providers must start providing programming by February 1 of
6 the current fiscal year.
7 (5) The department shall reimburse qualified program providers
8 for each month of satisfactory monthly progress as described in
9 section 23a of the state school aid act of 1979, 1979 PA 94, MCL
10 388.1623a, at a rate of $500.00 per month. The following amounts
11 must be paid to a qualified program provider for the completion of
12 any of the following by an eligible participant:
13 (a) $500.00 for the completion of an employability skills
14 program equal to at least 1 unit of high school credit obtained
15 through classroom or online instruction.
16 (b) $250.00 for the attainment of an industry-recognized
17 credential requiring up to 50 hours of training.
18 (c) $500.00 for the attainment of an industry-recognized
19 credential requiring 50 to 100 hours of training.
20 (d) $750.00 for the attainment of an industry-recognized
21 credential requiring more than 100 hours of training.
22 (e) $1,000.00 for the attainment of a high school diploma
23 awarded for satisfying the merit curriculum requirements of this
24 state or an approved personal curriculum, as defined by the
25 department of education of this state.
26 (f) $2,500.00 for placement in a job in an in-demand career
27 pathway.
28 (6) The department shall develop policies and guidelines to
29 implement this section. The department may prioritize funding for
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
630
1 previously qualified program providers based on all of the
2 following criteria:
3 (a) The highest number of students served by a previously
4 qualified program provider during the previous fiscal year.
5 (b) The highest number of graduates in a previously qualified
6 program during the previous fiscal year.
7 (c) The previously qualified program provider's ability to
8 offer statewide access during the previous fiscal year.
9 Sec. 1002. The funds for at-risk youth grants in part 1 must
10 be provided to a nonprofit organization based in this state with
11 demonstrated experience delivering statewide youth career
12 development programming. The organization must operate a career
13 development-focused, multitiered system of supports, align
14 programming with the Michigan career development model, and provide
15 services to both in-school and out-of-school youth across multiple
16 regions of this state. The organization must maintain established
17 partnerships with local education agencies and workforce
18 development boards and possess the organizational capacity to
19 distribute funds statewide. In conjunction with the department, the
20 organization shall utilize a formula-based methodology to allocate
21 funds across all 10 prosperity regions based on regional program
22 performance, capacity, and the concentration of youth facing
23 barriers to employment or postsecondary success.

29 approved training.

632
1 (g) The number of applications received and the number of

16 Sec. 1005. (1) To the extent consistent with sections 7 and 9
17 of the Going pro talent fund act, 2018 PA 260, MCL 408.157 and
18 408.159, the department shall administer the program as follows:
19 (a) The department shall work cooperatively with grantees to
20 maximize the amount of funds from part 1 that are available for
21 direct training.
22 (b) The department, workforce development partners, including
23 regional Michigan works agencies, and employers shall collaborate
24 and work cooperatively to prioritize and streamline the expenditure
25 of the funds appropriated in part 1. The department shall ensure
26 that Going pro provides a collaborative statewide network of
27 workforce and employee skill development partners that addresses
28 the employee talent needs throughout this state.
29 (c) The department shall do all of the following:
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
633
1 (i) Develop program goals and detailed guidance for prospective
2 participants to follow to qualify under the program.
3 (ii) Post the program goals and detailed guidance on the
4 department's website and distribute the program goals and detailed
5 guidance to workforce development partners, including local
6 Michigan works agencies, not later than October 1.
7 (iii) Conduct periodic assessments of employer and employee
8 needs that are evaluated on a regional basis.
9 (iv) Identify solutions and goals to be implemented to satisfy
10 employer and employee needs.
11 (v) Add scoring criteria that incentivize awards to new and
12 diverse program applicants.
13 (d) The department shall use not more than 2% of the total
14 Going pro appropriation for administration of the program.
15 (e) Not less than 5% of available funding must be reserved for
16 businesses in talent fund priority industry sectors that submit
17 competitive applications.
18 (2) The department shall, to the extent possible, distribute
19 the Going pro appropriation to first-time grant recipients and to
20 grant recipients who did not receive an award under the program in
21 the previous fiscal year.

8 Sec. 1007. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for
9 Michigan small business, community growth, and entrepreneurship
10 support, $30,000,000.00 must be used to provide small business
11 development grants, loans, investments, technical assistance,
12 program support, outreach activities, or other economic assistance
13 for activities that support small business growth. Support
14 activities under this section may include any of the following:
15 (a) Assistance to small business support hubs and community
16 incubators.
17 (b) Business development grants and loans to small businesses.
18 (c) Support for community development financial institutions.
19 (d) Grants and loans to small business service providers.
20 (e) Grants or other funding for technical assistance for small
21 businesses.
22 (f) Matching funds for federal grants and programs that
23 benefit small businesses.
24 (g) Grants to hubs that connect Michigan entrepreneurs and
25 small businesses to global markets.
26 (2) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for Michigan small
27 business, community growth, and entrepreneurship support,
28 $28,550,000.00 must be used to provide support for community
29 revitalization, placemaking, business attraction, business
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
656
1 retention, business expansion, redevelopment, and other economic
2 development activities related to growing Michigan's economy.
3 Activities under this section may include any of the following:
4 (a) Michigan community support, which may include the
5 following:
6 (i) Grants, loans, or other economic assistance for
7 redevelopment and revitalization of brownfield sites and
8 rehabilitation of historic resources.
9 (ii) Grants, loans, or other economic assistance for projects
10 that enable population and tax revenue growth through
11 rehabilitation of vacant and blighted buildings and historic
12 structures, rehabilitation and development of vacant properties,
13 and development of permanent place-based infrastructure associated
14 with social zones and traditional downtowns and place-based public
15 spaces.
16 (b) Michigan business growth support, which may include the
17 following:
18 (i) Grants, loans, or other economic assistance for the
19 expansion, retention, and attraction of eligible businesses making
20 capital investments and creating jobs in this state.
21 (ii) Grants, loans, or other economic assistance for political
22 subdivisions of this state, local economic development
23 corporations, or local economic development nonprofit organizations
24 for industrial site development, including community planning
25 activities, technical assistance, and infrastructure improvement or
26 enhancement projects.
27 (3) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for Michigan small
28 business, community growth, and entrepreneurship support,
29 $9,684,500.00 must be used for entrepreneurship and innovation and
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
657
1 may include activities that strengthen this state's entrepreneurial
2 and innovation ecosystem and accelerate the creation, startup,
3 growth, retention, attraction, and commercialization of high-tech,
4 high-growth businesses. Assistance may be provided to
5 entrepreneurs, startups, growth-stage companies, investors,
6 universities, research institutions, nonprofit organizations,
7 economic development organizations, or other entities. Support for
8 entrepreneurship and innovation may include any of the following:
9 (a) Grants, loans, or other economic assistance to accelerate
10 technology transfer from this state's institutions of higher
11 education to the private sector for commercialization.
12 (b) Technical assistance for high-tech startups, early-stage
13 companies, or other entrepreneurs.
14 (c) Matching funds necessary to access federal funding
15 programs.
16 (d) Grants, loans, or other economic assistance to business
17 incubators and accelerators that support high-tech startups, early-
18 stage companies, or other entrepreneurs.
19 Sec. 1008. For the funds appropriated in part 1 for Michigan
20 small business, community growth, and entrepreneurship support, the
21 fund shall report quarterly on the amount of funds considered
22 appropriated, pre-encumbered, encumbered, and expended by current
23 fiscal year appropriation and each business attraction and
24 community revitalization work project for any previous fiscal
25 years. The report must also include a listing of all previous
26 appropriations for business attraction and community
27 revitalization, or a predecessor, that were considered
28 appropriated, pre-encumbered, encumbered, or expended that have
29 lapsed back to the fund for any purpose. The report must also
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
658
1 include information on how much business attraction and community
2 revitalization and Michigan small business, community growth, and
3 entrepreneurship support appropriations were spent on business
4 development, community revitalization, small business supports, or
5 community development financial institutions.
6 Sec. 1009. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for
7 Michigan small business, community growth, and entrepreneurship
8 support, the fund shall identify specific outcomes and performance
9 measures, including, but not limited to, all of the following:
10 (a) The total verified jobs created by the business attraction
11 program during the previous fiscal year.
12 (b) The total private investment obtained through the business
13 attraction and community revitalization programs during the
14 previous fiscal year.
15 (c) The amount of private and public square footage created
16 and reactivated through the community revitalization program during
17 the previous fiscal year.
18 (d) A summary of any metrics used to evaluate the outcomes and
19 performance of the programs.
20 (e) A list of grants to small business and community support
21 hubs and any local nonprofits.
22 (f) The amount of interest generated on loans paid back by
23 entities and the entities that have paid back a loan.
24 (g) A list of community development financial institutions
25 that received grants, the projected economic effect of each
26 project, loans that have been paid back, the default rate, the
27 average interest charged, and interest generated.
28 (h) The number of awards in each county that were funded with
29 business attraction and community revitalization appropriations.
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
659
1 (2) The fund shall submit a report not later than April 10.
2 The report must describe the specific outcomes and measures
3 required in subsection (1) and provide the results and data related
4 to these outcomes and measures for the previous fiscal year if
5 related information is available for the previous fiscal year. The
6 report must also contain a summary of any metrics used to evaluate
7 the outcomes and performance of any programs.
8 Sec. 1010. In addition to the appropriations in part 1, Travel
9 Michigan may receive and expend private revenue related to the use
10 of "Pure Michigan" and all other copyrighted slogans and images.
11 This revenue may come from the direct licensing of the name and
12 image or from the royalty payments from various merchandise sales.
13 Revenue collected is appropriated for the marketing of this state
14 as a travel destination. The funds are available for expenditure
15 when they are received by the department of treasury. If the fund
16 receives revenues from the use of "Pure Michigan", the fund shall
17 provide a report that lists the revenues by source received from
18 the use of "Pure Michigan" and all other copyrighted slogans and
19 images. The report must provide a detailed list of expenditures of
20 revenues received under this section. The report must be provided
21 not later than April 10.
22 Sec. 1011. (1) Funds appropriated in part 1 for Pure Michigan
23 must be used for the following purposes:
24 (a) Conduction of market research regionally, nationally, and
25 internationally for use in marketing campaigns.
26 (b) Production of advertisements that promote Michigan as a
27 travel and tourism destination.
28 (c) Placement of advertisements that have a diverse
29 representation in regional, national, and international marketing
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
660
1 campaigns to promote Michigan as a state that welcomes all
2 individuals and families.
3 (d) Administration of the program.
4 (2) With the goal of increasing travel and tourism in this
5 state, the fund must work in cooperation with local ethnic chambers
6 and nonprofits.
7 (3) The fund may work in cooperation with local units of
8 government, nonprofit entities, and private entities on Pure
9 Michigan promotion campaigns. The fund shall include agreements
10 before undertaking cooperative marketing campaigns.
11 (4) The department shall provide an annual report not later
12 than April 10 on the utilization of funds for eligible activities
13 in subsection (1), including a breakdown by eligible use, efforts
14 taken to broaden the scope of marketing activities to diverse
15 populations, a breakdown of funds spent within this state and
16 outside of this state, and how much was expended on market
17 research.
18 (5) As prescribed by the legislature, funds appropriated to
19 Pure Michigan must be used only for this state to market itself as
20 a travel and tourist destination with the sole purpose of
21 attracting new visitors and retaining former visitors. All of the
22 following apply to marketing under this subsection:
23 (a) Promotion may be made by print, television, radio, and
24 social media.
25 (b) The purpose of the advertisements under subdivision (a)
26 must be to attract tourism and leisure travelers to this state.
27 (c) Advertisements that incorporate the Pure Michigan Byways
28 campaign satisfy the requirement under subdivision (b).
29 (6) Each local visitor bureau can receive a distribution of
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
661
1 funds appropriated to Pure Michigan up to 2 times per fiscal year.
2 Sec. 1012. From the funds appropriated in part 1 for reentry
3 support, $750,000.00 must be allocated toward reentry employment
4 services grants that provide funding to nonprofit organizations
5 that operate a program that satisfies all of the following
6 conditions:
7 (a) The program provides services to parolees and probationers
8 assessed by the department of corrections as moderate or high risk
9 to recidivate.
10 (b) The program provides job readiness training, transitional
11 employment, job coaching and placement, and postplacement retention
12 services. As part of the transitional employment program phase, the
13 nonprofit program must provide low-skill, crew-based services to
14 other state agencies.
15 (c) The program has been independently and rigorously
16 evaluated and shown to reduce recidivism.
17 (d) The program demonstrates an ability to serve multiple
18 jurisdictions across this state.
19 Sec. 1013. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for
20 registered apprenticeships, the department shall maintain an online
21 form that allows individuals interested in receiving information on
22 registered apprenticeships to share their interest with the
23 department. The department shall provide an automated response that
24 the interest form has been received and will be made available to
25 actively hiring registered apprenticeship programs in the region.
26 The department shall make the information of individuals completing
27 the online form available to any entity that receives funding under
28 part 1 for registered apprenticeships, if the registered
29 apprenticeship funding recipient offers programs within 50 miles of
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
662
1 the geographic area. If an entity that receives funding under part
2 1 has not reached capacity for accepting registered apprentices and
3 receives information about an interested party, the entity must
4 attempt to contact the interested party with information on the
5 registered apprenticeship program not more than 15 business days
6 after receiving information from the department.
7 (2) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for registered
8 apprenticeships, the department shall prioritize registered
9 apprenticeship programs that produce the highest job placement and
10 starting wage rates after individuals complete the registered
11 apprenticeship program.
12 Sec. 1014. (1) The funds appropriated in part 1 for talent
13 partnership must be used to administer a grant program to support
14 implementation of transformational community development
15 initiatives.
16 (2) Talent partnership grant funding must be used to implement
17 transformational public space development projects in central city
18 neighborhoods or concentrated districts and leverage
19 interdepartmental and cross-sector coordination through local
20 talent plans that are designed to increase this state's population
21 of young talent by creating high-density, high-amenity, walkable,
22 vibrant-street-life neighborhoods or districts and to create
23 business ownership opportunities for local residents. Qualified
24 plan proposals must include all of the following:
25 (a) The transition of roadway usage from cars to alternative
26 transportation spaces, including, but not limited to, walking,
27 biking, and transit.
28 (b) Commercial corridor activation, including innovations to
29 fill vacant retail space with locally owned businesses.
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
663
1 (c) Mixed-use development that contributes to dense, walkable
2 areas.
3 (d) A plan to do all of the following:
4 (i) Support greater density.
5 (ii) Increase access to affordable or middle-income housing.
6 (iii) Improve direct access to multimodal transportation.
7 (iv) Improve quality of life through increased parks, green
8 spaces, outdoor recreation, and arts and cultural amenities.
9 (3) The legislature finds and declares that the appropriation
10 described in this section is for a public purpose and serves the
11 health, safety, and general welfare of the residents of this state.
12 (4) The department may do any of the following to implement
13 the grant program:
14 (a) Develop guidelines to accept and review local talent plans
15 from eligible applicants and award funding for approved local
16 talent plans to increase this state's population of young talent by
17 creating high-density, high-amenity, walkable, vibrant-street-life
18 neighborhoods or districts and to create business ownership
19 opportunities for local residents.
20 (b) Consult with local stakeholders, provide education and
21 consultation to the public during the application process, and
22 regularly monitor implementation progress of approved local talent
23 plans.
24 (c) Review existing best practices for similar programs and
25 consult with third-party experts, including academic and research
26 institutions based in this state.
27 (d) Lead a multiagency coordination effort to leverage all
28 available resources that will maximize the effectiveness of the
29 initiative. As necessary, the department may establish memoranda of
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
664
1 understanding with other state agencies or establish a committee of
2 state agency representatives to support the initiative.
3 (5) The department shall allocate funding for the grant
4 program through the development and submission of local talent
5 plans from eligible applicants that use a blend of layered,
6 multifaceted activities described in subsection (7) to meet the
7 goals of this section.
8 (6) Eligible applicants for a grant must be a consortium of
9 entities that may include local governments, local economic
10 development organizations, the nonprofit community, and the
11 business community. Consortium applicants must appoint a lead
12 applicant, which may be 1 of the consortium's entities or a
13 nonprofit organization, to serve as fiduciary and project manager
14 for the consortium. An eligible applicant may partner with other
15 government agencies, consortiums, authorities, and community
16 anchors or nonprofit entities to submit a local talent plan to the
17 department and implement an approved local talent plan.
18 Notwithstanding local talent plan revisions or subsequent funding
19 rounds, an eligible applicant shall not submit more than 1 local
20 talent plan. Only grant applicants that provide a minimum of 50%
21 local or private match funds may be considered for a grant under
22 this section.
23 (7) Approved local talent plans must include at least 1 of the
24 following eligible activities to meet the objectives of the grant
25 program:
26 (a) Planning, engineering, permitting review, and other local
27 assessments to support implementation of a local talent plan.
28 (b) Demonstrated community engagement, stakeholder support, or
29 commitment to the local talent plan. As applicable, stakeholders
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
665
1 may include, but are not limited to, any of the following:
2 (i) Neighborhood associations.
3 (ii) City councils, planning committees, or other local
4 government agencies, including public safety agencies.
5 (iii) Economic development organizations or local businesses or
6 business organizations.
7 (iv) Local anchor institutions.
8 (v) Local nonprofits, foundations, or community organizations.
9 (vi) Regional planning organizations or consortiums.
10 (vii) Public transit organizations.
11 (viii) Faith-based organizations.
12 (ix) Tribal governments.
13 (c) Plans for the redevelopment of existing housing stock.
14 (d) Plans to improve utilization of mixed-use and commercial
15 property, including, but not limited to, the conversion of
16 commercial space for affordable housing.
17 (e) Road repairs and other surface improvements that will
18 increase walkability, access to green space, dedicated nonmotorized
19 transportation, and access to rapid transit or high-speed rail.
20 (f) Supports for small businesses and emerging entrepreneurs,
21 including access to credit and professional development.
22 (g) Plans to enhance parks, green space, community recreation,
23 promotion of local artists or art installations, and outdoor social
24 spaces.
25 (h) Activities to pursue other sources of funding to directly
26 support a local talent plan, including other governmental funds or
27 private resources.
28 (8) In addition to the eligible activities under subsection
29 (7), approved local talent plans must include all of the following:
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
666
1 (a) A defined area, such as a neighborhood, district, or
2 corridor, in which a local talent plan will be implemented. Unless
3 identified as a supporting part of an approved talent plan, funds
4 allocated to approved plans must be used only within the plan's
5 defined area.
6 (b) A description of how local talent plan activities directly
7 support the objectives under subsection (2).
8 (c) A description of how funds appropriated under part 1 for
9 talent partnership will be used and any other funds or resources
10 that will be provided to ensure a plan meets all the objectives
11 under subsection (2).
12 (d) Identification of stakeholders that were engaged in the
13 development and are committed to the implementation of a local
14 talent plan.
15 (e) In addition to any state or federal resources,
16 identification of local or private match funds or resources that
17 will be committed.
18 (f) A proposed implementation timeline and demonstration of a
19 local talent plan's sustainability after any state or federal funds
20 are exhausted.
21 (g) Identifiable goals and measurable outcomes to be used to
22 monitor progress.
23 (9) To leverage existing programs and resources to support
24 talent concentration, the department may establish a state agency
25 workgroup to support the implementation of this section, including
26 from agencies that oversee any programs related to housing, home
27 repairs, blight elimination, business supports, community
28 development, transportation or mobility, arts, and outdoor
29 recreation.
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
667
1 (10) The department may retain up to 3% of funds under this
2 section for implementation costs. In addition, the department may
3 retain up to an additional 2% of funds under this section to
4 reimburse other state agencies' activities that are in support of a
5 talent partnership project. The department may contract with at
6 least 1 consultant that is a nonprofit research organization or
7 public education institution based in this state with experience in
8 at least placemaking research to support this section.
9 (11) The department shall post online the guidelines for local
10 talent plans and identify available research or resources that may
11 be used to support the development of a local talent plan.
12 (12) In evaluating each application, subject to subsection
13 (13), the department shall use objective criteria, including, but
14 not limited to, the comprehensive nature of the local talent plan,
15 the local support identified, long-term sustainability, and the
16 likelihood to achieve the goals of the talent partnership program.
17 The department must consider, but is not limited to, all of the
18 following when selecting grant recipients:
19 (a) The extent to which a proposed local talent plan will
20 support the creation and ongoing success of locally owned
21 businesses.
22 (b) The extent to which a proposed local talent plan will
23 create dense, walkable, vibrant spaces.
24 (c) The extent to which zoning and code restrictions have
25 been, or will need to be, modified to support high-density
26 residential development.
27 (d) The extent to which the proposed local talent plan
28 supports facilities and walkways that house or present cultural
29 arts programs, performances, and exhibitions.
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
668
1 (e) The extent to which the proposed local talent plan
2 provides mixed-income housing.
3 (f) The likelihood of successful implementation of a proposed
4 plan and its sustainability.
5 (13) The department shall award funds consistent with the
6 following:
7 (a) Grants must be awarded in a geographically diverse manner.
8 (b) Subject to the department's approval, the grant program
9 permits grant awards to continue implementation of existing talent
10 plans.
11 (14) The department shall publish approved local talent plans
12 and funding allocations under this section on the department's
13 website. The department may approve subsequent rounds of funding
14 for local talent plans if either additional funds become available
15 or there are remaining funds from the appropriation under part 1
16 for talent partnerships. Remaining funds must be awarded in
17 accordance with this section and, as necessary, prorated based on
18 availability of funds.
19 (15) The department shall ensure grant agreements with
20 applicants include regular progress reports and clawback provisions
21 to verify that all expenditures are made in accordance with an
22 approved local talent plan. Applicants receiving funds under this
23 section shall respond to all reasonable information requests from
24 the department related to the funds received under this section.
25 (16) The department shall provide an annual report by
26 September 30 to the standard report recipients on the
27 implementation of this program, including, but not limited to, all
28 of the following:
29 (a) The utilization of funds allocated under this section,
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
669
1 including the amount and status of any funds allocated for approved
2 local talent plans and the amount retained by the department or
3 state agencies to support implementation of this section.
4 (b) As applicable, identification of activities undertaken by
5 agency workgroup participants to communicate the implementation of
6 local talent plans to each of their respective agencies and
7 identification of any existing programs or resources that may be
8 used to support the implementation of a local talent plan.
9 Sec. 1015. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for Pure
10 Michigan, $2,000,000.00 must be used by the fund to promote this
11 state as a destination for special events that are considering 1 or
12 more locations outside of this state with a bid as a determining
13 factor, including, but not limited to, national conventions,
14 national conferences, major sporting events that are not held more
15 than 1 time each year in this state or any other state, including
16 professional sport draft celebrations, all-star games, NCAA
17 postseason tournament games, or other significant events that would
18 feature this state to a national audience with a goal of generating
19 overnight visitation and stays.
20 (2) The fund must develop program guidelines for the use of
21 these funds. Funds may be used to help attract eligible special
22 events and to support costs associated with hosting eligible
23 special events. Eligible special events must have a regular
24 attendance of more than 500 people and meet other eligibility
25 criteria as determined by the fund.
26 (3) Unexpended funds appropriated in part 1 for Pure Michigan
27 are designated as a work project appropriation. Unencumbered or
28 unallotted funds must not lapse at the end of the fiscal year and
29 must be available for expenditure until the project has been
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
670
1 completed. The following is in compliance with section 451a of the
2 management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1451a:
3 (a) The purpose of the project is to promote this state as a
4 destination for special events and to support costs related to
5 hosting eligible special events.
6 (b) The project will be accomplished by utilizing state
7 employees or contracts with service providers, or both.
8 (c) The total estimated cost of the project is $2,000,000.00.
9 (d) The tentative completion date for the work projects is
10 September 30, 2030.

18 Transportation.
19 (e) "EPA" means the United States Environmental Protection
20 Agency.
21 (f) "FOIA" means the freedom of information act, 1976 PA 442,
22 MCL 15.231 to 15.246.
23 (g) "FTE" means full-time equated position in the classified
24 service of this state.
25 (h) "HHS" means the United States Department of Health and
26 Human Services.

29 financial services.

682
1 (k) "PMECSEMA" means pain management education and controlled
2 substances electronic monitoring and antidiversion.

4 appropriations committees, the senate and house appropriations
5 subcommittees on licensing and regulatory affairs and insurance and
6 financial services, the senate and house fiscal agencies, the
7 senate and house policy offices, and the state budget office.
8 Sec. 204. If the state administrative board, acting under
9 section 3 of 1921 PA 2, MCL 17.3, transfers funds from an amount
10 appropriated under part 1, the legislature may, by a concurrent
11 resolution adopted by a majority of the members elected to and
12 serving in each house, intertransfer funds within part 1 for the
13 particular department, board, commission, officer, or institution.
14 Sec. 205. (1) The department shall use the internet to fulfill
15 the reporting requirements of this part and shall make each report
16 readily accessible to the public and conspicuously post each
17 required report in a single archivable location on the department's
18 website not later than the due date required for each report.
19 (2) In addition to placing all reports required in the current
20 fiscal year on the department's website, the department shall
21 maintain on its website all reports placed on the website from
22 previous fiscal years posted by fiscal year in the same single
23 archivable location.
24 (3) The department shall transmit all required reports for the
25 current fiscal year to the standard report recipients and any other
26 required recipients by email. The email shall include a copy of the
27 report and a link to access the report online.
28 Sec. 206. The department shall receive and retain copies of
29 all reports funded from appropriations in part 1. The department
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
683
1 shall follow federal and state law and guidelines for short-term
2 and long-term retention of records. The department may
3 electronically retain copies of reports unless otherwise required
4 by federal and state guidelines.

6 department of technology, management, and budget to maintain a

19 part, if the department is authorized under this part to expend

684
1 (iii) The amount of funding received.

3 purposes.
4 (b) Not later than 60 days after receipt of funds authorized
5 under this part, provide a report to the chairpersons of the house
6 and senate appropriations committees, the house and senate fiscal
7 agencies, and the state budget office that details all of the
8 following:
9 (i) The type of funding received.
10 (ii) When the funding was received.
11 (iii) The amount of funding received.
12 (iv) The anticipated or actual amount to be spent and the
13 specified purpose or purposes.
14 (c) Not later than February 15, provide a report to the
15 chairpersons of the house and senate appropriations committees, the
16 house and senate fiscal agencies, and the state budget office that
17 contains an estimate of funding authorized by this part that the
18 department anticipates it will receive in the subsequent fiscal
19 year, and identifies all of the following:
20 (i) The type or types of funding anticipated.
21 (ii) The amount or amounts of funding anticipated.
22 (iii) The purpose or purposes of the funding.
23 (2) If another reporting requirement under this part would
24 provide substantially similar information on a substantially
25 similar timeframe as would be reported under subsection (1),
26 subsection (1) does not apply.
27 Sec. 209. Not later than December 15, the state budget office

685
1 close of the previous fiscal year. The report must summarize the

5 Sec. 222. The department shall not take disciplinary action
6 against an employee of the department because the employee
7 communicates with a member of the legislature or legislative staff
8 unless the communication is prohibited by law and the department is
9 exercising its authority as provided by law.
10 Sec. 223. (1) The department shall maximize utilization of its
11 in-person state workforce. The department shall prioritize
12 occupancy utilization of office space for each division within the
13 department. Employees with job responsibilities that require
14 employees to serve in their capacities outside of an office shall
15 be monitored each pay period to ensure all work hours reported on
16 timesheets are actually worked.
17 (2) The department shall comply with requirements set forth by
18 the office of the state employer on in-person work and utilization
19 and occupancy rates of state buildings to ensure in-person work is
20 optimized and occupancy rates are 80% or higher, subject to market
21 conditions.
22 (3) The department shall adhere to civil service rules and
23 regulations that state the standard biweekly work period for a
24 full-time employee in the classified service of this state is the
25 equivalent of 80 hours of work. The department shall establish
26 policies and processes to ensure all employees are working their
27 jobs during agreed-upon business hours.
28 (4) The office of the state employer must create and implement
29 an occupancy utilization uniform policy on occupancy, utilization,
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
690
1 and in-person and remote work. The office of the state employer
2 must make each uniform policy publicly available on the
3 department's website.
4 Sec. 224. Each agency shall complete a space utilization
5 assessment by July 1, 2027, using a form developed by the
6 department of technology, management, and budget for all space
7 assigned under its building occupancy agreement and leased office
8 locations and post on their website. Agencies shall develop, in
9 coordination with the department of technology, management, and
10 budget, a plan to reduce, consolidate, or otherwise optimize
11 assigned space. The plans must prioritize the use of state-owned
12 facilities, wherever possible, and comply with department of
13 technology, management, and budget established space standards
14 unless an exception is approved by the department of technology,
15 management, and budget.

17 budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1101 to 18.1594, the director shall

20 services or supplies, or both. The director shall strongly

22 with certified geographically disadvantaged business enterprises
23 for services, supplies, or both. As used in this section,

25 as defined in Executive Directive No. 2019-8.

26 Sec. 301. (1) The public service commission administers the
27 low-income energy assistance grant program on behalf of the
28 Michigan department of health and human services via an interagency
29 agreement. Funds supporting the grant program are appropriated to
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
701
1 the department on the awarding of grants and may be expended for
2 grant payments and administrative-related expenses incurred in the

6 the grants administered, including:
7 (a) Median annual household income for recipients of the
8 grant.
9 (b) Number of grants administered in each county of this
10 state.
11 Sec. 302. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
12 Michigan public service commission shall conduct at least 4 public
13 hearings in this state. Subject to the requirements of this
14 section, if there is a city in this state with a population between
15 195,000 and 700,000 according to the most recent federal decennial
16 census, a public hearing required under this section must be
17 conducted in that city.
18 (2) Not later than September 30, the Michigan public service
19 commission shall submit a report to the standard report recipients
20 that details the outcomes of the public hearings required under
21 this section and summarizes the public comments that were received
22 during the public hearings.
23 Sec. 303. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for the
24 Michigan public service commission, the department shall designate
25 1 FTE to assist consumers with utility issues, utility disruption,
26 or outages.
27 (2) On a monthly basis, the commission shall make the
28 following available on its website:
29 (a) The average electric rates for commission-regulated
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
702
1 electric utilities.
2 (b) A comparison of monthly residential electric bills for
3 commission-regulated electric utilities.

5 regulated electric utilities.
6 (d) The natural gas rates for commission-regulated natural gas
7 utilities.
8 (e) The gas cost recovery factors for commission-regulated
9 natural gas utilities.
10 (f) Monthly reliability data for commission-rate-regulated
11 electric utilities.
12 (g) The number of utility consumers assisted by the commission
13 for utility disruption, outages, and other utility issues.
14 (h) The number of formal complaints received in the month.
15 Sec. 304. (1) The Michigan public service commission shall
16 create a webpage on the commission's website that includes a
17 summary of each rate case currently pending before the commission,
18 as well as a summary of the most recent rate case decided by the

25 residential customer, expressed in both dollars and percentage
26 change, consistent with the information to be provided by utilities

28 in Case No. U-21637.

ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
703
1 system for the specific rate case proceeding.

8 LIQUOR CONTROL COMMISSION
9 Sec. 401. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 from the
10 direct shipper enforcement revolving fund, the liquor control
11 commission shall expend the funds as required under section 203(11)
12 of the Michigan liquor control code of 1998, 1998 PA 58, MCL
13 436.1203, to investigate and audit unlawful direct shipments of
14 wine by unlicensed wineries and retailers, with priority directed
15 toward unlicensed out-of-state retailers and third-party marketers.
16 In addition to other investigative methods, the commission shall
17 use shipping records available to the commission under section
18 203(21) of the Michigan liquor control code of 1998, 1998 PA 58,
19 MCL 436.1203, to assist with the effort to investigate and audit
20 unlawful direct shipments of wine by unlicensed wineries and
21 retailers. The liquor control commission shall refer all identified
22 unlicensed out-of-state retailers and third-party marketers to the
23 attorney general.
24 (2) Not later than February 1, the liquor control commission
25 shall provide a report to the legislature and the standard report
26 recipients that details the commission's activities to investigate
27 and audit the illegal shipping of wine and the results of the
28 activities. The report must include all of the following:
29 (a) Work hours spent, specific actions performed, and the
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
704
1 number of full-time equated positions dedicated to identifying and
2 stopping unlicensed out-of-state retailers, third-party marketers,
3 and wineries that ship illegally in Michigan.
4 (b) General overview of expenditures associated with efforts
5 to identify and stop unlicensed out-of-state retailers, third-party
6 marketers, and wineries that ship illegally in this state.
7 (c) Number of out-of-state entities found to have illegally
8 shipped wine into this state and total number of 750 ml bottles,
9 number of cases with 750 ml bottles, number of liters, number of
10 gallons, or weight of illegally shipped wine. These items must be
11 itemized by total number of retailers and total number of wineries.
12 (d) Suggested areas of focus on how to address direct shipper
13 enforcement and illegal importation in the future.
14 (e) Number of unlicensed out-of-state entities found to have
15 illegally shipped wine into this state that were identified with
16 the shipping records described in subsection (1).
17 (f) Number of notices sent under subsection (3).
18 (3) From the funds appropriated in part 1 from the direct
19 shipper enforcement revolving fund, the liquor control commission
20 shall send a notice to each unlicensed out-of-state entity found to
21 have illegally shipped wine into this state. The notice must
22 include all of the following statements:
23 (a) That shipping wine into this state by unlicensed out-of-
24 state retailers and third-party marketers is illegal, and wineries
25 shipping into this state must obtain a direct shipper license.
26 (b) That under section 909 of the Michigan liquor control code
27 of 1998, 1998 PA 58, MCL 436.1909, making unlawful shipments of
28 wine into this state may be a felony punishable by imprisonment for
29 not more than 4 years or a fine of not more than $5,000.00, or
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
705
1 both.
2 (c) That the matter has been referred to the attorney general.

5 Sec. 504. Revenue collected by the department for the bureau
6 of community and health systems from fees and collections that
7 exceeds the amount appropriated in part 1, the revenue must be
8 carried forward into the subsequent fiscal year. The revenue
9 carried forward under this section must be used as the first source
10 of funds in the subsequent fiscal year.
11 Sec. 505. (1) To defray the costs associated with responding
12 to false final inspection appointments and to discourage the
13 practice of calling for final inspections when a project is
14 incomplete or noncompliant with a plan of correction previously
15 provided by the bureau of fire services, the bureau of fire
16 services may assess a fee of not more than $500.00 for responding
17 to a second or subsequent confirmed false inspection appointment.
18 Fees collected under this section must be deposited into the
19 restricted account described in section 2c of the fire prevention
20 code, 1941 PA 207, MCL 29.2c, and explicitly identified within the
21 statewide integrated governmental management applications system.
22 (2) Not later than September 30, the department shall submit a
23 report to the standard report recipients that provides all of the
24 following:
25 (a) The amount of the fee assessed under subsection (1).
26 (b) The number of fees assessed and issued per region.
27 (c) The cost allocation for the work performed and reduced as
28 a result of this section.
29 (d) Any recommendations for consideration by the legislature.

13 each entity to opt in to a primary user authentication system that

15 the entity.

18 act, 1972 PA 284, MCL 450.1101 to 450.2098, and the nonprofit
19 corporation act, 1982 PA 162, MCL 450.2101 to 450.3192, are enacted
20 that would allow the department to implement a process for entities
21 to opt in to a primary user authentication system, then this
22 section will go into effect.

28 Sec. 801. If Byrne formula grant funding is awarded to the
29 Michigan indigent defense commission created under section 5 of the
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
715
1 Michigan indigent defense commission act, 2013 PA 93, MCL 780.985,
2 the Michigan indigent defense commission may receive and expend
3 Byrne formula grant funds as an interdepartmental grant from the
4 department of state police. The Michigan indigent defense
5 commission may receive and expend federal grant funds from the
6 United States Department of Justice.
7 Sec. 802. From the funds appropriated in part 1, not later
8 than March 1, the Michigan indigent defense commission shall submit
9 a report to the standard report recipients on all of the following
10 information:
11 (a) A detailed list of the total amount approved for each
12 funding unit for the previous fiscal year to implement all approved
13 indigent defense standards. The list must include the amount of
14 state funding and local share for each system.
15 (b) A listing of expenditures by category for the previous
16 fiscal year for each funding unit.
17 (c) A comparison of the total system cost for the previous
18 five fiscal years organized by Michigan indigent defense commission
19 region.
20 (d) A description of each approved standard and how compliance
21 was assessed. This must include data associated with the assessment
22 such as the number of arraignments with appointed counsel, the
23 percentage of cases with assigned attorneys representing
24 defendants, information about the use of experts and investigators,
25 and the percentage of attorneys meeting with clients within the
26 required timeframe.
27 (e) Identification of any challenges facing the commission.
28 Sec. 803. If Senate Bill No. 81 of the 103rd Legislature or
29 House Bill No. 4070 of the 103rd Legislature are enacted, then the
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
716
1 Michigan indigent defense commission shall utilize available
2 funding to begin the development and implementation of minimum
3 standards for youth defense services in accordance with the
4 Michigan indigent defense commission act, 2013 PA 93, MCL 780.981
5 to 780.1003.
6 Sec. 804. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the Michigan
7 indigent defense commission shall notify the standard report
8 recipients not more than 60 days after the adoption of any new
9 indigent defense standard. The notification must include an
10 estimated cost projection to fund the adopted indigent defense
11 standard for the initial and subsequent fiscal years.
12 Sec. 805. A grant distributed by the Michigan indigent defense
13 commission must not be used by an indigent defense system to
14 support any construction expenses for a new structure. This section
15 does not prohibit expenditures for renovations to existing
16 structures, if such a renovation is part of an indigent criminal
17 defense system's approved compliance plan.

21 marihuana operation and oversight grants, the department shall
22 expend the funds for grants to counties for education and outreach
23 programs that relate to the Michigan medical marihuana program and
24 the adult-use marihuana program, in accordance with section 6(l) of
25 the Michigan Medical Marihuana Act, 2008 IL 1, MCL 333.26426, and
26 section 14 of the Michigan Regulation and Taxation of Marihuana
27 Act, 2018 IL 1, MCL 333.27964. The grant funds may be generated
28 from application and license fees authorized under section 8(1)(b)
29 of the Michigan Regulation and Taxation of Marihuana Act, 2018 IL
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
717
1 1, MCL 333.27958. The grants must be distributed proportionately
2 based on the number of registry identification cards issued to or
3 renewed for the residents of each county that applied for a grant
4 under subsection (2). For the purposes of this subsection,
5 operation and oversight grants are for education, communication,
6 and outreach regarding the Michigan Medical Marihuana Act, 2008 IL
7 1, MCL 333.26421 to 333.26430, and the Michigan Regulation and
8 Taxation of Marihuana Act, 2018 IL 1, MCL 333.27951 to 333.27967.
9 Grants provided under this section must not be used for law
10 enforcement purposes.
11 (2) Not later than December 1, the department shall post a
12 listing of potential grant funds available to each county on the
13 department's website. In addition, the department shall work
14 collaboratively with counties regarding the availability of the
15 grant funds. A county that requests grant funds shall apply on a
16 form developed by the department and available on the department's
17 website. The form must contain the county's specific projected plan
18 for use of the grant funds and its agreement to maintain all
19 records and to submit documentation to the department to support
20 the use of the grant funds.
21 (3) To be eligible to receive a grant under subsection (1), a
22 county shall apply not later than January 1 and submit a report to
23 the department not later than September 15 on how the grant was
24 expended. The department shall submit a report to the standard
25 report recipients not later than October 15 of the subsequent
26 fiscal year that details the grant amounts by recipient and the
27 reported uses of the grants in the previous fiscal year and details
28 the calculation for the amount for which each county was eligible.

17 agency shall further develop the program established under section
18 8(1)(j) of the Michigan Regulation and Taxation of Marihuana Act,
19 2018 IL 1, MCL 333.27958, with all of the following goals:
20 (a) To encourage and increase participation in the social
21 equity program, with particular focus to promote and encourage
22 participation in the marihuana industry by people from communities
23 that have been disproportionately impacted by marihuana prohibition
24 and enforcement.
25 (b) To establish a minimum number of licensees that are
26 participating in the social equity program.
27 (c) To consider the area median income of a community in
28 designating communities that have been disproportionately impacted
29 by marihuana prohibition and enforcement.
