25 Sec. 1006. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for
26 renewable energy and electrification infrastructure enhancement and
27 development, except as otherwise provided in subsection (10), funds
28 must be expended only for grants to businesses, nonprofit
29 organizations, and units of local or state government for the
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
721
1 purpose of planning, developing, designing, acquiring, or
2 constructing renewable energy and electrification infrastructure
3 projects.
4 (2) From the total amount of funds for grants awarded under
5 this section for electric vehicle fast-charging infrastructure, to
6 the extent possible, grants should be awarded with a goal of
7 distributing not less than 50% of the total amount for charging
8 ports with 2 connectors that provide charging at a power level of
9 at least 200 kilowatts.
10 (3) The Michigan public service commission shall develop
11 guidelines for the grant program described in subsection (1) and
12 implement an application process for the grant program not later
13 than 6 months after the effective date of this act and must first
14 prioritize and approve grants that meet the goals of the governor's
15 MI healthy climate plan.
16 (4) Applicants for a grant under this section must perform an
17 impact study that includes an analysis of potential cost savings,
18 environmental impacts, and local economic benefits of the
19 applicant's proposed renewable energy and electrification
20 infrastructure project. A utility, at its sole discretion, may
21 prepare a single impact study that covers the utility's service
22 territory that accounts for likely proposals, evaluates regional
23 opportunities, and minimizes or eliminates the need for repetitive
24 studies. Sufficient detail must be provided in the study to allow
25 the Michigan public service commission to evaluate each proposed
26 project, including how the proposed project will align with the
27 governor's MI healthy climate plan.

29 develop RNG infrastructure shall include all of the following
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
722
1 information in the applicant's application:
2 (a) The project details, including the location of biogas and
3 the proposed interconnection.

5 gas conditioning equipment needed to connect to an existing
6 pipeline system.
7 (c) A summary of the environmental and health impacts of the

9 (d) Any local economic impact from the RNG infrastructure
10 development.
11 (e) The end-use application for the RNG infrastructure with a
12 focus on projects being used for opportunities in this state.
13 (6) After receipt of an application under this section, the
14 Michigan public service commission shall allow local units of
15 government, environmental groups, and business interests directly
16 affected by the proposal 45 days to review the application and
17 provide comments. The Michigan public service commission shall
18 allow the applicant not less than 15 days after the comments have
19 been received from interested parties, at the applicant's
20 discretion, to modify or maintain the applicant's initial proposal.
21 (7) The Michigan public service commission shall review all
22 proposals and award grants to applicants it determines have met the
23 criteria in this section. All grants must include full and timely
24 cost recovery from the fund for the infrastructure requirements of
25 the affected utility made necessary by the grant. All grants must
26 be awarded on a competitive basis with priority given to applicants
27 in a distressed area. As used in this section, "distressed area"
28 means any of the following:
29 (a) An area in a city, village, or township that has been
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
723
1 designated as blighted.
2 (b) A city, village, or township with a poverty rate and
3 unemployment rate greater than the statewide average.
4 (c) An area certified as a neighborhood enterprise zone under
5 the neighborhood enterprise zone act, 1992 PA 147, MCL 207.771 to
6 207.786.
7 (d) An area facing a disproportionate environmental burden,
8 using the department of environment, Great Lakes, and energy's
9 MiEJScreen or EPA's EJSCREEN.
10 (8) Not later than 30 days after the completion of the
11 relevant project, grant recipients under this section shall submit
12 a report to the Michigan public service commission that details how
13 the grant money was used.
14 (9) The funds appropriated in part 1 for renewable energy and
15 electrification infrastructure enhancement and development must not
16 be used to expand the use of conventional natural gas.
17 (10) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for renewable
18 energy and electrification infrastructure enhancement and
19 development, the Michigan public service commission shall not use
20 more than $125,000.00, upon awarding of the grants, for
21 administrative-related expenses incurred by the commission for the
22 operation of the grant program. Funds supporting the grant program
23 are appropriated in the Michigan public service commission upon
24 awarding of the grants.
25 (11) As used in this section:
26 (a) "Renewable energy and electrification infrastructure
27 projects" includes, but is not limited to, renewable natural gas
28 facilities and electric vehicle fast-charging infrastructure
29 upgrades on publicly owned land within 1/2 mile of a United States
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
724
1 highway or state trunkline roadway.
2 (b) "Renewable natural gas" or "RNG" means methane derived
3 from organic material and degradable carbon sources, including, but
4 not limited to, carbon sources and materials sourced from municipal
5 solid waste, septage feedstock, plant materials, or food waste.
6 Sec. 1007. (1) From the one-time funds appropriated in part 1
7 for tax tribunal – small claims, the department of licensing and
8 regulatory affairs may hire up to 2.0 limited-term employees to
9 support a backlog in small claims cases within the Michigan Tax
10 Tribunal.
11 (2) Unexpended funds appropriated in part 1 for tax tribunal –
12 small claims are designated as a work project appropriation.
13 Unencumbered or unallotted funds must not lapse at the end of the
14 fiscal year and must be available for expenditures under this
15 section until the project has been completed. The following is in
16 compliance with section 451a of the management and budget act, 1984
17 PA 431, MCL 18.1451a:
18 (a) The purpose of the project is to support a backlog in
19 small claims cases within the Michigan Tax Tribunal.
20 (b) The project will be accomplished by utilizing state
21 employees, contracting with vendors, or both.
22 (c) The total estimated cost of the project is $359,300.00.
23 (d) The estimated completion date is September 30, 2029.
24 Sec. 1008. Funds appropriated in part 1 for compact
25 implementation fees shall be used to pay for dues, IT systems, and
26 associated implementation costs for any interstate licensure
27 compacts of which the state is or becomes a member.

1 (2) The department shall comply with requirements set forth by

3 and occupancy rates of state buildings to ensure in-person work is

7 regulations that state the standard biweekly work period for a

18 OFFICE OF EDUCATION PARTNERSHIPS
19 Sec. 401. From the funds appropriated in part 1 for family and
20 community engagement, the department shall, at a minimum, do all of
21 the following:
22 (a) Establish or partner with family engagement centers across
23 this state to increase parent and guardian involvement in their
24 child's education.
25 (b) Ensure translation and interpretation services are
26 available and implemented pursuant to department guidance.
27 (c) Partner with intermediate school districts to assist in
28 getting information and resources to their constituent districts.
29 (d) Develop an early literacy engagement plan to help parents
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
742
1 or guardians become involved in their child's education.
2
3 OFFICE OF HIGHER EDUCATION
4 Sec. 701. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1, in
5 addition to other statutorily required duties, the department shall
6 do all of the following:
7 (a) Review and evaluate all state financial aid programs. The
8 department shall prioritize improving postsecondary educational
9 outcomes, including student completion rates, and improving
10 affordability of postsecondary programs in this state.
11 (b) Serve as the coordinating office for all agencies of the
12 executive branch of government that are responsible for financial
13 aid programs administered by this state.
14 (c) Survey stakeholders, including public, tribal, and private
15 not-for-profit colleges and universities, state departments and
16 agencies, and statewide postsecondary education associations on
17 student financial aid policy to improve this state's administration
18 of programs.
19 (d) Collaborate with the center for educational performance
20 and information and individual colleges and universities to ensure
21 streamlined and coordinated collection of data analyzing the
22 following:
23 (i) Postsecondary education costs, including a comparison to
24 national and regional averages.
25 (ii) Student enrollment.
26 (iii) Degree completion.
27 (e) Provide access to higher education institutional data
28 inventory on an accessible, public facing dashboard to assist
29 students, prospective students, and their families in making
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
743
1 decisions on postsecondary education.
2 (f) Coordinate with other state agencies and school districts
3 to increase utilization and awareness of postsecondary
4 opportunities, including, but not limited to, early and middle
5 college, dual enrollment, and private skills training scholarships.
6 (g) Promote, track, and provide resources to increase
7 completion of the free application for federal student aid.
8 (2) From the funds appropriated in part 1, the department
9 shall meet, at a minimum, the following transparency requirements:
10 (a) Collect data necessary to complete all statutory reporting
11 requirements. The department shall notify the chairs of the house
12 and senate appropriations committees within 10 days if an entity
13 receiving funds from part 1 fails to comply with data collection
14 requirements.
15 (b) Maintain a link on the department's website to find data
16 submitted by postsecondary institutions through higher education
17 institutional data inventory.
18 (c) Maintain a link on the department's website to the center
19 for educational performance and information's MI School Data page
20 on postsecondary enrollment and completion tracking.
21 (3) As used in this section, "center for educational
22 performance and information" means the center for educational
23 performance and information created in section 94a of the state
24 school aid act of 1979, 1979 PA 94, MCL 388.1694a.
25 Sec. 705. The funds appropriated in part 1 for dual enrollment
26 payments for an eligible student enrolled in a state-approved
27 nonpublic school shall be distributed as provided under the
28 postsecondary enrollment options act, 1996 PA 160, MCL 388.511 to
29 388.524, and the career and technical preparation act, 2000 PA 258,
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
744
1 MCL 388.1901 to 388.1913, in a form and manner as determined by the
2 department.

4 OFFICE OF EARLY CHILDHOOD EDUCATION
5 Sec. 1002. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
6 department shall ensure that the final child development and care
7 provider reimbursement rates are published on the department and
8 Great Start to Quality webpages.
9 (2) In addition to the funds appropriated in part 1, upon
10 receiving approval from the state budget director, the department
11 may receive and expend federal child care development block grant
12 funds that are at risk of lapsing back to the federal government.
13 The department may do this only if all of the following criteria
14 are met:
15 (a) The funds are at risk of lapsing back to the federal
16 government by the end of the current fiscal year.
17 (b) The department plans to expend the funds through a 1-time
18 rate increase to providers.
19 (c) The department makes the request to receive and expend the
20 grant funds to the state budget director not less than 30 days
21 before the expenditure of the funds.
22 (3) If the average cases over a 3-month period in the child
23 development and care program result in the current projected fiscal
24 year caseloads falling below the caseload agreement from the May
25 consensus revenue estimating conference, the department may
26 increase the hourly reimbursement rate to child care providers if
27 the following conditions are met:
28 (a) The level of expenditures for the remainder of the year is
29 estimated to be significantly below the level estimated from the
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
745
1 May consensus revenue estimating conference.
2 (b) The department plans to expend the funds through an
3 ongoing rate increase to providers for the remainder of the fiscal
4 year.
5 (c) The department makes this request to the state budget
6 director not less than 30 days before the expenditure of the funds
7 that includes the rate increase.
8 (4) Upon receiving approval from the state budget director
9 under subsection (2) or (3), the department must notify the senate
10 and house fiscal agencies of the amount being appropriated, the
11 estimated rate increase to providers, and if the rate increase to
12 providers is 1-time or ongoing in nature.
13 (5) The department may withdraw the intent to expend the funds
14 under subsection (2) or (3) by notifying the state budget director
15 in writing.
16 Sec. 1003. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for child
17 development and care contracted services, the department shall
18 create a report on all funding appropriated to contracts for the
19 early childhood comprehensive systems planning by this state during
20 the previous fiscal year. The report required under this section is
21 due by April 1 and must contain at least all of the following
22 information:
23 (a) Total funding appropriated to contracts for the early
24 childhood comprehensive systems planning by this state during the
25 previous fiscal year.
26 (b) The amount of funding for each grant awarded.
27 (c) The grant recipients.
28 (d) The activities funded by each grant.
29 (e) An analysis of each grant recipient's success in
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
746
1 addressing the development of a comprehensive system of early
2 childhood services and supports.
3 (2) All department contracts for early childhood comprehensive
4 systems planning must be bid out through a statewide request-for-
5 proposal process.
6 Sec. 1006. (1) The Early Childhood Investment Fund is created
7 as a restricted fund in the department of treasury. The fund shall
8 only be expended for eligible uses under this section.
9 (2) The state treasurer shall direct investment and credit to
10 the fund all amounts appropriated to the fund and may receive money
11 or other assets from any source for deposit into the fund.
12 (3) Money available in the fund at the close of the fiscal
13 year remains in the fund and does not lapse.
14 (4) The fund shall be utilized to support participating
15 providers, families, children, or early childhood workers, for
16 eligible initiatives that supplement any of the following programs:
17 (a) Child Development and Care program.
18 (b) Early On program.
19 (c) Great Start Readiness Program.
20 (d) Programs for infants and toddlers.
21 (5) Eligible uses include, but are not limited to, one-time or
22 recuring payments to licensed providers for any of the following
23 uses:
24 (a) Increased provider or reimbursements rates.
25 (b) Funding to increase eligibility or the number of available
26 program scholarships or slots, as applicable.
27 (c) Funding that would reduce co-pays, fees, or other costs to
28 families enrolled in eligible programs, as applicable.
29 (d) Grants for infrastructure or equipment, the creation or
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
747
1 expansion of licensed facilities.
2 (e) Provision of education programs, curriculum, or services
3 through licensed providers.
4 (f) Recruitment and retention initiatives for eligible early
5 childhood workers including, but not limited to, training or
6 professional development, supports for career advancement,
7 compensation, or benefits.
8 (g) Other community-based initiatives or pilot programs, that
9 will also leverage non-state funds, for eligible uses under this
10 section.
11 (6) Not later than January 1, the department shall transmit an
12 annual report to the house and senate fiscal agencies and standard
13 recipients on the initiatives funded under this section and the
14 amounts allocated.
15 (7) The department shall administer the fund for auditing
16 purposes.
17 (8) For the 2026-2027 fiscal year only, the eligible uses of
18 the fund are limited to those directly related to the child
19 development and care program listed within subsection (5)(a), (b),
20 (c), (d), and (e).
21 (9) As used in this section, "fund" means the Early Childhood
22 Investment Fund.
23 Sec. 1007. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for child
24 development and care – external support, child development and care
25 contracted services, and child care licensing and regulation, the
26 department shall create a joint report that includes, but is not
27 limited to, the following:
28 (a) The affordability of child care in this state, including,
29 but not limited to, the number of children eligible for and
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
748
1 participating in the child development and care program, the number
2 of children eligible for and participating in the child development
3 and care program for the last 5 years, and key takeaways from the
4 most recent market rate survey.
5 (b) The availability of child care in this state by county,
6 including, but not limited to, the number of licensed child care
7 providers, the change in the number of licensed child care
8 providers and slots over time, and the estimated demand for care.
9 (c) The health and safety of child care, including, but not
10 limited to, the 10 most common rule violations, the number of
11 licenses revoked and summarily suspended, and the number of license
12 violations for incomplete health and safety training and safe sleep
13 training.
14 (d) Any actions taken to strengthen health and safety of care,
15 including, but not limited to, the number of licensing consultants,
16 their average caseload, the number of on-site visits they complete
17 by provider type and region, the types of activities that are
18 intended to improve health and safety in licensed care, and the
19 number of times those activities are performed by licensing
20 consultants.
21 (e) Information on the child care licensing process,
22 including, but not limited to, all of the following:
23 (i) The number of initial applications, initial applications
24 denied, license renewals, and licenses allowed to expire,
25 aggregated by license type.
26 (ii) The average amount of time to approve or deny completed
27 applications and a description of the most common reasons
28 applications are denied.
29 (iii) A description of the types of complaints received, a
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
749
1 description of the process used to resolve complaints, the average
2 amount of time to complete investigations, and the percentage of
3 investigations completed on time.
4 (iv) The number of complaints received, investigated,
5 determined to be unsubstantiated, and that result in disciplinary
6 action or rule violations.
7 (v) The number of administrative hearing adjudications.
8 (f) The quality of child care, including, but not limited to,
9 the number of licensed providers participating in the Great Start
10 to Quality program and the workforce registry, the number of new
11 participants and how participation has changed over the last 5
12 years, and the number of children participating in the child
13 development and care program enrolled in an enhancing quality level
14 or higher program.
15 (g) Any actions taken to improve child care quality,
16 including, but not limited to, the number of quality consultants,
17 the average caseload, the number of on-site visits completed by
18 region, the types of activities that are intended to improve
19 quality and the number of times those activities are performed, and
20 the number of providers that have improved the provider's quality
21 rating since the start of the current fiscal year compared to the
22 same time period in the preceding fiscal year, reported as the
23 number of providers in each region.
24 (h) The child care workforce, including, but not limited to,
25 the number of child care professionals, average wages by role, the
26 number of individuals participating in the TEACH scholarship and
27 earning a credential, and the level of demand for staff.
28 (2) The department must post the joint report on the
29 department website and send the joint report to the state budget
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
750
1 director, the house and senate subcommittees that oversee the
2 department budget, and the house and senate fiscal agencies by
3 April 1 of the current fiscal year reflecting data for the previous
4 fiscal year.
5 Sec. 1008. From the funds appropriated in part 1 for office of
6 early childhood education, the department shall ensure efficient
7 service provision to coordinate services provided to families for
8 home visits, reduce duplication of state services and spending,
9 increase efficiencies including, if a new section 32p is added to
10 the state school aid act of 1979, 1979 PA 94, for the fiscal year
11 ending September 30, 2027, the home visits funded under that
12 section, and work with the DHHS as necessary.
13 Sec. 1009. From the funds appropriated in part 1 for child
14 development and care public assistance, the income entrance
15 eligibility threshold for the child development and care program is
16 set to not more than 200% of the federal poverty guidelines.
17 Sec. 1011. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for child
18 development and care public assistance, for eligible children in
19 the child development and care program, the department shall
20 implement payments to providers based on a child's care services
21 agreement detailing their care schedule and agreed upon hours per
22 week. This shall be done in a manner determined by the department.
23 (2) Providers must complete time and attendance records daily
24 for each approved CDC child in care. Records must include the
25 child's full name, the date, time in, time out, and the parent's
26 initials. Providers must certify that attendance data is correct
27 and affirm that if benefits are overpaid for any reason, the extra
28 payments will be repaid. If intentional misrepresentation caused
29 the overpayment, any and all responsible partners will be
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
751
1 disqualified from the program and will be prosecuted for fraud when
2 appropriate. Historical time and attendance records must be saved
3 for 4 years before they can be destroyed.
4 (3) Providers may only bill for care when children are present
5 or absent and expected to return per a signed care services
6 agreement. If a child is absent for more than 10 consecutive days
7 without a qualifying, documented reason for the absence, the
8 provider must stop billing for that child until the child returns
9 to care. Absence hours are limited to 360 hours per fiscal year or
10 20% of a child's approved hours, whichever is less.
11 Sec. 1012. From the funds appropriated in part 1 for child
12 development and care contracted services, $1,500,000.00 must be for
13 the department to work in collaboration with DHHS to continue the
14 network of infant and early childhood mental health consultation,
15 which provides mental health consultation to child care providers.
16 Sec. 1025. (1) Private revenues received by the department are
17 appropriated upon receipt and are available for expenditure by the
18 department as permitted under state and federal law.
19 (2) Not later than 10 days after the receipt of a private
20 revenues appropriated in subsection (1), the department shall
21 notify the standard report recipients of the receipt of the funds,
22 including source, purpose, and amount.
23 (3) The amount appropriated under subsection (1) must not
24 exceed $3,000,000.00.
25 Sec. 1030. (1) The funds appropriated in part 1 for the tri-
26 share child care program must be awarded for the continuation of
27 the child care facilitator program originally initiated and funded
28 as a pilot project in section 1047(31) of article 5 of 2020 PA 166.
29 (2) The department shall award funds to support regional
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
752
1 recruitment activities and statewide services.
2 (3) The department must create benchmarks for regional
3 recruitment efforts and statewide support services.
4 (4) Any entity receiving funds under this section must be a
5 nonprofit intermediate school district, limited liability company,
6 C-corporation, S-corporation, or a sole proprietor.
7 (5) Funds may be used to enroll in the tri-share child care
8 program families living outside of Michigan but who have a parent
9 or guardian who is employed in Michigan. A child care provider
10 providing care for a family described in this subsection must be
11 located and licensed in Michigan.
12 (6) The department may develop program and administration
13 options to attract Michigan employer program participation.

25 Center for Adult College Success must continue to improve adult
26 postsecondary enrollment and completion under the Michigan

1 $125,000.00 for public outreach to raise awareness among men of

6 $2,000,000.00 to invest in a time and attendance billing system

9 compliance provisions.

5 (iv) The anticipated or actual amount to be spent and the
6 specified purpose or purposes.
7 (c) Not later than February 15, provide a report to the
8 chairpersons of the senate and house appropriations committees, the
9 senate and house fiscal agencies, and the state budget office that
10 contains an estimate of funding authorized by this part that the
11 department anticipates it will receive in the subsequent fiscal
12 year and identifying all of the following:
13 (i) The type or types of funding anticipated.
14 (ii) The amount or amounts of funding anticipated.
15 (iii) The purpose or purposes of the funding.
16 (2) If another reporting requirement under this part would
17 provide substantially similar information on a substantially
18 similar time frame as would be reported under subsection (1),
19 subsection (1) does not apply.
20 Sec. 209. Not later than December 15, the state budget office

24 projected year-end general fund/general purpose appropriation

16 management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1261, all of the
17 following apply to the expenditure of funds appropriated in part 1:
18 (a) The funds must not be used for the purchase of foreign

21 available.

23 manufactured or provided by Michigan businesses, if they are
24 competitively priced and of comparable quality.

29 Sec. 222. The department shall not take disciplinary action
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
766
1 against an employee of the department because the employee
2 communicates with a member of the legislature or legislative staff
3 unless the communication is prohibited by law and the department is

5 Sec. 223. (1) The department shall maximize utilization of its
6 in-person state workforce. The department shall prioritize
7 occupancy utilization of office space for each division within the
8 department. Employees with job responsibilities that require the
9 employees to serve in their capacities outside of an office shall
10 be monitored each pay period to ensure all work hours reported on
11 timesheets were actually worked.
12 (2) The department shall comply with requirements set forth by
13 the office of the state employer on in-person work and utilization
14 and occupancy rates of state buildings to ensure at least 80% of
15 employees are in person and building occupancy and utilization
16 rates are at least 80% or higher, subject to market conditions.
17 (3) The department shall adhere to civil service rules and
18 regulations that state the standard biweekly work period for a
19 full-time employee in the classified service of this state is the
20 equivalent of 80 hours of work. The department shall establish
21 policies and processes to ensure all employees are working their
22 jobs during agreed-upon business hours.
23 (4) The office of the state employer must create and implement
24 an occupancy utilization uniform policy on occupancy, utilization,
25 in-person, and remote work. The office of the state employer must
26 make each uniform policy publicly available on the department's
27 website.
28 Sec. 224. Not later than July 1, the department shall complete
29 a space utilization assessment using a form developed by the
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
767
1 department of technology, management, and budget for all space
2 assigned under its building occupancy agreement and leased office
3 locations and post the assessment on the department's website. The
4 department shall develop, in coordination with the department of
5 technology, management, and budget, a plan to reduce, consolidate,
6 or otherwise optimize assigned space. The plans shall prioritize
7 the use of state-owned facilities, wherever possible, and comply
8 with space standards established by the department of technology,
9 management, and budget unless an exception is approved by the
10 department of technology, management, and budget.

20 National Guard armories and implement a system to measure the

1 relocation or consolidation of existing armories, or a change in

10 Sec. 307. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for
11 Michigan National Guard member benefit fund, the department shall
12 maintain a Michigan National Guard tuition assistance program as
13 provided under the Michigan National Guard tuition assistance act,
14 2014 PA 259, MCL 32.431 to 32.434. The Michigan National Guard
15 tuition assistance program must do all of the following:
16 (a) Bolster military readiness by increasing recruitment and
17 retention of Michigan Army and Air National Guard members.
18 (b) Fill federally authorized strength levels for the state.
19 (c) Improve the Michigan Army and Air National Guard's
20 competitive draw from other military enlistment options in the
21 state.
22 (d) Enhance the ability of the Michigan Army and Air National
23 Guard to compete for guard members and federal dollars with
24 surrounding states.
25 (e) Increase the pool of eligible candidates within the
26 Michigan Army and Air National Guard to become commissioned
27 officers.
28 (2) The department shall make efforts to increase the number
29 of National Guard members who have received a credential or are
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
777
1 still enrolled in the Michigan National Guard tuition assistance
2 program after their initial term of enlistment. To evaluate the
3 effectiveness of the program, the department shall monitor the
4 number of new recruits and new reenlistments and the percentage of
5 those who become participants in the program to determine whether
6 the percentage of authorized Michigan Army and Air National Guard
7 strength obtained and retained is competitive in comparison with
8 the neighboring army and air national guards from Illinois,
9 Indiana, Ohio, and Wisconsin.
10 (3) Not later than March 1, the department shall provide a
11 report on the Michigan National Guard tuition assistance program.
12 The report must include, but is not limited to, the following
13 information for the previous fiscal year:
14 (a) The number of guard members, spouses, children, and
15 dependents that received tuition assistance, broken down by the
16 number of each type of recipient.
17 (b) The educational institutions from which those guard
18 members, spouses, children, and dependents received education or
19 training under the program, broken down by the number of each type
20 of recipient and each type of educational or training program for
21 which tuition assistance was received.
22 (c) The total amount of financial assistance received by each
23 educational institution.
24 (d) The total funds expended on the program for financial
25 assistance for each type of recipient and each type of educational
26 or training program.
27 (e) The total funds expended on the program for administrative
28 costs of the department.
29 (f) For each FTE position appropriated in part 1 for the
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
778
1 Michigan National Guard tuition assistance program, a description
2 of the position's functions, assigned responsibilities, and, if
3 applicable, the length of time that the position has been vacant.
4 (g) The total number of applications for tuition assistance
5 approved and denied.
6 (h) The number of guard members, spouses, children, and
7 dependents receiving tuition assistance who successfully completed
8 an educational or training program for which tuition assistance was
9 received.
10 (i) A description of each educational or training program
11 offered through the Michigan National Guard tuition assistance

13 (j) A list of any educational institutions and training

15 removal.
16 (k) An explanation of any identified barriers to the

6 deposited in the fund.

8 in the fund and does not lapse to the general fund.
9 (5) The department is the administrator of the fund for
10 auditing purposes.
11 (6) Money in the fund is available for expenditure for the
12 support of program operations as appropriated in part 1.

4 Sec. 411. (1) The funds appropriated in part 1 for veterans
5 service grants must be deposited into the veterans service fund
6 created in this part. All funds available in the veterans service
7 fund are appropriated and available for expenditure as provided
8 under this section.
9 (2) The MVAA shall establish, administer, and award
10 competitive grants to 1 or more congressionally chartered VSOs or a
11 coalition of VSOs. The MVAA shall award grants to support efforts
12 to connect veterans and their dependents with federal compensation
13 and pension benefits and state veterans' benefits, including
14 emergency grants through the Michigan veterans' trust fund and
15 other local or nonprofit assistance that may be available to
16 veterans and their dependents. The MVAA shall establish a
17 competitive grant process that satisfies the following:
18 (a) Utilizes a service provision model to provide services
19 across the state and can be tracked regionally to ensure that
20 veterans and their dependents in this state, including those within
21 tribal communities, are provided with services, advocacy, and
22 outreach as close to the communities in which they live as
23 possible.
24 (b) Ensures that grantees are providing adequate veteran
25 services and advocacy, through in-person and virtual meetings, that
26 enables the organization to meet performance goals established in
27 the grant agreement.
28 (c) Fosters innovative and transformative approaches and
29 techniques for the grantee to use when providing services,
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
787
1 advocacy, and outreach for veterans and their dependents.
2 (d) Requires grantees to use an MVAA-designated internet-based
3 claims data system to manage caseloads. License fees associated
4 with the claims data system described in this subdivision are
5 considered an allowable expenditure and may be reimbursed with
6 grant funds.
7 (e) Requires grantees, in coordination with the MVAA, to
8 provide services to incarcerated veterans who are within 1 year of
9 their earliest release date.
10 (f) Ensures that each grantee is issued performance goals.
11 (g) Ensures that each grantee expends grant awards as
12 prescribed in the grant agreement.
13 (h) Requires each grantee to report not less than quarterly on
14 all of the following:
15 (i) An accounting for all grant fund expenditures.
16 (ii) The number and type of claims originated and submitted by
17 the grantee to the USDVA.
18 (iii) The number and type of claims originated by an
19 organization other than the grantee and submitted by the grantee to
20 the USDVA.
21 (iv) The services provided to veterans and their dependents.
22 (v) Progress in achieving monthly performance benchmark goals.
23 (i) Ensures that each grantee is issued monthly performance
24 benchmark goals that each grantee must aim to achieve and require
25 each grantee to report to the MVAA, in order to ensure that
26 benchmark goals are being achieved, or on target to be achieved, in
27 the fiscal year.
28 (3) The MVAA shall do all of the following:
29 (a) Follow all generally accepted accounting principles in
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
788
1 accordance with sections 141 and 485 of the management and budget
2 act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1141 and 18.1485.
3 (b) When establishing, modifying, or amending the competitive
4 grant process described in subsection (1), consult and collaborate
5 with congressionally chartered VSOs in the state, or a coalition of
6 VSOs, and other stakeholders to ensure a comprehensive approach to
7 providing services, advocacy, and outreach to veterans and their
8 dependents.
9 (c) Provide notice to current grantees of any MVAA-proposed
10 modifications or amendments to the competitive grant process and
11 provide those grantees with an opportunity to respond through
12 written communication.
13 (d) Assess the accuracy rate of claims reported by grantees.

16 section 470 of the management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL
17 18.1470.

19 MICHIGAN VETERANS' FACILITY AUTHORITY
20 Sec. 502. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1, the MVH
21 and the MVFA shall provide compassionate and quality nursing care
22 services at each veterans' facility in this state so that resident
23 members can achieve their highest potential of wellness,
24 independence, self-worth, and dignity. The MVFA and the MVH shall
25 provide nursing care services to veterans in accordance with
26 federal standards and report the results of the annual USDVA and
27 CMS surveys and certification as proof of compliance.
28 (2) Appropriations in part 1 for a veterans' facility shall
29 not be used for any purpose other than expenses related to the
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
793
1 operations of the veterans' facility.
2 Sec. 503. All contractors providing health care services at a
3 veterans' facility shall provide services in a manner that complies
4 with applicable USDVA and CMS regulations for state veterans' homes
5 and skilled nursing facilities, any rules governing the operation
6 of nursing homes licensed in this state, and any training and
7 education requirements associated with staff licensure or
8 certification.
9 Sec. 504. (1) The MVFA shall report and investigate all
10 complaints of abuse or neglect at a veterans' facility in
11 compliance with USDVA and CMS regulations for state veterans' homes
12 and skilled nursing facilities. The MVFA shall report on a
13 bimonthly basis the following information:
14 (a) A description of the process by which resident members and
15 others may file complaints of alleged abuse or neglect at a
16 veterans' facility.
17 (b) Summary statistics on the number and general nature of
18 complaints of abuse or neglect.
19 (c) Summary statistics on the final disposition of complaints
20 of abuse or neglect received.
21 (2) The MVFA shall display in high-traffic areas throughout
22 the veterans' facility the process by which visitors, resident
23 members, and staff of the veterans' facility may register
24 complaints.
25 Sec. 505. The MVH shall do the following regarding member
26 care:
27 (a) Provide board-certified psychiatric care for all resident
28 members with mental health disorders in order to ensure that those
29 resident members receive needed services in a professional and
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
794
1 timely manner.
2 (b) Provide all resident members and staff a safe and secure
3 environment.
4 (c) Ensure that the veterans' facility effectively develops,
5 executes, and monitors all comprehensive care plans in accordance
6 with federal regulations and the veterans' facility's internal
7 policies, with a goal that a comprehensive care plan is fully
8 developed for all resident members.
9 Sec. 506. The MVH shall establish and implement internal
10 controls regarding all of the following:
11 (a) The use and management of food, maintenance, and
12 pharmaceutical and medical supply inventories.
13 (b) Calculating resident member maintenance assessments in
14 order to accurately calculate resident member maintenance
15 assessments for each billing cycle and ensure that all past due
16 resident member maintenance assessments are addressed within 30
17 days.
18 (c) Monetary donations and donated goods.
19 (d) The handling of resident member funds to ensure the
20 release of funds within 15 calendar days upon the resident member
21 leaving the home and to ensure that a representative of a resident
22 member is provided a full accounting of that resident member's
23 funds within 30 calendar days after the death of that resident
24 member.
25 (e) Financial reporting and accounting.

7 Sec. 202. The appropriations under this part and part 1 are
8 subject to the management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1101
9 to 18.1594.
10 Sec. 203. As used in this part and part 1:
11 (a) "Department" means the department of natural resources.
12 (b) "Director" means the director of the department.
13 (c) "FTE" means full-time equated.
14 (d) "IDG" means interdepartmental grant.
15 (e) "Standard report recipients" means the senate and house
16 appropriations committees, the senate and house appropriations
17 subcommittee on agriculture and rural development and natural
18 resources, the senate and house policy offices, and the state
19 budget office.
20 Sec. 204. If the state administrative board, acting under
21 section 3 of 1921 PA 2, MCL 17.3, transfers funds from an amount
22 appropriated under part 1, the legislature may, by a concurrent
23 resolution adopted by a majority of the members elected to and
24 serving in each house, intertransfer funds within part 1 for the
25 particular department, board, commission, officer, or institution.
26 Sec. 205. (1) The department shall use the internet to fulfill
27 the reporting requirements of this part and shall make each report
28 readily accessible to the public and conspicuously post each
29 required report in a single archivable location on the department's
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
812
1 website not later than the due date required for each report.
2 (2) In addition to placing all reports required in the current
3 fiscal year on the department's website, the department shall
4 maintain on its website all reports placed on the website from
5 previous fiscal years posted by fiscal year in the same single
6 archivable location.
7 (3) The department shall transmit all required reports for the
8 current fiscal year to the standard report recipients and any other
9 required recipients by email. The email shall include a copy of the
10 report and a link to access the report online.
11 Sec. 206. The department shall receive and retain copies of
12 all reports funded from appropriations in part 1. The department
13 shall follow federal and state law and guidelines for short-term
14 and long-term retention of records. The department may
15 electronically retain copies of reports unless otherwise required
16 by federal and state guidelines.
17 Sec. 207. (1) The department shall cooperate with the
18 department of technology, management, and budget to maintain a
19 searchable website accessible by the public at no cost that
20 includes, but is not limited to, all of the following for the
21 department:
22 (a) Fiscal year-to-date expenditures by category.
23 (b) Fiscal year-to-date expenditures by appropriation unit.
24 (c) Fiscal year-to-date payments to a selected vendor,
25 including the vendor name, payment date, payment amount, and
26 payment description.
27 (2) The department shall cooperate with the department of
28 technology, management, and budget to update the searchable website
29 on a quarterly basis.

1 Sec. 208. (1) In addition to any other requirements under this
2 part, if the department is authorized under this part to expend
3 funds in addition to those appropriated in part 1, the department
4 must do all of the following:
5 (a) Not later than December 1, provide a report to the
6 chairpersons of the house and senate appropriations committees, the
7 house and senate fiscal agencies, and the state budget office that
8 details all of the following:
9 (i) The type of funding received during the previous fiscal
10 year that was authorized in part 2 of the article that made
11 appropriations for the department in the previous fiscal year.
12 (ii) When the funding was received.
13 (iii) The amount of funding received.
14 (iv) How much of the funding was spent and for what purpose or
15 purposes.
16 (b) Not later than 60 days after receipt of funds authorized
17 under this part, provide a report to the chairpersons of the house
18 and senate appropriations committees, the house and senate fiscal
19 agencies, and the state budget office that details all of the
20 following:
21 (i) The type of funding received.
22 (ii) When the funding was received.
23 (iii) The amount of funding received.
24 (iv) The anticipated or actual amount to be spent and the
25 specified purpose or purposes.
26 (c) Not later than February 15, provide a report to the
27 chairpersons of the house and senate appropriations committees, the
28 house and senate fiscal agencies, and the state budget office with
29 an estimate of funding authorized by this part that the department
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
814
1 anticipates it will receive in the subsequent fiscal year,
2 identifying all of the following:
3 (i) The type or types of funding anticipated.
4 (ii) The amount or amounts of funding anticipated.
5 (iii) The purpose or purposes of the funding.
6 (2) If another reporting requirement under this part would
7 provide substantially similar information on a substantially
8 similar time frame as would be reported under subsection (1),
9 subsection (1) does not apply.
10 Sec. 209. Not later than December 15, the state budget office
11 shall prepare and submit a report that provides estimates of the
12 total general fund/general purpose appropriation lapses at the
13 close of the previous fiscal year. The report must summarize the
14 projected year-end general fund/general purpose appropriation
15 lapses by major departmental program or program areas. The state
16 budget office shall submit the report to the standard report
17 recipients and to the chairpersons of the senate and house
18 appropriations committees.
19 Sec. 210. Not later than 14 days after the release of the
20 executive budget recommendation, the department shall cooperate
21 with the state budget office to provide an annual report on the
22 estimated state restricted fund balances, state restricted fund
23 projected revenues, and state restricted fund expenditures for the
24 previous 2 fiscal years. The state budget office shall submit the
25 report to the standard report recipients and to the chairpersons of
26 the senate and house appropriations committees.
27 Sec. 211. Not later than November 15, the department shall
28 disclose on a publicly accessible website private and other third-
29 party funds received by the department in the previous fiscal year.
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
815
1 The report must include the amount of funding received, the
2 specific source of funding received, the purpose for which funding
3 was expended, and the amount of any remaining funds. The report
4 must be submitted to the standard report recipients and to the
5 chairpersons of the senate and house appropriations committees.
6 Sec. 212. Consistent with section 217 of the management and
7 budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1217, the department shall prepare
8 a report on out-of-state travel expenses by not later than January
9 1. The report must list all travel by classified and unclassified
10 employees outside this state in the previous fiscal year that was
11 funded in whole or in part with funds appropriated in the
12 department's budget. The report must include all of the following
13 information:
14 (a) The dates of each travel occurrence.
15 (b) The total transportation and related costs of each travel
16 occurrence and the proportions funded with state general
17 fund/general purpose revenues, state restricted revenues, federal
18 revenues, and other revenues.
19 Sec. 213. On a quarterly basis, the department shall report on
20 the number of full-time equated positions in pay status by civil
21 service classification, including a comparison by line item of the
22 number of full-time equated positions authorized from funds
23 appropriated in part 1 to the actual number of full-time equated
24 positions employed by the department at the end of the reporting
25 period. The report must be submitted to the standard report
26 recipients and to the senate and house appropriations committees.
27 Sec. 214. Not later than April 1, the department shall report
28 on each specific policy change made to implement a public act
29 affecting the department that took effect during the previous
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
816
1 calendar year. The report must include reference to the public act
2 that necessitates the policy change. The department shall submit
3 the report to the standard report recipients and to the senate and
4 house appropriations committees, and the joint committee on
5 administrative rules.
6 Sec. 215. Not later than April 1, the department shall provide
7 to the standard report recipients a copy of its annual strategic
8 plan prepared in compliance with section 363 of the management and
9 budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1363. The plan must include the
10 mission, vision, goals, strategies, and performance measures of the
11 department.
12 Sec. 216. The department shall report on any court settlement
13 that may require further legislative review of state statutory
14 programs or regulations.
15 Sec. 217. In addition to the funds appropriated in part 1,
16 there is appropriated an amount not to exceed $5,000,000.00 for
17 state restricted contingency authorization. Amounts appropriated
18 are not available for expenditure until they have been transferred
19 to another line item in part 1 under section 393(2) of the
20 management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1393.
21 Sec. 218. Total authorized appropriations from all sources
22 under part 1 for legacy costs for the fiscal year ending September
23 30, 2027 are estimated at $23,478,400.00. From this amount, total
24 department appropriations for pension-related legacy costs are
25 estimated at $23,478,400.00. Total department appropriations for
26 retiree health care legacy costs for the department are estimated
27 at $0.00.
28 Sec. 219. To the extent possible, the department shall not
29 expend appropriations under part 1 until all existing authorized
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
817
1 work project funds available for the same purposes are exhausted.
2 Sec. 220. Not later than 6 months after the state budget
3 office issues work project letters, and again on or by April 15,
4 the department shall submit an annual report that summarizes all
5 work project accounts. The report must include all of the
6 following:
7 (a) A list of all work project accounts.
8 (b) The status of all work project accounts, including amounts
9 expended, amounts encumbered, and available balances for each
10 account.
11 (c) The amount of funds that lapsed from any previously
12 designated work project accounts, the name and description of the
13 work project account, and the funds that received the lapsed
14 amounts.
15 Sec. 221. To the extent permissible under section 261 of the
16 management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1261, all of the
17 following apply to the expenditure of funds appropriated in part 1:
18 (a) The funds must not be used for the purchase of foreign
19 goods or services, or both, if competitively priced and of
20 comparable quality American goods or services, or both, are
21 available.
22 (b) Preference must be given to goods or services, or both,
23 manufactured or provided by Michigan businesses, if they are
24 competitively priced and of comparable quality.
25 (c) Preference must be given to goods or services, or both,
26 that are manufactured or provided by Michigan businesses owned and
27 operated by veterans, if they are competitively priced and of
28 comparable quality.
29 Sec. 222. The department shall not take disciplinary action
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
818
1 against an employee of the department because the employee
2 communicates with a member of the legislature or legislative staff
3 unless the communication is prohibited by law and the department is
4 exercising its authority as provided by law.
5 Sec. 223. (1) The department shall maximize utilization of its
6 in-person state workforce. The department shall prioritize
7 occupancy utilization of office space for each division within the
8 department. Employees with job responsibilities that require the
9 employees to serve in their capacities outside of an office shall
10 be monitored each pay period to ensure all work hours reported on
11 timesheets were actually worked.
12 (2) The department shall comply with requirements set forth by
13 the office of the state employer on in-person work and utilization
14 and occupancy rates of state buildings to ensure in-person work is
15 optimized and occupancy rates are 80% or higher, subject to market
16 conditions.
17 (3) The department shall adhere to civil service rules and
18 regulations that state the standard biweekly work period for a
19 full-time employee in the classified service of this state is the
20 equivalent of 80 hours of work. The department shall establish
21 policies and processes to ensure all employees are working their
22 jobs during agreed upon business hours.
23 (4) The office of the state employer must create and implement
24 an occupancy utilization uniform policy on occupancy, utilization,
25 and in-person and remote work. The office of the state employer
26 must make each uniform policy publicly available on the
27 department's website.
28 Sec. 224. Each agency shall complete a space utilization
29 assessment by July 1, 2027, using a form developed by DTMB, for all
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
819
1 space assigned under its building occupancy agreement and leased
2 office locations and post on their website. Agencies shall develop,
3 in coordination with DTMB, a plan to reduce, consolidate, or
4 otherwise optimize assigned space. The plans shall prioritize the
5 use of state-owned facilities, wherever possible, and comply with
6 DTMB-established space standards unless an exception is approved by
7 DTMB. As used in this section, "DTMB" means the department of
8 technology, management, and budget.
9 Sec. 225. To the extent permissible under the management and
10 budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1101 to 18.1594, the director shall
11 take all reasonable steps to ensure geographically disadvantaged
12 business enterprises compete for and perform contracts to provide
13 services or supplies, or both. The director shall strongly
14 encourage firms with which the department contracts to subcontract
15 with certified geographically disadvantaged business enterprises
16 for services, supplies, or both. As used in this section,
17 "geographically disadvantaged business enterprises" means that term
18 as defined in Executive Directive No. 2019-8.
19 Sec. 226. No later than December 31, 2026, and again by June
20 1, 2027, the department must provide a report to the standard
21 report recipients that includes detail regarding any federal
22 guidelines, rules, regulations, or other significant federal policy
23 changes, including H.R. 1 and the Rural Health Transformation
24 Program, that do, or are expected to, significantly impact the
25 operations of the department, including increases or reductions in
26 federal revenue and changes that are likely to improve or impede
27 the department's ability to safeguard the health or welfare of the
28 public.
29 Sec. 227. (1) Within 30 days of enactment of this act, the
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
820
1 house and senate shall provide to the state budget office a jointly
2 agreed-upon list of legislatively directed spending items as that
3 term is defined in section 364 of the management and budget act,
4 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1364, funded in part 1. The list must include
5 all information and documents pertaining to the funded items as
6 publicly disclosed in accordance with sections 364 and 364a of the
7 management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1364 and 18.1364a.
8 (2) In accordance with section 364(4) of the management and
9 budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1364, the department or agency
10 administering the grant shall post a report in a publicly
11 accessible location on its website beginning March 15 of the
12 current fiscal year. The department or agency shall update the
13 report and shall post an updated report not later than June 15 of
14 the current fiscal year and again not later than September 15 of
15 the current fiscal year. The department shall include in the report
16 the most comprehensive information the department has available at
17 the time of posting for grants awarded.
18 Sec. 228. The state budget director shall take steps to ensure
19 that all state fiscal recovery funds allocated to this state under
20 the American rescue plan act of 2021, Public Law 117-2, are
21 expended by December 31, 2026, as required by law. Any state fiscal
22 recovery funds that would otherwise lapse after September 30, 2026,
23 are automatically reappropriated for the same purpose as originally
24 authorized and available for expenditure through December 31, 2026,
25 and any subsequent financial close out period.
26 Sec. 229. (1) The state budget director shall take steps to
27 ensure that all state fiscal recovery funds allocated to this state
28 under the American rescue plan act of 2021, Public Law 117-2, are
29 expended by December 31, 2026, as required by law. The state budget
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
821
1 director may reallocate appropriated funds for the purpose of fully
2 utilizing state fiscal recovery funds that are in jeopardy of not

4 are not limited to, completed projects coming in under budget or

6 director shall reallocate any of the funds reallocated under this
7 subsection to the programs or purposes specified in this section.
8 Any funds reallocated are unappropriated and immediately
9 reappropriated for the following purposes:

10 Sec. 284. The department shall not prohibit an individual from
11 feeding birds or wildlife within 300 feet of a residence if feed
12 quantity totals less than 2 gallons.
13 Sec. 287. The department shall prioritize right-of-way permits
14 or easements for construction or maintenance of broadband
15 facilities on state land and shall not require a centerline survey
16 as a condition of the road right-of-way permit or easement if the
17 applicant can provide detailed engineering plans and if the
18 broadband facilities are contained completely in the right-of-way.
19 If the broadband provider secures a road right-of-way permit to
20 construct or maintain broadband facilities required by the
21 municipal, county, or state entity that owns or controls the public
22 road, the department shall not require the broadband provider to
23 obtain a permit or easement if the broadband facility is contained
24 completely within the road right-of-way. If installation of
25 broadband facilities cannot be contained completely within the
26 right-of-way and requires placement of the infrastructure on public
27 lands, an easement will be required, and a certified survey may be
28 required. If installation of broadband facilities being placed in
29 the road right-of-way requires utilization of public lands, a
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
826
1 permit may be required.

3 DEPARTMENT SUPPORT SERVICES
4 Sec. 302. The department may charge land acquisition projects
5 appropriated for the fiscal year ending September 30, 2027, and for
6 prior fiscal years, a standard percentage fee to recover actual
7 costs, and may use the revenue derived to fund the land acquisition
8 service charges provided for in part 1.
9 Sec. 303. As appropriated in part 1, the department may charge
10 both application fees and transaction fees related to the exchange
11 or sale of state-owned land or rights in land authorized by part 21
12 of the natural resources and environmental protection act, 1994 PA
13 451, MCL 324.2101 to 324.2165. To the extent consistent with part
14 21, fees shall be set by the director at a rate that allows the
15 department to recover its costs for providing these services.
16 Sec. 304. In addition to the funds appropriated in part 1, the
17 department may receive and expend money from state restricted
18 sources to pay vendor costs associated with administering sales of
19 carbon offset credits.
20 Sec. 305. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 from the
21 snowmobile trail improvement fund and the off-road vehicle trail
22 improvement fund, the department shall expend revenues from those
23 funds for administrative purposes only to the extent that those
24 administrative expenditures directly aid or benefit the statewide
25 snowmobile program or the statewide off-road vehicle program,
26 respectively.
27 (2) Administrative expenditures that directly aid or benefit
28 the statewide snowmobile program or the statewide off-road vehicle
29 program include salaries and fringes for program-specific field and
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
827
1 grant staff, trail inspections, contract and grant management, law
2 enforcement activities directly related to snowmobile or off-road
3 vehicle program operations, marketing and outreach activities
4 directly related to trail signage and public safety, information
5 technology services directly supporting trail operations or groomer
6 or ORV management systems, and accounting functions directly
7 related to the administration and payment of snowmobile or off-road
8 vehicle local grants.
9 (3) The department shall not charge or assess indirect costs,
10 internal service charges, or other administrative overhead to the
11 snowmobile trail improvement fund or the off-road vehicle trail
12 improvement fund except as permitted under subsection (2).
13 (4) Nothing in this section prohibits the expenditure of funds
14 from the snowmobile trail improvement fund or the off-road vehicle
15 trail improvement fund for grooming, equipment, trail development
16 and maintenance, signage, local grants, or any other purpose
17 authorized under part 811 or 821 of the natural resources and
18 environmental protection act, 1994 PA 451, MCL 324.81101 to
19 324.81151 and 324.82101 to 324.82161, respectively.

10 ONE-TIME APPROPRIATIONS
11 Sec. 1201. (1) Funds appropriated in part 1 for whitefish
12 recovery shall be used by the department to support efforts to
13 recover and monitor not more than 3 lake whitefish stocks.
14 (2) Funds appropriated under this section shall be used for
15 activities that include, but are not limited to, spawning and
16 rearing activities and poststocking assessment and evaluation
17 necessary to support lake whitefish stock recovery.
18 (3) The department may contract with or enter into agreements
19 with qualified nonprofit, research-based organizations to implement
20 a lake whitefish stock recovery plan developed by the department.
21 (4) Activities under this section shall be conducted using
22 department-owned hatchery facilities or facilities owned or
23 operated by partners contracted under subsection (3).
24 (5) Funds appropriated under this section shall not be used
25 for operational expenses or for the support of full-time equated
26 positions or permanent state positions. The department may utilize
27 limited-term employees as necessary to carry out the purposes of
28 this section.

25 (a) "AFIS" means the automated fingerprint identification

1 Security.

24 (ii) When the funding was received.

11 Sec. 222. The department shall not take disciplinary action
12 against an employee of the department for communicating with a
13 member of the legislature or legislative staff, unless the
14 communication is prohibited by law and the department is exercising

16 Sec. 223. (1) The department shall maximize utilization of its
17 in-person state workforce. The department shall prioritize
18 occupancy utilization of office space for each division within the
19 department. Employees with job responsibilities that require the
20 employees to serve in their capacities outside of an office shall
21 be monitored each pay period to ensure all work hours reported on
22 the timesheet were actually worked.
23 (2) The department shall comply with requirements set forth by
24 the office of the state employer on in-person work and utilization
25 and occupancy rates of state buildings to ensure in-person work is
26 optimized and occupancy rates are 80% or higher, subject to market
27 conditions.
28 (3) The department shall adhere to civil service rules and
29 regulations that state the standard biweekly work period for a
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
850
1 full-time employee in the classified service of this state is the
2 equivalent of 80.0 hours of work. The department shall establish
3 policies and processes to ensure all employees are working their
4 jobs during agreed-upon business hours.
5 (4) The office of the state employer must create and implement
6 an occupancy utilization uniform policy on occupancy, utilization,
7 and in-person and remote work. The office of the state employer
8 must make each uniform policy publicly available on the
9 department's website.
10 Sec. 224. Each agency shall complete a space utilization
11 assessment by July 1, 2027, using a form developed by the
12 department of technology, management, and budget, for all space
13 assigned under its building occupancy agreement and leased office
14 locations and post on their website. Agencies shall develop, in
15 coordination with the department of technology, management, and
16 budget a plan to reduce, consolidate, or otherwise optimize
17 assigned space. The plans shall prioritize the use of state-owned
18 facilities, wherever possible, and comply with space standards
19 established by the department of technology, management, and budget
20 unless an exception is approved by the department of technology,
21 management, and budget.

11 Sec. 233. The department shall submit a biannual report on the
12 performance metrics cited or information required to be reported in
13 this part, reasons for nonachievement of metric targets, and
14 proposed corrective actions.
15 Sec. 234. (1) It is the intent of the legislature that the
16 department shall take all steps necessary to protect the data and
17 privacy of citizens who are not the focus of a departmental
18 investigation and to protect personal information from unauthorized
19 access or misuse. The protection required under this subsection
20 includes, but is not limited to, all of the following:
21 (a) Requiring vendors or service providers to protect data
22 shared with them.
23 (b) Ensuring that when personal data is collected, but no
24 longer utilized by the department, that reasonable steps be taken
25 to securely destroy records containing personal information when it
26 is to be discarded so that the information is rendered
27 indecipherable and is not sold for marketing or other purposes.
28 (2) The department shall provide written notification to any
29 data subject whose sensitive personal information is accessed or
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
855
1 acquired by an unauthorized person.
2 Sec. 235. (1) The department may accept monetary and
3 nonmonetary gifts, bequests, donations, contributions, or grants
4 from any private or public source to support, in whole or in part,
5 a departmental function or program. The department shall expend or
6 use the gifts, bequests, donations, contributions, or grants
7 accepted under this subsection for the purposes designated by the
8 private or public source, if the purpose is specified.
9 (2) Revenue collected by the department under this section
10 that is unexpended and unencumbered must not lapse to the general
11 fund but must be carried forward to the subsequent fiscal year.
12 (3) Private revenues received under this section that exceed
13 the appropriations in part 1 are appropriated and may be received
14 and expended by the department for the purposes for which the funds
15 are received.

12 DEPARTMENTAL ADMINISTRATION AND SUPPORT
13 Sec. 301. The department shall notify the standard report
14 recipients when it recommends to close or consolidate any state
15 police post. The notification must include a local and state impact
16 study of the proposed post closure or consolidation.
17 Sec. 302. If the department presents a plan to the state
18 employer to privatize, the department shall submit a complete
19 project plan to the standard report recipients. The plan must
20 include the criteria under which the privatization initiative will
21 be evaluated. The evaluation must be completed and submitted to the
22 standard report recipients within 30 months.
23 Sec. 303. (1) When the department provides contractual
24 services to a local unit of government, the department shall be
25 reimbursed for all costs incurred in providing the services.
26 (2) The department shall define service cost models for those
27 services requiring reimbursement.
28 (3) Contractual services provided to an entity other than a
29 local unit of government may be provided by department personnel,
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
857
1 but only on an overtime basis outside the normal work schedule of
2 the personnel. All costs incurred in providing the services are
3 eligible for reimbursement.
4 (4) This section does not apply to services provided to state
5 agencies.
6 (5) Revenues received for contractual or reimbursed services
7 in excess of the appropriations in part 1 are appropriated and may
8 be received and expended by the department for the purposes for
9 which the funds are received.
10 (6) If additional authorization is approved in SIGMA by the
11 state budget office under this section, the department shall notify
12 the senate and house appropriations subcommittees on state police
13 and the senate and house fiscal agencies within 10 days after the
14 approval. The notification must include the amount and funding
15 source of the additional authorization, the date of its approval,
16 and the projected use of the funds to be expended.
17 Sec. 304. The department may establish and collect fees for
18 publications, videos, conferences, workshops, and related
19 materials. Fees collected under this section must be used to offset
20 expenditures for costs of the publications, videos, workshops,
21 conferences, and related materials. The department shall not
22 collect fees under this section that exceed the cost of the
23 expenditures.
24 Sec. 305. A law enforcement officer funded under part 1 shall
25 not be required to issue a predetermined or specified number of
26 citations for violations of the Michigan vehicle code, 1949 PA 300,
27 MCL 257.1 to 257.923, or of a local ordinance that substantially
28 corresponds to the provisions of the Michigan vehicle code, 1949 PA
29 300, MCL 257.1 to 257.923, including parking or standing
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
858
1 violations. A law enforcement officer's performance evaluation
2 system must not require a predetermined or specified number of
3 citations to be issued.
4 Sec. 306. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
5 department may purchase real property and shall notify the standard
6 report recipients within 10 days of the purchase.

8 LAW ENFORCEMENT SERVICES
9 Sec. 401. (1) The department shall develop and deliver
10 professional, innovative, and quality training that supports the
11 enforcement and public safety efforts of the criminal justice
12 community.
13 (2) The department shall provide performance data, as provided
14 under section 233, for days of training being conducted by the
15 academy.
16 (3) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for training
17 operations, the department may provide or obtain the following
18 training:
19 (a) Training that directly relates to the individual's job
20 description and role within the department.
21 (b) Professional development training.
22 (c) Training that provides the individual with the ability to
23 seek expanded opportunities within the department.
24 (d) Advanced education training.
25 (4) Not later than January 1, the department shall submit a
26 report to the standard report recipients and to the senate and
27 house appropriations committees that includes the following
28 information about the funds appropriated in part 1 for training
29 operations:
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
859
1 (a) The training courses that the department's employees
2 completed.
3 (b) If a training course is developed by the department, a
4 description of that course's curriculum and its purpose.
5 (c) The number of the department's employees who have received
6 and completed training pursuant to this section.
7 (5) The department shall distribute and review course
8 evaluations to ensure that quality training is provided.
9 Sec. 402. (1) In accordance with applicable state and federal
10 laws and regulations, the department shall maintain and ensure
11 compliance with CJIS databases and applications in the support of
12 public safety and law enforcement communities.
13 (2) The department shall improve the accuracy, timeliness, and
14 completeness of criminal history information by conducting a
15 minimum of 30 outreach activities targeted to criminal justice
16 agencies. The department shall report the number of these outreach
17 activities conducted, as provided under section 233.
18 (3) The department shall provide for the compilation of crime
19 statistics consistent with the uniform crime reporting (UCR)
20 program and the national incident-based report system (NIBRS).
21 (4) The department shall provide for the compilation and
22 evaluation of traffic crash reports and the maintenance of the
23 state accident data collection system.
24 (5) The department shall make individual traffic crash reports
25 available for a fee of $15.00 per incident. The department may also
26 sell an extract of electronic traffic crash data for a fee of $0.25
27 per incident, provided that the name, address, and any other
28 personal identifying information have been excluded.
29 (6) By March 1, the department shall submit a report to the
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
860
1 standard report recipients detailing the number of traffic crash
2 reports provided, the amount of revenue collected, and all
3 expenditures incurred for activities under subsection (5) in the
4 preceding fiscal year. The report must include an analysis of
5 whether revenue from department activities under subsection (5) is
6 sufficient to offset all costs incurred for those activities and
7 must provide information regarding any deficit or surplus of
8 revenue.
9 (7) In accordance with applicable state and federal laws and
10 regulations, the department shall provide for the maintenance and
11 dissemination of criminal history records and juvenile records,
12 including to the extent necessary to exchange criminal history
13 records information with the Federal Bureau of Investigation and
14 other states through the interstate identification index, the
15 National Crime Information Center, and other federal CJIS databases
16 and indices.
17 (8) The department shall, in accordance with applicable state
18 and federal laws, provide for the maintenance of records, including
19 criminal history records regarding firearms licensure, as provided
20 under 1927 PA 372, MCL 28.421 to 28.435.
21 (9) The department shall provide information on the number of
22 background checks processed through the internet criminal history
23 access tool (ICHAT), as provided in section 233.
24 (10) The following unexpended and unencumbered revenues
25 deposited into the criminal justice information center service fees
26 must not lapse to the general fund, but must be carried forward
27 into the subsequent fiscal year:
28 (a) Fees for fingerprinting and criminal record checks and

12 Sec. 403. (1) The department shall provide forensic testing
13 and analysis/profiling of DNA evidence to aid in law enforcement
14 investigations in this state.
15 (2) The department shall ensure its ability to maintain
16 accreditation by a federally designated accrediting agency, as
17 provided under 34 USC 12592.
18 (3) The department shall provide forensic science services
19 with an average turnaround time of 55 days, assuming an annual
20 caseload volume commensurate with the average annual caseload
21 received by the forensic science division during the preceding 5
22 fiscal years, and shall work to achieve a goal of a 30-day average
23 turnaround time across all forensic science disciplines.
24 (4) The department shall provide the following data as
25 provided in section 233:
26 (a) The average turnaround time for processing forensic
27 evidence across all disciplines.
28 (b) Forensic laboratory staffing levels, including scientists
29 in training, and vacancies.
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
862
1 (c) The number of backlogged cases in each discipline.
2 Sec. 404. (1) The biometrics and identification division shall
3 maintain and manage the automated biometric identification system,
4 statewide network of agency photographs, and combined offender DNA
5 index system biometric databases.
6 (2) The department shall provide data on the number of 10-
7 print and palm-print submissions to the database, as provided in
8 section 233.
9 (3) The department shall maintain the staffing and resources
10 necessary to have a 28-day average wait time for scheduling a
11 polygraph examination, assuming an annual caseload received
12 commensurate with the average annual caseload received during the
13 preceding 5 fiscal years, with a goal of achieving a 15-day average
14 wait time.
15 (4) If changes are made to the department's protocol for
16 retaining and purging DNA analysis samples and records, the
17 department shall post a copy of the protocol changes on the
18 department's website.
