16 FIELD SERVICES
17 Sec. 601. (1) Department enlisted personnel who are employed
18 to enforce traffic laws as provided in section 629e of the Michigan
19 vehicle code, 1949 PA 300, MCL 257.629e, are not prohibited from
20 responding to crimes in progress or other emergency situations and
21 are responsible for making every effort to protect all residents of
22 this state.
23 (2) The department shall maintain the staffing and resources
24 necessary to continually work to enhance traffic safety throughout
25 this state and shall dedicate a minimum of 455,200 hours to
26 statewide patrol. The department shall work to improve public
27 safety efforts within distressed cities by enhancing data analysis
28 capabilities and identifying crime trends and areas with high
29 occurrence of crime.

868
1 (3) The department shall report on the number of residence
2 checks of registered sex offenders conducted, as provided under
3 section 233.
4 Sec. 602. (1) The department shall identify and apprehend
5 criminals through criminal investigations in this state.
6 (2) The department shall maintain the staffing and resources
7 necessary to provide a comparable number of hours investigating
8 crimes as the average annual number provided during the preceding 5
9 fiscal years.
10 (3) The department shall maintain the staffing and resources
11 necessary to annually meet or exceed a case clearance rate of 62%.
12 (4) The department shall provide training opportunities to
13 local law enforcement partners with the goal of increasing their
14 knowledge of gambling laws, legal issues, opioid-related
15 investigations, and other emerging law enforcement issues.
16 (5) The department shall maintain the staffing and resources
17 necessary to investigate the average annual number of opioid-
18 related investigations conducted by multijurisdictional task forces
19 and hometown security teams during the preceding 5 fiscal years.
20 The department shall work to enhance investigative and drug
21 interdiction efforts by enhancing data analysis capabilities and
22 linking investigations among multijurisdictional task forces and
23 hometown security teams.
24 Sec. 603. (1) The department shall provide protection to this
25 state, its economy, welfare, and vital state-sponsored programs
26 through the prevention and suppression of organized smuggling of
27 untaxed tobacco products in this state, through enforcement of the
28 tobacco products tax act, 1993 PA 327, MCL 205.421 to 205.436, and
29 other laws pertaining to combating criminal activity in this state,
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
869
1 and by maintaining a tobacco tax enforcement unit.
2 (2) The department shall submit an annual report on December 1
3 to the standard report recipients and to the senate and house
4 appropriations subcommittees on general government that details
5 expenditures and activities related to tobacco tax enforcement for
6 the previous fiscal year.
7 Sec. 604. The department shall provide fire investigation
8 training and investigative assistance to public safety agencies in
9 this state.
10 Sec. 606. The department shall make every reasonable effort to
11 provide, upon request, assistance or mutual aid to a law
12 enforcement agency that is actively responding to any of the
13 following:
14 (a) A public safety emergency.
15 (b) Civil unrest or a riot.
16 (c) One or more law enforcement officers in distress.

1 intelligence operations center for homeland security as this

6 public and private sector partners with timely and accurate
7 information regarding critical information key resource threats, as
8 reported to or discovered by the Michigan intelligence operations
9 center for homeland security, and shall increase public awareness

11 communications.

19 process improvement initiatives to effectively utilize staff
20 resources in providing investigatory assistance and evidentiary
21 analysis for law enforcement and criminal justice agencies

23 necessary to complete the average annual casework that the Michigan
24 cyber command center completed during the preceding 5 fiscal years.

2 Sec. 202. The appropriations under this part and part 1 are
3 subject to the management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1101
4 to 18.1594.
5 Sec. 203. As used in this part and part 1:
6 (a) "CTF" means comprehensive transportation fund.
7 (b) "Department" means the state transportation department.
8 (c) "DOT-FHWA" means DOT, Federal Highway Administration.
9 (d) "FTE" means full-time equated.
10 (e) "IDG" means interdepartmental grant.
11 (f) "IIJA" means the infrastructure investment and jobs act,
12 2021, Public Law 117-58.
13 (g) "MTF" means Michigan transportation fund.
14 (h) "SAF" means state aeronautics fund.
15 (i) "Standard report recipients" means the senate and house
16 appropriations committees, the senate and house appropriations
17 subcommittees on transportation, the senate and house fiscal
18 agencies, the senate and house policy offices, and the state budget
19 office.
20 (j) "STF" means state trunkline fund.
21 Sec. 204. If the state administrative board, acting under
22 section 3 of 1921 PA 2, MCL 17.3, transfers funds from an amount
23 appropriated under part 1, the legislature may, by a concurrent
24 resolution adopted by a majority of the members elected to and
25 serving in each house, intertransfer funds within part 1 for the
26 particular department, board, commission, officer, or institution.
27 Sec. 205. (1) The department shall use the internet to fulfill
28 the reporting requirements of this part and shall make each report
29 readily accessible to the public and conspicuously post each
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
890
1 required report in a single archivable location on the department's

3 (2) In addition to placing all reports required in the current
4 fiscal year on the department's website, the department shall
5 maintain on its website all reports placed on the website from
6 previous fiscal years posted by fiscal year in the same single
7 archivable location.
8 (3) The department shall transmit all required reports for the
9 current fiscal year to the standard report recipients and any other
10 required recipients by email. The email shall include a copy of the
11 report and a link to access the report online.

4 Sec. 221. To the extent permissible under section 261 of the
5 management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1261, all of the
6 following apply to the expenditure of funds appropriated in part 1:
7 (a) The funds must not be used for the purchase of foreign
8 goods or services, or both, if competitively priced and of
9 comparable quality American goods or services, or both, are
10 available.
11 (b) Preference must be given to goods or services, or both,
12 manufactured or provided by Michigan businesses, if they are
13 competitively priced and of comparable quality.
14 (c) Preference must be given to goods or services, or both,
15 that are manufactured or provided by Michigan businesses owned and
16 operated by veterans, if they are competitively priced and of
17 comparable quality.
18 Sec. 222. The department shall not take disciplinary action
19 against an employee of the department because the employee
20 communicates with a member of the legislature or legislative staff
21 unless the communication is prohibited by law and the department is
22 exercising its authority as provided by law.
23 Sec. 223. (1) A department or agency shall maximize
24 utilization of its in-person state workforce. A department or
25 agency shall prioritize occupancy utilization of office space for
26 each division within the department or agency. Employees with job
27 responsibilities that require the employees to serve in their
28 capacities outside of an office shall be monitored each pay period
29 to ensure all work hours reported on the timesheet were actually
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
897
1 worked.
2 (2) A department or agency shall comply with requirements set
3 forth by the office of the state employer on in-person work and
4 utilization and occupancy rates of state buildings to ensure in-
5 person work is optimized and occupancy rates are 80% or higher,
6 subject to market conditions.
7 (3) A department or agency shall adhere to civil service rules
8 and regulations that state the standard biweekly work period for a
9 full-time employee in the classified service of this state is the
10 equivalent of 80 hours of work. A department or agency shall
11 establish policies and processes to ensure all employees are
12 working their jobs during agreed-upon business hours.
13 (4) The office of the state employer must create and implement
14 an occupancy utilization uniform policy on occupancy, utilization,
15 in-person, and remote work. The office of the state employer must
16 make each uniform policy publicly available on the department's
17 website.
18 Sec. 224. Each agency shall complete a space utilization
19 assessment by July 1, 2027, using a form developed by the
20 department of technology, management and budget (DTMB), for all
21 space assigned under its building occupancy agreement and leased
22 office locations and post on their website. Agencies shall develop,
23 in coordination with DTMB, a plan to reduce, consolidate, or
24 otherwise optimize assigned space. The plans shall prioritize the
25 use of state-owned facilities, wherever possible, and comply with
26 DTMB-established space standards unless an exception is approved by
27 DTMB.

29 budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1101 to 18.1594, the director of
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
898
1 each department or agency receiving appropriations in part 1 shall

4 services or supplies, or both. The director shall strongly

6 with certified geographically disadvantaged business enterprises
7 for services or supplies, or both. As used in this section,
8 "geographically disadvantaged business enterprises" means that term
9 as defined in Executive Directive No. 2019-8.

9 Sec. 228. The state budget director shall take steps to ensure
10 that all state fiscal recovery funds allocated to this state under
11 the American rescue plan act of 2021, Public Law 117-2, are
12 expended by December 31, 2026, as required by law. Any state fiscal
13 recovery funds that would otherwise lapse after September 30, 2026
14 are automatically reappropriated for the same purpose as originally
15 authorized and available for expenditure through December 31, 2026
16 and any subsequent financial closeout period.
17 Sec. 229. (1) The state budget director shall take steps to
18 ensure that all state fiscal recovery funds allocated to this state
19 under the American rescue plan act of 2021, Public Law 117-2, are
20 expended by December 31, 2026, as required by law. The state budget
21 director may reallocate appropriated funds for the purpose of fully
22 utilizing state fiscal recovery funds that are in jeopardy of not
23 meeting the expenditure deadline for reasons that may include, but
24 are not limited to, completed projects coming in under budget or
25 funds unable to be fully used by subrecipients. The state budget
26 director shall reallocate any of the funds reallocated under this
27 subsection to the programs or purposes specified in this section.
28 Any funds reallocated are unappropriated and immediately
29 reappropriated for the following purposes:
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
900
1 (a) To reclassify general fund/general purpose appropriations

6 safety employees at the department of state police.
7 (2) All applicable guidance, implementation, and reporting
8 provisions of the American rescue plan act of 2021, Public Law 117-
9 2, must be followed for state fiscal recovery funds reallocated and
10 reappropriated under subsection (1).
11 (3) The state budget director shall notify the senate and the
12 house appropriations committees not later than 10 business days
13 after making any reallocations under subsection (1). The
14 notification must include the authorized program under which funds
15 were originally appropriated, the amount of the reallocation, the
16 program, or programs, or purpose, and the department to which the
17 funds are being reallocated under subsection (1), and the amount
18 reallocated to each program or purpose.
19 Sec. 230. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
20 department shall do the following:
21 (a) Report on any amounts of severance pay for a department
22 director, deputy director, or other high-ranking department
23 official not later than 14 days after a severance agreement with
24 the director, deputy director, or official is signed. The name of
25 the director, deputy director, or official and the amount of
26 severance pay must be included in the report required by this
27 subdivision.

29 severance pay remitted to former department employees during the
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
901
1 previous fiscal year and the total number of former department
2 employees that were remitted severance pay during the previous
3 fiscal year.
4 (2) As used in this section, "severance pay" means
5 compensation to which both of the following apply:
6 (a) The compensation is payable or paid upon the termination
7 of employment.
8 (b) The compensation is paid in addition to wages or benefits
9 earned during the course of employment or generally applicable
10 retirement benefits.
11 Sec. 231. The department must establish a policy for
12 conducting precontract risk assessments to evaluate contractor
13 financial risk, security risks, and insurance requirements prior to
14 contract execution. The department must report back to the standard
15 report recipients by March 31, 2027 on the assessments implemented
16 and used to evaluate contractors, as well as contracts executed
17 under the assessments.
18
19 DEPARTMENTAL ADMINISTRATION AND SUPPORT
20 Sec. 301. The department may establish a fee schedule and
21 collect fees sufficient to cover the costs to issue the permits
22 that the department is authorized by law to issue on request,
23 unless otherwise stipulated by law. All permit fees are
24 nonrefundable application fees and must be credited to the
25 appropriate fund to recover the direct and indirect costs of
26 receiving, reviewing, and processing the requests.
27 Sec. 302. Not later than December 31, the department shall
28 submit to the standard report recipients and to the chairpersons of
29 the senate and house of representatives appropriations committees a
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

1 report that includes the estimated available fund balances for all

15 Sec. 303. Bridge bundling projects may be utilized where
16 multiple structures are contiguous or nearly contiguous, or if
17 bundling is required for traffic control, cost efficiency, or
18 federal funding participation. The department shall not utilize
19 bridge bundling contracting methods for projects involving the
20 construction, rehabilitation, or replacement of bridges delivered
21 through the local agency program unless all of the following
22 conditions apply:
23 (a) A local agency program bridge bundling project package is
24 limited to a maximum of 5 bridges.
25 (b) A local agency program bridge bundling project package
26 must be confined to a single department region or limited to
27 contiguous counties.
28 (c) The department shall not impose any bidder qualification
29 requirements beyond standard prequalification. The department shall
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
903
1 not use requests for qualifications, statements of qualifications,
2 shortlisting, or other additional prerequisites for bidders.
3 Sec. 304. If, as a requirement of bidding on a highway
4 project, the department requires a contractor to submit financial
5 or proprietary documentation as to how the bid was calculated, the
6 department shall keep that bid documentation confidential and shall
7 not disclose that bid documentation other than to a department
8 representative without the contractor's written consent. The
9 department may disclose the bid documentation if necessary to
10 address or defend a claim by a contractor.
11 Sec. 306. (1) The amounts appropriated in part 1 to support
12 tax and fee collection, law enforcement, and other program services
13 provided to the department and to transportation funds by other
14 state departments must be expended from transportation funds
15 pursuant to annual contracts between the department and those other
16 state departments. The contracts must be executed before the
17 expenditure or obligation of those funds. The contracts must
18 provide, but are not limited to, the following data applicable to
19 each state department:
20 (a) Estimated costs to be recovered from transportation funds.
21 (b) Description of services provided to the department and/or
22 transportation funds and financed with transportation funds.
23 (c) Detailed cost allocation methods appropriate to the type
24 of services being provided and the activities financed with
25 transportation funds.
26 (2) Not later than 2 months after publication of the state of
27 Michigan annual comprehensive financial report, each state
28 department receiving funding pursuant to an interdepartment
29 contract with the department shall submit a written report to the

904
1 department, the state budget director, the senate and house fiscal

24 department properties, including, but not limited to, salt storage

ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

1 Transportation. The department shall administer the state

4 TRANSIT AND RAIL RELATED FUNDS
5 Sec. 701. The department shall establish an intercity bus
6 equipment and facility fund as a subsidiary fund within the
7 comprehensive transportation fund created under section 10b of 1951
8 PA 51, MCL 247.660b. Proceeds received by this state from the sale
9 of state-owned intercity bus equipment must be credited to the
10 intercity bus equipment and facility fund for the purchase and
11 repair of intercity bus equipment, as appropriated. Security
12 deposits not returned to a lessee of state-owned intercity bus
13 equipment under terms of the lease agreement must be credited to
14 the intercity bus equipment and facility fund for the repair of
15 intercity bus equipment, as appropriated. Money received by the
16 department from lease payments for state-owned intercity bus
17 equipment, and facility maintenance charges under terms of leases
18 of state-owned intercity facilities, must be credited to the
19 intercity bus equipment and facility fund for the purchase and
20 repair of intercity bus equipment or for the maintenance and
21 rehabilitation of state-owned intercity facilities, as
22 appropriated. At the close of the fiscal year, any funds remaining
23 in the intercity bus equipment and facility fund remain in the fund
24 and are carried forward into the succeeding fiscal year.
25 Sec. 702. Money that is received by this state as repayment
26 for loans made for rail or water freight capital projects, and as a
27 result of the sale of property or equipment used or projected to be
28 used for rail or water freight projects must be deposited in the
29 rail freight fund created by section 17 of the state transportation
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
912
1 preservation act of 1976, 1976 PA 295, MCL 474.67. At the close of
2 the fiscal year, any funds remaining in the rail freight fund
3 remain in the fund and are carried forward into the succeeding
4 fiscal year.
5 Sec. 704. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
6 department shall prepare and transmit a report that provides detail
7 regarding the department's obligations for programs funded under
8 the appropriation in part 1 for rail operations and infrastructure.
9 The report shall include a breakdown of the appropriation by

12 itemized by project for the remainder of the fiscal year. The
13 report shall also include a listing of all active rail related
14 federal grants. The initial report shall be submitted to the
15 standard report recipients on or before March 1. The department
16 also shall update and resubmit a final report on or before
17 September 30.
18 Sec. 705. After receiving notification from a railroad company
19 pursuant to section 8 of the state transportation preservation act
20 of 1976, 1976 PA 295, MCL 474.58, the department shall immediately
21 notify the senate and house appropriations subcommittees on

23 company has filed with the appropriate governmental agencies for
24 abandonment of a line.

25 than 5% of the cost of any project under this section, unless a

29 total appropriation in part 1 from state funds for airport

1 improvement programs.

3 necessary to match federal money available for airport construction
4 and improvement within this state and to meet the matching
5 requirements of the federal government. Whether acting alone or
6 jointly with another political subdivision or public agency or with
7 this state, a political subdivision or public agency of this state

9 MICHIGAN NATURAL RESOURCES TRUST FUND
10 Sec. 301. (1) The department of natural resources shall enter
11 into agreements with local units of government to administer the
12 grants identified in part 1. Among other provisions, the agreements
13 must require that grant recipients do all of the following:
14 (a) Dedicate to public outdoor recreation uses in perpetuity
15 the land acquired or developed.
16 (b) Replace lands converted or lost to other than public
17 outdoor recreation use.
18 (c) For parcels acquired that are larger than 5 acres, provide
19 this state with a nonparticipating 1/6 minimum royalty interest in
20 any acquired minerals that are retained by the grant recipient.
21 (2) The agreements under subsection (1) must also provide that
22 the full payments of grants be made only after proof of
23 acquisition, or proof of completion of a development project, is
24 submitted by the grant recipient and all costs are verified by the
25 department of natural resources. This requirement may be waived by
26 the department of natural resources.
27 Sec. 302. The appropriations in part 1 for capital outlay
28 shall be carried forward at the end of the fiscal year consistent
29 with section 248 of the management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
936
1 18.1248.

7 PART 2
8 PROVISIONS CONCERNING APPROPRIATIONS
9 FOR FISCAL YEAR 2025-2026
10 GENERAL SECTIONS
11 Sec. 201. In accordance with section 30 of article IX of the
12 state constitution of 1963, for the fiscal year ending September
13 30, 2026, total state spending from state sources under part 1 is
14 $810,388,600.00 and total state spending from state sources to be
15 paid to local units of government is $109,647,900.00. The following
16 itemized statement identifies appropriations from which spending to
17 local units of government will occur:
18 DEPARTMENT OF ENVIRONMENT, GREAT LAKES, AND
19 ENERGY
20 Executive direction $ 3,000,000
21 Water quality programs 1,331,000
22 Subtotal $ 4,331,000
23 DEPARTMENT OF HEALTH AND HUMAN SERVICES
24 Autism services $ 20,550,800
25 Child care fund 25,037,600
26 Healthy Michigan plan – behavioral health (3,025,500)
27 Medicaid mental health services 50,820,500
28 Medicaid substance use disorder services (3,966,500)
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
964
1 Nursing home PAS/ARR-OBRA 900,000
2 Subtotal $ 90,316,900
3 DEPARTMENT OF STATE POLICE
4 Secondary road patrol program $ 15,000,000
5 Subtotal $ 15,000,000
6 TOTAL $ 109,647,900
7 Sec. 202. The appropriations under this part and part 1 are
8 subject to the management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1101
9 to 18.1594.
10 Sec. 203. Funds appropriated in part 1 must be allocated and
11 expended in a manner consistent with federal rules and regulations.
12 Sec. 204. Funds appropriated in part 1 are subject to
13 applicable federal audit and reporting requirements. Prompt action
14 shall be taken if instances of noncompliance are identified,
15 including noncompliance identified in an audit finding. If any
16 instance of noncompliance is identified, including noncompliance
17 identified in an audit finding, the state budget director shall
18 take necessary and immediate action to rectify it. The state budget
19 director shall notify the senate and house appropriations
20 committees and the senate and house fiscal agencies when an
21 instance of noncompliance is identified.
22 Sec. 205. The state budget director shall take steps to ensure
23 that all state fiscal recovery funds allocated to this state under
24 the American rescue plan act of 2021, Public Law 117-2, are
25 expended by December 31, 2026, as required by law. Any state fiscal
26 recovery funds that would otherwise lapse after September 30, 2026
27 are automatically reappropriated for the same purpose as originally
28 authorized and available for expenditure through December 31, 2026
29 and any subsequent financial close out period.
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
965
1 Sec. 206. (1) To ensure that all state fiscal recovery funds
2 allocated to this state under the American rescue plan act of 2021,
3 Public Law 117-2, are expended by December 31, 2026, as required by
4 law, the state budget director may reallocate appropriated funds
5 for the purpose of fully utilizing state fiscal recovery funds that
6 are in jeopardy of not meeting the expenditure deadline for reasons
7 that may include, but are not limited to, completed projects coming
8 in under budget or funds unable to be fully used by subrecipients.
9 The state budget director shall reallocate any of the funds
10 reallocated under this subsection to the programs or purposes
11 specified in this section. Any funds reallocated are unappropriated
12 and immediately reappropriated for the following purposes:
13 (a) To reclassify general fund/general purpose appropriations
14 for payroll and covered benefits for eligible public health and
15 safety employees at the department of corrections.
16 (b) To reclassify general fund/general purpose appropriations
17 for payroll and covered benefits for eligible public health and
18 safety employees at the department of state police.
19 (2) All applicable guidance, implementation, and reporting
20 provisions of the American rescue plan act of 2021, Public Law 117-
21 2, must be followed for state fiscal recovery funds reallocated and
22 reappropriated under subsection (1).
23 (3) The state budget director shall notify the senate and
24 house appropriations committees not later than 10 business days
25 after making any reallocations under subsection (1). The
26 notification must include the authorized program under which funds
27 were originally appropriated, the amount of the reallocation, the
28 program or programs, or purpose, and the department to which the
29 funds are being reallocated under subsection (1), and the amount
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
966
1 reallocated to each program or purpose.
2
3 DEPARTMENT OF ENVIRONMENT, GREAT LAKES, AND ENERGY
4 Sec. 301. (1) Funds appropriated in part 1 for state sites
5 cleanup program must be deposited into the state site cleanup fund
6 from surplus funds in the cleanup and redevelopment trust fund in
7 accordance with section 3e of 1996 PA 384, MCL 445.573e. All funds
8 in the state site cleanup fund are appropriated and available for
9 expenditure to support the implementation of the cleanup of
10 environmentally contaminated sites, in accordance with section
11 20108c of the natural resources and environmental protection act,
12 1994 PA 451, MCL 324.20108c.
13 (2) As used in this section:

16 MCL 445.573e.

18 fund created by section 20108c of the natural resources and

12 DEPARTMENT OF LICENSING AND REGULATORY AFFAIRS
13 Sec. 551. (1) Not later than 30 days after enactment of this
14 act, the legislature shall provide to the department and the state
15 budget director a list of legislatively directed spending items,
16 which may be referred to in this section as grants or direct
17 appropriation grants, funded in part 1 consistent with the house or
18 senate rules and this section. The list must include all
19 information and documents pertaining to the funded items as
20 publicly disclosed in accordance with the house or senate rules and
21 this section.
22 (2) Notwithstanding any other conditions or requirements for
23 direct appropriation grants, the department shall perform, at a
24 minimum, at least all of the following activities to administer the
25 grants described in subsection (1):
26 (a) Establish a process to review, complete, and execute a
27 grant agreement with a grant recipient. The department shall not
28 execute a grant agreement unless all necessary documentation has
29 been submitted and reviewed.
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
985
1 (b) Verify to the extent possible that a grant recipient is a
2 not-for-profit entity and will use funds as publicly disclosed and
3 for a public purpose that serves the economic prosperity, health,
4 safety, or general welfare of the residents of this state.
5 (c) Review and verify all necessary information to ensure the
6 grant recipient is reasonably able to execute the grant agreement,
7 perform its fiduciary duty, and comply with all applicable state
8 and federal statutes. The department may deduct the cost of
9 background checks and any other efforts performed as part of this
10 verification from the amount of the designated grant award.
11 (d) Disburse the grant money per the grant disbursement
12 schedule in the executed grant agreement on a reimbursement basis
13 after the grantee has provided sufficient documentation, as
14 determined by the department, to verify that expenditures were made
15 in accordance with the project purpose.
16 (e) If the state budget director determines that information
17 provided by the grantee does not meet the disclosure requirements,
18 that the grant will be used to pay a tax lien, delinquent tax, or
19 other obligation owed to this state, or that the grant will create
20 a conflict of interest, the department shall not release the grant
21 money to the grantee. Money that is not released under this
22 subdivision lapses at the end of the fiscal year. There is not a
23 conflict of interest if the sponsoring legislator certifies that
24 the sponsoring legislator's immediate family members, the
25 legislative staff members that have worked for the sponsoring
26 legislator within the past 2 years, and the sponsoring legislator
27 do not have a direct or indirect pecuniary interest in the
28 legislatively directed spending item.
29 (3) An executed grant agreement under this section between the
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
986
1 department and a grant recipient must include at least all of the
2 following:
3 (a) All necessary identifying information for the grant
4 recipient, including any tax and financial information necessary
5 for the department to administer grant money under this section.
6 (b) A description of the project for which the grant money
7 will be expended, including tentative timelines and the estimated
8 budget. Project budget must include how all grant money will be
9 used and must indicate if any grant money will be provided to a
10 third party or subrecipient. The department shall not reimburse
11 expenditures that are outside of the project purpose, as stated in
12 the executed grant agreement, from appropriations in part 1. The
13 grantee shall return to the state treasury any interest in excess
14 of $1,000.00 earned on the grant money while unexpended and in
15 possession of the grantee.
16 (c) Unless otherwise specified in department policy, a
17 requirement that funds appropriated for the grants described in
18 subsection (1) may be used only for expenditures that occur on or
19 after the effective date of this act.
20 (d) A requirement for reporting by the grant recipient to the
21 department and the legislative sponsor that provides the status of
22 the project and an accounting of all money expended by the grant
23 recipient, as determined by the department.
24 (e) A clawback provision that allows the department of
25 treasury to recoup or otherwise collect any grant money that is
26 declined, unspent, or otherwise misused.
27 (f) The documents publicly disclosed under subsection (1).
28 (4) If appropriate to improve the administration or oversight
29 of a grant described in subsection (1), the department may adopt a
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
987
1 memorandum of understanding with another state department to
2 perform the required duties under this section.
3 (5) A grant recipient shall respond to all reasonable
4 information requests from the department related to grant
5 expenditures and retain grant records for not less than 7 years,
6 and the grant may be subject to monitoring, site visits, and audit
7 as determined by the department. The grant agreement required under
8 this section must include signed assurance by the chief executive
9 officer or other executive officer of the grant recipient
10 authorized to bind the grant recipient that the requirements of
11 this subsection will be met.
12 (6) The grant recipient shall expend all grant money awarded
13 and complete all projects not later than September 30, 2030. If at
14 that time any unexpended money remains, the grant recipient shall
15 return that money to the state treasury. If a grant recipient does
16 not provide information sufficient to execute a grant agreement
17 within 30 days after enactment of this act, the department shall
18 return money associated with the grant to the state treasury.
19 (7) Any grant money that is awarded to a state department is
20 appropriated in that department for the purpose of the intended
21 grant.
22 (8) Except as otherwise provided in subsection (9), beginning
23 March 15 of the current fiscal year, the department shall post a
24 report in a publicly accessible location on its website. The report
25 must list the grant recipient, project purpose, and location of the
26 project for each grant described in subsection (1), the status of
27 money allocated and disbursed under the grant agreement, and the
28 legislative sponsor, if applicable. The department shall update the
29 report and post the updated report in a publicly accessible
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
988
1 location on its website not later than June 15 of the current
2 fiscal year and again not later than September 15 of the current
3 fiscal year. The department shall include in the report the most
4 comprehensive information the department has available at the time
5 of posting for grants awarded.
6 (9) If the state budget office determines that it is more
7 efficient for the state budget office to compile all affected
8 departments' information and post a report of the compiled
9 information rather than the report required under subsection (8)
10 being posted by individual departments, the state budget office may
11 compile that information across all affected departments and post
12 the compiled report and any updates on the same time schedule as
13 identified in subsection (8).
14 (10) If the department reasonably determines that the money
15 allocated for an executed grant agreement under this section was
16 misused or that use of the money was misrepresented by the grant
17 recipient, the department shall not award any additional funds
18 under the executed grant agreement and shall refer the grant for
19 review following internal audit protocols, which may include
20 referral for criminal investigation.
21 Sec. 552. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for
22 Michigan Saves, the Michigan public service commission may award a
23 $1,500,000.00 grant to Michigan Saves to conduct a grant program
24 for clean energy improvement and on-site wastewater system
25 replacement or repair. Michigan Saves shall grant funds to
26 individuals or small businesses within this state who have a
27 history of having difficulty obtaining traditional capital or
28 households with a combined income not exceeding 300% of the federal
29 poverty level and to businesses that indicate a state of financial
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
989
1 need or vulnerability. The amount granted to a single individual or
2 business may not exceed $100,000.00.
3 (2) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for Michigan Saves,
4 the Michigan public service commission may award a $1,500,000.00
5 grant to a nonprofit green bank with experience in leveraging
6 energy-efficiency and renewable energy improvements, for the
7 purpose of making such loans more affordable for Michigan families,
8 businesses, and public entities. Grant funds may be used to support
9 a loan loss reserve fund or other comparable financial instrument
10 to further leverage private investment in clean energy
11 improvements.
12 (3) Recognizing the unique financial instrument and extended
13 term for which loans may be outstanding, section 551(2)(d) and (6)
14 does not apply to funds used to support a loan loss reserve as
15 described in subsection (2). Fifty percent of funds shall be paid
16 to the grantee not later than 60 days after a grant agreement has
17 been executed, and the remaining funds disbursed after verification
18 that the initial payment has been fully leveraged, in accordance
19 with the project purpose. The remaining funds shall be disbursed in
20 a manner specified in the grant agreement. The grantee must provide
21 sufficient documentation, as determined by the department, to
22 verify that all expenditures were made in accordance with the
23 project purpose. The funds shall be fully deposited into a loan
24 loss reserve fund not later than September 30, 2030. The grant
25 recipient shall return any money not used to support the loan loss
26 reserve fund to the state treasury.
27 Sec. 554. Funds appropriated in part 1 for Michigan indigent
28 defense commission grants are designated as a work project
29 appropriation. Any unencumbered or unallotted funds shall not lapse
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
990
1 at the end of the fiscal year and shall be available for
2 expenditures for projects under this section until the projects
3 have been completed. The following is in compliance with section
4 451a of the management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1451a:
5 (a) The purpose of the project is to support court-mandated
6 resentencing of youthful defendants, planning or implementation
7 costs related to the possible expansion of commission standards to
8 include juvenile defendants, enhanced cost verification of local
9 compliance plans, and grants to local indigent defense systems to
10 support costs not covered by other funding.
11 (b) The project will be accomplished by utilizing state
12 employees or contracts with service providers, or both.
13 (c) The total estimated cost of the project is $50,000,000.00.
14 (d) The tentative completion date is September 30, 2030.

10 Sec. 704. (1) The make it in Michigan competitiveness fund is
11 created within the state treasury.
12 (2) Funds may be spent from the make it in Michigan
13 competitiveness fund only on appropriation or administrative
14 transfer pursuant to subsection (3).
15 (3) A transfer of funds from federal or state restricted
16 contingency funds into the make it in Michigan competitiveness fund
17 may be made by the state budget director not less than 30 days
18 after notifying each member of the senate and house of
19 representatives appropriations committees. Those transfers may be
20 disapproved by either appropriations committee within the 30 days
21 and, if disapproved within that time, are not effective.
22 (4) A transfer approved under this section constitutes
23 authorization to transfer the amount recommended and approved.
24 However, the amount must be reduced by the state budget director to
25 be within the current unobligated amount of the appropriation.
26 (5) Transfers must not be authorized under any of the
27 following circumstances:
28 (a) To create a new line-item appropriation or to create a new
29 state program.
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
994
1 (b) To or from an operating appropriation line item that did
2 not appear in the fiscal year appropriation bills for which the
3 transfer is being made.
4 (c) To or from a work project as designated under section 451a
5 of the management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1451a.
6 (d) Between state governmental funds.
7 (6) Interest and earnings from the investment of funds
8 deposited in the make it in Michigan competitiveness fund must be
9 deposited in the general fund.
10 (7) Funds in the make it in Michigan competitiveness fund at
11 the close of a fiscal year remain in the make it in Michigan
12 competitiveness fund and do not lapse to the general fund.
13 (8) Funds appropriated or transferred from the make it in
14 Michigan competitiveness fund are available to leverage federal
15 funding opportunities or to support critical investments that
16 benefit Michigan residents or businesses, that include, but are not
17 limited to, infrastructure, health, housing, public governance and
18 safety, mobility and electrification, climate and the environment,
19 economic development, or other funding opportunities. Funding
20 opportunities may be in the form of formula- or competitive-based
21 grants, cooperative agreements, or contracts, and may include funds
22 contained in the infrastructure investment and jobs act, Public Law
23 117-58, the CHIPS act of 2022, division A of Public Law 117-167,
24 the inflation reduction act of 2022, Public Law 117-169, or any
25 other federal acts.
26 (9) The Michigan infrastructure office, in collaboration with
27 the state budget director, shall form an interagency evaluation
28 committee that includes the department of environment, Great Lakes,
29 and energy, the department of labor and economic opportunity, the
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
995
1 state transportation department, the Michigan strategic fund, or
2 other entities at the discretion of the Michigan infrastructure
3 office, to develop program guidelines and selection criteria for
4 the recommended appropriation or transfer of funds. The interagency
5 evaluation committee shall make recommendations to the director of
6 the department of technology, management, and budget and the state
7 budget director on the disbursement of funds. Funding must also be
8 used to cover all costs related to the administration of this
9 section.
10 (10) The department of technology, management, and budget
11 shall inform the legislature not later than 30 days after any
12 federal funds are received that would be used as the basis for
13 recommended appropriations or transfers from the make it in
14 Michigan competitiveness fund.
15 (11) Not later than 90 days after the close of each fiscal
16 year, the department of technology, management, and budget shall
17 report to the legislature on the projects funded with make it in
18 Michigan competitiveness fund money.
19 (12) All unexpended funds appropriated in accordance with the
20 provisions of this section are designated as a work project
21 appropriation, and any unencumbered or unallotted funds shall not
22 lapse at the end of the fiscal year, and shall be available for
23 expenditures for projects under this section until the projects
24 have been completed. The following is in compliance with section
25 451a of the management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1451a:
26 (a) The purpose of the project is to leverage federal funding
27 opportunities or to support critical investments that benefit
28 Michigan residents or businesses, that include, but are not limited
29 to, infrastructure, health, housing, public governance and safety,
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
996
1 mobility and electrification, climate and the environment, economic
2 development, or other funding opportunities
3 (b) The project will be accomplished by utilizing state
4 employees or contracts with service providers, or both.
5 (c) The total estimated cost of the project is commensurate to
6 the amounts appropriated under this section.
7 (d) The tentative completion date is September 30, 2030.
8 Sec. 705. In addition to the funds appropriated in part 1,
9 there is appropriated an amount not to exceed $50,000,000.00 for
10 state restricted contingency authorization. Amounts appropriated

12 have been transferred to another line item in part 1 under section

14 Sec. 706. (1) The emergency services contracted providers fund
15 is created within the state treasury. The department of technology,
16 management, and budget shall administer the emergency services
17 contracted providers fund.
18 (2) Funds appropriated in part 1 for emergency services
19 contracted providers must be made available for expenditure for
20 reimbursement payments to be made to ambulance providers that are
21 in full compliance with section 20921 of the public health code,
22 1978 PA 368, MCL 333.20921, and that can verify they are owed
23 payments by a provider of health care services that had a contract
24 with the department of corrections to provide services after
25 December 31, 2022 and before January 1, 2025.
26 (3) Not later than 30 days after the effective date of this
27 act, the department of technology, management, and budget shall
28 collaborate with the department of corrections to develop a grant
29 program to provide monetary awards to ambulance providers for
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
997
1 reimbursement of payments owed as described in subsection (2). The
2 department of technology, management, and budget shall create a
3 grant application that includes, but is not limited to, all of the
4 following:
5 (a) The name of the ambulance provider applying for a grant.
6 (b) The location of the principal office of the ambulance
7 provider.
8 (c) The grant amount sought by the ambulance provider.
9 (d) Documentation supporting the grant amount sought by the
10 ambulance provider.
11 (4) The department of technology, management, and budget shall
12 publish the grant application developed under subsection (3) on the
13 department of technology, management, and budget's publicly
14 accessible website immediately upon development.
15 (5) The department of technology, management, and budget shall
16 accept applications for a period of 30 days beginning on the date
17 that the department of technology, management, and budget publishes
18 the application under subsection (4).
19 (6) The department of technology, management, and budget shall
20 distribute grant funding not later than 30 days after the period
21 described in subsection (5) ends.
22 (7) If a recipient of grant funding under this section
23 receives reimbursement of money owed as described in subsection (2)
24 through a litigation judgment or settlement, the recipient shall
25 pay the money reimbursed to this state.

4 DEPARTMENT OF TREASURY
5 Sec. 901. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for public
6 safety revenue sharing grants, the department shall create and
7 administer a block grant program to support training provided by an
8 entity holding an oral transliteration certificate. The oral
9 transliteration certificate must be held by an officer, board
10 member, or principal of the entity, and must have been held for 1
11 year or more. Any training course provided for under this section
12 must be certified by the International Accreditors for Continuing
13 Education and Training. That certification may be obtained and
14 maintained through a developer who has current status with the
15 International Accreditors for Continuing Education and Training.
16 The purpose of the grant is to train officers to better assist in
17 their communication with members of the public who experience a
18 language barrier or may be hard of hearing or deaf.
19 (2) The unexpended funds appropriated in part 1 for public
20 safety revenue sharing grants are designated as a work project
21 appropriation, and any unencumbered or unallotted funds must not
22 lapse at the end of the fiscal year and must be available for
23 expenditures for projects under this section until the projects
24 have been completed. The following is in compliance with section
25 451a(1) of the management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL
26 18.1451a:
27 (a) The purpose of the project is the training of law
28 enforcement officers.
29 (b) The project will be accomplished by utilizing contracts
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri
1000
1 with service providers.
2 (c) The estimated cost of this project is $1,000,000.00.
3 (d) The tentative completion date for the work project is
4 September 30, 2030.
5 (3) The department may use the funds appropriated in part 1
6 for public safety revenue sharing grants for costs associated with
7 the administration and oversight of the block grant program.

FIRST CONFERENCE REPORT
The Committee of Conference on the matters of difference
between the two Houses concerning
Senate Bill No. 878, entitled
A bill to make, supplement, adjust, and consolidate
appropriations for various state departments and agencies, the
judicial branch, and the legislative branch for the fiscal year
ending September 30, 2027; to provide for certain conditions on
appropriations; and to provide for the expenditure of the
appropriations.
Recommends:
First: That the House recede from the Substitute of the
House as passed by the House.
Second: That the Senate and House agree to the Substitute of
the Senate as passed by the Senate, amended to read as follows:
(attached)
Third: That the Senate and House agree to the title of the
bill to read as follows:
A bill to make, supplement, adjust, and consolidate
appropriations for various state departments and agencies, the
judicial branch, the legislative branch, and capital outlay
purposes for the fiscal years ending September 30, 2026 and
September 30, 2027; to provide for certain conditions on
appropriations; to provide for the expenditure of the
appropriations; and to repeal acts and parts of acts.
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 cs mbfz3k

_______________________ ________________________
Sarah E. Anthony Ann Bollin
_______________________ ________________________
Sean McCann Matt Maddock
_______________________ ________________________
Rick Outman Joseph Tate
Conferees for the Senate Conferees for the House
Final Page

21 Sec. 201. In accordance with section 30 of article IX of the
22 state constitution of 1963, for the fiscal year ending September
23 30, 2027, total state spending under part 1 from state sources is
24 $120,784,100.00 and total state spending under part 1 from state
25 sources to be paid to local units of government is $10,400,000.00.
26 The following itemized statement identifies appropriations from
27 which spending to local units of government will occur:
28 DEPARTMENT OF AGRICULTURE AND RURAL DEVELOPMENT
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

9
1 Agriculture preservation easement grants $ 1,900,000
2 Environmental stewardship/MAEAP 4,100,000
3 Local conservation districts 3,000,000
4 Qualified forest program 1,400,000
5 TOTAL $ 10,400,000
6 Sec. 202. The appropriations under this part and part 1 are
7 subject to the management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1101
8 to 18.1594.
9 Sec. 203. As used in part 1 and this part:
10 (a) "Department" means the department of agriculture and rural
11 development.
12 (b) "Director" means the director of the department.
13 (c) "Fiscal agencies" means the Michigan house fiscal agency
14 and the Michigan senate fiscal agency.
15 (d) "FTE" means full-time equated.
16 (e) "IDG" means interdepartmental grant.
17 (f) "MAEAP" means the Michigan agriculture environmental
18 assurance program.
19 (g) "MDEGLE" means the Michigan department of environment,
20 Great Lakes, and energy.
21 (h) "Standard report recipients" means the house and senate
22 appropriations committees, the house and senate appropriations
23 subcommittees on agriculture and rural development, the house and
24 senate fiscal agencies, the house and senate policy offices, and
25 the state budget office.
26 (i) "Subcommittees" means all members of the subcommittees of
27 the house and senate appropriations committees with jurisdiction
28 over the budget for the department.
29 (j) "TB" means tuberculosis.
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

10
1 (k) "USDA" means the United States Department of Agriculture.
2 Sec. 204. If the state administrative board, acting under
3 section 3 of 1921 PA 2, MCL 17.3, transfers funds from an amount
4 appropriated under part 1, the legislature may, by a concurrent
5 resolution adopted by a majority of the members elected to and
6 serving in each house, intertransfer funds within part 1 for the
7 particular department, board, commission, officer, or institution.
8 Sec. 205. (1) The department shall use the internet to fulfill
9 the reporting requirements of this part and shall make each report
10 readily accessible to the public and conspicuously post each
11 required report in a single archivable location on the department's
12 website not later than the due date required for each report.
13 (2) In addition to placing all reports required in the current
14 fiscal year on the department's website, the department shall
15 maintain on its website all reports placed on the website from
16 previous fiscal years posted by fiscal year in the same single
17 archivable location.
18 (3) The department shall transmit all required reports for the
19 current fiscal year to the standard report recipients and any other
20 required recipients by email. The email shall include a copy of the
21 report and a link to access the report online.
22 Sec. 206. The department shall receive and retain copies of
23 all reports funded from appropriations in part 1. The department

25 and long-term retention of records. The department may

3 Sec. 223. (1) The department shall maximize utilization of its
4 in-person state workforce. The department shall prioritize
5 occupancy utilization of office space for each division within the
6 department. Employees with job responsibilities that require the
7 employees to serve in their capacities outside of an office shall
8 be monitored each pay period to ensure all work hours reported on
9 the timesheet were actually worked.
10 (2) The department shall comply with requirements set forth by
11 the office of the state employer on in-person work and utilization
12 and occupancy rates of state buildings to ensure in-person work is
13 optimized and occupancy rates are 80% or higher, subject to market
14 conditions.
15 (3) The department shall adhere to civil service rules and
16 regulations that state the standard biweekly work period for a
17 full-time employee in the classified service of this state is the
18 equivalent of 80 hours of work. The department shall establish
19 policies and processes to ensure all employees are working their
20 jobs during agreed-upon business hours.
21 (4) The office of the state employer must create and implement
22 an occupancy utilization uniform policy on occupancy, utilization,
23 and in-person and remote work. The office of the state employer
24 must make each uniform policy publicly available on the
25 department's website.

23 BUREAU OF ENVIRONMENT AND SUSTAINABILITY
24 Sec. 501. The department shall report on the previous calendar
25 year's activities of the bureau of environment and sustainability
26 on or before April 1 of each year.
27 Sec. 502. (1) The purpose of the part 1 appropriation for soil
28 health/regenerative agriculture is to advance the adoption and
29 implementation of best regenerative agricultural practices and new
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

26
1 environmentally sustainable technologies, promoting soil health and
2 regenerative agriculture principles throughout the state.
3 (2) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for soil
4 health/regenerative agriculture, the department shall do both of
5 the following:
6 (a) Promote the principles of soil health and regenerative
7 agriculture through at least the following:
8 (i) The maintenance of soil cover.
9 (ii) The minimization of soil disturbance.
10 (iii) The maximization of plant and crop diversity.
11 (iv) The maximization of the presence of living roots.
12 (v) The integration of livestock into the cropping systems.
13 (b) Ensure that program outcomes include at least the
14 following:
15 (i) The increase of soil organic matter content.
16 (ii) The improvement of soil water infiltration capacity.
17 (iii) The increase in soil water holding capacity.
18 (iv) The improvement of soil biological capacity to break down
19 plant residue and other substances and to maintain soil
20 aggregation.
21 (v) The improvement of soil nutrient sequestration and cycling
22 capacity.
23 (vi) The reduction of nutrient losses.
24 (3) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for soil
25 health/regenerative agriculture, the department shall promote
26 practices of soil health and regenerative agriculture, including
27 the use of no-till farming, intercropping, cover crops,
28 multispecies cover crops, roller crimping, managed rotational
29 grazing, and other practices identified that utilize natural
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

27
1 biological processes to advance the goals of soil health and
2 regenerative agriculture.
3 (4) Funds appropriated in part 1 for soil health/regenerative
4 agriculture shall not be used for applied research into the
5 precision application of fertilizer, pesticides, or herbicides.
6 (5) It is the intention of the legislature that the department
7 engage with program partners to achieve the purposes of the soil
8 health/regenerative agriculture programs through research,
9 education, and outreach. Agreements with program partners receiving
10 funds through soil health/regenerative agriculture appropriations
11 must describe intended outcomes and how intended outcomes will be
12 measured and require the provision of a report to the department on
13 uses of funding received and a progress report on outcomes.
14 (6) In the report required under section 501 of this part, the
15 department shall provide information on the program described in
16 this section, including department activities, uses of program
17 funds by activity or project, contractors, grantees, and a summary
18 of projects and project results.
19 (7) Of the funds appropriated in part 1 for soil
20 health/regenerative agriculture, not less than $1,000,000.00 must
21 be used by the department to partner with the state land grant
22 university through MSU Extension and AgBioResearch to develop,
23 implement, and evaluate a soil health/regenerative agriculture
24 program. The partnership described in this subsection must be
25 focused on researching and assisting the agricultural industry in
26 implementing soil health/regenerative agricultural principles and
27 techniques. Partnership goals must include, but are not limited to,
28 establishing program priorities, developing metrics, implementing
29 goals, evaluating outcomes, and engaging with stakeholders.
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

28

13 AGRICULTURE DEVELOPMENT BUREAU
14 Sec. 601. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for the
15 food and agriculture investment program, the department shall
16 operate a food and agriculture investment program.
17 (2) The food and agriculture investment program shall do all
18 of the following:
19 (a) Expand the Michigan food and agriculture sector.
20 (b) Promote food security.
21 (c) Develop local and regional food systems.
22 (d) Grow Michigan exports.
23 (e) Promote the development of value-added agricultural
24 production.
25 (f) Support urban farms, food hubs, food incubators, and
26 community-based processing facilities with a focus on new and
27 expanding protein processors.
28 (g) Promote the expansion of farm markets, flower markets, and
29 urban agriculture, including hoop houses.
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

30
1 (h) Increase food processing activities within this state by
2 accelerating investment projects and infrastructure development
3 that support growth in production agriculture and food and
4 agriculture processing, expand opportunity to new agricultural
5 producers and processors, promote agriculture tourism and
6 agricultural heritage, and develop agricultural education and
7 interpretation activities.
8 (3) In addition to the funds appropriated in part 1, the
9 department may receive and expend funds received from outside
10 sources for the food and agriculture investment program.
11 (4) Before the allocation of funding, all projects must
12 receive approval from the Michigan commission of agriculture and
13 rural development, except for projects selected through a
14 competitive process by a joint evaluation committee selected by the
15 director and consisting of representatives that have agriculture,
16 food security, local and regional food systems, business, and
17 economic development expertise. Projects funded through the food
18 and agriculture investment program will be required to have a grant
19 agreement that outlines milestones and activities that must be met
20 in order to receive a disbursement of funds. Projects must also
21 identify measurable project outcomes.
22 (5) The department shall include, in the agriculture
23 development annual report, a report on the food and agriculture
24 investment program for the previous fiscal year that includes a
25 listing of the grantees, award amounts, match funding, project
26 locations, and project outcomes.
27 (6) The unexpended funds appropriated in part 1 for the food
28 and agriculture investment program are designated as a work project
29 appropriation, and any unencumbered or unallotted funds do not
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

31
1 lapse at the end of the fiscal year and are available for
2 expenditures for projects under this section until the projects
3 have been completed. The following is in compliance with section
4 451a(1) of the management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL
5 18.1451a:
6 (a) The purpose of the project is to promote and expand the
7 Michigan food and agriculture sector, grow Michigan exports, and
8 increase food processing activities within the state.
9 (b) The project will be accomplished by utilizing state

24 described in this section, includes, but is not limited to:

26 requirements.

1 (a) The counties in this state with participating program

6 of vendors, as of May 1, 2026. The report must highlight counties

10 Sec. 902. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for Fresh
11 Food in Schools, funds must be allocated to the scratch cooking
12 pilot program.
13 (2) A local education agency providing locally produced
14 agriculture products for schools may submit an application to the
15 department for a grant under the pilot program. The application
16 must include both of the following:
17 (a) A needs assessment to evaluate equipment needs, equipment
18 utilization, procurement process, and workforce capabilities for
19 the pilot program.
20 (b) A strategic plan identifying all of the following:
21 (i) How the local education agency will use the grant funding.
22 (ii) How project activities will be monitored and evaluated.
23 (iii) All proposed costs with a clear explanation about how the
24 costs were determined.
25 (3) Grants shall be used by a local education agency to
26 promote scratch cooking, which may include any of the following:
27 (a) Implementing professional development and training related
28 to preparing, procuring, advertising, serving, and creating menus
29 of scratch-cooked meals that prioritize fresh, locally procured
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

37
1 items.
2 (b) Purchasing kitchen equipment or making infrastructure
3 modifications necessary for scratch cooking.
4 (c) Hiring of staff or third-party entities whose primary
5 focus is creating sustainable, actionable change within a school
6 food program to increase their scratch cooking that sustains beyond
7 the grant period.
8 (d) Providing technical assistance, pupil engagement, and
9 education related to scratch cooking, such as taste tests, recipe
10 development, and culinary education.
11 (e) Investing in K to 12 software and technology systems for
12 procurement to support scratch cooking.
13 (f) Carrying out any additional activities to promote scratch
14 cooking that will help local education agencies meet or exceed
15 nutrition standards.
16 (4) In evaluating grant applications, the department shall
17 give priority to local education agencies that serve the greatest
18 proportion of students eligible for free or reduced-price lunch.
19 (5) Not later than 180 days after the expiration of the pilot
20 program, each local education agency that received a grant shall
21 submit a report to the department that compares the change at the
22 end of the grant period to the school year immediately preceding
23 the beginning of the grant period, including all of the following:
24 (a) Recipes prepared.
25 (b) Nutrient analysis information.
26 (c) Invoices or bid lists identifying the cost of equipment
27 needed for scratch cooking.
28 (d) The number of staff, type of staff, and time required to
29 prepare each meal.
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

38
1 (e) Salary and benefit rates for each food service employee
2 involved in food preparation of selected meals.

5 lunch participation.
6 (h) Pre- and post-program scratch cooked menu analysis.
7 (i) Pre- and post-program local procured item analysis.
8 (j) Program cost savings.
9 (k) Any other information required by the department.

18 Sec. 202. The appropriations under this part and part 1 are
19 subject to the management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1101
20 to 18.1594.Sec. 203. As used in this part and part 1:
21 (a) "Administrative segregation" means confinement for
22 maintenance of order or discipline to a cell or room apart from
23 accommodations provided for inmates who are participating in
24 programs of the facility.
25 (b) "Department" means the department of corrections.
26 (c) "Director" means the director of the department.
27 (d) "DOJ" means the United States Department of Justice.
28 (e) "DOJ-BOP" means the DOJ Bureau of Prisons.
29 (f) "Evidence-based" means a decision-making process that
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

47
1 integrates the best available research, clinician expertise, and
2 client characteristics.
3 (g) "FTE" means full-time equated position in the classified
4 service of this state.
5 (h) "Jail" means a facility operated by a local unit of
6 government for the physical detention and correction of individuals
7 charged with or convicted of criminal offenses.
8 (i) "OCC" means the office of community corrections.
9 (j) "Offender success" means that an offender has, with the
10 support of the community, intervention of the field agent, and
11 benefit of any participation in programs and treatment, made an
12 adjustment while at liberty in the community such that the offender
13 has not been sentenced to or returned to prison for the conviction
14 of a new crime or the revocation of probation or parole.
15 (k) "Recidivism" means that term as defined in section 1 of
16 2017 PA 5, MCL 798.31.
17 (l) "Serious emotional disturbance" means that term as defined
18 in section 100d(3) of the mental health code, 1974 PA 258, MCL
19 330.1100d.
20 (m) "Serious mental illness" means that term as defined in
21 section 100d(4) of the mental health code, 1974 PA 258, MCL
22 330.1100d.
23 (n) "SSA" means the United States Social Security
24 Administration.
25 (o) "SSA-SSI" means SSA supplemental security income.
26 (p) "Standard report recipients" means the senate and house
27 appropriations committees, the senate and house appropriations
28 subcommittees on corrections and judiciary, the senate and house
29 fiscal agencies, the senate and house policy offices, the
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

48
1 legislative corrections ombudsman, and the state budget office.
2 Sec. 204. If the state administrative board, acting under
3 section 3 of 1921 PA 2, MCL 17.3, transfers funds from an amount
4 appropriated under part 1, the legislature may, by a concurrent
5 resolution adopted by a majority of the members elected to and
6 serving in each house, intertransfer funds within part 1 for the
7 particular department, board, commission, officer, or institution.
8 Sec. 205. (1) The department shall use the internet to fulfill
9 the reporting requirements of this part and shall make each report
10 readily accessible to the public and conspicuously post each
11 required report in a single archivable location on the department's
12 website not later than the due date required for each report.
13 (2) In addition to placing all reports required in the current
14 fiscal year on the department's website, the department shall
15 maintain on its website all reports placed on the website from
16 previous fiscal years posted by fiscal year in the same single
17 archivable location.
18 (3) The department shall transmit all required reports for the
19 current fiscal year to the standard report recipients and any other
20 required recipients by email. The email shall include a copy of the
21 report and a link to access the report online.
22 Sec. 206. The department shall receive and retain copies of
23 all reports funded from appropriations in part 1. The department
24 shall follow federal and state law and guidelines for short-term
25 and long-term retention of records. The department may
26 electronically retain copies of reports unless otherwise required
27 by federal and state guidelines.

12 Sec. 208. (1) In addition to any other requirements under this
13 part, if the department is authorized under this part to expend
14 funds in addition to those appropriated in part 1, the department
15 shall do all of the following:
16 (a) Not later than December 1, provide a report to the
17 chairpersons of the senate and house appropriations committees, the
18 senate and house fiscal agencies, and the state budget office that
19 details all of the following:
20 (i) The type of funding received during the previous fiscal
21 year that was authorized in part 2 of the article that made
22 appropriations for the department in the previous fiscal year.
23 (ii) When the funding was received.

25 Sec. 318. (1) The department shall administer a county jail
26 reimbursement program from the funds appropriated in part 1 for the
27 purpose of reimbursing counties for housing in jails certain felons
28 who otherwise would have been sentenced to prison.
29 (2) The county jail reimbursement program must be used to
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

66
1 reimburse counties for convicted felons in the custody of the
2 sheriff if the conviction was for a crime committed on or after
3 January 1, 1999 and 1 of the following applies:
4 (a) The felon's sentencing guidelines recommended range upper
5 limit is more than 18 months, the felon's sentencing guidelines
6 recommended range lower limit is 12 months or less, the felon's
7 prior record variable score is 35 or more points, and the felon's
8 sentence is not for commission of a crime in crime class G or crime
9 class H or a nonperson crime in crime class F under chapter XVII of
10 the code of criminal procedure, 1927 PA 175, MCL 777.1 to 777.69.
11 (b) The felon's minimum sentencing guidelines range minimum is
12 more than 12 months under the sentencing guidelines described in
13 subdivision (a).
14 (c) The felon was sentenced to jail for a felony committed
15 while the felon was on parole and under the jurisdiction of the
16 parole board and for which the sentencing guidelines recommended
17 range for the minimum sentence has an upper limit of more than 18
18 months.
19 (3) State reimbursement under this section must be $70.00 per
20 diem per diverted offender for offenders with a presumptive prison
21 guideline score, $60.00 per diem per diverted offender for
22 offenders with a straddle cell guideline for a group 1 crime, and
23 $45.00 per diem per diverted offender for offenders with a straddle
24 cell guideline for a group 2 crime. Reimbursements must be paid for
25 sentences up to a 1-year total.
26 (4) County jail reimbursement program expenditures must not
27 exceed the amount appropriated in part 1 for the county jail
28 reimbursement program. Payments to counties under the county jail
29 reimbursement program must be made in the order in which properly
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

67
1 documented requests for reimbursements are received. A request is
2 properly documented if it meets departmental requirements for
3 documentation. Not later than October 15, the department shall
4 distribute the documentation requirements to all counties.

6 the purpose of housing in jails certain felons who otherwise would
7 have been sentenced to prison shall, as a condition of receiving
8 the funding, report not later than September 30 an annual average

1 reimbursement program as inmates of a state prison.
2 (7) As used in this section:
3 (a) "Group 1 crime" means a crime in 1 or more of the
4 following offense categories: arson, assault, assaultive other,
5 burglary, criminal sexual conduct, homicide or resulting in death,
6 other sex offenses, robbery, and weapon possession as determined by
7 the department based on specific crimes for which counties received
8 reimbursement under the county jail reimbursement program in fiscal
9 year 2007 and fiscal year 2008, and listed in the county jail
10 reimbursement program document titled "FY 2007 and FY 2008 Group
11 One Crimes Reimbursed", dated March 31, 2009.
12 (b) "Group 2 crime" means a crime that is not a group 1 crime,
13 including larceny, fraud, forgery, embezzlement, motor vehicle
14 offenses, malicious destruction of property, controlled substance
15 offense, felony drunk driving, and other nonassaultive offenses.
16 (c) "In the custody of the sheriff" means that the convicted
17 felon has been sentenced to the county jail and either is housed in
18 a county jail, is in custody but is being housed at a hospital or
19 medical facility for a medical or mental health purpose, or has
20 been released from jail and is being monitored through the use of
21 the sheriff's electronic monitoring system.
22 Sec. 319. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
23 department shall provide all of the following information on the

25 (a) Prison population by facility and security level,

20 expenditures, including amounts spent on each project funded,
21 itemized by service provided and service provider. Reported
22 performance factors must be reported by region and must include,
23 but not be limited to, all of the following:
24 (a) The number of individuals who received transitional
25 housing services.
26 (b) The average length of stay in transitional housing.
27 (c) The number of individuals who received a referral for
28 economic stability assistance and the number of referred
29 individuals who secured employment or enrolled in
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

71
1 education/training to increase economic stability.
2 (d) The number of referred individuals who maintained
3 employment for 12 months or more.
4 (e) The total amount of leveraged services secured by the
5 contractor.
6 (2) As used in this section, "leveraged services" means
7 services that benefit clients that are not directly paid for by the
8 department, such as educational scholarships or grants, workforce
9 training grants, or housing choice vouchers.
10 (3) The department may accept cash or in-kind donations to
11 supplement funds for prison education training, supplies, and
12 materials necessary to complete the academic and jobs skills
13 related programs. All funds received are appropriated and may be
14 expended by the department. Any unexpended or unencumbered
15 donations at the end of the fiscal year shall not lapse to the
16 general fund but shall be carried forward to the subsequent fiscal
17 year.
18 Sec. 402. From the funds appropriated in part 1 for offender
19 success services, the department, when reasonably possible, shall
20 strive to do the following:
21 (a) Develop workforce and training opportunities in
22 consultation with employers and state and local workforce agencies.
23 (b) Assist individuals leaving prison with securing employment
24 or training prior to or following release, or both.
25 (c) Work with faith-based and secular organizations to provide
26 reentry supports and voluntary programming opportunities that have
27 been demonstrated to reduce prison violence and recidivism.
28 (d) Provide information on accessing mental health care
29 following release and, if appropriate, coordinate referral to
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

1 community mental health services.
2 (e) Ensure that inmates have potential employer matches in the
3 communities to which the inmates will return prior to each inmate's
4 initial parole hearing.

6 part 1 must provide for all of the following:
7 (a) An initial client assessment reimbursement of $200.00.
8 (b) A per diem reimbursement of not more than $70.00.

24 Michigan sources.

29 previous fiscal year. To calculate the cost per prisoner per day,
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

83
1 the department shall divide the prisoner-related costs by the total
2 number of prisoner days for each custody level and correctional
3 facility. For multilevel facilities, costs that cannot be
4 accurately allocated to each custody level may be included in the
5 calculation on a per-prisoner basis for each facility. A report
6 summarizing these calculations must be submitted not later than
7 January 15. Prisoner-related costs included in the cost per
8 prisoner per day calculation must include all expenditures for the
9 following, from all fund sources:
10 (a) New custody staff training.

23 (n) Transportation.
24 (o) Health care.
25 (p) Correctional facilities.

4 Sec. 705. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
5 department shall report all of the following regarding critical
6 incidents by facility:
7 (a) Within 72 hours of occurrence, any critical incident
8 occurring at a correctional facility. The report must identify the
9 facility at which the incident occurred.
10 (b) Not later than March 1, the number of critical incidents
11 occurring each month at each facility during the previous calendar
12 year, categorized by type and severity of each incident.
13 (2) As used in this section, "critical incident" includes a
14 prisoner assault on staff that results in a serious physical injury
15 to staff, an escape or attempted escape, a prisoner disturbance
16 that causes facility operation concerns, the implementation of a
17 phase plan or similar significant restriction on activity within a
18 facility, a drug overdose or suspected overdose that results in
19 inpatient hospitalization, and an unexpected death of a prisoner.

1 individuals who are past their earliest release date and have not
