23 Sec. 708. If a pregnant prisoner in a facility funded from
24 appropriations in part 1 consents to a visitor being present, the
25 department shall allow 1 person to be present during the prisoner's
26 labor and delivery, in addition to a doula being present if the
27 pregnant prisoner wants to work with a doula. The person allowed to
28 accompany the prisoner must be an immediate family member, legal
29 guardian, spouse, or domestic partner. The department is authorized
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

86
1 to deny access to a visitor if the department has a safety concern
2 with that visitor's access. The department is authorized to conduct
3 a criminal background check on the visitor.
4 Sec. 709. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
5 department shall evaluate all prisoners at intake for substance use
6 disorders, serious developmental disorders, serious mental illness,
7 and other mental health disorders. Prisoners with serious mental
8 illness or serious developmental disorders must not be removed from
9 the general population as a punitive response to behavior caused by
10 their serious mental illness or serious developmental disorder. A
11 prisoner with serious mental illness or serious developmental
12 disorder that is unresponsive to treatment who presents a
13 persistent high violence risk or engages in severe disruptive
14 behavior may be placed in secure residential housing programs that
15 facilitate access to institutional programming and ongoing mental
16 health services funded from appropriations in part 1. A prisoner
17 with serious mental illness or serious developmental disorder who
18 is confined in these specialized housing programs must be evaluated
19 or monitored by a medical professional at a frequency of not less
20 than every 12 hours.
21 Sec. 710. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
22 department shall submit a report by April 1, for the previous
23 fiscal year, with the following information:
24 (a) The number of times prisoners were placed in punitive

29 following time periods:
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

87
1 (i) A continuous period of 3 months or longer but less than 6
2 months.
3 (ii) A continuous period of 6 months or longer but less than 12
4 months.
5 (iii) A continuous period of 12 months or longer.
6 (c) For any prisoner housed in administrative segregation for
7 12 months or longer, an explanation of the circumstances
8 surrounding the prisoner's placement in administrative segregation.
9 (d) The department shall report the number of prisoners
10 classified to an administrative segregation unit or cell who, at
11 any time during the current or a prior prison term, were diagnosed
12 with serious mental illness or have a developmental disorder.
13 Sec. 711. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
14 department shall do all of the following:
15 (a) Ensure that any inmate care and control staff in contact

20 number and types of staff receiving annual training under that
21 curriculum.
22 (b) Provide appropriate placement for prisoners less than 18
23 years of age who have serious mental illness, serious emotional
24 disturbance, or a serious developmental disorder and need to be
25 housed separately from the general population. Prisoners less than
26 18 years of age who have serious mental illness, serious emotional
27 disturbance, or a serious developmental disorder must not be
28 removed from an existing placement as a punitive response to
29 behavior caused by their serious mental illness, serious emotional
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

88
1 disturbance, or a serious developmental disorder. A prisoner who is
2 less than 18 years of age with serious mental illness or a serious
3 developmental disorder that is unresponsive to treatment who
4 presents a persistent high violence risk or engages in severe
5 disruptive behavior may be placed in secure residential housing
6 programs that facilitate access to institutional programming and
7 ongoing mental health services. A prisoner less than 18 years of
8 age with serious mental illness, serious emotional disturbance, or
9 a serious developmental disorder who is confined in these
10 specialized housing programs must be evaluated or monitored by a
11 medical professional at a frequency of not less than every 12
12 hours.
13 (c) Implement a specialized offender success program that
14 recognizes the needs of prisoners less than 18 years of age for
15 supervised offender success.
16 Sec. 712. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
17 department shall submit quarterly reports on the number of youth in
18 prison. The report must include, but not be limited to, all of the
19 following information:
20 (a) The total number of inmates less than 18 years of age who
21 are not on Holmes youthful trainee act status.
22 (b) The total number of inmates less than 18 years of age who
23 are on Holmes youthful trainee act status.
24 (c) The total number of inmates between the ages of 18 and 23
25 who are on Holmes youthful trainee act status.
26 Sec. 713. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
27 department shall submit a report on the number of prisoners who
28 lost visiting privileges. The report required under this section
29 must be submitted not later than November 15 and include data for
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

89
1 the previous fiscal year. The report must include all of the
2 following information:
3 (a) The number of prisoners who lost visiting privileges by
4 race and by violation type.
5 (b) The number of prisoners who applied to have visiting
6 privileges restored.
7 (c) The number of prisoners who had visiting privileges
8 restored.
9 (d) The number of prisoners who had visiting restrictions
10 extended.
11 Sec. 714. Funds appropriated in part 1 for intelligence unit
12 must be used by the department to maintain an intelligence unit to
13 conduct investigatory and intelligence operations for the
14 department. Intelligence operations must include, but not be
15 limited to, intelligence operations for prisoner phone services.
16 Sec. 715. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
17 department shall submit a preliminary report on the department's
18 plans to close, consolidate, or relocate any correctional facility
19 in the state. The preliminary report must be provided as soon as
20 reasonably possible before program closure, consolidation, or
21 relocation. The preliminary report must include the projected
22 savings to the state from closure, consolidation, or relocation of
23 the facility and must include a projection of the potential impact
24 on staff positions.
25 (2) After a prison closure, consolidation, or relocation, the
26 department shall submit a report on the actual savings achieved by
27 the department and the impact on staff positions. Savings amounts
28 and impact on staff positions must be itemized by facility. The
29 report required under this subsection must be submitted as soon as
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

90
1 reasonably possible after the prison closure, consolidation, or
2 relocation.
3 Sec. 716. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
4 department shall consult with the legislature and other appropriate
5 state agencies to develop a framework to provide investment in
6 communities that have formerly operational state correctional
7 facilities that have been closed. This framework must include plans
8 to ensure that vacant state correctional facilities do not become a
9 nuisance or danger to the community.
10 Sec. 717. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
11 department shall make an information packet for the families of
12 incoming prisoners available on the department's website. The
13 information packet must be reviewed not later than February 1 and
14 updated as necessary. The department may partner with external
15 advocacy groups and actual families of prisoners in the packet-
16 writing process to ensure that the information is useful and
17 complete. The packet must provide information on topics including,
18 but not limited to, all of the following:
19 (a) How to put money into prisoner accounts.
20 (b) How to make telephone calls or create Jpay email accounts.
21 (c) How to visit in person.
22 (d) Proper procedures for filing complaints or grievances.
23 (e) The rights of prisoners to physical and mental health
24 care.
25 (f) The purpose and importance of prisoners signing a medical
26 release of information form.
27 (g) How to utilize the offender tracking information system
28 (OTIS).
29 (h) Truth in sentencing and how it applies to minimum
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

91
1 sentences.
2 (i) The parole process.
3 (j) Guidance on the importance of the role of families in the
4 reentry process.
5 Sec. 718. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
6 department shall pursue all opportunities to reduce costs for
7 prisoners and prisoners' families for financial deposit fees and
8 commissary fees when the department negotiates or renews any
9 contract to provide these services.
10 Sec. 719. (1) Funds appropriated in part 1 for contraband
11 prevention must be used by the department to enhance a multifaceted
12 approach to contraband prevention that combines technology,
13 rigorous policies, vigilant staff, intelligence gathering, and a
14 commitment to addressing the root causes of contraband, all of
15 which are necessary for preventing contraband introductions and
16 maintaining safe and secure correctional facilities.
17 (2) Funds appropriated in part 1 for contraband prevention
18 must be used by the department to support the prevention of
19 contraband in correctional facilities, including increasing the
20 frequency and enhancing the methods of screening all items and all
21 individuals entering into correctional facilities. Under this
22 section, individuals who must be screened include, but are not
23 limited to, all department employees, contractors, third-party
24 vendors, visitors, and volunteers. Emergency response personnel may
25 be exempt from being screened when arriving at correctional
26 facilities in response to emergent emergencies.
27 (3) From the funds appropriated in part 1, the department
28 shall develop and implement contraband prevention policies that are
29 uniform across all correctional facilities.
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

92
1 (4) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for contraband
2 prevention, the department shall submit a report not later than
3 March 1 on contraband and prevention efforts in correctional
4 facilities. The report must include, but not be limited to, all of
5 the following:
6 (a) Prevention efforts and strategies utilized by the
7 department.
8 (b) Challenges faced by correctional staff and other staff in
9 addressing contraband.
10 (c) Ideas and recommendations on how the legislature can
11 better assist the department with contraband prevention efforts and
12 strategies.
13 Sec. 720. The department shall ensure that Policy Directive
14 04.01.110 "Access to Correctional Facilities" does not require
15 active legislative members in good standing to provide prior notice
16 before being granted access to a department facility when
17 conducting official legislative business.
18 Sec. 721. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
19 department shall conduct a 1-year pilot program covering at least 5
20 facilities selected by the department that does the following:
21 (a) At the selected facilities, all incarcerated individuals
22 participating in contact visits at the facility shall pass through
23 a body scanner at the conclusion of their visits to search for
24 contraband. If a body scanner is temporarily unavailable or
25 inoperable, the pilot site shall comply with existing policy
26 requiring a routine strip search after each contact visit. The
27 department shall document these body scanner searches.
28 (b) At the selected facilities, incarcerated individuals
29 participating in contact visits at the facility may also be
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

93
1 randomly selected to be strip searched to search for contraband at
2 the conclusion of a visit in addition to passing through the body
3 scanner. An incarcerated individual who is randomly selected to
4 participate in a strip search shall comply with all requirements.
5 This random sampling shall be selected via an impartial process,
6 such as a random number selection generator. The department shall
7 document these strip searches.
8 (2) The department shall retain the authority to conduct
9 suspicion-based strip searches of any individual who has
10 participated in a visit based on the belief that they may possess
11 contraband or be involved in smuggling. The department shall
12 document these suspicion-based searches.
13 (3) At the conclusion of the 1-year pilot program, the
14 department shall produce data including, but not limited to, the
15 following:
16 (a) The number of individuals who are searched using a body
17 scanner after a visit and the incidents of contraband being
18 discovered during this search process at the pilot facilities.
19 (b) The number of individuals who were randomly selected to be
20 strip searched after a contact visit and the incidents of
21 contraband being discovered during this search process at the pilot
22 facilities.
23 (c) The number of individuals who were subject to suspicion-
24 based searches after a contact visit at the pilot facilities and
25 the incidents of contraband being discovered during this search
26 process at the pilot facilities.
27 (d) The total number of substance use and smuggling
28 misconducts issued during the year within each facility.
29 (e) Any other information the department believes should be
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

94
1 publicly released related to the results of the pilot.
2 (4) The department shall review the process of conducting
3 strip searches to ensure that the search process is conducted in a
4 consistent manner that allows for complete and thorough searches
5 while minimizing the time that a person is in a state of complete
6 undress.
7 Sec. 722. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
8 department shall provide a report, by facility, not later than
9 April 1 on the prisoner benefit fund. The report must include, for
10 each facility, all of the following information:
11 (a) The balance of the fund as of September 30 of the previous
12 fiscal year.
13 (b) The total amount deposited into the fund in the previous
14 fiscal year, categorized by the source of deposit.
15 (c) The total amount spent from the fund in the previous
16 fiscal year.
17 (d) The general categories of the expenditure of the funds and
18 the amounts spent by category.
19 Sec. 723. From the funds appropriated in part 1, it is the
20 intent of the legislature that except as otherwise provided under
21 this section, the department shall allow a prisoner at least 1 in-
22 person visit per 6-month period, regardless of the prisoner's
23 violation status, and including a prisoner who is on restricted
24 visitation due to substance use. However, a prisoner who is on
25 restricted visitation for violence against other prisoners or
26 violence against any department staff is excluded from the 1 in-
27 person visit requirement under this section. In addition, the
28 department shall do all of the following regarding the visits
29 required under this section:
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

95
1 (a) Explore the use of staggered time frames for visiting sign
2 up.
3 (b) Ensure visitation areas are child-friendly by providing
4 toys and other child-friendly games and activities.
5 (c) Work with the vending contractor to provide healthy food
6 choices in vending machines located in visitation areas of
7 correctional facilities subject to there being sufficient demand to
8 consistently sell these products.
9 (d) Provide a report, not later than April 1, detailing the
10 efforts taken to meet the requirements of subdivisions (a), (b),
11 and (c) and the outcomes of these changes and effects on visitation
12 privileges for prisoners.
13 Sec. 724. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
14 department shall provide a report not later than April 1 that
15 includes the number of medical accommodations issued for
16 wheelchairs, by type and facility. This report must also include a
17 count of the number of wheelchairs available for use at each
18 facility, as well as the number of incarcerated persons assigned
19 institutional jobs as wheelchair attendants, and the number of
20 individuals with an accommodation for an attendant.
21 Sec. 725. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
22 department shall provide a copy of the medical examiner's report
23 and autopsy report, subject to appropriate redactions consistent
24 with the freedom of information act, 1976 PA 442, MCL 15.231 to
25 15.246, to the subcommittee chairs, vice chairs, and minority vice
26 chairs, as well as the legislative corrections ombudsman. These
27 reports shall be shared within 30 days of being received by the
28 department. This section is not subject to boilerplate requirements
29 under this act requiring the posting or dissemination of
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

96
1 information to the public.
2
3 ONE-TIME APPROPRIATIONS
4 Sec. 801. (1) The one-time funds appropriated in part 1 for
5 Goodwill Flip the Script must be expended for administration of the
6 program. The program must serve a population of individuals aged 16
7 to 39 and must target individuals who are entering the criminal
8 justice system for the first or second time. The program must
9 assist those individuals through the following program types:
10 (a) Alternative sentencing programs in partnership with a
11 local district or circuit court.
12 (b) Educational recovery for special adult populations with
13 high rates of illiteracy.
14 (c) Career development and continuing education.
15 (d) Financial counseling and coaching services.
16 (2) Not later than March 30, Goodwill Flip the Script shall
17 report on all of the following:
18 (a) Program performance measurements.
19 (b) The number of individuals diverted from incarceration.
20 (c) The number of individuals served.
21 (d) The outcomes of participants who completed the program.
22 Sec. 802. From the one-time funds appropriated in part 1 for
23 peer-supported reentry, the department shall conduct a competitive
24 process for selecting an organization that primarily employs
25 individuals who have been incarcerated to provide reentry or other
26 supportive services, as determined by the department, to those
27 leaving prison and returning to 1 or more regions of this state.

6 Sec. 202. The appropriations under this part and part 1 are
7 subject to the management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1101
8 to 18.1594.
9 Sec. 203. As used in this part and part 1:
10 (a) "Department" means the department of education.
11 (b) "DHHS" means the department of health and human services.
12 (c) "District" means a local school district as that term is
13 defined in section 6 of the revised school code, 1976 PA 451, MCL
14 380.6, or a public school academy as that term is defined in
15 section 5 of the revised school code, 1976 PA 451, MCL 380.5.
16 (d) "FTE" means full-time equated position in the classified
17 service of this state.
18 (e) "HHS" means the United States Department of Health and
19 Human Services.
20 (f) "Standard report recipients" means the senate and house
21 appropriations committees, the senate and house appropriations
22 subcommittees on the department, the senate and house fiscal
23 agencies, the senate and house policy offices, and the state budget
24 office.

26 section 3 of 1921 PA 2, MCL 17.3, transfers funds from an amount
27 appropriated under part 1, the legislature may, by a concurrent
28 resolution adopted by a majority of the members elected to and
29 serving in each house, intertransfer funds within part 1 for the
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

106
1 particular department, board, commission, officer, or institution.
2 Sec. 205. (1) The department shall use the internet to fulfill
3 the reporting requirements of this part and shall make each report
4 readily accessible to the public and conspicuously post each
5 required report in a single archivable location on the department's
6 website not later than the due date required for each report.
7 (2) In addition to placing all reports required in the current
8 fiscal year on the department's website, the department shall
9 maintain on its website all reports placed on the website from
10 previous fiscal years posted by fiscal year in the same single
11 archivable location.
12 (3) The department shall transmit all required reports for the
13 current fiscal year to the standard report recipients and any other
14 required recipients by email. The email shall include a copy of the
15 report and a link to access the report online.
16 Sec. 206. The department shall receive and retain copies of
17 all reports funded from appropriations in part 1. The department

19 and long-term retention of records. The department may
20 electronically retain copies of reports unless otherwise required
21 by federal and state guidelines.

22 Sec. 222. The department shall not take disciplinary action
23 against an employee of the department because the employee
24 communicates with a member of the legislature or legislative staff,
25 unless the communication is prohibited by law and the department is
26 exercising its authority as provided by law.
27 Sec. 223. (1) The department shall maximize utilization of its
28 in-person state workforce. The department shall prioritize
29 occupancy utilization of office space for each division within the
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

113
1 department. Employees with job responsibilities that require the
2 employees to serve in their capacities outside of an office shall
3 be monitored each pay period to ensure all work hours reported on
4 the timesheet were actually worked.

6 the office of the state employer on in-person work and utilization
7 and occupancy rates of state buildings to ensure in-person work is
8 optimized and occupancy rates are 80% or higher, subject to market
9 conditions.
10 (3) The department shall adhere to civil service rules and
11 regulations that state the standard biweekly work period for a
12 full-time employee in the classified service of this state is the
13 equivalent of 80 hours of work. The department shall establish
14 policies and processes to ensure all employees are working their
15 jobs during agreed-upon business hours.
16 (4) The office of the state employer must create and implement
17 an occupancy utilization uniform policy on occupancy, utilization,
18 and in-person and remote work. The office of the state employer
19 must make each uniform policy publicly available on the
20 department's website.

6 Michigan Schools for the Deaf and Blind may promote its residential
7 program as a possible appropriate option for children who are deaf
8 or hard of hearing or who are blind or visually impaired. From the

12 be disbursed to Michigan public and cooperative libraries pursuant
13 to the state aid to public libraries act, 1977 PA 89, MCL 397.551
14 to 397.576. The remaining $375,000.00 shall be allocated to the
15 department's eLibrary services for the purpose of expanding the
16 Michigan eLibrary's free online access to articles, books, and
17 other research information to the public.

8 Sec. 202. The appropriations under this part and part 1 are
9 subject to the management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1101
10 to 18.1594.
11 Sec. 203. As used in this part and part 1:
12 (a) "Department" means the department of environment, Great
13 Lakes, and energy.
14 (b) "Director" means the director of the department.
15 (c) "FTE" means full-time equated.
16 (d) "IDG" means interdepartmental grant.
17 (e) "NPDES" means the national pollutant discharge elimination
18 system.
19 (f) "Standard report recipients" means the senate and house
20 appropriations committees, the senate and house appropriations
21 subcommittees on environment, Great Lakes, and energy, the senate
22 and house fiscal agencies, the senate and house policy offices, and
23 the state budget office.
24 Sec. 204. If the state administrative board, acting under
25 section 3 of 1921 PA 2, MCL 17.3, transfers funds from an amount
26 appropriated under part 1, the legislature may, by a concurrent
27 resolution adopted by a majority of the members elected to and
28 serving in each house, intertransfer funds within part 1 for the
29 particular department, board, commission, officer, or institution.
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

143
1 Sec. 205. (1) The department shall use the internet to fulfill
2 the reporting requirements of this part and shall make each report
3 readily accessible to the public and conspicuously post each
4 required report in a single archivable location on the department's
5 website not later than the due date required for each report.
6 (2) In addition to placing all reports required in the current
7 fiscal year on the department's website, the department shall
8 maintain on its website all reports placed on the website from
9 previous fiscal years posted by fiscal year in the same single
10 archivable location.
11 (3) The department shall transmit all required reports for the
12 current fiscal year to the standard report recipients and any other
13 required recipients by email. The email shall include a copy of the
14 report and a link to access the report online.
15 Sec. 206. The department shall receive and retain copies of
16 all reports funded from appropriations in part 1. The department
17 shall follow federal and state law and guidelines for short-term
18 and long-term retention of records. The department may
19 electronically retain copies of reports unless otherwise required
20 by federal and state guidelines.
21 Sec. 207. (1) The department shall cooperate with the
22 department of technology, management, and budget to maintain a
23 searchable website accessible by the public at no cost that
24 includes, but is not limited to, all of the following for the
25 department:
26 (a) Fiscal year-to-date expenditures by category.
27 (b) Fiscal year-to-date expenditures by appropriation unit.
28 (c) Fiscal year-to-date payments to a selected vendor,
29 including the vendor name, payment date, payment amount, and
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

144
1 payment description.
2 (2) The department shall cooperate with the department of
3 technology, management, and budget to update the searchable website
4 on a quarterly basis.
5 Sec. 208. (1) In addition to any other requirements under this
6 part, if the department is authorized under this part to expend
7 funds in addition to those appropriated in part 1, the department
8 must do all of the following:
9 (a) Not later than December 1, provide a report to the
10 chairpersons of the senate and house appropriations committees, the
11 senate and house fiscal agencies, and the state budget office that
12 details all of the following:
13 (i) The type of funding received during the previous fiscal
14 year that was authorized in part 2 of the article that made
15 appropriations for the department in the previous fiscal year.
16 (ii) When the funding was received.
17 (iii) The amount of funding received.
18 (iv) How much of the funding was spent and for what purpose or
19 purposes.
20 (b) Not later than 60 days after receipt of funds authorized
21 under this part, provide a report to the chairpersons of the senate
22 and house appropriations committees, the senate and house fiscal
23 agencies, and the state budget office that details all of the
24 following:
25 (i) The type of funding received.
26 (ii) When the funding was received.
27 (iii) The amount of funding received.
28 (iv) The anticipated or actual amount to be spent and the
29 specified purpose or purposes.

1 (c) Not later than February 15, provide a report to the
2 chairpersons of the senate and house appropriations committees, the

4 includes an estimate of funding authorized by this part that the
5 department anticipates it will receive in the subsequent fiscal
6 year, and identifies all of the following:

15 shall prepare and submit a report that provides estimates of the

17 close of the previous fiscal year. The report must summarize the
18 projected year-end general fund/general purpose appropriation
19 lapses by major departmental program or program areas. The state

21 recipients and to the chairpersons of the senate and house
22 appropriations committees.
23 Sec. 210. Not later than 14 days after the release of the
24 executive budget recommendation, the department shall cooperate

26 estimated state restricted fund balances, state restricted fund

28 previous 2 fiscal years. The report must be submitted to the
29 standard report recipients and to the chairpersons of the senate
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

146
1 and house appropriations committees.
2 Sec. 211. (1) Not later than November 15, the department shall
3 disclose on a publicly accessible website private and other third-
4 party funds received by the department in the previous fiscal year.
5 The report must include all of the following information:
6 (a) The amount of funding received.
7 (b) The specific source of funding received.
8 (c) The purpose for which funding was expended.
9 (d) The amount of any remaining funds.
10 (2) The report must be submitted to the standard report
11 recipients and to the chairpersons of the senate and house
12 appropriations committees.
13 Sec. 212. Consistent with section 217 of the management and
14 budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1217, each department and agency
15 receiving appropriations in part 1 shall prepare a report on out-
16 of-state travel expenses by not later than January 1. The report
17 must list all travel by classified and unclassified employees
18 outside this state in the previous fiscal year that was funded in
19 whole or in part with funds appropriated in the department's or
20 agency's budget. The department or agency shall submit the report
21 to the standard report recipients and to the senate and house
22 appropriations committees. The report must include all of the
23 following information:
24 (a) The dates of each travel occurrence.
25 (b) The total transportation and related expenses of each
26 travel occurrence and the proportions funded with state general
27 fund/general purpose revenues, state restricted revenues, federal
28 revenues, and other revenues.
29 Sec. 213. On a quarterly basis, the department or agency
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

147
1 receiving appropriations in part 1 shall report on the number of
2 full-time equated positions in pay status by civil service
3 classification, including a comparison by line item of the number
4 of full-time equated positions authorized from funds appropriated
5 in part 1 to the actual number of full-time equated positions
6 employed by the department at the end of the reporting period. The
7 report must be submitted to the standard report recipients and to

9 Sec. 214. Not later than April 1, the department shall report
10 on each specific policy change made to implement a public act
11 affecting the department that took effect during the previous
12 calendar year. The report must include reference to the public act
13 that necessitates the policy change. The department shall submit
14 the report to the standard report recipients, to the senate and

16 administrative rules.
17 Sec. 215. Not later than April 1, the department shall provide
18 to the standard report recipients a copy of its annual strategic
19 plan prepared in compliance with section 363 of the management and
20 budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1363. The plan must include the
21 mission, vision, goals, strategies, and performance measures of the
22 department.
23 Sec. 216. The department shall report on any court settlement
24 that may require further legislative review of state statutory
25 programs or regulations.
26 Sec. 217. (1) In addition to the funds appropriated in part 1,

17 30, 2027 are estimated at $19,364,100.00. From this amount, total

21 Sec. 219. To the extent possible, the department shall not
22 expend appropriations in part 1 until all existing authorized work
23 project funds available for the same purposes are exhausted.
24 Sec. 220. Not later than 6 months after the state budget
25 office issues work project letters, and again on or not later than
26 April 15, the department shall submit an annual report that
27 summarizes all work project accounts. The report must include all
28 of the following:
29 (a) A list of all work project accounts.
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

149
1 (b) The status of all work project accounts, including amounts
2 expended, amounts encumbered, and available balances for each
3 account.
4 (c) The amount of funds that lapsed from any previously
5 designated work project accounts, the name and description of the
6 work project account, and the funds that received the lapsed
7 amounts.
8 Sec. 221. To the extent permissible under section 261 of the
9 management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1261, all of the
10 following apply to the expenditure of funds appropriated in part 1:
11 (a) The funds must not be used for the purchase of foreign
12 goods or services, or both, if competitively priced and of
13 comparable quality American goods or services, or both, are
14 available.
15 (b) Preference must be given to goods or services, or both,
16 manufactured or provided by Michigan businesses, if they are
17 competitively priced and of comparable quality.
18 (c) Preference must be given to goods or services, or both,
19 that are manufactured or provided by Michigan businesses owned and
20 operated by veterans, if they are competitively priced and of
21 comparable quality.
22 Sec. 222. The department shall not take disciplinary action
23 against an employee of the department because the employee
24 communicates with a member of the legislature or legislative staff
25 unless the communication is prohibited by law and the department is
26 exercising its authority as provided by law.
27 Sec. 223. (1) The department shall maximize utilization of its
28 in-person state workforce. The department shall prioritize
29 occupancy utilization of office space for each division within the
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

150
1 department. Employees with job responsibilities that require the
2 employees to serve in their capacities outside of an office shall
3 be monitored each pay period to ensure all work hours reported on
4 the timesheet were actually worked.
5 (2) The department shall comply with requirements set by the
6 office of the state employer to meet the in-person and vacancy rate
7 objectives outlined by the office of the state employer.
8 (3) The department shall adhere to the rules and regulations
9 of civil service, which state that the standard biweekly work
10 period for a full-time employee in the classified service is the
11 equivalent of 80 hours of work. The department shall establish
12 policies and processes to ensure all employees are working their
13 jobs during agreed-upon business hours.
14 (4) The office of the state employer must create and implement
15 an occupancy utilization uniform policy on occupancy, utilization,
16 and in-person and remote work. The office of the state employer
17 must make each uniform policy publicly available on the
18 department's website.
19 Sec. 224. Each agency shall complete a space utilization
20 assessment by July 1, 2027, using a form developed by the
21 department of technology, management, and budget for all space
22 assigned under its building occupancy agreement and leased office
23 locations and posted on their website. Agencies shall develop, in
24 coordination with the department of technology, management, and
25 budget, a plan to reduce, consolidate, or otherwise optimize
26 assigned space. The plans shall prioritize the use of state-owned
27 facilities, wherever possible, and comply with department of
28 technology, management, and budget-established space standards
29 unless an exception is approved by the department of technology,

11 Sec. 258. (1) For any permit application, as defined in part
12 13 of the natural resources and environmental protection act, 1994
13 PA 451, MCL 324.1301 to 324.1317, the department shall approve or
14 deny the permit application by the processing deadline. For any
15 permit application where the department fails to approve or deny
16 the permit by the processing deadline, the department shall refund
17 the applicant an amount equal to 15% of the greater of the
18 following, as applicable:
19 (a) The amount of the application fee for that permit.
20 (b) If an assessment or other fee is charged on an annual or
21 other periodic basis by the department to a person holding the
22 permit for which the application was submitted, the amount of the
23 first periodic charge of that assessment or other fee for that
24 permit.
25 (2) The department shall report annually on the fiscal impact
26 of the discounts under this section to the standard report
27 recipients.

11 department shall compile a report by November 1 of every fiscal
12 year ending in an odd number on the status of the implementation
13 plan for the western Lake Erie basin collaborative agreement. In an
14 effort to learn more about the presence and timing of harmful algal
15 blooms, the report must contain all of the following:

17 at the 4 major wastewater treatment plants.
18 (b) A description of the grants that have been awarded.
19 (c) A description of the work that has commenced on the issue
20 of dissolved reactive phosphorus, the expected objectives and
21 outcomes of that work, and a list of the parties involved in that
22 effort.
23 (d) A description of the efforts and outcomes aimed at the
24 total phosphorus reduction for the River Raisin watershed.

17 section 3 of 1921 PA 2, MCL 17.3, transfers funds from an amount
18 appropriated under part 1, the legislature may, by a concurrent

22 Sec. 205. (1) A department or agency shall use the internet to
23 fulfill the reporting requirements of this part and shall make each
24 report readily accessible to the public and conspicuously post each
25 required report in a single archivable location on the department's
26 or agency's website not later than the due date required for each
27 report.
28 (2) In addition to placing all reports required in the current
29 fiscal year on the department's or agency's website, a department
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

198
1 or agency shall maintain on its website all reports placed on the
2 website from previous fiscal years posted by fiscal year in the
3 same single archivable location.
4 (3) A department or agency shall transmit all required reports
5 for the current fiscal year to the standard report recipients and
6 any other required recipients by email. The email shall include a
7 copy of the report and a link to access the report online.
8 Sec. 206. A department or agency shall receive and retain
9 copies of all reports funded from appropriations in part 1. A
10 department or agency shall follow federal and state law and
11 guidelines for short-term and long-term retention of records. A
12 department or agency may electronically retain copies of reports
13 unless otherwise required by federal and state guidelines.
14 Sec. 207. (1) A department or agency shall cooperate with the
15 MDTMB to maintain a searchable website accessible by the public at
16 no cost that includes, but is not limited to, all of the following
17 for the department or agency:
18 (a) Fiscal year-to-date expenditures by category.
19 (b) Fiscal year-to-date expenditures by appropriation unit.
20 (c) Fiscal year-to-date payments to a selected vendor,
21 including the vendor name, payment date, payment amount, and
22 payment description.
23 (2) A department or agency shall cooperate with the MDTMB to
24 update the searchable website on a quarterly basis.

5 Sec. 209. Not later than December 15, the state budget office
6 shall prepare and submit a report that provides estimates of the
7 total GF/GP appropriation lapses at the close of the previous
8 fiscal year. The report must summarize the projected year-end GF/GP

12 and house appropriations committees.

14 executive budget recommendation, a department or agency shall
15 cooperate with the state budget office to provide an annual report
16 on estimated state restricted fund balances, state restricted fund
17 projected revenues, and state restricted fund expenditures for the
18 previous 2 fiscal years. The report must be submitted to the
19 standard report recipients and to the chairpersons of the senate
20 and house appropriations committees.

22 shall report on private and other third-party funds received by the
23 department or agency in the previous fiscal year. The report must
24 include the amount of funding received, the specific source of
25 funding received, the purpose for which funding was expended, and
26 the amount of any remaining funds. The report must be submitted to
27 the standard report recipients and to the chairpersons of the
28 senate and house appropriations committees.

1 budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1217, a department or agency shall

20 department or agency or the office of the auditor general at the

23 report recipients.

5 strategic plan prepared in compliance with section 363 of the

11 statutory programs or regulations.

13 not expend appropriations under part 1 until all existing

29 Sec. 221. To the extent permissible under section 261 of the
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

203
1 management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1261, all of the
2 following apply to the expenditure of funds appropriated in part 1:
3 (a) The funds must not be used for the purchase of foreign
4 goods or services, or both, if competitively priced and of
5 comparable quality American goods or services, or both, are
6 available.
7 (b) Preference must be given to goods or services, or both,
8 manufactured or provided by Michigan businesses, if they are
9 competitively priced and of comparable quality.
10 (c) Preference must be given to goods or services, or both,
11 that are manufactured or provided by Michigan businesses owned and
12 operated by veterans, if they are competitively priced and of
13 comparable quality.
14 Sec. 222. A department or agency shall not take disciplinary
15 action against an employee of the department or agency because the
16 employee communicates with a member of the legislature or
17 legislative staff unless the communication is prohibited by law and
18 the department or agency is exercising its authority as provided by
19 law.
20 Sec. 223. (1) A department or agency shall maximize the
21 efficiency of the state workforce and utilization and occupancy of
22 office space, leased or owned, for each division within the
23 department. Employees with job responsibilities that require
24 employees to serve in their capacities outside of an office shall
25 be monitored each pay period to ensure all work hours reported on
26 timesheets are actually worked.
27 (2) A department or agency shall comply with requirements set
28 forth by the office of the state employer on in-person work and
29 utilization and occupancy rates of state buildings to ensure in-
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

204
1 person work is optimized and occupancy rates are at least 80% or
2 higher, subject to market conditions.
3 (3) A department or agency shall adhere to civil service rules
4 and regulations that state the standard biweekly work period for a
5 full-time employee in the classified service of this state is the
6 equivalent of 80 hours of work. Each department or agency shall
7 establish policies and processes to ensure all employees are
8 working their jobs during agreed upon business hours.
9 (4) The office of the state employer must create and implement
10 uniform policies on occupancy, utilization, in-person work, and
11 remote work. The office of the state employer must make each
12 uniform policy publicly available on the department's or agency's
13 website.
14 Sec. 224. A department or agency shall complete a space
15 utilization assessment by July 1 using a form developed by the
16 MDTMB, for all space assigned under the department's or agency's
17 building occupancy agreement and leased office locations and shall
18 post the assessment on the department's or agency's website. A
19 department or agency shall develop, in coordination with the MDTMB,
20 a plan to reduce, consolidate, or otherwise optimize assigned
21 space. The plans must prioritize the use of state-owned facilities,
22 wherever possible, and comply with the MDTMB-established space
23 standards unless an exception is approved by the MDTMB.

12 authorized and available for expenditure through December 31, 2026,

21 are not limited to, completed projects coming in under budget or

24 Sec. 302. (1) The attorney general shall perform all legal
25 services, including representation before courts and administrative
26 agencies, rendering legal opinions, and providing legal advice to a
27 principal executive department or state agency. A principal
28 executive department or state agency shall not employ or enter into

17 council entities.

1 Sec. 630. On a quarterly basis, the auditor general shall
2 submit a report to the standard report recipients, the chairpersons
3 of the senate and house of representatives appropriations
4 committees, and the senate and house of representatives oversight
5 committees that contains all of the following information for each
6 project completed during the immediately preceding quarter:
7 (a) Audit title.
8 (b) Date the audit was initiated.
9 (c) Date the audit report was released.
10 (d) Results of the audit, including the number and type of
11 findings.
12 (e) Details regarding total auditor general resources spent on
13 the audit.
14 (f) To the extent authorized by law, details regarding any
15 inquiry, tip, or request related to the audit that the auditor
16 general received before initiating the audit.
17 Sec. 631. The auditor general shall conduct an audit of the
18 procedure used by the secretary of state to maintain and update the
19 voter rolls in accordance with the generally accepted government
20 accounting standard. The audit must be completed not later than
21 September 15.

8 (iii) How underlying assumptions are disclosed in the guide.
9 (4) The report must include the amount of money that each
10 school district received, on a per pupil basis, in foundation
11 allowances that was spent on Michigan public school employees'
12 retirement system costs in the immediately preceding fiscal year.
13 (5) The office of retirement services must post the most
14 recent year's comprehensive annual financial report for each plan
15 described in subsection (1) not later than 90 days after the end of
16 the fiscal year.

1 Sec. 850. (1) In accordance with section 5 of article XI of

5 basis of actual 1% restricted sources total aggregate payroll of

8 part 1 of any appropriations act. The civil service commission
9 shall return any unexpended funds appropriated under this
10 subsection to each 1% fund source not later than 6 months after the
11 end of the fiscal year.
12 (2) The appropriations in part 1 are estimates of actual
13 charges based on payroll appropriations. With the approval of the
14 state budget director, the civil service commission may adjust
15 financing sources for civil service charges based on actual payroll
16 expenditures, if the adjustments do not increase the total
17 appropriation for the civil service commission.

19 the civil service commission by the end of the second fiscal
20 quarter.

22 purpose, financing from restricted sources must be credited to the
23 civil service commission. For restricted sources of funding within
24 the general fund that have the legislative authority for carryover,
25 if current spending authorization or revenues are insufficient to
26 accept the charge, the shortage must be taken from carryforward

29 service commission operating deductions first and civil service
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

1 commission obligations second. General fund dollars are

6 accounts, and COBRA, represents amounts, in part, included within
7 the various appropriations throughout state government for the
8 current fiscal year to fund the flexible spending account program
9 included within the civil service commission. Deposits against
10 state-sponsored group insurance, flexible spending accounts, and
11 COBRA for the flexible spending account program must be made from

18 Sec. 866. (1) The energy efficiency revolving fund is created
19 within the state treasury. The state treasurer may receive money or
20 other assets from any source for deposit into the energy efficiency
21 revolving fund. The state treasurer shall direct the investment of
22 the energy efficiency revolving fund. The state treasurer shall
23 credit to the energy efficiency revolving fund interest and
24 earnings from energy efficiency revolving fund investments.
25 (2) Money in the energy efficiency revolving fund at the close
26 of the fiscal year remains in the energy efficiency revolving fund
27 and does not lapse to the general fund.

29 energy efficiency revolving fund is appropriated to support
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

1 qualifying projects in accordance with the terms and conditions
2 established in subsections (4) and (5).
3 (4) The MDTMB shall provide oversight and direction for the
4 energy efficiency revolving fund, coordinate a call for projects,
5 and prioritize the award of projects that will contribute to a
6 reduction in this state's carbon footprint. State administrative
7 costs must be not more than 10% of the total project cost.
8 (5) The MDTMB shall set terms with agencies participating in
9 the energy efficiency revolving fund program that include the scope
10 of each project, funding commitments, data collection and reporting
11 requirements, and any other financial terms related to realization
12 of energy savings related to implementation of the project. The
13 MDTMB may enter into a memorandum of understanding to memorialize
14 these terms.

24 CAPITAL OUTLAY - UNIVERSITIES AND COMMUNITY COLLEGES
25 Sec. 873. (1) This section applies only to projects for
26 community colleges.
27 (2) State support is directed towards the remodeling and
28 additions, special maintenance, or construction of certain
29 community college buildings. The community college shall obtain or
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

267
1 provide for site acquisition and initial main utility installation
2 to operate the facility. The funding must be composed of local and
3 state shares and not more than 50% of a capital outlay project, not
4 including a lump-sum special maintenance project or remodeling and
5 addition project, for a community college may be appropriated from
6 state and federal funds, unless otherwise appropriated by the
7 legislature.
8 (3) An expenditure under this part and part 1 is authorized
9 when the release of the appropriation is approved by the board on
10 the recommendation of the director. The director may recommend to
11 the board the release of any appropriation in part 1 only after the
12 director is assured that the legal entity operating the community
13 college to which the appropriation is made has complied with this
14 part and part 1 and has matched the amounts appropriated as
15 required by this part and part 1. A release of funds in part 1 must
16 not exceed 50% of the total cost of planning and construction of
17 any project, not including lump-sum remodeling and additions and
18 special maintenance, unless otherwise appropriated by the
19 legislature. Further planning and construction of a project
20 authorized by this part and part 1 or applicable sections of the
21 management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1101 to 18.1594,
22 must be in accordance with the purpose and scope as defined and
23 delineated in the approved program statements and planning
24 documents. This part and part 1 are applicable to all projects for
25 which planning appropriations were made in previous PAs.
26 (4) The community college shall take the steps necessary to
27 secure available federal construction and equipment money for
28 projects funded for construction in this part and part 1 if an
29 application was not previously made. If there is a reasonable
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

268
1 expectation that a previous year unfunded application may receive
2 federal money in a subsequent year, the community college shall
3 take whatever action necessary to keep the application active.
4 Sec. 874. If university and community college matching
5 revenues are received in an amount less than the appropriations for
6 capital projects contained in this part and part 1, the state funds
7 must be reduced in proportion to the amount of matching revenue
8 received.
9 Sec. 875. (1) The director may require that community colleges
10 and universities that have an authorized project described in part
11 1 submit documentation regarding the project match and governing
12 board approval of the authorized project not more than 60 days
13 after the beginning of the fiscal year.
14 (2) If the documentation required by the director under
15 subsection (1) is not submitted, or does not adequately
16 authenticate the availability of the project match or governing
17 board approval of the authorized project, the director may
18 terminate the authorization. The authorization terminates 30 days
19 after the director notifies the JCOS of the intent to terminate the
20 project unless the JCOS approves an extension of the authorization.

17 Sec. 952. (1) The funds appropriated in part 1 for city,
18 village, and township revenue sharing are for grants to cities,
19 villages, and townships and must be distributed as provided in this
20 section.
21 (2) From the first $299,126,400.00 appropriated in part 1 for
22 city, village, and township revenue sharing, each city, village, or
23 township shall receive an amount equal to 100.0% of the revenue
24 sharing payment for which the city, village, or township received
25 under section 952(2) of article 5 of 2025 PA 22 rounded to the
26 nearest dollar.

28 village, and township revenue sharing after the distributions under
29 subsection (2) must be distributed as follows:
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

295
1 (a) 1/3 shall be distributed as taxable value payments as
2 provided under subsection (4).
3 (b) 1/3 must be distributed as unit type population payments
4 as provided under subsection (5).
5 (c) 1/3 must be distributed as yield equalization payments as
6 provided under subsection (6).
7 (4) A taxable value payment must be made to each city,
8 village, and township, determined as follows:
9 (a) Determine the per capita taxable value for each city,
10 village, and township by dividing the taxable value of that city,
11 village, or township by the population of that city, village, or
12 township.
13 (b) Determine the statewide per capita taxable value by
14 dividing the total taxable value of all cities, villages, and
15 townships by the total population of all cities, villages, and
16 townships.
17 (c) Determine the per capita taxable value ratio for each
18 city, village, and township by dividing the statewide per capita
19 taxable value by the per capita taxable value for that city,
20 village, or township.
21 (d) Determine the adjusted taxable value population for each
22 city, village, and township by multiplying the per capita taxable
23 value ratio as determined under subdivision (c) for that city,
24 village, or township by the population of that city, village, or
25 township.
26 (e) Determine the total statewide adjusted taxable value
27 population, which is the sum of all adjusted taxable value
28 population for all cities, villages, and townships.
29 (f) Determine the taxable value payment rate by dividing the
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

296
1 amount to be distributed under this subsection by the total
2 statewide adjusted taxable value population as determined under
3 subdivision (e).
4 (g) Determine the taxable value payment for each city,
5 village, and township by multiplying the result under subdivision
6 (f) by the adjusted taxable value population for that city,
7 village, or township.

17 (a) The guaranteed tax base is the maximum combined state and

19 fiscal year to each city, village, and township for a local tax
20 effort, not to exceed 0.02, if an amount equal to the amount
21 described in subsection (3)(c) is distributed to cities, villages,

23 guaranteed tax base.
24 (b) The full yield equalization payment to each city, village,
25 and township is the product of the amounts determined under
26 subparagraphs (i) and (ii):
27 (i) An amount greater than zero that is equal to the difference
28 between the guaranteed tax base determined in subdivision (a) and
29 the per capita taxable value of the city, village, or township.

2 Sec. 956. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for
3 financially distressed cities, villages, or townships, the
4 department of treasury shall create and operate a grant program to
5 award grants to cities, villages, and townships that have 1 or more
6 conditions that indicate probable financial distress, as determined
7 by the department of treasury. A city, village, or township with 1
8 or more conditions that indicate probable financial distress may
9 apply in a manner determined by the department of treasury for a
10 grant to pay for specific projects or services that move the city,
11 village, or township toward financial stability. Grants must be
12 used for specific projects or services that move the city, village,
13 or township toward financial stability. The city, village, or
14 township must use the grants under this section to do 1 or more of
15 the following:
16 (a) Make payments to reduce unfunded accrued liability.
17 (b) Repair or replace critical infrastructure and equipment
18 owned or maintained by the city, village, or township.
19 (c) Reduce debt obligations.
20 (d) Pay for costs associated with a transition to shared
21 services with another jurisdiction.
22 (e) Administer other projects that move the city, village, or
23 township toward financial stability.
24 (2) The department of treasury shall award not more than
25 $2,000,000.00 to any city, village, or township under this section.
26 (3) Not later than 30 days after grants are awarded, the
27 department of treasury shall submit a report to the standard report
28 recipients that includes all of the following for each grant
29 recipient.
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

303
1 (a) The name of the grant recipient.
2 (b) The date the grant was approved.
3 (c) The amount of the grant.
4 (d) A description of the project or projects that will be paid
5 by the grant.
6 Sec. 957. A term that is defined in the Glenn Steil state

8 same meaning when used in sections 950 to 956.
9 Sec. 959. (1) The department of treasury shall distribute
10 funds appropriated in part 1 for public safety revenue sharing
11 grants as provided for in subsection (9) and as follows:
12 (a) $3,250,000.00 to the MDHHS to establish and administer a
13 grant program to award funds to community violence intervention
14 programs.
15 (b) $35,062,500.00 for a public safety assistance payment to
16 each city, village, or township. The public safety assistance
17 payment must be calculated as follows:
18 (i) Determine the average violent crime count for each city,
19 village, and township by adding the 2 highest annual violent crime
20 counts for each city, village, and township from the 3 most
21 recently available annual crime reports published by the MDSP as of
22 the first day of the current fiscal year and dividing by 2.
23 (ii) Determine the statewide total violent crime count by
24 summing the average violent crime count for each city, village, and
25 township as determined under subparagraph (i).
26 (iii) Determine the proportional factor for each city, village,
27 and township by dividing the average violent crime count for each
28 city, village, and township as determined under subparagraph (i) by
29 the statewide total violent crime count determined under
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

304
1 subparagraph (ii).
2 (iv) Multiply the proportional factor determined in
3 subparagraph (iii), for each city, village, and township by the total
4 amount available for distribution under this subdivision, and round
5 to the nearest dollar.
6 (2) A public safety assistance payment to a city, village, or
7 township as determined under subsection (1)(b) is limited to not
8 more than 25% of the total amount available for distribution under
9 subsection (1)(b).
10 (3) All of the following apply to a distribution under
11 subsection (1)(b):
12 (a) A city, village, or township must use the distribution
13 only for operational and capital expenditures that serve the
14 purposes of public safety.
15 (b) Not less than 75% of a public safety assistance payment
16 distributed under subsection (1)(b) to a city, village, or township
17 must be used to fund, either directly or indirectly through a
18 subgrant to another governmental entity, a law enforcement agency
19 or law enforcement officers as defined in section 2 of the Michigan
20 commission on law enforcement standards act, 1965 PA 203, MCL
21 28.602.
22 (c) Not more than 25% of a public safety assistance payment
23 distributed under subsection (1)(b) to a city, village, or township
24 must be used to fund other non-law-enforcement-related public
25 safety purposes, which include, but are not limited to: public
26 safety initiatives to improve recruitment or retention efforts;
27 training programs; equipment purchases; programs designed to reduce
28 identified risks to public safety; crime diversion programs;
29 operational emergency medical or firefighter services; or capital
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

305
1 improvements to public safety buildings or structures. All local
2 public safety initiative expenses must be related to public safety
3 and designed to reduce identified risks to public safety and cannot
4 include unproven intervention solutions to community violence.
5 (d) A distribution made under subsection (1)(b) must not be
6 used for the following nonoperating expenses:
7 (i) Pension and other post employee benefit (OPEB) payments.
8 (ii) Lawsuits and claims payments.
9 (iii) Debt service payments.
10 (iv) The acquisition or use of a vehicle weighing more than
11 15,000 pounds that is designed or used for a tactical police
12 purpose.
13 (v) The acquisition or use of facial recognition technology.
14 (vi) The acquisition or use of a chemical weapon.
15 (4) A city, village, or township may subgrant all or part of
16 the distribution under subsection (1)(b) if the subgrant is used
17 for the purpose of public safety as described under subsection (3).
18 (5) Subject to subsections (6), (7), and (8), not later than
19 November 30, the director of the MDSP shall provide the department
20 of treasury with a certified list that contains all of the
21 following:
22 (a) Base crime level.
23 (b) Current violent crime counts.
24 (c) Current violent crime rates, as determined by the director
25 of the MDSP.
26 (6) The current violent crime data described in subsection
27 (5)(b) and (c) mean the calendar year annual violent crime data for
28 each city, village, and township received and finalized by the MDSP
29 during the immediately preceding state fiscal year and the 2
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

306
1 immediately preceding calendar years before the immediately
2 preceding state fiscal year.
3 (7) Crimes reported by a city, village, township, or reported
4 by a county on behalf of the city, village, or township, must be
5 included in the certified list under subsection (5), but crimes
6 reported by other authorities must be omitted from the certified
7 list under subsection (5).
8 (8) The certified list under subsection (5) must contain all
9 cities, villages, and townships in this state and must report a
10 zero for cities, villages, and townships that did not submit crime
11 data.
12 (9) $11,687,500.00 must be used for public safety assistance
13 payments to counties. The payment to each county must be calculated
14 by dividing the amount to be distributed under this subsection by
15 the total statewide adjusted taxable value population as determined
16 under section 955(3)(e) and multiplying the result by the adjusted
17 taxable value population for that county as determined under
18 section 955(3)(d). All of the following apply to a distribution
19 made under this subsection:
20 (a) A county must use the distribution only for operational
21 and capital expenditures that serve the purposes of public safety.
22 (b) Not less than 75% of a public safety assistance payment
23 distributed to a county under this subsection must be used to fund,
24 either directly or indirectly through a subgrant to another
25 governmental entity, a law enforcement agency or law enforcement
26 officers as defined in section 2 of the Michigan commission on law
27 enforcement standards act, 1965 PA 203, MCL 28.602.
28 (c) Not more than 25% of a public safety assistance payment
29 distributed to a county under this subsection must be used to fund
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

307
1 other non-law-enforcement-related public safety purposes, which
2 include, but are not limited to: public safety initiatives to
3 improve recruitment or retention efforts; training programs;
4 equipment purchases; programs designed to reduce identified risks
5 to public safety; crime diversion programs; operational emergency
6 medical or firefighter services; or capital improvements to public
7 safety buildings or structures. All local public safety initiative
8 expenses must be related to public safety and designed to reduce
9 identified risks to public safety and cannot include unproven
10 intervention solutions to community violence.
11 (d) A distribution made under this subsection must not be used
12 for the following nonoperating expenses:
13 (i) Pension and other post employee benefit (OPEB) payments.
14 (ii) Lawsuits and claims payments.
15 (iii) Debt service payments.
16 (iv) The acquisition or use of a vehicle weighing more than
17 15,000 pounds that is designed or used for a tactical police
18 purpose.
19 (v) The acquisition or use of facial recognition technology.
20 (vi) The acquisition or use of a chemical weapon.
21 (10) A county may subgrant all or part of the distribution
22 under subsection (9) if the subgrant is used for the purpose of
23 public safety as described in subsection (9).
24 (11) As used in subsections (1) to (8):
25 (a) "Base crime level" means the average of a city, village,
26 or township's 2 highest annual rates of violent crime, as certified
27 by the director of the MDSP and determined by the annual crime
28 reports published by the MDSP in the 3 calendar years immediately
29 preceding the current calendar year.
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

308
1 (b) "Population" means the counts, as defined by the Federal
2 Bureau of Investigation and used by the director of the MDSP, to
3 determine the population for each city, village, and township.
4 (c) "Violent crime" means that term as defined by the director
5 of the MDSP in accordance with the department's incident crime
6 reporting program and the corresponding annual crime reports.
7 (d) "Violent crime count" means the number of violent crimes
8 based on victim counts, as certified by the director of the MDSP.
9 When a victim is connected to multiple offenses, the victim is
10 counted under the highest-ranked offense, as defined by the
11 director of the MDSP.
12 (e) "Violent crime rate" means the number of crimes per
13 100,000 people, determined by dividing a particular city, village,
14 or township violent crime count by the population, then multiplying
15 by 100,000 and rounding to the nearest whole number.
16 (12) As used in this section:
17 (a) "Chemical weapon" means a munition or device that is
18 specifically designed to cause death or other harm through a toxic
19 chemical that would be released as a result of the employment of
20 the munition or device.
21 (b) "Facial recognition technology" means an automated or a
22 semiautomated technological process that assists in identifying or
23 verifying an individual based on the individual's face.
24 (13) The department of treasury shall submit a report to the
25 standard report recipients by August 30 that includes the following
26 information for each grant authorized under this section:
27 (a) The date the grant was disbursed.
28 (b) The grant recipient.
29 (c) The planned use of grant funds, including whether the
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

309
1 grant is to be used as a subgrant.
2 (14) It is the intent of the legislature that not less than
3 $50,000,000.00 be appropriated for the purposes outlined in this
4 section in fiscal years 2026-2027 and 2027-2028.

13 Bowman bingo act, 1972 PA 382, MCL 432.101 to 432.152, including
14 expenditures for contractually mandated payments for vendor
15 commissions, contractually mandated payments for instant tickets
16 intended for resale, the contractual costs of providing and
17 maintaining the online system communications network, and incentive
18 and bonus payments to lottery retailers.

20 appropriated 1% of the lottery's immediately preceding fiscal
21 year's gross sales for promotion and advertising.

22 budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1242, and on the approval of the
23 state building authority, the department of treasury may expend
24 from the general fund of this state during the fiscal year an
25 amount necessary to meet the cash flow requirements of those state
26 building authority projects solely for lease to a state agency
27 identified in both part 1 and this section, and for which state
28 building authority bonds or notes have not been issued, and for the
29 sole acquisition by the state building authority of equipment and
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

318
1 furnishings for lease to a state agency as permitted by 1964 PA
2 183, MCL 830.411 to 830.425, for which the issuance of bonds or
3 notes is authorized by an appropriations PA that is effective for
4 the immediately preceding fiscal year. Any general fund advances
5 for which state building authority bonds have not been issued must
6 bear an interest cost to the state building authority at a rate
7 that is not greater than the rate earned by the state treasurer's
8 common cash fund during the period in which the advances are
9 outstanding and are repaid to the general fund of this state.
10 (2) On sale of bonds or notes for the projects identified in
11 part 1 or for equipment as authorized by an appropriations PA and
12 in this section, the state building authority shall credit the
13 general fund of this state an amount equal to the amount expended
14 from the general fund plus interest, if any, as described in this
15 section.
16 (3) For state building authority projects for which bonds or
17 notes have been issued and on the request of the state building
18 authority, the state treasurer shall make advances without interest
19 from the general fund as necessary to meet cash flow requirements
20 for the projects. The state building authority shall reimburse the
21 state treasurer for the advances when the investments earmarked for
22 the financing of the projects mature.
23 (4) If a project identified in part 1 is terminated after
24 final design is complete, advances made on behalf of the state
25 building authority for the costs of final design must be repaid to
26 the general fund in a manner recommended by the director of the
27 state building authority.
28 Sec. 1102. (1) The state building authority shall not release
29 state building authority funding to a university or community
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

319
1 college to finance the construction or renovation of a facility
2 that collects revenue in excess of money required for the operation
3 of that facility unless the university or community college agrees
4 to use that excess revenue to reimburse the state building
5 authority. The excess revenue received by the state building
6 authority as reimbursement must be credited to the general fund to
7 offset rent obligations associated with the retirement of bonds
8 issued for the applicable facility. The auditor general shall
9 annually identify and audit the facilities that are subject to this
10 section. Costs associated with the administration of the audit must
11 be charged against money received by the state building authority
12 as reimbursement under this section.
13 (2) As used in this section, "revenue" includes state
14 appropriations, facility opening money, other state aid, indirect
15 cost reimbursement, and other revenue generated by the activities
16 of the facility.

14 Sec. 203. As used in this part and part 1:
15 (a) "AIDS" means acquired immunodeficiency syndrome.
16 (b) "CCBHC" means certified community behavioral health
17 clinic.
18 (c) "CMHSP" means a community mental health services program
19 as that term is defined in section 100a of the mental health code,
20 1974 PA 258, MCL 330.1100a.
21 (d) "CMS" means the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid
22 Services.
23 (e) "CPT" means current procedural terminology.
24 (f) "Current fiscal year" means the fiscal year ending
25 September 30, 2027.
26 (g) "Department" means the department of health and human
27 services.
28 (h) "Director" means the director of the department.
29 (i) "EPSDT" means early and periodic screening, diagnosis, and
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

349
1 treatment.
2 (j) "Federal poverty level" means the poverty guidelines
3 revised periodically and published in the Federal Register by the
4 Secretary of the United States Department of Health and Human
5 Services under the Secretary's authority to revise the poverty line
6 under 42 USC 9902.
7 (k) "FQHC" means federally qualified health center.
8 (l) "FTE" means full-time equated.
9 (m) "GME" means graduate medical education.
10 (n) "Health plan" means, at a minimum, an organization that
11 meets the criteria for delivering the comprehensive package of
12 services under the department's comprehensive health plan.
13 (o) "HEDIS" means health care effectiveness data and
14 information set.
15 (p) "HMO" means health maintenance organization.
16 (q) "IDEA" means the individuals with disabilities education
17 act, 20 USC 1400 to 1482.
18 (r) "IDG" means interdepartmental grant.
19 (s) "MCH" means maternal and child health.
20 (t) "Medicaid" means benefits under the medical assistance
21 program established under title XIX of the social security act, 42
22 USC 1396 to 1396w-8, and administered by the department under the
23 social welfare act, 1939 PA 280, MCL 400.1 to 400.119b.
24 (u) "Medicare" means benefits under the federal Medicare
25 program established under title XVIII of the social security act,
26 42 USC 1395 to 1395mmm.
27 (v) "MiCAFE" means Michigan's coordinated access to food for
28 the elderly.

1 this part.

3 welfare information system.

5 elderly.

7 resident review required under the omnibus budget reconciliation

9 1396r.
10 (aa) "PATH" means Partnership. Accountability. Training. Hope.
11 (bb) "PFAS" means perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl
12 substances.

22 September 30, 2026.

21 appropriated under part 1, the legislature may, by a concurrent
22 resolution adopted by a majority of the members elected to and

25 Sec. 205. (1) The department shall use the internet to fulfill
26 the reporting requirements of this part and shall make each report
27 readily accessible to the public and conspicuously post each
28 required report in a single archivable location on the department's
29 website not later than the due date required for each report.

5 archivable location.
6 (3) The department shall transmit all required reports for the
7 current fiscal year to the standard report recipients and any other
8 required recipients by email. The email shall include a copy of the
9 report and a link to access the report online.

23 against an employee of the department because the employee
24 communicates with a member of the legislature or legislative staff
25 unless the communication is prohibited by law and the department is
26 exercising its authority as provided by law.
27 Sec. 223. (1) The department shall maximize the utilization of
28 its in-person state workforce. The department shall prioritize
29 occupancy utilization of office space for each division within the
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

359
1 department. Employees with job responsibilities that require
2 employees to serve in their capacities outside of an office shall
3 be monitored each pay period to ensure all work hours reported on
4 timesheets are actually worked.
5 (2) The department shall comply with requirements set forth by
6 the office of the state employer on in-person work and utilization
7 and occupancy rates of state buildings to ensure in-person work is
8 optimized and occupancy rates are 80 percent or higher, subject to
9 market conditions.
10 (3) The department shall adhere to the rules and regulations
11 of civil service, which state that the standard biweekly work
12 period for a full-time employee in the classified services is the
13 equivalent of 80 hours of work. The department shall establish
14 policies and processes to ensure all employees are working their
15 jobs during agreed-upon business hours.
16 (4) The office of the state employer must create and implement
17 an occupancy utilization uniform policy on occupancy, utilization,
18 in-person, and remote work. The office of the state employer must
19 make each uniform policy publicly available on the department's
20 website.

1 Sec. 251. (1) In addition to funds appropriated in part 1 for
2 all programs and services, there is appropriated, for write-offs of
3 accounts receivable, deferrals, and for prior year obligations in
4 excess of applicable prior year appropriations, an amount equal to
5 total write-offs and prior year obligations, but not to exceed
6 amounts available in prior year revenues.
7 (2) The department's ability to satisfy appropriation fund
8 sources in part 1 is not limited to collections and accruals
9 pertaining to services provided in the current fiscal year and

12 penalty interest in an amount that exceeds the amount of the
