18 CHILD SUPPORT ENFORCEMENT
19 Sec. 401. (1) The appropriations in part 1 assume a total
20 federal child support incentive payment of $26,500,000.00.
21 (2) From the federal money received for child support
22 incentive payments, $12,000,000.00 must be retained by this state
23 and expended for child support program expenses.
24 (3) From the federal money received for child support
25 incentive payments, $14,500,000.00 must be paid to counties based
26 on each county's performance level for each of the performance
27 measures under 45 CFR 305.2.
28 (4) If the child support incentive payment to this state from
29 the federal government is greater than $26,500,000.00, then 100% of
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

377
1 the amount in excess must be retained by this state and is
2 appropriated until the total retained by this state reaches
3 $15,397,400.00.
4 (5) If the child support incentive payment to this state from
5 the federal government is greater than the amount needed to satisfy
6 subsections (1), (2), (3), and (4), the additional funds are
7 subject to appropriation by the legislature.
8 (6) If the child support incentive payment to this state from
9 the federal government is less than $26,500,000.00, then the state
10 share and the county share must each be reduced by 50% of the
11 shortfall.
12 Sec. 409. (1) If statewide retained child support collections
13 exceed $38,300,000.00, 75% of the amount in excess of
14 $38,300,000.00 is appropriated to legal support contracts. The
15 excess appropriation may be distributed to eligible counties to
16 supplement, but not supplant, county title IV-D funding.
17 (2) Each county whose retained child support collections in
18 the current fiscal year exceed its fiscal year 2004-2005 retained
19 child support collections, excluding tax offset and financial
20 institution data match collections in both the current fiscal year
21 and fiscal year 2004-2005, shall receive its proportional share of
22 the 75% excess appropriation.

24 are escheated, the state budget director is authorized to adjust

7 COMMUNITY SERVICES AND OUTREACH
8 Sec. 453. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for
9 homeless programs, the department shall allocate funds to the
10 emergency shelter program to support efforts of shelter providers
11 to move homeless individuals and households into permanent housing
12 as quickly as possible. The funds must be equal to or exceed the
13 amount that a provider would receive if the provider is paid a
14 $19.00 per diem rate. Expected outcomes are increased shelter
15 discharges to stable housing destinations, decreased recidivism
16 rates for shelter clients, and a reduction in the average length of
17 stay in emergency shelters.
18 (2) Not later than March 1 of the current fiscal year, the
19 department shall submit, to the standard report recipients, a
20 report on the total amount expended for the emergency shelter
21 program in the prior 2 fiscal years, the total number of shelter
22 nights provided, and the average length of stay in an emergency
23 shelter.
24 Sec. 454. The department shall allocate the full amount of
25 funds appropriated in part 1 for homeless programs to provide
26 services for homeless individuals and families, including, but not
27 limited to, third-party contracts for emergency shelter services.
28 Sec. 455. As a condition of receipt of federal TANF revenue,
29 after admitting a family to a homeless shelter, the homeless
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

379
1 shelter and human services agencies shall collaborate with the
2 department to obtain necessary TANF eligibility information on the
3 family as soon as possible. From the funds appropriated in part 1
4 for homeless programs, the department is authorized to make
5 allocations of TANF revenue only to the homeless shelters and human
6 services agencies that report necessary data to the department to
7 meet TANF eligibility reporting requirements. Homeless shelters or
8 human services agencies that do not report necessary data to the
9 department to meet TANF eligibility reporting requirements shall
10 not receive reimbursements that exceed the per diem amount the
11 homeless shelters or human service agencies received in fiscal year
12 2000. The use of TANF revenue under this section is not an ongoing
13 commitment of funding.
14 Sec. 456. From the funds appropriated in part 1 for homeless
15 programs, the department shall allocate $10,000.00 to reimburse
16 public service agencies that provide documentation of paying birth
17 certificate fees on behalf of category 1 homeless clients at county
18 clerk's offices. Each public service agency must be reimbursed for
19 the cost of the birth certificate fees quarterly until the
20 allocation is fully spent.

22 programs, the department shall allocate $8,500,000.00 of TANF
23 revenue to support family shelters or families who are homeless and
24 at risk of being homeless. Funds appropriated under this section
25 must be used as follows:
26 (a) $3,000,000.00 for emergency hotels for families
27 experiencing homelessness.

29 engaged with child welfare. This may include, but is not limited
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

380
1 to, eviction diversion, first month's rent and deposit, and utility
2 arrears.
3 (c) $2,000,000.00 for creating additional spaces at family
4 homeless shelters that have been in operation for at least 24
5 months.
6 Sec. 458. From the funds appropriated in part 1 for homeless
7 programs, the department shall require any entities receiving
8 direct or indirect state funds to report data to a Homeless
9 Management Information System that satisfies the baseline data
10 collection requirements.
11 Sec. 459. From the funds appropriated in part 1 for homeless
12 programs, the department shall allocate $2,000,000.00 of TANF
13 revenue to acquire and develop for individuals and families
14 noncongregate shelter that utilizes options under a Housing First
15 model and prioritizes providing stable and permanent housing
16 without preconditions or requirements, such as sobriety or
17 participation in treatment programs. Eligible uses for this funding
18 may include, but are not limited to, hotels, motels, dormitories,
19 recuperative care facilities, and other facilities that offer
20 noncongregate shelter.
21 Sec. 460. From the funds appropriated in part 1 for kids' food
22 basket, the department shall allocate $525,000.00 to Kids' Food
23 Basket. Kids' Food Basket shall use the funds to expand its
24 services to additional schools and communities. The funding may be
25 used to cover employee costs, food and supplies, equipment, and
26 other operational costs identified by the organization to support
27 its mission and goals.

27 Sec. 513. (1) The department shall not expend funds

ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

1 conditions are met:
2 (a) An appropriate placement is not available in this state,

5 child's home than the closest appropriate in-state placement, as
6 determined by the department's interstate compact office.
7 (c) The out-of-state facility meets all of the licensing
8 standards for a comparable facility in this state.
9 (d) The out-of-state facility meets all of the applicable
10 licensing standards of the state in which it is located.
11 (e) The department has visited the site of the out-of-state
12 facility; has reviewed the facility records, licensing records, and

14 for the child.

5 Sec. 550. (1) The department shall not offset against
6 reimbursements to counties or seek reimbursement from counties for

11 service or original status determination when initially submitted
12 by the county.

14 service provider shall submit a request for payment. A request for
15 payment submitted later than 12 months after the date of service
16 requires the provider to submit an exception request to the county
17 or the department for approval or denial.

19 reimbursement liability for a prior expenditure resulting from an

8 Sec. 578. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for foster
9 care payments, the department shall allocate Title IV-E passthrough
10 funds for educational programs to strengthen this state's child
11 welfare workforce. The department shall enter into contractual
12 arrangements with 1 or more state universities to provide bachelor
13 of social work and master of social work educational training,
14 including field placements and stipends for tuition and educational
15 expenses. In exchange, students completing eligible educational
16 programs are contractually obligated to work for Michigan child
17 welfare agencies for a minimum of 4 months for every semester they
18 receive the stipend. The matching funds for the Title IV-E funds
19 must be provided by the participating state universities from the
20 expenses incurred for training child welfare students who
21 participate in the program.

28 Sec. 604. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for state
29 disability assistance payments, the department shall operate a
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

406
1 state disability assistance program. Except as provided in
2 subsection (3), to be eligible for the program, an individual must
3 be a needy citizen of the United States or alien exempted from the
4 SSI citizenship requirement who is not less than 18 years of age,
5 or an emancipated minor, and meets 1 or more of the following
6 requirements:
7 (a) Is a recipient of SSI, Social Security, or medical
8 assistance due to disability or being 65 years of age or older.
9 (b) Is an individual with a physical or mental impairment that
10 meets federal SSI disability standards, except that the minimum
11 duration of the disability must be 90 days. Substance use disorder
12 alone is not a basis for eligibility.
13 (c) Is a resident of an adult foster care facility, a home for
14 the aged, a county infirmary, or a substance use disorder treatment
15 center.
16 (d) Is an individual receiving 30-day postresidential
17 substance use disorder treatment.
18 (e) Is an individual diagnosed as having AIDS.
19 (f) Is an individual receiving special education services
20 through a local intermediate school district.
21 (g) Is a caretaker of a disabled individual who meets the
22 requirements specified in subdivision (a), (b), (e), or (f).
23 (2) An applicant for or recipient of state disability
24 assistance is considered needy if the applicant or recipient does
25 both of the following:
26 (a) Meets the same asset test as is applied for the family
27 independence program.
28 (b) Has a monthly budgetable income that is less than the
29 payment standards.
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

407
1 (3) Except for an individual described in subsection (1)(c) or
2 (d), an individual is not disabled under this section if the
3 individual's drug addiction or alcoholism is a contributing factor
4 material to the determination of disability.
5 (4) As used in this section:
6 (a) "Material to the determination of disability" means that,
7 if the individual stopped using drugs or alcohol, the individual's
8 remaining physical or mental limitations would not be disabling. If
9 the individual's remaining physical or mental limitations would be
10 disabling, then the drug addiction or alcoholism is not material to
11 the determination of disability and the individual may receive
12 state disability assistance, but the individual must actively
13 participate in a substance abuse treatment program, and the
14 assistance must be paid to a third party or through vendor
15 payments.
16 (b) "Substance abuse treatment" includes receipt of inpatient
17 or outpatient services or participation in Alcoholics Anonymous or
18 a similar program.

6 CHILDREN'S SERVICES AGENCY – JUVENILE JUSTICE
7 Sec. 701. Unless required by a change to federal law or the
8 law of this state or at the request of a provider, the department
9 shall not alter the terms of a signed contract with a private
10 residential facility that serves children who are under state or
11 court supervision without receiving written consent from a
12 representative of the private residential facility.
13 Sec. 702. (1) Not later than December 1 of the current fiscal
14 year, the department shall submit a report to the standard report
15 recipients on all of the following for the prior fiscal year:
16 (a) The average daily population by month of youths residing
17 at state-run juvenile justice facilities.
18 (b) The total number of beds at each facility, itemized by
19 those that are staffed and unstaffed, for each month.
20 (c) The average daily number of available beds by month.
21 (d) The average number of staff vacancies by month.
22 (2) The information in subsection (1) must be itemized by each
23 facility.
24 Sec. 706. A county is subject to a 50% chargeback for the use
25 of an alternative regional detention service, if the detention
26 service does not fall under the basic grant provision of section
27 117e of the social welfare act, 1939 PA 280, MCL 400.117e, or if a
28 county operates the detention service program primarily with
29 professional rather than volunteer staff.

1 Sec. 707. To be reimbursed for child care fund expenditures, a

6 Sec. 708. (1) As a condition of receiving funds appropriated
7 in part 1 for the child care fund line item, by October 15 of the
8 current fiscal year, a county shall have an approved service
9 spending plan for the current fiscal year. Not later than August 15
10 of the current fiscal year, a county shall submit the county's
11 service spending plan for the following fiscal year to the
12 department for approval. The department shall approve a county's
13 service spending plan not later than 30 calendar days after the
14 department receives a properly completed service spending plan from
15 the county that complies with the requirements of the social
16 welfare act, 1939 PA 280, MCL 400.1 to 400.119b. The department
17 shall notify and submit revisions to a service spending plan to a
18 county whose service spending plan is not approved after initial
19 submission. The department shall not request any additional
20 revisions to a county's service spending plan outside of the
21 requested revision notification submitted to the county by the
22 department. The department shall notify a county that its service
23 spending plan is approved not later than 30 days after the
24 department considers the county's revisions to the county's service
25 spending plan.
26 (2) A county shall submit an amendment to its county service
27 spending plan for the current fiscal year to the department not
28 later than August 30 of the current fiscal year. A county shall
29 submit payable estimates for the current fiscal year to the
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

1 department not later than September 15 of the current fiscal year.

6 service spending plans not approved by October 15. The report must

12 Sec. 709. The department's master contract for juvenile
13 justice residential foster care services must prohibit a contractor
14 from denying a referral for placing a youth, or terminating a
15 youth's placement, if the youth's assessed treatment needs are in
16 alignment with the facility's residential program type and
17 treatment capacity, as approved by the department in the facility's
18 licensing program statement. A contractor is not required to accept
19 or retain a youth whose assessed treatment, behavioral, medical,
20 psychiatric, safety, or supervision needs exceed or are
21 inconsistent with the facility's approved residential program type,
22 treatment capacity, staffing model, or ability to safely and
23 appropriately serve the youth. The master contract must also
24 require that a youth placed in a juvenile justice residential
25 foster care facility receives clinically appropriate treatment
26 services, including regularly scheduled treatment sessions with a
27 licensed clinician and regularly scheduled reviews with a licensed
28 psychiatrist for medication management when clinically indicated,

1 Sec. 942. A CMHSP shall provide at least 30 days' notice
2 before reducing, terminating, or suspending a service provided by

4 physician and the service no longer meets established criteria for
5 medical necessity.
6 Sec. 960. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for autism
7 services, the department shall continue to cover all Medicaid
8 autism services to Medicaid enrollees eligible for the services
9 that were covered on January 1, 2019.
10 (2) To restrain cost increases in the autism services line
11 item, the department shall do all of the following:
12 (a) Maintain specific written guidance for standardization of
13 Medicaid PIHPs and CMHSPs autism spectrum disorder administrative
14 services, including, but not limited to, reporting requirements,
15 coding, and reciprocity of credentialing and training between PIHPs
16 and CMHSPs to reduce administrative duplication at the PIHP, CMHSP,
17 and service provider levels.
18 (b) Require consultation with the client's evaluation
19 diagnostician and PIHP to approve the client's ongoing therapy for
20 3 years, unless the client's evaluation diagnostician recommended
21 an evaluation before the 3 years or if a clinician on the treatment
22 team recommended an evaluation for the client before the third
23 year.
24 (c) Limit the authority to perform a diagnostic evaluation for
25 Medicaid autism services to qualified licensed practitioners as
26 determined by the department.
27 (d) Allow and expand the utilization of telemedicine and
28 telepsychiatry to increase access to diagnostic evaluation
29 services.
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

441
1 (e) Coordinate with the department of insurance and financial
2 services on oversight for compliance with the Paul Wellstone and
3 Pete Domenici mental health parity and addiction equity act of
4 2008, Public Law 110-343, as it relates to autism spectrum disorder
5 services, to ensure appropriate cost sharing between public and
6 private payers.
7 (f) Require that Medicaid eligibility be confirmed through
8 prior evaluations conducted by qualified licensed practitioners as
9 determined by the department.
10 (g) Maintain regular statewide provider trainings on autism
11 spectrum disorder standard clinical best practice guidelines for
12 treatment and diagnostic services.

25 Sec. 1008. (1) A PIHP and CMHSP shall do all of the following:
26 (a) Work to reduce administration costs by ensuring that PIHP
27 and CMHSP responsible functions are efficient in allowing optimal
28 transition of dollars to the direct services considered most
29 effective in assisting individuals served. Any consolidation of
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

448
1 administrative functions must demonstrate, by independent analysis,
2 a reduction in dollars spent on administration resulting in greater
3 dollars spent on direct services. Savings resulting from increased
4 efficiencies must not be applied to PIHP and CMHSP net assets,
5 internal service fund increases, building costs, increases in the
6 number of PIHP and CMHSP personnel, or other areas not directly
7 related to the delivery of improved services.
8 (b) Take an active role in managing mental health care by
9 ensuring consistent and high-quality service delivery throughout
10 its network and promote a conflict-free care management
11 environment.
12 (c) Ensure that direct service rate variances are related to
13 the level of need or other quantifiable measures to ensure that the
14 most money possible reaches direct services.
15 (d) Whenever possible, promote fair and adequate direct care
16 reimbursement, including, but not limited to, fair wages for direct
17 service workers.

21 and services in place for the individuals currently in the

24 have been admitted to the hospital, center, or agency.

28 described in subsection (1). A discharge and aftercare plan must
29 address an individual's housing needs. A homeless shelter or
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

1 similar temporary shelter arrangement is inadequate to meet an

6 report recipients.
7 (4) On the closure of a hospital, center, or agency and after
8 transitional costs have been paid, the remaining balances of funds
9 appropriated for the hospital, center, or agency must be
10 transferred to CMHSPs or PIHPs responsible for providing services

12 status of sexual assault evidence kits.
13 (b) A uniform statewide system to audit untested kits that
14 were collected on or before March 1, 2015 and were released by
15 victims to law enforcement.
16 (c) Secure electronic access for victims.
17 (d) The ability to accommodate concurrent data entry with kit
18 collection through mechanisms that include, but are not limited to,
19 web entry through computers or smartphones, and through scanning
20 devices.
21 (2) The sexual assault evidence tracking fund established in
22 section 1451 of article X of 2017 PA 158 shall continue to be
23 maintained in the department of treasury. Money in the sexual
24 assault evidence tracking fund at the close of a fiscal year
25 remains in the sexual assault evidence tracking fund, does not
26 revert to the general fund, and is appropriated as provided by law
27 for the development and implementation of a uniform statewide
28 sexual assault evidence kit tracking system as described in
29 subsection (1).

9 EPIDEMIOLOGY, EMERGENCY MEDICAL SERVICES, AND LABORATORY
10 Sec. 1180. From the funds appropriated in part 1 for
11 epidemiology administration and for childhood lead program, the
12 department shall maintain a public health drinking water program
13 and maintain enhanced efforts to monitor child blood lead levels.
14 The public health drinking water program must ensure that
15 appropriate investigations of potential health hazards occur for
16 all community and noncommunity drinking water supplies where
17 chemical exceedances of action levels, health advisory levels, or
18 maximum contaminant limits are identified. The goals of the
19 childhood lead program must include improving the identification of
20 children affected by lead exposure, improving the timeliness of
21 case follow-up, and attaining nurse care management for children
22 with lead exposure, and to achieve a long-term reduction in the
23 percentage of children in this state with elevated blood lead
24 levels.
25 Sec. 1181. From the funds appropriated in part 1 for
26 epidemiology administration, the department shall maintain a vapor
27 intrusion response program. The vapor intrusion response program
28 shall assess risks to public health at vapor intrusion sites and
29 respond to vapor intrusion risks if appropriate. The goals of the
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

462
1 vapor intrusion response program must include reducing the number
2 of individuals who are exposed to toxic substances through vapor
3 intrusion and improving health outcomes for individuals who are
4 identified as having been exposed to vapor intrusion.

6 the department shall submit a report to the standard report

5 (a) Provide the legislature with a recommended definition for
6 palliative care in this state.

8 (c) Make recommendations that will expand the provision of
9 palliative care.

11 measures for reimbursement of the services.
12 (e) Develop key program metrics for palliative care services

18 make informed decisions on preferred care.

20 provide palliative care services.

24 option.

8 HEALTH SERVICES
9 Sec. 1605. The protected income level for Medicaid coverage
10 determined under section 106(1)(b)(iii) of the social welfare act,
11 1939 PA 280, MCL 400.106, is 100% of the related public assistance
12 standard.
13 Sec. 1606. For the purpose of guardian and conservator
14 charges, the department may deduct up to $83.00 per month as an
15 allowable expense against a recipient's income when determining
16 Medicaid eligibility and patient pay amounts.
17 Sec. 1607. (1) The department shall immediately presume that
18 an applicant for Medicaid whose qualifying condition is pregnancy
19 is eligible for Medicaid coverage, unless the preponderance of
20 evidence in the applicant's application indicates otherwise. The
21 applicant who is qualified as described in this subsection is
22 allowed to select or remain with the Medicaid participating
23 obstetrician of the applicant's choice.
24 (2) Each qualifying applicant is entitled to receive all
25 medically necessary obstetrical and prenatal care without
26 preauthorization from a health plan. All claims submitted for
27 payment for obstetrical and prenatal care must be paid at the
28 Medicaid fee-for-service rate if a contract does not exist between
29 the Medicaid participating obstetrical or prenatal care provider
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

479
1 and the managed care plan. The applicant must receive a listing of
2 Medicaid physicians and managed care plans in the immediate
3 vicinity of the applicant's residence.
4 (3) If an applicant, presumed to be eligible for Medicaid
5 under subsection (1), is subsequently found to be ineligible, a
6 Medicaid physician or managed care plan that has been providing
7 pregnancy services to the applicant is entitled to reimbursement
8 for the services until the Medicaid physician or managed care plan
9 is notified by the department that the applicant was found to be
10 ineligible for Medicaid.
11 (4) If the preponderance of evidence in an application under
12 subsection (1) indicates that the applicant is not eligible for
13 Medicaid, the department shall refer the applicant to the nearest
14 public health clinic or similar entity as a potential source for
15 receiving pregnancy-related services.
16 (5) The department shall develop an enrollment process for
17 applicants covered under this section that facilitates the
18 selection of a managed care plan at the time of application.
19 (6) The department shall require that Medicaid managed care
20 plans enroll women whose qualifying condition for Medicaid is
21 pregnancy.
22 (7) The department shall encourage physicians to provide an
23 applicant whose qualifying condition for Medicaid is pregnancy with
24 a referral to a Medicaid participating dentist at the applicant's
25 first pregnancy-related appointment.
26 Sec. 1611. (1) For care provided to Medicaid recipients with
27 other third-party sources of payment, Medicaid reimbursement shall
28 not exceed, in combination with such other resources, including
29 Medicare, those amounts established for Medicaid-only patients. The
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

480
1 Medicaid payment rate shall be accepted as payment in full. Other
2 than an approved Medicaid copayment, no portion of a provider's
3 charge shall be billed to the recipient or any person acting on
4 behalf of the recipient. This section does not affect the level of
5 payment from a third-party source other than the Medicaid program.
6 The department shall require a nonenrolled provider to accept
7 Medicaid payments as payment in full.
8 (2) Notwithstanding subsection (1), if a hospital service is
9 provided to a dual Medicare/Medicaid recipient with only Medicare
10 part B coverage, the Medicaid reimbursement must equal, when
11 combined with a payment for Medicare or other third-party source of
12 payment, the amount established for a Medicaid-only patient,
13 including a capital payment.
14 Sec. 1620. (1) If a Medicaid claim is a fee-for-service
15 Medicaid claim, the professional dispensing fee for a drug that is
16 listed as a medication on the Michigan pharmaceutical products list
17 is $20.02 or the pharmacy's submitted dispensing fee, whichever is
18 less.
19 (2) If a Medicaid claim is a fee-for-service Medicaid claim,
20 the professional dispensing fee for a drug that is not listed as a
21 specialty medication on the Michigan pharmaceutical products list
22 is as follows:
23 (a) If the drug is indicated as preferred on the department's
24 preferred drug list, $10.80 or the pharmacy's submitted dispensing
25 fee, whichever is less.
26 (b) If the drug is not on the department's preferred drug
27 list, $10.64 or the pharmacy's submitted dispensing fee, whichever
28 is less.
29 (c) If the drug is indicated as nonpreferred on the
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

481
1 department's preferred drug list, $9.00 or the pharmacy's submitted
2 dispensing fee, whichever is less.

ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

494
1 committee and the drug utilization review board account for net
2 costs of individual prescription drugs for this state's Medicaid
3 program before recommending or approving the addition of a drug to
4 this state's single preferred drug list.
5 (2) For the purpose of this section, "net cost" means a cost
6 associated with a pharmaceutical that includes, but is not limited
7 to, all of the following:
8 (a) Wholesale acquisition cost.
9 (b) A cost of a brand name drug with a generic alternative,
10 compared to the lowest-cost generic version of the drug if a
11 maximum allowable cost price has been determined, and the lowest-
12 cost alternative is preferred.
13 (c) A cost associated with federal and supplemental rebates
14 that are available, or anticipated, under 42 USC 1396r-8.
15 (d) Dispensing and administrative costs associated with the
16 provision of a drug.
17 (3) The department shall require that the pharmacy and
18 therapeutics committee and the drug utilization review board
19 document their consideration of cost information, including
20 comparative cost effectiveness among clinically appropriate
21 alternatives, in the meeting materials or minutes supporting each
22 recommendation.
23 (4) A drug must not be included in this state's single
24 preferred drug list unless the pharmacy and therapeutics committee
25 or drug utilization review board have affirmatively determined that
26 the drug's inclusion is both clinically appropriate and cost
27 effective.

18 providers whose primary practice is as a non-primary-care
19 subspecialty. The department shall establish policies that most
20 effectively limit the increase to primary care providers for
21 primary care services only. As used in this section, "primary care
22 provider" means a physician, or a practitioner working in
23 collaboration with a physician, who is either licensed under part
24 170 or part 175 of the public health code, 1978 PA 368, MCL
25 333.17001 to 333.17097 and 333.17501 to 333.17556, and who works as
26 a primary care provider in general practice or is board-eligible or
27 certified with a specialty designation of family medicine, general
28 internal medicine, or pediatric medicine, or is a provider who
29 provides the department with documentation of equivalency.
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

499
1 Sec. 1802. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for

13 the current fiscal year, a hospital that met established occupied

2 489, MCL 12.253, to programs and services to address the opioid
3 crisis in a manner consistent with the opioid judgment, settlement,
4 or compromise of claims pertaining to violations, or alleged
5 violations, of law related to the manufacture, marketing,
6 distribution, dispensing, or sale of opioids. The funds are not
7 intended to be used for the purchase of drug paraphernalia that may
8 be used to snort or smoke drugs. The funds must be allocated as
9 follows:
10 (a) $2,500,000.00 must be allocated for funding for public
11 health communications and outreach to educate and provide tools to
12 parents to increase knowledge about the impact of substance use and
13 available resources.
14 (b) $7,000,000.00 must be allocated for harm reduction, as
15 follows:
16 (i) $2,000,000.00 must be used to continue distributing
17 naloxone through community organizations, health systems, first
18 responders, and directly to individuals across this state to
19 prevent fatal overdoses.
20 (ii) $5,000,000.00 must be used to continue reducing overdose
21 deaths and preventing infectious disease spread in communities most
22 impacted by substance use through grants to acquire physical
23 locations, increase sustainability, and improve efficiency in
24 delivering services.
25 (c) $17,000,000.00 must be allocated to substance use disorder
26 treatment for increasing treatment retention and access to care by
27 expanding medications for opioid use disorders and crisis services
28 throughout this state, including the physical expansion of youth
29 treatment facilities, and the expansion of mobile health services
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

1 in urban and rural communities.
2 (d) $42,000,000.00 must be allocated for recovery investments,
3 as follows:
4 (i) $34,500,000.00 must be used to invest in a series of
5 transitional and permanent supportive recovery housing new
6 developments, acquisitions, and renovations throughout this state,
7 as follows:
8 (A) Up to $7,500,000.00 must be used to support short-term
9 recovery housing.
10 (B) Up to $27,000,000.00 must be used to support long-term

12 (ii) $7,500,000.00 must be used to support housing,
13 transportation, benefits navigation assistance, and other

5 Sec. 1965. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for water
6 affordability, the department shall allocate $5,000,000.00 as
7 grants to qualified providers to assist eligible residents who have
8 a financial burden, have accumulated a balance on their water
9 utility bill, have had their water service shut off, and/or are at
10 risk of having their water service shut off. Eligible expenditures
11 from these funds must be income-based and must include all of the
12 following:
13 (a) Restoring residential water service.
14 (b) Paying down water bills currently in arrears.
15 (c) Supporting reasonable water affordability plans that are
16 based on an individual's ability to pay, including capped payments
17 based on household income to prevent accumulating a balance on
18 future water bills and funding to qualified providers to cover the
19 remaining cost of service.
20 (d) Protecting participating residents from water shutoffs.
21 (2) To be considered a qualified provider under this section,
22 the provider must be 1 of the following:
23 (a) A community water system.
24 (b) A community action agency.
25 (c) A nonprofit, community-based organization organized under
26 the laws of this state that is exempt from federal income tax under
27 section 501(c)(3) of the internal revenue code of 1986, 26 USC 501,
28 with a history of doing utility assistance work.
29 (3) Qualified providers receiving grants under this section
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

527
1 may spend not more than 3% of the total grant award for
2 administrative services related to the implementation of this
3 section.
4 (4) Qualified providers receiving grants under this section
5 shall report to the department by September 30 of the current
6 fiscal year on outcomes and performance measures for the program,
7 including, but not limited to, all of the following:
8 (a) The total grant award received by the qualified provider.
9 (b) The percentage of the grant award that was used for
10 administrative costs.
11 (c) The total dollars spent broken down by type of assistance
12 provided.
13 (d) The number of individuals helped broken down by type of
14 assistance provided.
15 (e) The number of individual applicants denied assistance.
16 (5) Upon receipt of the information required under subsection
17 (4), the department shall compile and forward the report to the
18 standard report recipients.
19 (6) The unexpended funds appropriated in part 1 for water
20 affordability are designated as a work project appropriation, and
21 any unencumbered or unallotted funds shall not lapse at the end of
22 the fiscal year and shall be available for expenditures for
23 projects under this section until the projects have been completed.

24 GENERAL SECTIONS
25 Sec. 201. In accordance with section 30 of article IX of the
26 state constitution of 1963, for the fiscal year ending September
27 30, 2026, total state spending under part 1 from state sources is
28 $81,292,200.00 and total state spending under part 1 from state
29 sources to be paid to local units of government is $0.00.
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

535
1 Sec. 202. The appropriations under this part and part 1 are
2 subject to the management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1101
3 to 18.1594.
4 Sec. 203. As used in this part and part 1:
5 (a) "Department" means the department of insurance and
6 financial services.
7 (b) "Director" means the director of the department.
8 (c) "FTE" means full-time equated position in the classified
9 service of this state.
10 (d) "IDG" means interdepartmental grant.
11 (e) "MBLSLA fund" means the restricted account established
12 under section 8 of the mortgage brokers, lenders, and servicers
13 licensing act, 1987 PA 173, MCL 445.1658.
14 (f) "MDLARA" means the Michigan department of licensing and
15 regulatory affairs.
16 (g) "Standard report recipients" means the senate and house
17 appropriations committees, senate and house appropriations
18 subcommittees on licensing and regulatory affairs and insurance and
19 financial services, the senate and house fiscal agencies, the
20 senate and house policy offices, and the state budget office.
21 Sec. 204. If the state administrative board, acting under
22 section 3 of 1921 PA 2, MCL 17.3, transfers funds from an amount
23 appropriated under part 1, the legislature may, by a concurrent
24 resolution adopted by a majority of the members elected to and
25 serving in each house, intertransfer funds within part 1 for the
26 particular department, board, commission, officer, or institution.
27 Sec. 205. (1) The department shall use the internet to fulfill
28 the reporting requirements of this part and shall make each report
29 readily accessible to the public and conspicuously post each
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

536
1 required report in a single archivable location on the department's
2 or agency's Michigan.gov website not later than the due date
3 required for each report.
4 (2) In addition to placing all reports required in the current
5 fiscal year on the department's or agency's website, the department
6 or agency shall maintain on its website all reports placed on the
7 website from previous years posted by fiscal year in the same
8 archivable location.
9 (3) The department or agency shall also transmit all required
10 reports for the current fiscal year to the standard recipients and
11 any other required recipients by email.
12 Sec. 206. The department shall receive and retain copies of
13 all reports funded from appropriations in part 1. The department
14 shall follow federal and state law and guidelines for short-term
15 and long-term retention of records. The department may
16 electronically retain copies of reports unless otherwise required
17 by federal and state guidelines.
18 Sec. 207. The department shall cooperate with the department
19 of technology, management, and budget to maintain a searchable
20 website accessible by the public at no cost that includes, but is
21 not limited to, all of the following for the department:
22 (a) Fiscal year-to-date expenditures by category.
23 (b) Fiscal year-to-date expenditures by appropriation unit.
24 (c) Fiscal year-to-date payments to a selected vendor,
25 including the vendor name, payment date, payment amount, and
26 payment description.
27 Sec. 208. (1) In addition to any other requirements under this
28 part, if the department is authorized under this part to expend
29 funds in addition to those appropriated in part 1, the department
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

537
1 must do all of the following:
2 (a) Not later than December 1, provide a report to the
3 chairpersons of the house and senate appropriations committees, the
4 house and senate fiscal agencies, and the state budget office that
5 details all of the following:
6 (i) The type of funding received during the previous fiscal
7 year that was authorized in part 2 of the article that made
8 appropriations for the department in the previous fiscal year.
9 (ii) When the funding was received.
10 (iii) The amount of funding received.
11 (iv) How much of the funding was spent and for what purpose or
12 purposes.

9 Sec. 223. (1) The department shall maximize the utilization of
10 its in-person state workforce. The department shall prioritize
11 occupancy utilization of office space for each division within the
12 department. Employees with job responsibilities that require the
13 employees to serve in their capacities outside of an office shall
14 be monitored each pay period to ensure all work hours reported on
15 the timesheet were actually worked.
16 (2) The department shall comply with requirements set forth by
17 the office of the state employer on in-person work and utilization
18 and occupancy rates of state buildings to ensure in-person work is
19 optimized and occupancy rates are 80% or higher, subject to market
20 conditions.
21 (3) The department shall adhere to civil service rules and
22 regulations that state the standard biweekly work period for a
23 full-time employee in the classified service of this state is the
24 equivalent of 80 hours of work. The department shall establish
25 policies and processes to ensure all employees are working their
26 jobs during agreed-upon business hours.
27 (4) The office of the state employer must create and implement
28 an occupancy utilization uniform policy on occupancy, utilization,
29 and in-person and remote work. The office of the state employer
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

2 (g) "Standard report recipients" means the senate and house
3 appropriations committees, the senate and house appropriations
4 subcommittees on corrections and judiciary, the senate and house
5 fiscal agencies, the senate and house policy offices, and the state
6 budget office.
7 (h) "Title IV-D" means the part of the federal social security
8 act, 42 USC 301 to 1397mm, pertaining to the child support
9 enforcement program.
10 (i) "Title IV-E" means the part of the federal social security

12 Sec. 205. (1) The judicial branch shall use the internet to
13 fulfill the reporting requirements of this part and shall make each
14 report readily accessible to the public and conspicuously post each
15 required report in a single archivable location on the judicial
16 branch's website not later than the due date required for each
17 report.
18 (2) In addition to placing all reports required in the current
19 fiscal year on the judicial branch's website, the judicial branch
20 shall maintain on its website all reports placed on the website
21 from previous fiscal years posted by fiscal year in the same single
22 archivable location.
23 (3) The judicial branch shall transmit all required reports
24 for the current fiscal year to the standard report recipients and
25 any other required recipients by email. The email shall include a
26 copy of the report and a link to access the report online.
27 Sec. 206. The judicial branch shall receive and retain copies
28 of all reports funded from appropriations in part 1. The judicial
29 branch shall follow federal and state law and guidelines for short-
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

558
1 term and long-term retention of records. The judicial branch may
2 electronically retain copies of reports unless otherwise required
3 by federal and state guidelines.
4 Sec. 207. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the judicial
5 branch shall maintain a searchable website accessible by the public
6 at no cost that posts all of the expenditures made by the judicial
7 branch within a fiscal year. A post must include the purpose of the
8 expenditure. The judicial branch shall not provide financial
9 information on the public website that would violate a federal or
10 state law, rule, regulation, or guideline that establishes privacy
11 or security standards applicable to that financial information.
12 Sec. 209. Not later than December 15, the judicial branch
13 shall cooperate with the state budget office to prepare and submit
14 a report that provides estimates of the total general fund/general
15 purpose appropriation lapses at the close of the previous fiscal
16 year. The report must summarize the projected year-end general
17 fund/general purpose appropriation lapses by major judicial program
18 or program areas. The state budget office shall submit the report
19 to the standard report recipients and to the chairpersons of the
20 senate and house appropriations committees.
21 Sec. 210. Not later than 14 days after the release of the
22 executive budget recommendation, the judicial branch shall
23 cooperate with the state budget office to provide an annual report
24 on estimated state restricted fund balances, state restricted fund
25 projected revenues, and state restricted fund expenditures for the
26 previous 2 fiscal years. The report must be submitted to the
27 standard report recipients and to the chairpersons of the senate
28 and house appropriations committees.
29 Sec. 211. Not later than November 15, the judicial branch
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

559
1 shall disclose on a publicly accessible website private and other
2 third-party funds received by the judicial branch in the previous

11 administrative office shall prepare a report on out-of-state travel

19 Sec. 228. The chief justice shall take steps to ensure that
20 all state fiscal recovery funds received under the American rescue
21 plan act of 2021, Public Law 117-2, by the judicial branch are
22 expended by December 31, 2026, as required by law. Any state fiscal
23 recovery funds that would otherwise lapse after September 30, 2026
24 are automatically reappropriated for the same purpose as originally
25 authorized and available for expenditure through December 31, 2026
26 and any subsequent financial close out period.

4 Sec. 302. A member of the legislature may request a report or
5 data from the data collected in the judicial data warehouse. The
6 report must be made available to the public upon request, unless
7 disclosure is prohibited by court order or state or federal law. If
8 data is provided under this section, the data must be public and
9 nonidentifying information, as determined by the state court
10 administrative office. As used in this section, "nonidentifying
11 information" means information that does not include personal
12 information that, if released, would be considered invasion of
13 privacy.
14 Sec. 303. From the funds appropriated in part 1 for community
15 dispute resolution, community dispute resolution centers shall
16 provide dispute resolution services specified in the community
17 dispute resolution act, 1988 PA 260, MCL 691.1551 to 691.1564, help
18 reduce suspensions and truancy, and improve school environment. The
19 funds appropriated in part 1 for community dispute resolution may
20 be used to develop or expand juvenile diversion services in
21 coordination with local prosecutors.
22 Sec. 304. If funds in the court fee fund are insufficient to
23 pay judges' compensation, the difference between the appropriated
24 amount from that fund for judges' compensation and the actual
25 amount available after the amount appropriated for trial court
26 reimbursement is made is appropriated from the state general fund
27 for judges' compensation. If an appropriation from the state
28 general fund is necessary under this section, not later than 14
29 days after the appropriation, the state court administrative office
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

562
1 shall submit a report to the standard report recipients and the
2 senate and house appropriations committees.
3 Sec. 305. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the state
4 court administrative office shall submit a report on drug
5 treatment, mental health, and veterans court programs in this state
6 not later than March 1. The report must include all of the
7 following information for each individual court, by program:
8 (a) The number of each type of program.
9 (b) The number of program participants.
10 (c) The impact of the programs on offender criminal
11 involvement and recidivism.
12 (d) An accounting of previous fiscal year expenditures,
13 including grant amounts requested, grant amounts awarded, and grant
14 amounts expended.
15 Sec. 306. (1) The funds appropriated in part 1 for problem-
16 solving courts must be administered by the state court
17 administrative office to operate problem-solving court programs. A
18 problem-solving court shall use all available county and state
19 personnel involved in the disposition of cases, including, but not
20 limited to, parole and probation agents, prosecuting attorneys,
21 defense attorneys, and community corrections providers. The funds
22 may be used in connection with other federal, state, and local
23 funding sources.
24 (2) From the funds appropriated in part 1, the chief justice
25 shall allocate sufficient funds for the Michigan judicial institute
26 to provide in-state training for those identified in subsection (1)
27 and new problem-solving court judges.
28 (3) The state court administrative office may prioritize
29 funding for courts that have a higher number of filed substance use
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

563
1 disorder cases.
2 (4) To assist the department of corrections and avoid prison
3 bed space growth for nonviolent offenders, the judicial branch
4 shall receive $1,500,000.00 in Byrne formula grant funding through
5 an interdepartmental grant from the department of state police to
6 be used to support problem-solving court costs consistent with
7 Byrne grant program criteria.
8 (5) A problem-solving court may include, but is not limited
9 to, any of the following:
10 (a) Drug treatment courts under chapter 10A of the revised
11 judicature act of 1961, 1961 PA 236, MCL 600.1060 to 600.1088.
12 (b) Mental health courts under chapter 10B of the revised

15 revised judicature act of 1961, 1961 PA 236, MCL 600.1099b to
16 600.1099m.
17 (d) Family treatment courts under chapter 10D of the revised

20 judicature act of 1961, 1961 PA 236, MCL 600.1200 to 600.1212.

1 previous fiscal year.
2 Sec. 309. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the state
3 court administrative office shall submit a report on the statewide
4 judicial case management system not later than March 1. The report
5 must provide a status update on development and implementation of
6 the statewide judicial case management system and must include all
7 appropriation and expenditure data for all previous and the current
8 fiscal years.
9 Sec. 310. The state court administrative office shall not
10 impose local user fees or collect local user fees from trial courts
11 that are using the statewide judicial case management system.
12 Sec. 311. (1) If Byrne formula grant funding is awarded to the
13 state appellate defender office in excess of the amount
14 appropriated in part 1, the state appellate defender office may
15 receive and expend not more than $250,000.00 of Byrne formula grant
16 funds as an interdepartmental grant from the department of state
17 police.
18 (2) If the state appellate defender office receives federal
19 grant funding from the United States Department of Justice in
20 excess of the amount appropriated in part 1, the state appellate
21 defender office may receive and expend not more than $300,000.00 in
22 federal grant funds.
23 Sec. 313. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1, the state
24 appellate defender office shall operate the program to ensure this
25 state's compliance with Montgomery v Louisiana, 577 US 190 (2016),
26 People v Parks, 510 Mich 225 (2022), People v Stovall, 510 Mich 301
27 (2022), People v Poole, 349 Mich App 594 (2024), People v
28 Czarnecki, Mich (2025), and People v Taylor, Mich (2025). The
29 purpose of the program is to ensure competent, resourced, and
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

565
1 supervised counsel in cases that involve resentencing individuals
2 who are serving a life sentence for an offense committed when the
3 individuals were 20 years of age or younger.
4 (2) The state appellate defender office shall submit a report
5 not later than March 1 on the number of cases investigated and
6 prepared by the state appellate defender office under subsection
7 (1). The report must include a calculation of the hours spent and
8 the incremental costs associated with the investigation and robust
9 examination of each case.
10 Sec. 314. (1) The funds appropriated in part 1 for Michigan
11 appellate assigned counsel system roster attorney compensation
12 grants must be deposited into the restricted Michigan appellate
13 assigned counsel system attorney compensation fund created in
14 subsection (2).
15 (2) The Michigan appellate assigned counsel system attorney
16 compensation fund is created in the state treasury. The state
17 treasurer may receive money or other assets from any source for
18 deposit into the fund. The state treasurer shall direct the
19 investment of the fund and credit to the fund interest and earnings
20 from fund investments. Unexpended funds at the close of the fiscal
21 year must remain in the fund and shall not lapse to the general
22 fund. The judicial branch shall be the administrator of the fund
23 for auditing purposes. The judicial branch shall expend money from
24 the fund to provide payments to indigent defense systems as
25 provided under section 8a of the appellate defender act, 1978 PA
26 620, MCL 780.718a.
27 (3) All funds available in the Michigan appellate assigned
28 counsel system attorney compensation fund are appropriated and
29 available for expenditure as provided by law.

1
2 ONE-TIME APPROPRIATIONS
3 Sec. 401. From the one-time funds appropriated in part 1 for
4 branchwide appropriations, the judicial branch may do the
5 following:
6 (a) The supreme court may hire 1.0 limited term employee to
7 serve as an editor for the office of reporter of decisions. The
8 editor shall work to reduce the court of appeals backlog of
9 opinions awaiting review and editing.
10 (b) The judicial tenure commission may hire up to 3.0 limited
11 term employees to assist the commission with addressing the
12 judicial complaint backlog.
13 (c) The state appellate defender office may hire up to 14.0
14 limited term employees to support the financial impact of recent
15 supreme court decisions that require resentencing of individuals
16 who were sentenced to life without parole for crimes they committed
17 at 19 or 20 years of age.

4 Sec. 202. The appropriations under this part and part 1 are
5 subject to the management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1101
6 to 18.1594.
7 Sec. 203. As used in this part and part 1:
8 (a) "Department" means the department of labor and economic
9 opportunity and entities contained within its organization,
10 including, but not limited to, the fund.
11 (b) "Director" means the director of the department.
12 (c) "DTMB" means the department of technology, management, and
13 budget.
14 (d) "FTE" means full-time equated.
15 (e) "Fund", unless the context clearly implies a different
16 meaning, means the Michigan strategic fund.
17 (f) "MEDC" means the Michigan economic development
18 corporation, which is the public body corporate created under
19 section 28 of article VII of the state constitution of 1963 and the

10 Sec. 223. (1) The department shall maximize utilization of its
11 in-person state workforce. The department shall prioritize
12 occupancy utilization of office space for each division within the
13 department. Employees with job responsibilities that require the
14 employees to serve in their capacities outside of an office must be
15 monitored each pay period to ensure all work hours reported on
16 timesheets were actually worked.

18 the office of the state employer on in-person work and utilization
19 and occupancy rates of state buildings to ensure in-person work is
20 optimized and occupancy rates are 80% or higher, subject to market
21 conditions.
22 (3) The department shall adhere to civil service rules and
23 regulations that state the standard biweekly work period for a
24 full-time employee in the classified service of this state is the
25 equivalent of 80 hours of work. The department shall establish
26 policies and processes to ensure all employees are working their
27 jobs during agreed upon business hours.

29 an occupancy utilization uniform policy on occupancy, utilization,

6 space assigned under its building occupancy agreement and leased
7 office locations and post on their website. Agencies shall develop,
8 in coordination with DTMB, a plan to reduce, consolidate, or
9 otherwise optimize assigned space. The plans shall prioritize the
10 use of state-owned facilities, wherever possible, and comply with

12 DTMB.

7 (c) The amended amount of qualified investment committed
8 relative to the prior amount of qualified investment committed.
9 (d) A description of any change in scope of the project.
10 (e) A description of any change in project benchmarks,
11 deadlines, or completion dates.
12 (f) The reason or justification for the amendment approval.

1 and education into the STEM network, and ensures high-quality STEM

4 likely to pass.

6 program must include all of the following:
7 (a) Instructional and tutorial assistances.
8 (b) High school equivalency test practice.
9 (c) Required attendance at program instructional sessions.
10 (d) A curriculum that prepares students for opportunities in
11 postsecondary education and the job market.
12 (e) Information on potential postsecondary and career
13 pathways.
14 (f) Counseling on preparing for and applying to college.
15 (g) Personal and job readiness skills development.
16 (h) Comprehensive information on college costs and financial
17 aid.
18 (i) College and career assessments.
19 (j) Computer-based instruction, practice, or remediation.
20 (3) The department shall post online an announcement of the
21 Michigan high school equivalency-to-school program, minimum
22 standards for high school equivalency preparation program approval,
23 and approval procedures.

7 Sec. 1001. (1) The funds appropriated in part 1 for 23+ high
8 school diploma program must be awarded for a program to assist
9 Michigan residents who are 23 years of age or older in obtaining
10 high school diplomas and placement in career training programs.
11 (2) For purposes of this section, an eligible program provider
12 may be a public, nonprofit, or private accredited diploma-granting
13 institution, but must have not less than 2 years of experience
14 providing dropout recovery services in this state.
15 (3) The department shall issue a request for qualifications
16 for eligible program providers to participate in the program. To be
17 considered a qualified program provider, the institution must offer
18 all of the following:
19 (a) Dropout reengagement services.
20 (b) Academic intake assessments.
21 (c) An integrated learning plan.
22 (d) A course catalog that includes all graduation
23 requirements.
24 (e) Remediation coursework.
25 (f) Academic resilience assessment and intervention.
26 (g) Employability skills development, such as WorkKeys
27 preparation.
28 (h) Industry recognized credentials.
29 (i) Credit for on-the-job training.
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

629
1 (j) A robust support framework, including technology, social
2 support, and academic support.
3 (4) The department shall announce qualified program providers
4 not later than January 1 of the current fiscal year. Qualified
5 program providers must start providing programming by February 1 of
6 the current fiscal year.
7 (5) The department shall reimburse qualified program providers
8 for each month of satisfactory monthly progress as described in
9 section 23a of the state school aid act of 1979, 1979 PA 94, MCL
10 388.1623a, at a rate of $500.00 per month. The following amounts
11 must be paid to a qualified program provider for the completion of
12 any of the following by an eligible participant:
13 (a) $500.00 for the completion of an employability skills
14 program equal to at least 1 unit of high school credit obtained
15 through classroom or online instruction.
16 (b) $250.00 for the attainment of an industry-recognized
17 credential requiring up to 50 hours of training.
18 (c) $500.00 for the attainment of an industry-recognized
19 credential requiring 50 to 100 hours of training.
20 (d) $750.00 for the attainment of an industry-recognized
21 credential requiring more than 100 hours of training.
22 (e) $1,000.00 for the attainment of a high school diploma
23 awarded for satisfying the merit curriculum requirements of this
24 state or an approved personal curriculum, as defined by the
25 department of education of this state.
26 (f) $2,500.00 for placement in a job in an in-demand career
27 pathway.

29 implement this section. The department may prioritize funding for

21 businesses.

23 benefit small businesses.

8 Sec. 1010. In addition to the appropriations in part 1, Travel
9 Michigan may receive and expend private revenue related to the use
10 of "Pure Michigan" and all other copyrighted slogans and images.
11 This revenue may come from the direct licensing of the name and
12 image or from the royalty payments from various merchandise sales.
13 Revenue collected is appropriated for the marketing of this state
14 as a travel destination. The funds are available for expenditure
15 when they are received by the department of treasury. If the fund
16 receives revenues from the use of "Pure Michigan", the fund shall
17 provide a report that lists the revenues by source received from
18 the use of "Pure Michigan" and all other copyrighted slogans and
19 images. The report must provide a detailed list of expenditures of
20 revenues received under this section. The report must be provided
21 not later than April 10.

2 Sec. 1012. From the funds appropriated in part 1 for reentry
3 support, $750,000.00 must be allocated toward reentry employment
4 services grants that provide funding to nonprofit organizations
5 that operate a program that satisfies all of the following
6 conditions:
7 (a) The program provides services to parolees and probationers
8 assessed by the department of corrections as moderate or high risk
9 to recidivate.
10 (b) The program provides job readiness training, transitional
11 employment, job coaching and placement, and postplacement retention
12 services. As part of the transitional employment program phase, the
13 nonprofit program must provide low-skill, crew-based services to
14 other state agencies.
15 (c) The program has been independently and rigorously
16 evaluated and shown to reduce recidivism.
17 (d) The program demonstrates an ability to serve multiple
18 jurisdictions across this state.

12 Sec. 1014. (1) The funds appropriated in part 1 for talent
13 partnership must be used to administer a grant program to support
14 implementation of transformational community development
15 initiatives.
16 (2) Talent partnership grant funding must be used to implement
17 transformational public space development projects in central city
18 neighborhoods or concentrated districts and leverage
19 interdepartmental and cross-sector coordination through local
20 talent plans that are designed to increase this state's population
21 of young talent by creating high-density, high-amenity, walkable,
22 vibrant-street-life neighborhoods or districts and to create
23 business ownership opportunities for local residents. Qualified
24 plan proposals must include all of the following:
25 (a) The transition of roadway usage from cars to alternative
26 transportation spaces, including, but not limited to, walking,
27 biking, and transit.
28 (b) Commercial corridor activation, including innovations to
29 fill vacant retail space with locally owned businesses.
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

663
1 (c) Mixed-use development that contributes to dense, walkable
2 areas.
3 (d) A plan to do all of the following:
4 (i) Support greater density.
5 (ii) Increase access to affordable or middle-income housing.
6 (iii) Improve direct access to multimodal transportation.
7 (iv) Improve quality of life through increased parks, green
8 spaces, outdoor recreation, and arts and cultural amenities.
9 (3) The legislature finds and declares that the appropriation
10 described in this section is for a public purpose and serves the
11 health, safety, and general welfare of the residents of this state.
12 (4) The department may do any of the following to implement
13 the grant program:
14 (a) Develop guidelines to accept and review local talent plans
15 from eligible applicants and award funding for approved local
16 talent plans to increase this state's population of young talent by
17 creating high-density, high-amenity, walkable, vibrant-street-life
18 neighborhoods or districts and to create business ownership
19 opportunities for local residents.
20 (b) Consult with local stakeholders, provide education and
21 consultation to the public during the application process, and
22 regularly monitor implementation progress of approved local talent
23 plans.
24 (c) Review existing best practices for similar programs and
25 consult with third-party experts, including academic and research
26 institutions based in this state.
27 (d) Lead a multiagency coordination effort to leverage all
28 available resources that will maximize the effectiveness of the
29 initiative. As necessary, the department may establish memoranda of
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

664
1 understanding with other state agencies or establish a committee of
2 state agency representatives to support the initiative.
3 (5) The department shall allocate funding for the grant
4 program through the development and submission of local talent
5 plans from eligible applicants that use a blend of layered,
6 multifaceted activities described in subsection (7) to meet the
7 goals of this section.
8 (6) Eligible applicants for a grant must be a consortium of
9 entities that may include local governments, local economic
10 development organizations, the nonprofit community, and the
11 business community. Consortium applicants must appoint a lead
12 applicant, which may be 1 of the consortium's entities or a
13 nonprofit organization, to serve as fiduciary and project manager
14 for the consortium. An eligible applicant may partner with other
15 government agencies, consortiums, authorities, and community
16 anchors or nonprofit entities to submit a local talent plan to the
17 department and implement an approved local talent plan.
18 Notwithstanding local talent plan revisions or subsequent funding
19 rounds, an eligible applicant shall not submit more than 1 local
20 talent plan. Only grant applicants that provide a minimum of 50%
21 local or private match funds may be considered for a grant under
22 this section.
23 (7) Approved local talent plans must include at least 1 of the
24 following eligible activities to meet the objectives of the grant
25 program:
26 (a) Planning, engineering, permitting review, and other local
27 assessments to support implementation of a local talent plan.
28 (b) Demonstrated community engagement, stakeholder support, or
29 commitment to the local talent plan. As applicable, stakeholders
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

665
1 may include, but are not limited to, any of the following:
2 (i) Neighborhood associations.
3 (ii) City councils, planning committees, or other local
4 government agencies, including public safety agencies.
5 (iii) Economic development organizations or local businesses or
6 business organizations.
7 (iv) Local anchor institutions.
8 (v) Local nonprofits, foundations, or community organizations.
9 (vi) Regional planning organizations or consortiums.
10 (vii) Public transit organizations.
11 (viii) Faith-based organizations.
12 (ix) Tribal governments.
13 (c) Plans for the redevelopment of existing housing stock.
14 (d) Plans to improve utilization of mixed-use and commercial
15 property, including, but not limited to, the conversion of
16 commercial space for affordable housing.
17 (e) Road repairs and other surface improvements that will
18 increase walkability, access to green space, dedicated nonmotorized
19 transportation, and access to rapid transit or high-speed rail.
20 (f) Supports for small businesses and emerging entrepreneurs,
21 including access to credit and professional development.
22 (g) Plans to enhance parks, green space, community recreation,
23 promotion of local artists or art installations, and outdoor social
24 spaces.
25 (h) Activities to pursue other sources of funding to directly
26 support a local talent plan, including other governmental funds or
27 private resources.
28 (8) In addition to the eligible activities under subsection
29 (7), approved local talent plans must include all of the following:
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

666

25 criteria as determined by the fund.

24 state constitution of 1963, for the fiscal year ending September

28 statement below identifies appropriations from which spending to

8 Sec. 202. The appropriations under this part and part 1 are
9 subject to the management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1101
10 to 18.1594.
11 Sec. 203. As used in this part and part 1:
12 (a) "Department" means the department of licensing and
13 regulatory affairs.
14 (b) "DHS" means the United States Department of Homeland
15 Security.
16 (c) "Director" means the director of the department.
17 (d) "DOT" means the United States Department of
18 Transportation.
19 (e) "EPA" means the United States Environmental Protection
20 Agency.
21 (f) "FOIA" means the freedom of information act, 1976 PA 442,
22 MCL 15.231 to 15.246.
23 (g) "FTE" means full-time equated position in the classified
24 service of this state.
25 (h) "HHS" means the United States Department of Health and
26 Human Services.
27 (i) "IDG" means interdepartmental grant.
28 (j) "MDIFS" means the Michigan department of insurance and
29 financial services.
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

682
1 (k) "PMECSEMA" means pain management education and controlled
2 substances electronic monitoring and antidiversion.
3 (l) "Standard report recipients" means the senate and house
4 appropriations committees, the senate and house appropriations
5 subcommittees on licensing and regulatory affairs and insurance and
6 financial services, the senate and house fiscal agencies, the
7 senate and house policy offices, and the state budget office.
8 Sec. 204. If the state administrative board, acting under
9 section 3 of 1921 PA 2, MCL 17.3, transfers funds from an amount
10 appropriated under part 1, the legislature may, by a concurrent
11 resolution adopted by a majority of the members elected to and
12 serving in each house, intertransfer funds within part 1 for the
13 particular department, board, commission, officer, or institution.
14 Sec. 205. (1) The department shall use the internet to fulfill
15 the reporting requirements of this part and shall make each report
16 readily accessible to the public and conspicuously post each
17 required report in a single archivable location on the department's
18 website not later than the due date required for each report.
19 (2) In addition to placing all reports required in the current
20 fiscal year on the department's website, the department shall
21 maintain on its website all reports placed on the website from
22 previous fiscal years posted by fiscal year in the same single
23 archivable location.
24 (3) The department shall transmit all required reports for the
25 current fiscal year to the standard report recipients and any other
26 required recipients by email. The email shall include a copy of the
27 report and a link to access the report online.
28 Sec. 206. The department shall receive and retain copies of
29 all reports funded from appropriations in part 1. The department
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

683
1 shall follow federal and state law and guidelines for short-term
2 and long-term retention of records. The department may
3 electronically retain copies of reports unless otherwise required
4 by federal and state guidelines.

6 department of technology, management, and budget to maintain a
7 searchable website accessible by the public at no cost that

13 including the vendor name, payment date, payment amount, and
14 payment description.

17 on a quarterly basis.
18 Sec. 208. (1) In addition to any other requirements under this
19 part, if the department is authorized under this part to expend
20 funds in addition to those appropriated in part 1, the department
21 shall do all of the following:
22 (a) Not later than December 1, provide a report to the
23 chairpersons of the house and senate appropriations committees, the
24 house and senate fiscal agencies, and the state budget office that
25 details all of the following:
26 (i) The type of funding received during the previous fiscal
27 year that was authorized in part 2 of the article that made

684
1 (iii) The amount of funding received.
2 (iv) How much of the funding was spent and for what purpose or
3 purposes.

5 under this part, provide a report to the chairpersons of the house
6 and senate appropriations committees, the house and senate fiscal
7 agencies, and the state budget office that details all of the
8 following:
9 (i) The type of funding received.
10 (ii) When the funding was received.
11 (iii) The amount of funding received.

13 specified purpose or purposes.

17 contains an estimate of funding authorized by this part that the
18 department anticipates it will receive in the subsequent fiscal
19 year, and identifies all of the following:
20 (i) The type or types of funding anticipated.
21 (ii) The amount or amounts of funding anticipated.
22 (iii) The purpose or purposes of the funding.
23 (2) If another reporting requirement under this part would
24 provide substantially similar information on a substantially
25 similar timeframe as would be reported under subsection (1),
26 subsection (1) does not apply.

22 committee on administrative rules.

20 Sec. 221. To the extent permissible under section 261 of the
21 management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1261, all of the
22 following apply to the expenditure of funds appropriated in part 1:
23 (a) The funds must not be used for the purchase of foreign
24 goods or services, or both, if competitively priced and of
25 comparable quality American goods or services, or both, are
26 available.
27 (b) Preference must be given to goods or services, or both,
28 manufactured or provided by Michigan businesses, if they are
29 competitively priced and of comparable quality.
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

689
1 (c) Preference must be given to goods or services, or both,
2 that are manufactured or provided by Michigan businesses owned and
3 operated by veterans, if they are competitively priced and of
4 comparable quality.
5 Sec. 222. The department shall not take disciplinary action
6 against an employee of the department because the employee
7 communicates with a member of the legislature or legislative staff
8 unless the communication is prohibited by law and the department is
9 exercising its authority as provided by law.
10 Sec. 223. (1) The department shall maximize utilization of its
11 in-person state workforce. The department shall prioritize
12 occupancy utilization of office space for each division within the
13 department. Employees with job responsibilities that require
14 employees to serve in their capacities outside of an office shall
15 be monitored each pay period to ensure all work hours reported on
16 timesheets are actually worked.
17 (2) The department shall comply with requirements set forth by
18 the office of the state employer on in-person work and utilization
19 and occupancy rates of state buildings to ensure in-person work is
20 optimized and occupancy rates are 80% or higher, subject to market
21 conditions.
22 (3) The department shall adhere to civil service rules and
23 regulations that state the standard biweekly work period for a
24 full-time employee in the classified service of this state is the
25 equivalent of 80 hours of work. The department shall establish
26 policies and processes to ensure all employees are working their
27 jobs during agreed-upon business hours.
28 (4) The office of the state employer must create and implement
29 an occupancy utilization uniform policy on occupancy, utilization,
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

690
1 and in-person and remote work. The office of the state employer
2 must make each uniform policy publicly available on the
3 department's website.
4 Sec. 224. Each agency shall complete a space utilization
5 assessment by July 1, 2027, using a form developed by the
6 department of technology, management, and budget for all space
7 assigned under its building occupancy agreement and leased office
8 locations and post on their website. Agencies shall develop, in
9 coordination with the department of technology, management, and
10 budget, a plan to reduce, consolidate, or otherwise optimize
11 assigned space. The plans must prioritize the use of state-owned
12 facilities, wherever possible, and comply with department of
13 technology, management, and budget established space standards
14 unless an exception is approved by the department of technology,
15 management, and budget.
16 Sec. 225. To the extent permissible under the management and
17 budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1101 to 18.1594, the director shall
18 take all reasonable steps to ensure geographically disadvantaged
19 business enterprises compete for and perform contracts to provide
20 services or supplies, or both. The director shall strongly
21 encourage firms with which the department contracts to subcontract
22 with certified geographically disadvantaged business enterprises
23 for services, supplies, or both. As used in this section,
24 "geographically disadvantaged business enterprises" means that term
25 as defined in Executive Directive No. 2019-8.
26 Sec. 226. No later than December 31, 2026, and again by June
27 1, 2027, the department shall provide a report to the standard
28 report recipients that includes detail regarding any federal
29 guidelines, rules, regulations, or other significant federal policy
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

691
1 changes, including H.R. 1 and the Rural Health Transformation
2 Program, that do, or are expected to, significantly impact the
3 operations of the department, including increases or reductions in
4 federal revenue and changes that are likely to improve or impede
5 the department's ability to safeguard the health or welfare of the
6 public.
7 Sec. 227. (1) Not later than 30 days after the enactment of
8 this act, the house and senate shall provide to the state budget

12 1. The list must include all information and documents pertaining

15 431, MCL 18.1364 and 18.1364a.
16 (2) In accordance with section 364(4) of the management and
