18 administering the grant shall post a report in a publicly
19 accessible location on its website beginning March 15 of the
20 current fiscal year. The department or agency shall update the
21 report and shall post an updated report not later than June 15 of
22 the current fiscal year and again not later than September 15 of
23 the current fiscal year. The department shall include in the report
24 the most comprehensive information the department has available at
25 the time of posting for grants awarded.
26 Sec. 228. The state budget director shall take steps to ensure
27 that all state fiscal recovery funds allocated to this state under
28 the American rescue plan act of 2021, Public Law 117-2,
29 are expended by December 31, 2026, as required by law. Any state
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

692
1 fiscal recovery funds that would otherwise lapse after September
2 30, 2026 are automatically reappropriated for the same purpose as
3 originally authorized and available for expenditure through
4 December 31, 2026, and any subsequent financial close-out period.
5 Sec. 229. (1) The state budget director shall take steps to
6 ensure that all state fiscal recovery funds allocated to this state
7 under the American rescue plan act of 2021, Public Law 117-2,
8 are expended by December 31, 2026, as required by law. The state
9 budget director may reallocate appropriated funds for the purpose
10 of fully utilizing state fiscal recovery funds that are in jeopardy
11 of not meeting the expenditure deadline for reasons that may
12 include, but are not limited to, completed projects coming in under
13 budget or funds unable to be fully used by subrecipients. The state
14 budget director shall reallocate any of the funds reallocated under
15 this subsection to the programs or purposes specified in this
16 section. Any funds reallocated are unappropriated and immediately
17 reappropriated for the following purposes:
18 (a) To reclassify general fund/general purpose appropriations
19 for payroll and covered benefits for eligible public health and
20 safety employees at the department of corrections.
21 (b) To reclassify general fund/general purpose appropriations
22 for payroll and covered benefits for eligible public health and
23 safety employees at the department of state police.
24 (2) All applicable guidance, implementation, and reporting
25 provisions of Public Law 117-2 must be followed for state fiscal
26 recovery funds reallocated and reappropriated under subsection (1).
27 (3) The state budget director shall notify the senate and
28 house appropriations committees not later than 10 business days
29 after making any reallocations under subsection (1). The
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

693
1 notification must include the authorized program under which funds
2 were originally appropriated, the amount of the reallocation, the
3 program, or programs, or purpose, and the department to which the
4 funds are being reallocated under subsection (1), and the amount
5 reallocated to each program or purpose.
6 Sec. 230. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
7 department shall do the following:
8 (a) Report on any amounts of severance pay for a department
9 director, deputy director, or other high-ranking department
10 official not later than 14 days after a severance agreement with
11 the director, deputy director, or official is signed. The name of
12 the director, deputy director, or official and the amount of
13 severance pay must be included in the report required by this
14 subdivision.
15 (b) Not later than February 1, report on the total amount of
16 severance pay remitted to former department employees during the
17 previous fiscal year and the total number of former department
18 employees that were remitted severance pay during the previous
19 fiscal year.
20 (2) As used in this section, "severance pay" means
21 compensation to which both of the following apply:
22 (a) The compensation is payable or paid upon the termination
23 of employment.
24 (b) The compensation is paid in addition to wages or benefits
25 earned during the course of employment or generally applicable
26 retirement benefits.

21 special events.

23 department's costs of sponsoring informational, training, or
24 special events carries forward to the subsequent fiscal year and
25 does not lapse to the general fund.

8 LIQUOR CONTROL COMMISSION
9 Sec. 401. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 from the
10 direct shipper enforcement revolving fund, the liquor control
11 commission shall expend the funds as required under section 203(11)
12 of the Michigan liquor control code of 1998, 1998 PA 58, MCL
13 436.1203, to investigate and audit unlawful direct shipments of
14 wine by unlicensed wineries and retailers, with priority directed
15 toward unlicensed out-of-state retailers and third-party marketers.
16 In addition to other investigative methods, the commission shall
17 use shipping records available to the commission under section
18 203(21) of the Michigan liquor control code of 1998, 1998 PA 58,
19 MCL 436.1203, to assist with the effort to investigate and audit
20 unlawful direct shipments of wine by unlicensed wineries and
21 retailers. The liquor control commission shall refer all identified
22 unlicensed out-of-state retailers and third-party marketers to the
23 attorney general.
24 (2) Not later than February 1, the liquor control commission
25 shall provide a report to the legislature and the standard report
26 recipients that details the commission's activities to investigate
27 and audit the illegal shipping of wine and the results of the
28 activities. The report must include all of the following:
29 (a) Work hours spent, specific actions performed, and the
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

704
1 number of full-time equated positions dedicated to identifying and
2 stopping unlicensed out-of-state retailers, third-party marketers,
3 and wineries that ship illegally in Michigan.
4 (b) General overview of expenditures associated with efforts
5 to identify and stop unlicensed out-of-state retailers, third-party
6 marketers, and wineries that ship illegally in this state.
7 (c) Number of out-of-state entities found to have illegally
8 shipped wine into this state and total number of 750 ml bottles,
9 number of cases with 750 ml bottles, number of liters, number of
10 gallons, or weight of illegally shipped wine. These items must be
11 itemized by total number of retailers and total number of wineries.
12 (d) Suggested areas of focus on how to address direct shipper
13 enforcement and illegal importation in the future.
14 (e) Number of unlicensed out-of-state entities found to have
15 illegally shipped wine into this state that were identified with
16 the shipping records described in subsection (1).
17 (f) Number of notices sent under subsection (3).
18 (3) From the funds appropriated in part 1 from the direct
19 shipper enforcement revolving fund, the liquor control commission
20 shall send a notice to each unlicensed out-of-state entity found to
21 have illegally shipped wine into this state. The notice must
22 include all of the following statements:
23 (a) That shipping wine into this state by unlicensed out-of-
24 state retailers and third-party marketers is illegal, and wineries
25 shipping into this state must obtain a direct shipper license.
26 (b) That under section 909 of the Michigan liquor control code
27 of 1998, 1998 PA 58, MCL 436.1909, making unlawful shipments of
28 wine into this state may be a felony punishable by imprisonment for
29 not more than 4 years or a fine of not more than $5,000.00, or
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

705
1 both.
2 (c) That the matter has been referred to the attorney general.

19 (u) A list of the fees charged for license applications,
20 license renewals, and registry cards.
21 (v) The costs of administering the licensing program under
22 each act.
23 Sec. 602. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the cannabis
24 regulatory agency shall post on a publicly accessible website a
25 list of all of the following:
26 (a) The number of investigative reports that identify
27 violations of the Michigan Medical Marihuana Act, 2008 IL 1, MCL
28 333.26421 to 333.26430, the medical marihuana facilities licensing
29 act, 2016 PA 281, MCL 333.27101 to 333.27801, or the Michigan
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

1 Regulation and Taxation of Marihuana Act, 2018 IL 1, MCL 333.27951
2 to 333.27967, or violations of administrative rules promulgated
3 under the Michigan Medical Marihuana Act, 2008 IL 1, MCL 333.26421
4 to 333.26430, the medical marihuana facilities licensing act, 2016
5 PA 281, MCL 333.27101 to 333.27801, or the Michigan Regulation and
6 Taxation of Marihuana Act, 2018 IL 1, MCL 333.27951 to 333.27967.
7 (b) The number of investigative reports that identify
8 suspected marihuana product that does not have the tracking numbers
9 assigned by the statewide monitoring system affixed, tagged, or
10 labeled as required by law.

14 assigned by the statewide monitoring system affixed, tagged, or
15 labeled as required by law.

8 than March 1, the Michigan indigent defense commission shall submit
9 a report to the standard report recipients on all of the following
10 information:

12 funding unit for the previous fiscal year to implement all approved
13 indigent defense standards. The list must include the amount of
14 state funding and local share for each system.

18 five fiscal years organized by Michigan indigent defense commission

22 such as the number of arraignments with appointed counsel, the

25 and the percentage of attorneys meeting with clients within the

15 does not prohibit expenditures for renovations to existing

1 purpose of planning, developing, designing, acquiring, or

10 (3) The Michigan public service commission shall develop
11 guidelines for the grant program described in subsection (1) and
12 implement an application process for the grant program not later
13 than 6 months after the effective date of this act and must first
14 prioritize and approve grants that meet the goals of the governor's

17 impact study that includes an analysis of potential cost savings,
18 environmental impacts, and local economic benefits of the
19 applicant's proposed renewable energy and electrification
20 infrastructure project. A utility, at its sole discretion, may
21 prepare a single impact study that covers the utility's service
22 territory that accounts for likely proposals, evaluates regional
23 opportunities, and minimizes or eliminates the need for repetitive
24 studies. Sufficient detail must be provided in the study to allow
25 the Michigan public service commission to evaluate each proposed
26 project, including how the proposed project will align with the
27 governor's MI healthy climate plan.

ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

722
1 information in the applicant's application:
2 (a) The project details, including the location of biogas and
3 the proposed interconnection.

5 gas conditioning equipment needed to connect to an existing
6 pipeline system.

17 provide comments. The Michigan public service commission shall
18 allow the applicant not less than 15 days after the comments have
19 been received from interested parties, at the applicant's
20 discretion, to modify or maintain the applicant's initial proposal.
21 (7) The Michigan public service commission shall review all
22 proposals and award grants to applicants it determines have met the
23 criteria in this section. All grants must include full and timely
24 cost recovery from the fund for the infrastructure requirements of
25 the affected utility made necessary by the grant. All grants must
26 be awarded on a competitive basis with priority given to applicants
27 in a distressed area. As used in this section, "distressed area"
28 means any of the following:
29 (a) An area in a city, village, or township that has been
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

723
1 designated as blighted.

3 unemployment rate greater than the statewide average.

5 the neighborhood enterprise zone act, 1992 PA 147, MCL 207.771 to
6 207.786.
7 (d) An area facing a disproportionate environmental burden,
8 using the department of environment, Great Lakes, and energy's
9 MiEJScreen or EPA's EJSCREEN.
10 (8) Not later than 30 days after the completion of the

4 provide substantially similar information on a substantially
5 similar time frame as would be reported under subsection (1),
6 subsection (1) does not apply.

7 regulations that state the standard biweekly work period for a
8 full-time employee in the classified service of this state is the

3 OFFICE OF HIGHER EDUCATION
4 Sec. 701. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1, in
5 addition to other statutorily required duties, the department shall
6 do all of the following:
7 (a) Review and evaluate all state financial aid programs. The
8 department shall prioritize improving postsecondary educational
9 outcomes, including student completion rates, and improving
10 affordability of postsecondary programs in this state.
11 (b) Serve as the coordinating office for all agencies of the
12 executive branch of government that are responsible for financial
13 aid programs administered by this state.
14 (c) Survey stakeholders, including public, tribal, and private
15 not-for-profit colleges and universities, state departments and
16 agencies, and statewide postsecondary education associations on
17 student financial aid policy to improve this state's administration
18 of programs.
19 (d) Collaborate with the center for educational performance
20 and information and individual colleges and universities to ensure
21 streamlined and coordinated collection of data analyzing the
22 following:
23 (i) Postsecondary education costs, including a comparison to
24 national and regional averages.
25 (ii) Student enrollment.
26 (iii) Degree completion.
27 (e) Provide access to higher education institutional data
28 inventory on an accessible, public facing dashboard to assist
29 students, prospective students, and their families in making
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

743
1 decisions on postsecondary education.
2 (f) Coordinate with other state agencies and school districts
3 to increase utilization and awareness of postsecondary
4 opportunities, including, but not limited to, early and middle
5 college, dual enrollment, and private skills training scholarships.
6 (g) Promote, track, and provide resources to increase
7 completion of the free application for federal student aid.
8 (2) From the funds appropriated in part 1, the department
9 shall meet, at a minimum, the following transparency requirements:
10 (a) Collect data necessary to complete all statutory reporting
11 requirements. The department shall notify the chairs of the house
12 and senate appropriations committees within 10 days if an entity
13 receiving funds from part 1 fails to comply with data collection
14 requirements.
15 (b) Maintain a link on the department's website to find data
16 submitted by postsecondary institutions through higher education
17 institutional data inventory.
18 (c) Maintain a link on the department's website to the center
19 for educational performance and information's MI School Data page
20 on postsecondary enrollment and completion tracking.
21 (3) As used in this section, "center for educational
22 performance and information" means the center for educational
23 performance and information created in section 94a of the state
24 school aid act of 1979, 1979 PA 94, MCL 388.1694a.

6 Sec. 1006. (1) The Early Childhood Investment Fund is created
7 as a restricted fund in the department of treasury. The fund shall
8 only be expended for eligible uses under this section.
9 (2) The state treasurer shall direct investment and credit to
10 the fund all amounts appropriated to the fund and may receive money
11 or other assets from any source for deposit into the fund.
12 (3) Money available in the fund at the close of the fiscal
13 year remains in the fund and does not lapse.
14 (4) The fund shall be utilized to support participating
15 providers, families, children, or early childhood workers, for
16 eligible initiatives that supplement any of the following programs:
17 (a) Child Development and Care program.
18 (b) Early On program.
19 (c) Great Start Readiness Program.
20 (d) Programs for infants and toddlers.
21 (5) Eligible uses include, but are not limited to, one-time or
22 recuring payments to licensed providers for any of the following
23 uses:
24 (a) Increased provider or reimbursements rates.
25 (b) Funding to increase eligibility or the number of available
26 program scholarships or slots, as applicable.
27 (c) Funding that would reduce co-pays, fees, or other costs to
28 families enrolled in eligible programs, as applicable.
29 (d) Grants for infrastructure or equipment, the creation or
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

747
1 expansion of licensed facilities.
2 (e) Provision of education programs, curriculum, or services
3 through licensed providers.

5 childhood workers including, but not limited to, training or
6 professional development, supports for career advancement,
7 compensation, or benefits.
8 (g) Other community-based initiatives or pilot programs, that
9 will also leverage non-state funds, for eligible uses under this
10 section.
11 (6) Not later than January 1, the department shall transmit an

13 Sec. 1009. From the funds appropriated in part 1 for child
14 development and care public assistance, the income entrance
15 eligibility threshold for the child development and care program is
16 set to not more than 200% of the federal poverty guidelines.
17 Sec. 1011. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for child
18 development and care public assistance, for eligible children in
19 the child development and care program, the department shall
20 implement payments to providers based on a child's care services
21 agreement detailing their care schedule and agreed upon hours per
22 week. This shall be done in a manner determined by the department.
23 (2) Providers must complete time and attendance records daily
24 for each approved CDC child in care. Records must include the
25 child's full name, the date, time in, time out, and the parent's
26 initials. Providers must certify that attendance data is correct
27 and affirm that if benefits are overpaid for any reason, the extra
28 payments will be repaid. If intentional misrepresentation caused
29 the overpayment, any and all responsible partners will be
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

751
1 disqualified from the program and will be prosecuted for fraud when
2 appropriate. Historical time and attendance records must be saved
3 for 4 years before they can be destroyed.
4 (3) Providers may only bill for care when children are present
5 or absent and expected to return per a signed care services
6 agreement. If a child is absent for more than 10 consecutive days
7 without a qualifying, documented reason for the absence, the
8 provider must stop billing for that child until the child returns
9 to care. Absence hours are limited to 360 hours per fiscal year or
10 20% of a child's approved hours, whichever is less.
11 Sec. 1012. From the funds appropriated in part 1 for child
12 development and care contracted services, $1,500,000.00 must be for
13 the department to work in collaboration with DHHS to continue the
14 network of infant and early childhood mental health consultation,
15 which provides mental health consultation to child care providers.
16 Sec. 1025. (1) Private revenues received by the department are
17 appropriated upon receipt and are available for expenditure by the
18 department as permitted under state and federal law.
19 (2) Not later than 10 days after the receipt of a private
20 revenues appropriated in subsection (1), the department shall
21 notify the standard report recipients of the receipt of the funds,
22 including source, purpose, and amount.
23 (3) The amount appropriated under subsection (1) must not
24 exceed $3,000,000.00.
25 Sec. 1030. (1) The funds appropriated in part 1 for the tri-
26 share child care program must be awarded for the continuation of
27 the child care facilitator program originally initiated and funded
28 as a pilot project in section 1047(31) of article 5 of 2020 PA 166.
29 (2) The department shall award funds to support regional
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

752
1 recruitment activities and statewide services.
2 (3) The department must create benchmarks for regional
3 recruitment efforts and statewide support services.
4 (4) Any entity receiving funds under this section must be a
5 nonprofit intermediate school district, limited liability company,
6 C-corporation, S-corporation, or a sole proprietor.
7 (5) Funds may be used to enroll in the tri-share child care
8 program families living outside of Michigan but who have a parent
9 or guardian who is employed in Michigan. A child care provider
10 providing care for a family described in this subsection must be
11 located and licensed in Michigan.
12 (6) The department may develop program and administration
13 options to attract Michigan employer program participation.
14
15 ONE-TIME APPROPRIATIONS
16 Sec. 1101. From the funds appropriated in part 1 for
17 reenrollment services, the department shall deliver reenrollment
18 services for Michigan residents with some college and no degree for
19 the purpose of reengaging learners to increase the number of
20 Michigan adults completing postsecondary degrees or credentials in
21 this state. If necessary, the department may contract with private
22 organizations with prior reenrollment work for an amount up to
23 $500,000.00 to accomplish the purpose of this section.
24 Sec. 1102. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the Michigan
25 Center for Adult College Success must continue to improve adult
26 postsecondary enrollment and completion under the Michigan
27 reconnect grant act, 2020 PA 84, MCL 390.1701 to 390.1709.

29 postsecondary opportunities, the department may expend up to
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

753
1 $125,000.00 for public outreach to raise awareness among men of
2 postsecondary opportunities to address disparities in postsecondary
3 credential attainment by men.
4 Sec. 1105. From the one-time funds appropriated in part 1 for
5 time and attendance billing system, the department shall use
6 $2,000,000.00 to invest in a time and attendance billing system
7 that enhances the transparency and accountability while
8 streamlining administrative burden to meet federal provider payment
9 compliance provisions.

1 against an employee of the department because the employee

5 Sec. 223. (1) The department shall maximize utilization of its
6 in-person state workforce. The department shall prioritize
7 occupancy utilization of office space for each division within the
8 department. Employees with job responsibilities that require the
9 employees to serve in their capacities outside of an office shall
10 be monitored each pay period to ensure all work hours reported on
11 timesheets were actually worked.
12 (2) The department shall comply with requirements set forth by
13 the office of the state employer on in-person work and utilization
14 and occupancy rates of state buildings to ensure at least 80% of
15 employees are in person and building occupancy and utilization
16 rates are at least 80% or higher, subject to market conditions.
17 (3) The department shall adhere to civil service rules and
18 regulations that state the standard biweekly work period for a
19 full-time employee in the classified service of this state is the
20 equivalent of 80 hours of work. The department shall establish
21 policies and processes to ensure all employees are working their
22 jobs during agreed-upon business hours.
23 (4) The office of the state employer must create and implement
24 an occupancy utilization uniform policy on occupancy, utilization,
25 in-person, and remote work. The office of the state employer must
26 make each uniform policy publicly available on the department's
27 website.

4 official not later than 14 days after a severance agreement with
5 the director, deputy director, or official is signed. The name of
6 the director, deputy director, or official and the amount of
7 severance pay must be included in the report required by this
8 subdivision.
9 (b) Not later than February 1, report on the total amount of
10 severance pay remitted to former department employees during the
11 previous fiscal year and the total number of former department
12 employees that were remitted severance pay during the previous
13 fiscal year.
14 (2) As used in this section, "severance pay" means
15 compensation to which both of the following apply:
16 (a) The compensation is payable or paid upon the termination
17 of employment.
18 (b) The compensation is paid in addition to wages or benefits
19 earned during the course of employment or generally applicable
20 retirement benefits.

24 contract execution. Not later than March 31, the department shall

27 as contracts executed under the assessments.

10 Sec. 307. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for
11 Michigan National Guard member benefit fund, the department shall
12 maintain a Michigan National Guard tuition assistance program as
13 provided under the Michigan National Guard tuition assistance act,
14 2014 PA 259, MCL 32.431 to 32.434. The Michigan National Guard
15 tuition assistance program must do all of the following:
16 (a) Bolster military readiness by increasing recruitment and
17 retention of Michigan Army and Air National Guard members.
18 (b) Fill federally authorized strength levels for the state.
19 (c) Improve the Michigan Army and Air National Guard's
20 competitive draw from other military enlistment options in the
21 state.
22 (d) Enhance the ability of the Michigan Army and Air National
23 Guard to compete for guard members and federal dollars with
24 surrounding states.
25 (e) Increase the pool of eligible candidates within the
26 Michigan Army and Air National Guard to become commissioned
27 officers.
28 (2) The department shall make efforts to increase the number
29 of National Guard members who have received a credential or are
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

777
1 still enrolled in the Michigan National Guard tuition assistance
2 program after their initial term of enlistment. To evaluate the
3 effectiveness of the program, the department shall monitor the
4 number of new recruits and new reenlistments and the percentage of
5 those who become participants in the program to determine whether
6 the percentage of authorized Michigan Army and Air National Guard
7 strength obtained and retained is competitive in comparison with
8 the neighboring army and air national guards from Illinois,
9 Indiana, Ohio, and Wisconsin.
10 (3) Not later than March 1, the department shall provide a

4 Sec. 411. (1) The funds appropriated in part 1 for veterans
5 service grants must be deposited into the veterans service fund
6 created in this part. All funds available in the veterans service
7 fund are appropriated and available for expenditure as provided
8 under this section.
9 (2) The MVAA shall establish, administer, and award
10 competitive grants to 1 or more congressionally chartered VSOs or a
11 coalition of VSOs. The MVAA shall award grants to support efforts
12 to connect veterans and their dependents with federal compensation
13 and pension benefits and state veterans' benefits, including
14 emergency grants through the Michigan veterans' trust fund and
15 other local or nonprofit assistance that may be available to
16 veterans and their dependents. The MVAA shall establish a
17 competitive grant process that satisfies the following:
18 (a) Utilizes a service provision model to provide services
19 across the state and can be tracked regionally to ensure that
20 veterans and their dependents in this state, including those within
21 tribal communities, are provided with services, advocacy, and
22 outreach as close to the communities in which they live as
23 possible.
24 (b) Ensures that grantees are providing adequate veteran
25 services and advocacy, through in-person and virtual meetings, that
26 enables the organization to meet performance goals established in
27 the grant agreement.
28 (c) Fosters innovative and transformative approaches and
29 techniques for the grantee to use when providing services,
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

787
1 advocacy, and outreach for veterans and their dependents.
2 (d) Requires grantees to use an MVAA-designated internet-based
3 claims data system to manage caseloads. License fees associated
4 with the claims data system described in this subdivision are
5 considered an allowable expenditure and may be reimbursed with
6 grant funds.
7 (e) Requires grantees, in coordination with the MVAA, to
8 provide services to incarcerated veterans who are within 1 year of
9 their earliest release date.
10 (f) Ensures that each grantee is issued performance goals.
11 (g) Ensures that each grantee expends grant awards as
12 prescribed in the grant agreement.
13 (h) Requires each grantee to report not less than quarterly on
14 all of the following:
15 (i) An accounting for all grant fund expenditures.
16 (ii) The number and type of claims originated and submitted by
17 the grantee to the USDVA.
18 (iii) The number and type of claims originated by an
19 organization other than the grantee and submitted by the grantee to
20 the USDVA.
21 (iv) The services provided to veterans and their dependents.
22 (v) Progress in achieving monthly performance benchmark goals.
23 (i) Ensures that each grantee is issued monthly performance
24 benchmark goals that each grantee must aim to achieve and require
25 each grantee to report to the MVAA, in order to ensure that
26 benchmark goals are being achieved, or on target to be achieved, in
27 the fiscal year.
28 (3) The MVAA shall do all of the following:
29 (a) Follow all generally accepted accounting principles in
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

788
1 accordance with sections 141 and 485 of the management and budget
2 act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1141 and 18.1485.
3 (b) When establishing, modifying, or amending the competitive
4 grant process described in subsection (1), consult and collaborate
5 with congressionally chartered VSOs in the state, or a coalition of
6 VSOs, and other stakeholders to ensure a comprehensive approach to
7 providing services, advocacy, and outreach to veterans and their
8 dependents.
9 (c) Provide notice to current grantees of any MVAA-proposed
10 modifications or amendments to the competitive grant process and
11 provide those grantees with an opportunity to respond through
12 written communication.
13 (d) Assess the accuracy rate of claims reported by grantees.

17 18.1470.

7 department may partner with the DHHS to facilitate and administer a

17 responders.

19 MICHIGAN VETERANS' FACILITY AUTHORITY
20 Sec. 502. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1, the MVH
21 and the MVFA shall provide compassionate and quality nursing care
22 services at each veterans' facility in this state so that resident
23 members can achieve their highest potential of wellness,
24 independence, self-worth, and dignity. The MVFA and the MVH shall
25 provide nursing care services to veterans in accordance with
26 federal standards and report the results of the annual USDVA and
27 CMS surveys and certification as proof of compliance.
28 (2) Appropriations in part 1 for a veterans' facility shall
29 not be used for any purpose other than expenses related to the
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

793
1 operations of the veterans' facility.
2 Sec. 503. All contractors providing health care services at a
3 veterans' facility shall provide services in a manner that complies
4 with applicable USDVA and CMS regulations for state veterans' homes
5 and skilled nursing facilities, any rules governing the operation
6 of nursing homes licensed in this state, and any training and
7 education requirements associated with staff licensure or
8 certification.
9 Sec. 504. (1) The MVFA shall report and investigate all
10 complaints of abuse or neglect at a veterans' facility in
11 compliance with USDVA and CMS regulations for state veterans' homes
12 and skilled nursing facilities. The MVFA shall report on a
13 bimonthly basis the following information:
14 (a) A description of the process by which resident members and
15 others may file complaints of alleged abuse or neglect at a
16 veterans' facility.
17 (b) Summary statistics on the number and general nature of
18 complaints of abuse or neglect.
19 (c) Summary statistics on the final disposition of complaints
20 of abuse or neglect received.
21 (2) The MVFA shall display in high-traffic areas throughout
22 the veterans' facility the process by which visitors, resident
23 members, and staff of the veterans' facility may register
24 complaints.
25 Sec. 505. The MVH shall do the following regarding member
26 care:
27 (a) Provide board-certified psychiatric care for all resident
28 members with mental health disorders in order to ensure that those
29 resident members receive needed services in a professional and
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

794
1 timely manner.
2 (b) Provide all resident members and staff a safe and secure
3 environment.
4 (c) Ensure that the veterans' facility effectively develops,
5 executes, and monitors all comprehensive care plans in accordance
6 with federal regulations and the veterans' facility's internal
7 policies, with a goal that a comprehensive care plan is fully
8 developed for all resident members.
9 Sec. 506. The MVH shall establish and implement internal
10 controls regarding all of the following:
11 (a) The use and management of food, maintenance, and
12 pharmaceutical and medical supply inventories.
13 (b) Calculating resident member maintenance assessments in
14 order to accurately calculate resident member maintenance
15 assessments for each billing cycle and ensure that all past due
16 resident member maintenance assessments are addressed within 30
17 days.
18 (c) Monetary donations and donated goods.
19 (d) The handling of resident member funds to ensure the
20 release of funds within 15 calendar days upon the resident member
21 leaving the home and to ensure that a representative of a resident
22 member is provided a full accounting of that resident member's
23 funds within 30 calendar days after the death of that resident
24 member.
25 (e) Financial reporting and accounting.
26 Sec. 507. (1) The MVH shall post on its website the following:
27 (a) All policies adopted by the MVFA and the veterans'
28 facility related to the administrative operations of the veterans'
29 facility.
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

795
1 (b) The agenda and minutes of public meetings of the MVFA
2 board.
3 (2) The MVH shall provide a report that includes statistics
4 and information that demonstrates the performance of MVH compared
5 to available state and national veterans' homes or nursing homes.
6 (3) Not later than January 15, the MVH shall provide a report
7 on the following:
8 (a) Census data for each veterans' facility, including
9 information on level of care, service era of its resident members,
10 payer source, and average income and assessment rate.
11 (b) Per patient daily care hours provided by each veterans'
12 facility, by level of care.
13 (4) The MVH shall provide a bimonthly report on the financial
14 status of each veterans' facility and central MVFA/MVH
15 administration. Information shall include, but not be limited to,
16 actual year-to-date and projected year-end revenues and
17 expenditures, by fund source.
18 (5) The MVH shall provide a report on the results of any
19 annual or for-cause survey conducted by any entity with oversight
20 over the veterans' facility and any corresponding corrective action
21 plan. This information shall also be made available publicly
22 through the MVH website.
23 (6) In addition to the information required under section
24 12(1) of the Michigan veterans' facility authority act, 2016 PA
25 560, MCL 36.112, not later than January 31, the MVFA shall provide
26 a report detailing the strategies and actions taken to maximize
27 revenues from non-general fund sources and cost savings strategies.
28 Sec. 508. In addition to the funds appropriated in part 1,
29 private revenues held by the MVH on a nonfiduciary basis for a
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

796
1 resident member of a veterans' facility are appropriated to pay
2 medical expenses, member assessments, and other expenses incurred
3 by that resident member. Any unexpended or unencumbered private
4 revenues held on a nonfiduciary basis by the MVH at the close of
5 the fiscal year do not lapse to the general fund and must be
6 carried forward into the subsequent fiscal year.
7 Sec. 509. Not later than January 15 , the MVFA shall provide a
8 report on the construction, operation, and finances of the new
9 Marquette veterans home funded in article 14 of 2022 PA 166.
10 Sec. 510. Except as otherwise provided by law, any unexpended
11 and unencumbered federal revenues received by the MVFA do not lapse
12 to the state general fund and must be carried forward into the
13 subsequent fiscal year.
14 Sec. 511. The department, with the approval of the state
15 budget office, is authorized to realign federal revenues sources of
16 the MVFA. This realignment of federal fund sourcing must not
17 produce a gross increase or decrease in the total authorization for
18 the individual MVFA line-item appropriations. The department shall
19 provide a quarterly report to the standard report recipients on
20 actions taken under this section.

1 special maintenance - National Guard must be expended in accordance

12 Sec. 603. (1) The appropriations for special maintenance –
13 veterans' facility must be expended in accordance with the
14 requirements of section 502 and must be expended according to the
15 maintenance priorities of the MVFA to repair and modernize the
16 state's veterans' facility, which may include physical plant
17 expansions, renovations, or enhancements, and other projects
18 designed to enhance the quality of life and medical care of
19 resident members.

21 providing information on the status, projected costs, and projected
22 completion date of current and planned special maintenance projects
23 at each veterans' facility funded from capital outlay
24 appropriations made in part 1 and in previous fiscal years.

8 subject to the management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1101
9 to 18.1594.
10 Sec. 203. As used in this part and part 1:
11 (a) "Department" means the department of natural resources.
12 (b) "Director" means the director of the department.
13 (c) "FTE" means full-time equated.
14 (d) "IDG" means interdepartmental grant.
15 (e) "Standard report recipients" means the senate and house
16 appropriations committees, the senate and house appropriations
17 subcommittee on agriculture and rural development and natural
18 resources, the senate and house policy offices, and the state
19 budget office.
20 Sec. 204. If the state administrative board, acting under
21 section 3 of 1921 PA 2, MCL 17.3, transfers funds from an amount
22 appropriated under part 1, the legislature may, by a concurrent
23 resolution adopted by a majority of the members elected to and
24 serving in each house, intertransfer funds within part 1 for the
25 particular department, board, commission, officer, or institution.
26 Sec. 205. (1) The department shall use the internet to fulfill
27 the reporting requirements of this part and shall make each report
28 readily accessible to the public and conspicuously post each
29 required report in a single archivable location on the department's
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

812
1 website not later than the due date required for each report.
2 (2) In addition to placing all reports required in the current
3 fiscal year on the department's website, the department shall
4 maintain on its website all reports placed on the website from
5 previous fiscal years posted by fiscal year in the same single
6 archivable location.
7 (3) The department shall transmit all required reports for the
8 current fiscal year to the standard report recipients and any other
9 required recipients by email. The email shall include a copy of the
10 report and a link to access the report online.
11 Sec. 206. The department shall receive and retain copies of
12 all reports funded from appropriations in part 1. The department
13 shall follow federal and state law and guidelines for short-term
14 and long-term retention of records. The department may
15 electronically retain copies of reports unless otherwise required
16 by federal and state guidelines.
17 Sec. 207. (1) The department shall cooperate with the
18 department of technology, management, and budget to maintain a
19 searchable website accessible by the public at no cost that
20 includes, but is not limited to, all of the following for the
21 department:
22 (a) Fiscal year-to-date expenditures by category.
23 (b) Fiscal year-to-date expenditures by appropriation unit.
24 (c) Fiscal year-to-date payments to a selected vendor,
25 including the vendor name, payment date, payment amount, and
26 payment description.
27 (2) The department shall cooperate with the department of
28 technology, management, and budget to update the searchable website
29 on a quarterly basis.

15 Sec. 221. To the extent permissible under section 261 of the
16 management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1261, all of the
17 following apply to the expenditure of funds appropriated in part 1:
18 (a) The funds must not be used for the purchase of foreign
19 goods or services, or both, if competitively priced and of
20 comparable quality American goods or services, or both, are
21 available.
22 (b) Preference must be given to goods or services, or both,
23 manufactured or provided by Michigan businesses, if they are
24 competitively priced and of comparable quality.
25 (c) Preference must be given to goods or services, or both,
26 that are manufactured or provided by Michigan businesses owned and
27 operated by veterans, if they are competitively priced and of
28 comparable quality.

ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

818
1 against an employee of the department because the employee
2 communicates with a member of the legislature or legislative staff
3 unless the communication is prohibited by law and the department is

5 Sec. 223. (1) The department shall maximize utilization of its
6 in-person state workforce. The department shall prioritize
7 occupancy utilization of office space for each division within the
8 department. Employees with job responsibilities that require the
9 employees to serve in their capacities outside of an office shall
10 be monitored each pay period to ensure all work hours reported on
11 timesheets were actually worked.
12 (2) The department shall comply with requirements set forth by
13 the office of the state employer on in-person work and utilization
14 and occupancy rates of state buildings to ensure in-person work is
15 optimized and occupancy rates are 80% or higher, subject to market
16 conditions.
17 (3) The department shall adhere to civil service rules and
18 regulations that state the standard biweekly work period for a
19 full-time employee in the classified service of this state is the
20 equivalent of 80 hours of work. The department shall establish
21 policies and processes to ensure all employees are working their
22 jobs during agreed upon business hours.
23 (4) The office of the state employer must create and implement
24 an occupancy utilization uniform policy on occupancy, utilization,
25 and in-person and remote work. The office of the state employer
26 must make each uniform policy publicly available on the
27 department's website.

29 assessment by July 1, 2027, using a form developed by DTMB, for all
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

819
1 space assigned under its building occupancy agreement and leased
2 office locations and post on their website. Agencies shall develop,
3 in coordination with DTMB, a plan to reduce, consolidate, or
4 otherwise optimize assigned space. The plans shall prioritize the
5 use of state-owned facilities, wherever possible, and comply with
6 DTMB-established space standards unless an exception is approved by
7 DTMB. As used in this section, "DTMB" means the department of
8 technology, management, and budget.

12 quantity totals less than 2 gallons.
13 Sec. 287. The department shall prioritize right-of-way permits
14 or easements for construction or maintenance of broadband
15 facilities on state land and shall not require a centerline survey
16 as a condition of the road right-of-way permit or easement if the
17 applicant can provide detailed engineering plans and if the
18 broadband facilities are contained completely in the right-of-way.
19 If the broadband provider secures a road right-of-way permit to
20 construct or maintain broadband facilities required by the
21 municipal, county, or state entity that owns or controls the public
22 road, the department shall not require the broadband provider to
23 obtain a permit or easement if the broadband facility is contained
24 completely within the road right-of-way. If installation of
25 broadband facilities cannot be contained completely within the
26 right-of-way and requires placement of the infrastructure on public
27 lands, an easement will be required, and a certified survey may be
28 required. If installation of broadband facilities being placed in
29 the road right-of-way requires utilization of public lands, a
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

826
1 permit may be required.

5 appropriated for the fiscal year ending September 30, 2027, and for

10 both application fees and transaction fees related to the exchange
11 or sale of state-owned land or rights in land authorized by part 21
12 of the natural resources and environmental protection act, 1994 PA
13 451, MCL 324.2101 to 324.2165. To the extent consistent with part
14 21, fees shall be set by the director at a rate that allows the
15 department to recover its costs for providing these services.

18 sources to pay vendor costs associated with administering sales of
19 carbon offset credits.
20 Sec. 305. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 from the
21 snowmobile trail improvement fund and the off-road vehicle trail
22 improvement fund, the department shall expend revenues from those
23 funds for administrative purposes only to the extent that those
24 administrative expenditures directly aid or benefit the statewide
25 snowmobile program or the statewide off-road vehicle program,
26 respectively.
27 (2) Administrative expenditures that directly aid or benefit
28 the statewide snowmobile program or the statewide off-road vehicle
29 program include salaries and fringes for program-specific field and
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

827
1 grant staff, trail inspections, contract and grant management, law
2 enforcement activities directly related to snowmobile or off-road
3 vehicle program operations, marketing and outreach activities
4 directly related to trail signage and public safety, information
5 technology services directly supporting trail operations or groomer
6 or ORV management systems, and accounting functions directly
7 related to the administration and payment of snowmobile or off-road
8 vehicle local grants.
9 (3) The department shall not charge or assess indirect costs,
10 internal service charges, or other administrative overhead to the
11 snowmobile trail improvement fund or the off-road vehicle trail
12 improvement fund except as permitted under subsection (2).
13 (4) Nothing in this section prohibits the expenditure of funds

7 expended unless the state budget director recommends the
8 expenditure and the department notifies the house and senate
9 committees on appropriations. Not later than December 1 each year,
10 the department shall provide a report to the senate and house
11 fiscal agencies and the state budget office on the use of the
12 disaster and emergency contingency fund during the prior fiscal
13 year.
14 (3) If Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA)
15 reimbursement is approved for costs paid from the disaster and
16 emergency contingency fund, the federal revenue shall be deposited
17 into the disaster and emergency contingency fund.
18
19 GRANTS

1 director on all amounts appropriated under this section during the

4 CAPITAL OUTLAY
5 Sec. 1103. The appropriations in part 1 for capital outlay
6 shall be carried forward at the end of the fiscal year consistent
7 with section 248 of the management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL
8 18.1248.
9
10 ONE-TIME APPROPRIATIONS
11 Sec. 1201. (1) Funds appropriated in part 1 for whitefish
12 recovery shall be used by the department to support efforts to
13 recover and monitor not more than 3 lake whitefish stocks.
14 (2) Funds appropriated under this section shall be used for
15 activities that include, but are not limited to, spawning and
16 rearing activities and poststocking assessment and evaluation
17 necessary to support lake whitefish stock recovery.
18 (3) The department may contract with or enter into agreements
19 with qualified nonprofit, research-based organizations to implement
20 a lake whitefish stock recovery plan developed by the department.
21 (4) Activities under this section shall be conducted using
22 department-owned hatchery facilities or facilities owned or
23 operated by partners contracted under subsection (3).
24 (5) Funds appropriated under this section shall not be used
25 for operational expenses or for the support of full-time equated
26 positions or permanent state positions. The department may utilize
27 limited-term employees as necessary to carry out the purposes of
28 this section.
29 Sec. 1202. Funds appropriated in part 1 for state water trail
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

832
1 organizations shall be distributed equally across the State's
2 water trails and may not be expended to employ permanent staff.

8 Sec. 201. In accordance with section 30 of article IX of the
9 state constitution of 1963, for fiscal year ending September 30,
10 2027, total state spending under part 1 from state sources is
11 $812,959,600.00 and total state spending under part 1 from state
12 sources to be paid to local units of government is $34,879,300.00.
13 The following itemized statement identifies appropriations from
14 which spending to local units of government will occur:
15 DEPARTMENT OF STATE POLICE

21 Sec. 202. The appropriations under this part and part 1 are
22 subject to the management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1101
23 to 18.1594.
24 Sec. 203. As used in this part and part 1:
25 (a) "AFIS" means the automated fingerprint identification
26 system.
27 (b) "CJIS" means Criminal Justice Information Systems.
28 (c) "Department" means the department of state police.
29 (d) "DHS" means the United States Department of Homeland
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

842
1 Security.
2 (e) "Director" means the director of the department.
3 (f) "DNA" means deoxyribonucleic acid.
4 (g) "DOJ" means the United States Department of Justice.
5 (h) "DOT" means the United States Department of
6 Transportation.
7 (i) "FTE" means full-time equated position in the classified
8 service of this state.
9 (j) "IDG" means interdepartmental grant.
10 (k) "LEIN" means the law enforcement information network.
11 (l) "MCOLES" means the Michigan commission on law enforcement
12 standards created in section 3 of the Michigan commission on law
13 enforcement standards act, 1965 PA 203, MCL 28.603.
14 (m) "SIGMA" means the statewide integrated governmental
15 management application.
16 (n) "SRMS" means the state records management system.
17 (o) "Standard report recipients" means the senate and house
18 appropriations committees, senate and house appropriations
19 subcommittees on state police, the senate and house fiscal
20 agencies, the senate and house policy offices, and the state budget
21 office.
22 Sec. 204. If the state administrative board, acting under
23 section 3 of 1921 PA 2, MCL 17.3, transfers funds from an amount
24 appropriated under part 1, the legislature may, by a concurrent
25 resolution adopted by a majority of the members elected to and
26 serving in each house, intertransfer funds within part 1 for the
27 particular department, board, commission, officer, or institution.
28 Sec. 205. (1) The department shall use the internet to fulfill
29 the reporting requirements of this part and shall make each report
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

843
1 readily accessible to the public and conspicuously post each
2 required report in a single archivable location on the department's
3 website not later than the due date required for each report.
4 (2) In addition to placing all reports required in the current
5 fiscal year on the department's website, the department shall
6 maintain on its website all reports placed on the website from
7 previous fiscal years posted by fiscal year in the same single
8 archivable location.
9 (3) The department shall transmit all required reports for the
10 current fiscal year to the standard report recipients and any other
11 required recipients by email. The email shall include a copy of the
12 report and a link to access the report online.

16 and long-term retention of records. The department may

18 by federal and state guidelines.

4 part, if the department is authorized under this part to expend
5 funds in addition to those appropriated in part 1, the department
6 shall do all of the following:

12 year that was authorized in part 2 of the article that made

15 (iii) The amount of funding received.

17 purposes.

22 following:

24 (ii) When the funding was received.
25 (iii) The amount of funding received.
26 (iv) The anticipated or actual amount to be spent and the
27 specified purpose or purposes.

29 chairpersons of the house and senate appropriations committees, the
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

845
1 house and senate fiscal agencies, and the state budget office with

3 anticipates it will receive in the subsequent fiscal year,

5 (i) The type or types of funding anticipated.
6 (ii) The amount or amounts of funding anticipated.
7 (iii) The purpose or purposes of the funding.
8 (2) If another reporting requirement under this part would
9 provide substantially similar information on a substantially
10 similar time frame as would be reported under subsection (1),

12 Sec. 209. Not later than December 15, the state budget office
13 shall prepare and submit a report that provides estimates of the

19 recipients and to the chairpersons of the senate and house
20 appropriations committees.

22 executive budget recommendation, the department shall cooperate

27 standard report recipients and to the chairpersons of the senate

ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

1 disclose on a publicly accessible website private and other third-
2 party funds received by the department in the previous fiscal year.

8 Sec. 212. Consistent with section 217 of the management and
9 budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1217, each department and agency
10 receiving appropriations in part 1 shall prepare a report on out-
11 of-state travel expenses not later than January 1 of each year. The
12 report must list all travel by classified and unclassified

14 funded in whole or in part with funds appropriated in the

16 submit the report to the standard report recipients and to the

21 occurrence and the proportions funded with state general
22 fund/general purpose revenues, state restricted revenues, federal
23 revenues, and other revenues.

1 employed by the department at the end of the reporting period. The

12 expend appropriations under part 1 until all existing authorized
13 work project funds available for the same purposes are exhausted.
14 Sec. 220. Not later than 6 months after the state budget
15 office issues work project letters, and again on or by April 15,
16 the department shall submit an annual report that summarizes all
17 work project accounts. The report must include the following:
18 (a) A list of all work project accounts.

20 expended, amounts encumbered, and available balances for each
21 account.

24 work project account, and the funds that received the lapsed
25 amounts.
26 Sec. 221. To the extent permissible under section 261 of the
27 management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1261, all of the
28 following apply to the expenditure of funds appropriated in part 1:
29 (a) The funds must not be used for the purchase of foreign
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

849
1 goods or services, or both, if competitively priced and of
2 comparable quality American goods or services, or both, are
3 available.
4 (b) Preference must be given to goods or services, or both,
5 manufactured or provided by Michigan businesses, if they are
6 competitively priced and of comparable quality.
7 (c) Preference must be given to goods or services, or both,
8 that are manufactured or provided by Michigan businesses owned and
9 operated by veterans, if they are competitively priced and of
10 comparable quality.
11 Sec. 222. The department shall not take disciplinary action
12 against an employee of the department for communicating with a
13 member of the legislature or legislative staff, unless the
14 communication is prohibited by law and the department is exercising
15 its authority as provided by law.
16 Sec. 223. (1) The department shall maximize utilization of its
17 in-person state workforce. The department shall prioritize
18 occupancy utilization of office space for each division within the
19 department. Employees with job responsibilities that require the
20 employees to serve in their capacities outside of an office shall
21 be monitored each pay period to ensure all work hours reported on
22 the timesheet were actually worked.
23 (2) The department shall comply with requirements set forth by
24 the office of the state employer on in-person work and utilization
25 and occupancy rates of state buildings to ensure in-person work is
26 optimized and occupancy rates are 80% or higher, subject to market
27 conditions.
28 (3) The department shall adhere to civil service rules and
29 regulations that state the standard biweekly work period for a
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

850
1 full-time employee in the classified service of this state is the
2 equivalent of 80.0 hours of work. The department shall establish
3 policies and processes to ensure all employees are working their
4 jobs during agreed-upon business hours.
5 (4) The office of the state employer must create and implement
6 an occupancy utilization uniform policy on occupancy, utilization,
7 and in-person and remote work. The office of the state employer
8 must make each uniform policy publicly available on the
9 department's website.
10 Sec. 224. Each agency shall complete a space utilization
11 assessment by July 1, 2027, using a form developed by the
12 department of technology, management, and budget, for all space
13 assigned under its building occupancy agreement and leased office
14 locations and post on their website. Agencies shall develop, in
15 coordination with the department of technology, management, and
16 budget a plan to reduce, consolidate, or otherwise optimize
17 assigned space. The plans shall prioritize the use of state-owned
18 facilities, wherever possible, and comply with space standards
19 established by the department of technology, management, and budget
20 unless an exception is approved by the department of technology,
21 management, and budget.
22 Sec. 225. To the extent permissible under the management and
23 budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1101 to 18.1594, the director of
24 each department or agency receiving appropriations in part 1 shall
25 take all reasonable steps to ensure that geographically
26 disadvantaged business enterprises compete for and perform
27 contracts to provide services or supplies, or both. The director
28 shall strongly encourage firms with which the department contracts
29 to subcontract with geographically disadvantaged business
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

851
1 enterprises for services or supplies, or both. As used in this
2 section, "geographically disadvantaged business enterprises" means
3 that term as defined in Executive Directive No. 2019-8.
4 Sec. 226. Not later than December 31, 2026, and again by June
5 1, 2027, the department must provide a report to the standard
6 report recipients that includes details regarding any federal
7 guidelines, rules, regulations, or other significant federal policy
8 changes, including H.R. 1 and the Rural Health Transformation
9 Program, that do, or are expected to, significantly impact the
10 operations of the department, including increases or reductions in
11 federal revenue and changes that are likely to improve or impede
12 the department's ability to safeguard the health or welfare of the
13 public.
14 Sec. 227. (1) Within 30 days after enactment of this act, the
15 house and senate shall provide to the state budget office a jointly
16 agreed-upon list of legislatively directed spending items funded in
17 part 1 as defined in section 364 of the management and budget act,
18 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1364. The list must include all information and
19 documents pertaining to the funded items as publicly disclosed in

21 act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1364 and 18.1364a.
22 (2) In accordance with section 364(4) of the management and
23 budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1364, the department or agency
24 administering the grant shall post a report in a publicly
25 accessible location on its website beginning March 15 of the
26 current fiscal year. The department or agency shall update the
27 report and shall post an updated report not later than June 15 of
28 the current fiscal year, and again not later than September 15 of
29 the current fiscal year. The department shall include in the report
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

852
1 the most comprehensive information the department has available at
2 the time of posting for grants awarded.
3 Sec. 228. The state budget director shall take steps to ensure
4 that all state fiscal recovery funds allocated to this state under
5 the American rescue plan act of 2021, Public Law 117-2, are
6 expended by December 31, 2026, as required by law. Any state fiscal
7 recovery funds that would otherwise lapse after September 30, 2026,
8 are automatically reappropriated for the same purpose as originally
9 authorized and available for expenditure through December 31, 2026,
10 and any subsequent financial closeout period.
11 Sec. 229. (1) The state budget director shall take steps to
12 ensure that all state fiscal recovery funds allocated to this state
13 under the American rescue plan act of 2021, Public Law 117-2, are
14 expended by December 31, 2026, as required by law. The state budget
15 director may reallocate appropriated funds for the purpose of fully
16 utilizing state fiscal recovery funds that are in jeopardy of not
17 meeting the expenditure deadline for reasons that may include, but
18 are not limited to, completed projects coming in under budget or
19 funds unable to be fully used by subrecipients. The state budget
20 director shall reallocate any of the funds reallocated under this
21 subsection to the programs or purposes specified in this section.
22 Any funds reallocated are unappropriated and immediately
23 reappropriated for the following purposes:
24 (a) To reclassify general fund/general purpose appropriations
25 for payroll and covered benefits for eligible public health and
26 safety employees at the department of corrections.
27 (b) To reclassify general fund/general purpose appropriations
28 for payroll and covered benefits for eligible public health and
29 safety employees at the department of state police.

4 Sec. 231. The department must establish a policy for
5 conducting precontract risk assessments to evaluate contractor
6 financial risk, security risks, and insurance requirements before
7 contract execution. The department must report back to the standard
8 report recipients by March 31, 2027 on the assessments implemented

10 under the assessments.
11 Sec. 233. The department shall submit a biannual report on the
12 performance metrics cited or information required to be reported in
13 this part, reasons for nonachievement of metric targets, and
14 proposed corrective actions.
15 Sec. 234. (1) It is the intent of the legislature that the
16 department shall take all steps necessary to protect the data and
17 privacy of citizens who are not the focus of a departmental
18 investigation and to protect personal information from unauthorized
19 access or misuse. The protection required under this subsection
20 includes, but is not limited to, all of the following:
21 (a) Requiring vendors or service providers to protect data
22 shared with them.
23 (b) Ensuring that when personal data is collected, but no
24 longer utilized by the department, that reasonable steps be taken
25 to securely destroy records containing personal information when it
26 is to be discarded so that the information is rendered
27 indecipherable and is not sold for marketing or other purposes.
28 (2) The department shall provide written notification to any
29 data subject whose sensitive personal information is accessed or
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

855
1 acquired by an unauthorized person.
2 Sec. 235. (1) The department may accept monetary and
3 nonmonetary gifts, bequests, donations, contributions, or grants
4 from any private or public source to support, in whole or in part,
5 a departmental function or program. The department shall expend or
6 use the gifts, bequests, donations, contributions, or grants
7 accepted under this subsection for the purposes designated by the
8 private or public source, if the purpose is specified.
9 (2) Revenue collected by the department under this section
10 that is unexpended and unencumbered must not lapse to the general
11 fund but must be carried forward to the subsequent fiscal year.
12 (3) Private revenues received under this section that exceed
13 the appropriations in part 1 are appropriated and may be received
14 and expended by the department for the purposes for which the funds
15 are received.
16 (4) If additional authorization is approved in SIGMA by the
17 state budget office under this section, the department shall notify
18 the senate and house appropriations subcommittees on state police
19 and the senate and house fiscal agencies within 10 days after the
20 approval. The notification must include the amount and funding
21 source of the additional authorization, the date of the approval,
22 and the projected use of the funds to be expended.

24 from the federal government in excess of the appropriations in part
25 1 are appropriated and may be received and expended by the
26 department for purposes authorized under state law and subject to

12 DEPARTMENTAL ADMINISTRATION AND SUPPORT
13 Sec. 301. The department shall notify the standard report
14 recipients when it recommends to close or consolidate any state
15 police post. The notification must include a local and state impact
16 study of the proposed post closure or consolidation.
17 Sec. 302. If the department presents a plan to the state
18 employer to privatize, the department shall submit a complete
19 project plan to the standard report recipients. The plan must
20 include the criteria under which the privatization initiative will
21 be evaluated. The evaluation must be completed and submitted to the
22 standard report recipients within 30 months.
23 Sec. 303. (1) When the department provides contractual
24 services to a local unit of government, the department shall be
25 reimbursed for all costs incurred in providing the services.
26 (2) The department shall define service cost models for those
27 services requiring reimbursement.
28 (3) Contractual services provided to an entity other than a
29 local unit of government may be provided by department personnel,
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

857
1 but only on an overtime basis outside the normal work schedule of
2 the personnel. All costs incurred in providing the services are
3 eligible for reimbursement.
4 (4) This section does not apply to services provided to state
5 agencies.
6 (5) Revenues received for contractual or reimbursed services
7 in excess of the appropriations in part 1 are appropriated and may
8 be received and expended by the department for the purposes for
9 which the funds are received.
10 (6) If additional authorization is approved in SIGMA by the
11 state budget office under this section, the department shall notify
12 the senate and house appropriations subcommittees on state police
13 and the senate and house fiscal agencies within 10 days after the
14 approval. The notification must include the amount and funding
15 source of the additional authorization, the date of its approval,
16 and the projected use of the funds to be expended.
17 Sec. 304. The department may establish and collect fees for
18 publications, videos, conferences, workshops, and related
19 materials. Fees collected under this section must be used to offset
20 expenditures for costs of the publications, videos, workshops,
21 conferences, and related materials. The department shall not
22 collect fees under this section that exceed the cost of the
23 expenditures.

28 corresponds to the provisions of the Michigan vehicle code, 1949 PA

10 laws and regulations, the department shall maintain and ensure

18 to enforce traffic laws as provided in section 629e of the Michigan
19 vehicle code, 1949 PA 300, MCL 257.629e, are not prohibited from
20 responding to crimes in progress or other emergency situations and
21 are responsible for making every effort to protect all residents of
22 this state.
23 (2) The department shall maintain the staffing and resources
24 necessary to continually work to enhance traffic safety throughout
25 this state and shall dedicate a minimum of 455,200 hours to
26 statewide patrol. The department shall work to improve public
27 safety efforts within distressed cities by enhancing data analysis
28 capabilities and identifying crime trends and areas with high
29 occurrence of crime.

1 (3) The department shall report on the number of residence
2 checks of registered sex offenders conducted, as provided under

4 Sec. 602. (1) The department shall identify and apprehend
5 criminals through criminal investigations in this state.
6 (2) The department shall maintain the staffing and resources
7 necessary to provide a comparable number of hours investigating
8 crimes as the average annual number provided during the preceding 5
9 fiscal years.
10 (3) The department shall maintain the staffing and resources
11 necessary to annually meet or exceed a case clearance rate of 62%.
12 (4) The department shall provide training opportunities to
13 local law enforcement partners with the goal of increasing their
14 knowledge of gambling laws, legal issues, opioid-related
15 investigations, and other emerging law enforcement issues.
16 (5) The department shall maintain the staffing and resources
17 necessary to investigate the average annual number of opioid-
18 related investigations conducted by multijurisdictional task forces
19 and hometown security teams during the preceding 5 fiscal years.
20 The department shall work to enhance investigative and drug
21 interdiction efforts by enhancing data analysis capabilities and
22 linking investigations among multijurisdictional task forces and
23 hometown security teams.

25 state, its economy, welfare, and vital state-sponsored programs
26 through the prevention and suppression of organized smuggling of

1 and by maintaining a tobacco tax enforcement unit.

4 appropriations subcommittees on general government that details

6 the previous fiscal year.
7 Sec. 604. The department shall provide fire investigation
8 training and investigative assistance to public safety agencies in
9 this state.

11 provide, upon request, assistance or mutual aid to a law
12 enforcement agency that is actively responding to any of the

28 SPECIALIZED SERVICES
29 Sec. 701. (1) The department shall operate the Michigan
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

870
1 intelligence operations center for homeland security as this
2 state's primary federally designated fusion center to receive,
3 analyze, gather, and disseminate threat-related information among
4 federal, state, local, tribal, and private sector partners.
5 (2) The department shall ensure public safety by providing
6 public and private sector partners with timely and accurate
7 information regarding critical information key resource threats, as
8 reported to or discovered by the Michigan intelligence operations
9 center for homeland security, and shall increase public awareness
10 on how to report suspicious activity through website or telephone
11 communications.
12 (3) The department shall maintain the staffing and resources
13 necessary to support the cyber section, including the Michigan
14 cyber command center, the computer crimes unit, and the internet
15 crimes against children task force. The department shall maintain
16 the staffing and resources necessary to complete the average annual
17 number of cases completed by the computer crimes unit during the
18 preceding 5 fiscal years. The computer crimes unit shall pursue
19 process improvement initiatives to effectively utilize staff
20 resources in providing investigatory assistance and evidentiary
21 analysis for law enforcement and criminal justice agencies
22 statewide. The department shall maintain the staffing and resources
23 necessary to complete the average annual casework that the Michigan
24 cyber command center completed during the preceding 5 fiscal years.
25 (4) The department shall maintain the staffing and resources
26 necessary to provide digital forensic analysis services with a goal
27 of decreasing backlogs of digital forensic analysis cases annually
28 until the department maintains a 60-day turnaround time.
29 Sec. 702. (1) The department shall provide specialized
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

871
1 services in support of, and to enhance, local, state, and federal
2 law enforcement operations within this state, in accordance with
3 all applicable state and federal laws and regulations.
4 (2) The department shall maintain the staffing and resources
5 necessary to provide training to maintain readiness to respond
6 appropriately to at least the average annual number of requests for
7 specialty services which occurred during the preceding 5 fiscal
8 years.
9 (3) The canine unit shall be available for call out statewide
10 100% of the time.
11 (4) The bomb squad unit shall be available for call out
12 statewide 100% of the time.
13 (5) The emergency support teams shall be available for call
14 out statewide 100% of the time.
15 (6) The marine services team shall be available for call out
16 statewide 100% of the time.

18 statewide 100% of the time, unless prohibited by weather or
19 unexpected mechanical breakdowns.

24 28.6c. The department shall also maintain the staff and resources

1 facilities.
2 Sec. 703. (1) The department shall maintain commercial vehicle
3 regulation, school bus inspections, and enforcement activities,
4 including enforcement of requirements concerning size, weight, and
5 load restrictions; operating authority; registration; fuel taxes;
6 transportation of hazardous materials; new entrant operations;
7 commercial driver licenses; and inspections pursuant to the federal
8 motor carrier assistance program.

10 necessary to meet inspection goals consistent with the department's
11 federal motor carrier assistance program activities.
12 (3) Revenue collected under the motor carrier act, 1933 PA
13 254, MCL 475.1 to 479.42, must be expended in accordance with that
14 act. Unexpended and unencumbered revenues must not lapse to the
15 general fund but must be carried forward into the subsequent fiscal
16 year.
17 Sec. 704. (1) The department shall coordinate the mitigation,
18 preparation, response, and recovery activities of municipal,
19 county, state, and federal governments, and other governmental
20 entities, for all hazards, disasters, and emergencies.
21 (2) The state director of emergency management may expend
22 money appropriated under part 1 to call on any agency or department
23 of this state or any resource of this state to protect life or
24 property or to provide for the health or safety of the population
25 in any area of this state in which the governor proclaims a state
26 of emergency or state of disaster under the emergency management
27 act, 1976 PA 390, MCL 30.401 to 30.421. The state director of
28 emergency management may expend the amounts the director considers
29 necessary to accomplish these purposes. The director shall submit
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

873
1 to the state budget director, as soon as possible, a complete
2 report of all actions taken under the authority of this section.
3 The report must contain, as a separate item, a statement of all
4 money expended that is not reimbursable from federal funding. The
5 state budget director shall review the expenditures and submit
6 recommendations to the legislature in regard to any possible need
7 for a supplemental appropriation.
8 (3) In addition to the funds appropriated in part 1, the
9 department may receive and expend money from local, private,
10 federal, or state sources for the purpose of providing emergency
11 management training to local or private interests and for the
12 purpose of supporting emergency preparedness, response, recovery,
13 and mitigation activity. If additional expenditure authorization in
14 SIGMA is approved by the state budget office under this section,
15 the department and the state budget office shall notify the senate
16 and house appropriations subcommittees on state police and the
17 senate and house fiscal agencies within 10 days after the approval.
18 The notification must include the amount and source of the
19 additional authorization, the date of its approval, and the
20 projected use of the funds to be expended under the authorization.
21 The total amount of federal revenues that may be received and
22 expended under this section and section 236 must not exceed
23 $750,000,000.00.
24 (4) The department shall foster, promote, and maintain
25 partnerships to protect this state and homeland from all hazards.
26 (5) The department shall maintain the staffing and resources
27 necessary to do all of the following:
28 (a) Serve approximately 105 local emergency management
29 preparedness programs and 88 local emergency planning committees in
ERE S06102'26 (S-1) CR-1 pgymri

874
1 this state.
2 (b) Operate and maintain the state's emergency operations
3 center and provide command and control in support of emergency
4 response services.
5 (c) Maintain readiness, including training and equipment to
6 respond to civil disorders and natural disasters commensurate with
7 the capabilities of fiscal year 2010-2011.
8 (d) Perform hazardous materials response training.
9 (6) The department shall conduct a minimum of 3 training
10 sessions to enhance safe response in the event of natural or
11 manmade incidents, emergencies, or disasters.

13 appropriated from the disaster and emergency contingency fund an

24 report required under this subsection must be submitted within 30
25 days after the end of the month during which funds from the
26 disaster and emergency contingency fund were expended.

1 including the protection status of critical infrastructure items
